docs.title: Welcome to My Frontend Guide (  )
docs.text: This guide will walk you through everything you need to know to build modern, scalable, and type-safe web applications using React, TypeScript, and Next.js.

docs.title: 📘 What You’ll Learn
docs.text:
You'll explore:

  • ✅ Core React principles, history, and why it’s the UI library of choice.
  • 🧱 How to build reusable components with JSX, props, state, and hooks.
  • 🔄 The Virtual DOM and how React efficiently handles UI updates.
  • ⚙️ Modern features like React Fiber, Concurrent Rendering, and Suspense.
  • 🧠 TypeScript fundamentals and how it improves developer productivity.
  • ✅ Typing React props, context, hooks, and reducers using TypeScript.
  • 🧩 Combining React with TypeScript for scalable, bug-resistant code.
  • 🚀 Building full-stack React apps with Next.js.
  • 🧭 Mastering Next.js features: file-based routing, SSR, SSG, ISR, and Server Components.
  • 🗃️ Using API routes, database integration, and deploying with Vercel.
  • 🔁 Comparing React to other frameworks (Vue, Angular, Svelte).

docs.title: React: The UI Library
docs.text:
React is a powerful JavaScript library for building user interfaces, focusing on component-based architecture and a declarative approach.

In this section, you’ll learn:

  • What React is and how it differs from other frameworks.
  • Creating components with JSX.
  • Using state and props to manage dynamic data.
  • The power of hooks: useState, useEffect, useContext, and more.
  • One-way data flow and how it simplifies app logic.
  • The Virtual DOM and performance benefits.

docs.title: TypeScript: Static Typing for JavaScript
docs.text:
TypeScript adds type safety to JavaScript, helping you catch errors early and refactor confidently.

You’ll learn:

  • TypeScript syntax: types, interfaces, enums, and generics.
  • Type checking functions, arrays, objects, and more.
  • Writing reusable, type-safe utilities and components.
  • Working with external libraries and @types.
  • Using tsconfig.json to customize your build settings.
  • Setting up React + TypeScript projects.
  • Typing props, state, refs, and context.
  • Strongly typing useReducer and custom hooks.
  • Handling form validation and API calls with TypeScript.
  • Creating scalable patterns with reusable types.

docs.title: Next.js: The React Framework
docs.text:
Next.js builds on React by adding routing, data fetching, API routes, and full-stack capabilities—all with great defaults and performance.

With Next.js, you’ll learn:

  • App routing: pages vs App Router, dynamic and nested routes.
  • Static Generation (SSG), Server-Side Rendering (SSR), and Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR).
  • Server Components and client hydration in the new App directory.
  • Creating API routes and integrating databases (e.g., Prisma).
  • Authentication with NextAuth.js and using middleware.
  • Optimizing and deploying to Vercel.

Whether you're just starting out or leveling up, this guide gives you a solid foundation for building high-performance front-end applications.

docs.title: 🗂 Repository Board

docs.title: 📌 Structure
docs.text:
Learning are grouped into sections:

  • Hooks → useState, useEffect, useReducer, etc.
  • Routing → React Router, navigation patterns, etc.
  • Data Fetching and Caching → Axios, JSON Server, React Query, etc.
  • State Management → Context API, Redux, Zustand, etc.

docs.title: 🎯 Goal
docs.text:
The goal of this journey is to:

  • Track learning progress in an organized way
  • Practice React fundamentals through projects
  • Build a portfolio of React apps that demonstrate different concepts

✨ Follow along as I keep adding projects and leveling up my React skills!

docs.title: Welcome to My Blog
docs.text: Hi there! 👋 Welcome to my collection of blog posts. Here, I share my thoughts, tutorials, experiments, and anything else that sparks my interest. Whether you're here to learn something new or just explore, I hope you find something valuable.

docs.title: These are my blog posts — feel free to explore
👋 Welcome to My Frontend Guide
docs.text:
Fast, simple, and beautiful documentation — powered by MkDocs + Material.

This is my living, open notebook for learning modern frontend development with React, TypeScript, and the surrounding ecosystem. I write these pages as I learn, so they double as a personal reference and (hopefully) a useful resource for anyone walking the same path.

docs.title: 📚 What you'll find here
docs.text:
• React Docs — components, state, hooks, forms, routing, and state management (Redux Toolkit, Zustand).
  • API Integration — Fetch & Axios, authentication, TanStack Query, real-time data, security, and file handling.
  • TypeScript Docs — from the basics through generics, advanced types, and React + TypeScript patterns.

Use the tabs in the top navigation to jump between sections, and the search box (press F or S) to find anything quickly.

docs.title: 💡 Why a guide?
docs.text:
• ✅ Written while building real projects — see the React Learning Journey.
  • 🎨 Styled with Material for MkDocs.
  • 🌓 Dark and light themes — toggle with the icon in the header.
  • 📄 Plain Markdown, version-controlled, and continuously deployed.

Following along

Each chapter is numbered. If you're new, start at Introduction to React and work your way down — but feel free to jump straight to whatever you need.

docs.title: Introduction to Next.js
docs.text:
Plain React gives you a component model and nothing else. The moment you need routing, server rendering, data fetching, bundling, and a deployment story, you assemble those yourself from a dozen libraries. Next.js is the framework that packages all of it—and, crucially, lets you render on the server so your HTML ships fast and is fully crawlable.

This chapter introduces Next.js: what it is, what it adds over plain React, when to reach for it, a quick recap of the rendering strategies (CSR / SSR / SSG / ISR), the two routers (App vs Pages), and how to scaffold and run a project. We target Next.js 15 with the App Router and TypeScript throughout.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs · https://react.dev/learn/start-a-new-react-project

Builds on the React section

This assumes you're comfortable with React components, hooks, and the rendering-strategy concepts. If SSR/SSG/ISR or React Server Components are new, read React → SSR & SSG first; this chapter recaps them but doesn't re-teach them from scratch.

docs.title: 1. What is Next.js?
docs.text:
Next.js is a full-stack React framework, maintained by Vercel, built on top of React. Where a tool like Vite gives you a fast dev server and a bundler and leaves the rest to you, Next.js is opinionated: it provides a complete, integrated set of answers to the questions every real React app eventually asks.

You still write React components. Next.js adds the framework around them.

docs.title: What it adds over plain React
docs.text:
A "plain React" app (Vite, or the old Create React App) is a client-side single-page app: the browser downloads a JS bundle and React builds the page on the user's device. That's it—no server, no routing, no rendering choices. Next.js layers the following on top:

Capability Plain React (Vite/CRA) Next.js Routing Add React Router, configure routes manually File-system routing—folders are routes Rendering Client-side only (CSR) CSR, SSR, SSG, ISR—chosen per route Server code Separate backend project Route Handlers & Server Actions in the same app Data fetching useEffect + a client library await directly inside Server Components Server Components Not available Default—components that ship zero JS Image optimization DIY / external service next/image (resize, lazy-load, modern formats) Font optimization Manual <link> tags next/font (self-hosted, no layout shift) Code splitting Manual React.lazy Automatic per-route + next/dynamic SEO / metadata react-helmet or manual Built-in Metadata API Deployment Build static files, host an API yourself Zero-config on Vercel; adapters elsewhere

The headline additions are server rendering and React Server Components. Plain React can only run in the browser; Next.js can run your components on the server—at build time, at request time, or a blend—so users (and crawlers) receive real HTML instead of an empty <div id="root">.

docs.title: Why use it
docs.text:
• Routing — Create a folder and a page.tsx file and you have a route. Nested layouts, dynamic segments, and loading/error states are all file conventions, not boilerplate.
  • Rendering — Each route picks how its HTML is produced (static, dynamic, or streamed), so you get the right trade-off of speed, freshness, and cost per page.
  • Data — Server Components fetch data with plain async/await, with a built-in caching layer. Mutations use Server Actions—no hand-written API for forms.
  • Optimizations — Images, fonts, scripts, and code splitting are optimized by default, which directly improves Core Web Vitals.
  • Deployment — A Next.js app deploys to Vercel with zero config (SSR, ISR, and API routes become serverless/edge functions automatically), and to other hosts via adapters or a Node server.

Next.js is React, not an alternative to it

You don't choose between React and Next.js. Next.js is a way to build React apps—it adds the framework scaffolding that React deliberately leaves out. If you know React, you already know most of Next.js.

docs.title: 2. Why and when to use it
docs.text: Next.js is the default choice for most production React apps today, but it isn't mandatory. Match the tool to the job.

docs.title: Reach for Next.js when…
docs.text:
• SEO matters — marketing sites, blogs, docs, e-commerce, landing pages. Pre-rendered HTML gives crawlers and social scrapers complete content and Open Graph tags immediately.
  • You want fast first paint — server-rendered or static HTML shows real content before any app JS runs, which helps LCP on slow networks and low-end devices.
  • You need a backend too — Route Handlers and Server Actions let you build the API in the same project, no separate server.
  • You're deploying full-stack — auth, databases, server-side data fetching, and a frontend in one deployable unit.
  • You want batteries included — image/font optimization, routing, and caching without wiring up libraries.

docs.title: A lighter tool may be enough when…
docs.text:
• It's a purely client-side, auth-gated app with no SEO needs—an internal admin dashboard behind a login. A Vite SPA is simpler and cheaper to host.
  • You're building a component library or embeddable widget, not an application.
  • You need an existing non-React backend and just want a frontend bundler—Vite is leaner.

SSR is not automatically faster end-to-end

Server rendering adds server work on every request plus a hydration step on the client. A poorly-cached SSR page with a heavy data fetch can have a worse time-to-first-byte than a static SPA. Next.js wins on first contentful paint and SEO, not unconditionally on total cost—prefer static/ISR whenever the data doesn't truly need to be per-request.

docs.title: 3. Rendering strategies recap
docs.text: The rendering strategy answers: who turns my components into HTML, and at what moment? Next.js supports all four of the common strategies, and in the App Router you choose per route—not once for the whole app.

docs.title: CSR — Client-Side Rendering
docs.text:
The browser downloads a near-empty HTML shell plus a JS bundle, then React builds the DOM on the user's device. The classic SPA model.

  • ✅ Cheap to host, great for highly interactive, auth-gated apps where SEO is irrelevant.
  • ❌ Blank screen until JS loads and runs; weaker SEO; performance depends on the user's device.

docs.title: SSR — Server-Side Rendering
docs.text:
The server runs your components on every request and returns fully-formed HTML, which the browser then hydrates into an interactive page.

  • ✅ Always-fresh, personalized content; good SEO.
  • ❌ Server compute on every request → higher cost and latency than serving a static file.

docs.title: SSG — Static Site Generation
docs.text:
HTML is generated once at build time and served from a CDN. Every visitor gets the same pre-built file.

  • ✅ Fastest delivery, cheapest hosting, excellent SEO.
  • ❌ Data is frozen at build time; rebuilding a huge site is slow.

docs.title: ISR — Incremental Static Regeneration
docs.text:
SSG's pragmatic upgrade: pages are static but regenerate in the background on a schedule or on demand, without a full rebuild. CDN speed with data that stays reasonably fresh.

  • ✅ Static performance with content that updates—ideal for blogs, docs, catalogs.
  • ❌ Visitors may briefly see stale content until the next regeneration completes.

docs.title: Where Next.js fits
docs.text:
Strategy HTML generated Data freshness Best for CSR In the browser Live (client fetch) Auth-only apps, dashboards SSR Per request on the server Always fresh Personalized, request-time data SSG Once at build Frozen at build Marketing, docs, rarely-changing content ISR At build, refreshed in background Stale-then-fresh Blogs, catalogs, content that changes sometimes

In the App Router you don't pick one of these for the whole site. A route is static by default and becomes dynamic when it reads request-time data (cookies, headers, an uncached fetch); ISR is a per-route or per-fetch revalidate setting; and you can even stream a static shell with dynamic regions inside it. Think of CSR/SSR/SSG/ISR as a spectrum, not four separate buckets.

The throughline: ship less JavaScript

Across SEO and Core Web Vitals, the recurring win is shipping less JS to the client. React Server Components (the App Router default) render data-only components on the server and send zero JS for them—making "less JS" the default rather than an optimization you bolt on later.

The full mechanics of these strategies—hydration, caching, generateStaticParams, revalidate—are covered in Rendering and Data Fetching & Caching later in this section.

docs.title: 4. App Router vs Pages Router
docs.text: Next.js has two routers, and which one you use determines the entire programming model. You'll meet both, but they are not equal in 2025.

docs.title: Pages Router (legacy)
docs.text: The original model, living in a pages/ directory. Routing is file-based, but rendering is configured with special exported functions—getServerSideProps (SSR), getStaticProps + getStaticPaths (SSG/ISR)—and API endpoints live in pages/api/. It predates React Server Components. It's still fully supported and extremely common in existing codebases.

docs.title: App Router (default & recommended)
docs.text:
Introduced in Next.js 13, stable since 13.4, and the default in 14 and 15. It lives in an app/ directory and is built on React Server Components.

  • Components are Server Components by default—they run only on the server, can be async, can await data directly, and ship zero JavaScript to the browser. Opt into the client with "use client".
  • Data fetching is just await inside a component—no getServerSideProps/getStaticProps.
  • Nested layouts let route segments share UI that persists across navigation without re-rendering.
  • Streaming and Suspense are first-class via loading.tsx and <Suspense>.
  • Mutations use Server Actions ("use server"), often without any hand-written API endpoint.

docs.title: Why the App Router is recommended for new code
docs.text:
Concern Pages Router App Router Data fetching getServerSideProps / getStaticProps async Server Component + await Server Components Not available Default Layouts One _app.tsx; manual per-page wrappers Nested layout.tsx, persist across navigation Loading / error UI Manual loading.tsx / error.tsx conventions Streaming Limited Built in via Suspense Mutations API route + client fetch Server Actions Status Maintained, legacy Default & actively developed

Don't mix them carelessly—and prefer one model

A single project can contain both app/ and pages/ (Next.js routes app/ first), which is useful for incrementally migrating. But for new projects, use the App Router exclusively. The Pages Router data functions (getServerSideProps, etc.) do not exist in the App Router—they're replaced by async components, fetch caching, generateStaticParams, and revalidate. This chapter and the rest of the section are App Router-first; Pages Router is noted only where it still matters for existing code.

docs.title: 5. Creating a project
docs.text: The official scaffolding tool is create-next-app. It sets up the project structure, TypeScript, linting, and dependencies for you.

docs.title: create-next-app with TypeScript
docs.text:
Run the interactive scaffolder with npx (no global install needed):

npx create-next-app@latest my-app

It will ask a series of questions. For a modern App Router + TypeScript project, answer roughly like this:

✔ Would you like to use TypeScript? … Yes
✔ Would you like to use ESLint? … Yes
✔ Would you like to use Tailwind CSS? … (your choice)
✔ Would you like your code inside a `src/` directory? … (your choice)
✔ Would you like to use App Router? (recommended) … Yes
✔ Would you like to use Turbopack? (recommended) … Yes
✔ Would you like to customize the import alias (`@/*`)? … No

To skip the prompts and accept those defaults non-interactively, pass flags:

npx create-next-app@latest my-app \
  --typescript \
  --eslint \
  --app \
  --src-dir \
  --import-alias "@/*" \
  --use-npm

Then start the dev server:

cd my-app
npm run dev

Open http://localhost:3000 and you'll see the starter page.

The @/* import alias

create-next-app configures a path alias in tsconfig.json so you can write import { db } from "@/lib/db" instead of brittle ../../../lib/db relative paths. With --src-dir, @/* points at src/*; without it, at the project root. Examples in this guide use the @/ alias.

docs.title: Project structure & file conventions
docs.text:
A fresh App Router project (with src/) looks roughly like this:

my-app/
├── src/
│   └── app/                 # App Router — folders are routes
│       ├── layout.tsx       # Root layout (required) — wraps <html>/<body>
│       ├── page.tsx         # UI for "/"
│       ├── globals.css      # Global styles
│       └── favicon.ico
├── public/                  # Static assets served from "/"
├── next.config.ts           # Next.js configuration
├── tsconfig.json            # TypeScript config (incl. the @/* alias)
├── eslint.config.mjs        # ESLint flat config
├── package.json
└── next-env.d.ts            # Next.js TS types (auto-generated, don't edit)

app/ is where your routing and UI live. Folders define URL segments; specially-named files give those segments behavior. public/ holds static files (images, fonts, robots.txt) served from the root—public/logo.png is reachable at /logo.png.

The power of the App Router is in its special files. Each is a convention: drop a file with the right name into a route folder and Next.js wires it up automatically.

File Purpose layout.tsx Shared UI wrapping a segment and its children; persists across navigation. The root layout is required. page.tsx The unique UI for a route (makes the segment publicly routable). loading.tsx Instant loading UI for a segment, shown via an automatic Suspense boundary while it loads. error.tsx Error boundary for a segment (must be a Client Component). not-found.tsx UI rendered for notFound() calls or unmatched routes. template.tsx Like a layout, but re-mounts on every navigation (fresh state each time). route.ts A Route Handler—an API endpoint, using Web Request/Response. [folder] A dynamic route segment, e.g. [slug] → /blog/:slug. (folder) A route group—organizes files without affecting the URL. _folder A private folder—opted out of routing (for colocated helpers/components).

Here's the minimal pair that every App Router app starts with—a root layout and a home page:

// src/app/layout.tsx — the required root layout
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import "./globals.css";

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "My App",
  description: "Built with Next.js 15 and the App Router",
};

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: Readonly<{ children: React.ReactNode }>) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

// src/app/page.tsx — a Server Component (no "use client"), the UI for "/"
export default function HomePage() {
  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Hello, Next.js 15 👋</h1>
      <p>Edit this file and the page updates instantly.</p>
    </main>
  );
}

The root layout is mandatory and owns <html>/<body>

Every App Router app must have app/layout.tsx, and it must render the <html> and <body> tags—Next.js does not add them for you. Don't add a <head> tag manually; use the Metadata API (the metadata export above) so Next.js manages it. Routing, layouts, and the full set of special files get their own deep dive in the Routing chapter.

docs.title: Dev / build / start scripts and Turbopack
docs.text:
create-next-app adds these scripts to package.json:

{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev --turbopack",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "lint": "next lint"
  }
}
Command What it does npm run dev Starts the development server (default port 3000) with Hot Module Replacement and Fast Refresh. Use this while coding. npm run build Creates an optimized production build in .next/—bundles, minifies, and pre-renders static routes. npm run start Serves the production build (next start). Run build first; this is what runs in production on a Node server. npm run lint Runs ESLint over the project.

The dev and production servers are distinct: next dev prioritizes fast feedback (no minification, instant updates), while next build + next start produces the optimized output you actually ship.

Turbopack is Next.js's Rust-based bundler, the successor to Webpack. It's the default for next dev in Next.js 15 (hence the --turbopack flag in the dev script) and delivers dramatically faster cold starts and HMR on large apps. Production builds (next build --turbopack) are also available and increasingly stable in Next.js 15.

# Dev with Turbopack (the create-next-app default)
next dev --turbopack

# Production build with Turbopack
next build --turbopack

If a tool chokes on Turbopack, drop the flag

Turbopack is the recommended default, but a few legacy Webpack plugins and next.config customizations aren't fully supported yet. If something breaks, remove --turbopack to fall back to the stable Webpack pipeline while you investigate—your app code doesn't change either way.

Node version

Next.js 15 requires Node.js 18.18 or later. Check with node -v; if you're behind, use a version manager like nvm or fnm to upgrade before scaffolding.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Next.js A full-stack React framework (by Vercel) adding routing, rendering, data, and optimization to React vs plain React Plain React is client-only; Next.js adds server rendering, Server Components, file routing, and a backend When to use SEO-sensitive, fast-first-paint, or full-stack apps; a Vite SPA may suffice for auth-only dashboards CSR / SSR / SSG / ISR Per-route rendering strategies—where and when HTML is generated App Router Default app/ model built on Server Components; recommended for all new projects Pages Router Legacy pages/ model with getServerSideProps/getStaticProps; known for existing code create-next-app Official scaffolder; use --typescript --app for a modern setup app/ Routing + UI; folders are routes, special files add behavior public/ Static assets served from / Special files layout, page, loading, error, not-found, template, route Root layout Required app/layout.tsx; renders <html>/<body> dev / build / start Dev server, production build, production server Turbopack Rust bundler, default for next dev in Next.js 15; faster than Webpack

docs.text:
• Next.js Docs — Getting Started

docs.title: Authentication (Auth.js v5)
docs.text:
Authentication answers who is making this request; authorization answers what they may do. In the App Router both questions are best answered on the server, before any HTML is sent — there's no client to trust, no token to leak, and no protected UI to flash.

This chapter uses Auth.js v5 (the package formerly called NextAuth.js, now published as next-auth@5). v5 is a substantial rewrite for the App Router: a single auth.ts config exports everything (handlers, auth, signIn, signOut), the universal auth() helper reads the session anywhere on the server, and the same config powers the route handler, middleware, Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers. The result is far less boilerplate than v4 and a model that fits RSC naturally.

📚 For more: https://authjs.dev · Next.js — Authentication

The security throughline of this guide

The React Authentication chapter established the rules this chapter keeps: tokens/sessions live in HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies (never localStorage); client-side checks are UX, not security; and every authorization decision is re-enforced on the server. Auth.js follows all three by default — its session cookie is HttpOnly and the auth() check runs server-side. The one thing it adds that you must internalize: middleware is not a security boundary (see Protecting routes).

docs.title: 1. Why Auth.js v5
docs.text:
Building auth by hand (the custom system in the React chapter) is instructive but error-prone — you own password hashing, OAuth flows, CSRF, cookie flags, session rotation, and breach response. Auth.js gives you:

  • Dozens of OAuth/OIDC providers (GitHub, Google, Apple, Microsoft, …) with one-line configs.
  • A Credentials provider for email/password, if you must.
  • HttpOnly session cookies with CSRF protection out of the box.
  • JWT or database sessions, switchable with one option.
  • First-class App Router integration — auth() works in Server Components, Server Actions, Route Handlers, and middleware.

Auth.js vs NextAuth vs next-auth@5

They're the same project. NextAuth.js was the original Next-only name. The project generalized into Auth.js (with SvelteKit, Express, etc. adapters), but the Next.js package is still installed as next-auth — version 5 is what this chapter calls "Auth.js v5." At time of writing v5 is published as next-auth@beta; the API below is the current beta API.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
npm install next-auth@beta

Generate a strong secret used to sign/encrypt the session cookie and JWTs:

npx auth secret   # writes AUTH_SECRET to .env.local

AUTH_SECRET is mandatory in production

Without it, sessions can't be signed and v5 will throw at runtime. Keep it server-only (no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix) and rotate it as you would any signing key — rotating invalidates existing sessions.

docs.title: The central auth.ts
docs.text:
The heart of v5 is a single config file at the project root that you call NextAuth() on and re-export its pieces. Everything else imports from here.

// auth.ts
import NextAuth from "next-auth";
import GitHub from "next-auth/providers/github";

export const { handlers, auth, signIn, signOut } = NextAuth({
  providers: [GitHub], // reads AUTH_GITHUB_ID / AUTH_GITHUB_SECRET from env
});

What each export is for:

Export What it is Where you use it handlers { GET, POST } route handlers app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts auth Universal session reader and middleware wrapper Server Components, Route Handlers, Server Actions, middleware.ts signIn Server-side sign-in trigger Server Actions / Route Handlers signOut Server-side sign-out trigger Server Actions / Route Handlers

Env var naming convention

v5 auto-detects provider credentials from env vars named AUTH_<PROVIDER>_ID and AUTH_<PROVIDER>_SECRET (e.g. AUTH_GITHUB_ID). You can still pass clientId/clientSecret explicitly, but the convention means many providers need zero config in auth.ts.

docs.title: The catch-all Route Handler
docs.text:
Auth.js needs HTTP endpoints for the OAuth callbacks, the CSRF token, session JSON, etc. In v5 you don't write them — you re-export the generated handlers:

// app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts
import { handlers } from "@/auth";

export const { GET, POST } = handlers;

This one file wires up /api/auth/signin, /api/auth/callback/:provider, /api/auth/session, /api/auth/csrf, /api/auth/signout, and so on.

docs.title: middleware.ts (optional but common)
docs.text:
Exporting auth as middleware keeps the session fresh and lets you gate routes at the edge. The minimal form:

// middleware.ts
export { auth as middleware } from "@/auth";

export const config = {
  // Run on everything except static assets and Next internals
  matcher: ["/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)"],
};

A richer form runs your own logic with the session attached to req.auth:

// middleware.ts
import { auth } from "@/auth";
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export default auth((req) => {
  const isLoggedIn = !!req.auth;            // session decoded for you
  const isOnDashboard = req.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/dashboard");

  if (isOnDashboard && !isLoggedIn) {
    const url = new URL("/login", req.nextUrl);
    url.searchParams.set("callbackUrl", req.nextUrl.pathname);
    return NextResponse.redirect(url);
  }
  return NextResponse.next();
});

export const config = {
  matcher: ["/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)"],
};

Keep auth.ts edge-compatible if it runs in middleware

Middleware runs on the Edge runtime, which has no Node APIs. If your auth.ts imports a database adapter or Node-only crypto (common with the Credentials provider or database sessions), it cannot run in middleware as-is. The fix is the split config pattern below.

docs.title: Split config for the Edge (auth.config.ts)
docs.text:
Put the edge-safe parts (providers that don't need Node, callbacks, pages) in auth.config.ts, and assemble the full config — with the database adapter — in auth.ts. Middleware imports only the edge-safe slice.

// auth.config.ts — edge-safe, no DB adapter, no Node-only providers
import type { NextAuthConfig } from "next-auth";
import GitHub from "next-auth/providers/github";

export const authConfig = {
  pages: { signIn: "/login" },
  providers: [GitHub], // OAuth providers are edge-safe
  callbacks: {
    authorized({ auth, request: { nextUrl } }) {
      const isLoggedIn = !!auth?.user;
      const isOnDashboard = nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/dashboard");
      if (isOnDashboard) return isLoggedIn; // false → redirect to signIn page
      return true;
    },
  },
} satisfies NextAuthConfig;

// auth.ts — full config, runs on Node (Server Components / Actions / Route Handler)
import NextAuth from "next-auth";
import { PrismaAdapter } from "@auth/prisma-adapter";
import Credentials from "next-auth/providers/credentials";
import { prisma } from "@/lib/prisma";
import { authConfig } from "./auth.config";

export const { handlers, auth, signIn, signOut } = NextAuth({
  ...authConfig,
  adapter: PrismaAdapter(prisma),
  session: { strategy: "jwt" },
  providers: [
    ...authConfig.providers,
    Credentials({ /* … see below … */ }),
  ],
});

// middleware.ts — imports ONLY the edge-safe config
import NextAuth from "next-auth";
import { authConfig } from "./auth.config";

export const { auth: middleware } = NextAuth(authConfig);

export const config = {
  matcher: ["/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)"],
};

The authorized callback is the idiomatic v5 way to gate routes from middleware: return true to allow, false to bounce to the sign-in page, or a Response/NextResponse for custom handling.

docs.title: OAuth (GitHub, Google, …)
docs.text:
OAuth providers are the easy, recommended path: the provider verifies the user, Auth.js completes the code exchange on the server, and sets your HttpOnly session cookie. No password ever touches your app.

// auth.ts
import NextAuth from "next-auth";
import GitHub from "next-auth/providers/github";
import Google from "next-auth/providers/google";

export const { handlers, auth, signIn, signOut } = NextAuth({
  providers: [
    GitHub,                       // AUTH_GITHUB_ID / AUTH_GITHUB_SECRET
    Google({                      // explicit form, if you prefer
      clientId: process.env.AUTH_GOOGLE_ID,
      clientSecret: process.env.AUTH_GOOGLE_SECRET,
    }),
  ],
});

Register the callback URL with each provider. For Auth.js it is:

https://yourapp.com/api/auth/callback/github
https://yourapp.com/api/auth/callback/google

This mirrors the React chapter's OAuth advice

The React chapter insisted the token exchange happens on the backend so provider tokens never reach the browser. Auth.js does exactly that — the callback handler runs server-side and only an HttpOnly session cookie comes back to the client.

docs.title: Credentials (email/password) — with caveats
docs.text:
The Credentials provider lets you authenticate against your own user table. Auth.js supports it but deliberately makes it harder, because rolling your own password auth means owning hashing, rate limiting, and breach response yourself.

// auth.ts (Credentials provider)
import Credentials from "next-auth/providers/credentials";
import { z } from "zod";
import bcrypt from "bcryptjs";
import { getUserByEmail } from "@/lib/data/user";

const credentialsSchema = z.object({
  email: z.string().email(),
  password: z.string().min(8),
});

Credentials({
  async authorize(rawCredentials) {
    const parsed = credentialsSchema.safeParse(rawCredentials);
    if (!parsed.success) return null; // null → "invalid credentials", no error detail leaked

    const { email, password } = parsed.data;
    const user = await getUserByEmail(email); // your DB query
    if (!user || !user.passwordHash) return null;

    const ok = await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash);
    if (!ok) return null;

    // What you return becomes the session user — return ONLY safe fields.
    return { id: user.id, name: user.name, email: user.email, role: user.role };
  },
});

Credentials provider constraints — read before using

  • You must hash passwords yourself (bcrypt/argon2). Auth.js stores nothing for you and does no hashing.
  • The Credentials provider forces session.strategy: "jwt" — database sessions are not supported with it, because there's no OAuth account row to link a DB session to.
  • bcrypt and most DB drivers are Node-only, so a Credentials auth.ts is not edge-compatible — use the split config so middleware never imports it.
  • Return null for any failure (bad email, wrong password, validation error). Throwing leaks which step failed and enables user enumeration.
  • You still owe rate limiting / brute-force protection on the sign-in route — Auth.js does not provide it.

Prefer OAuth or email magic links

For most apps, GitHub/Google OAuth or the Nodemailer/Resend email (magic link) provider is safer and less work than passwords. Reach for Credentials only when you genuinely need self-managed accounts.

docs.title: Triggering sign-in / sign-out
docs.text:
From a Server Component, render a form whose action is a Server Action calling signIn/signOut:

// app/components/auth-buttons.tsx (Server Component)
import { signIn, signOut, auth } from "@/auth";

export async function SignIn() {
  return (
    <form
      action={async () => {
        "use server";
        await signIn("github", { redirectTo: "/dashboard" });
      }}
    >
      <button type="submit">Sign in with GitHub</button>
    </form>
  );
}

export async function SignOut() {
  return (
    <form
      action={async () => {
        "use server";
        await signOut({ redirectTo: "/" });
      }}
    >
      <button type="submit">Sign out</button>
    </form>
  );
}

For Credentials, pass the form data and handle the thrown error:

// app/login/actions.ts
"use server";
import { signIn } from "@/auth";
import { AuthError } from "next-auth";

export async function login(_prev: unknown, formData: FormData) {
  try {
    await signIn("credentials", {
      email: formData.get("email"),
      password: formData.get("password"),
      redirectTo: "/dashboard",
    });
  } catch (error) {
    if (error instanceof AuthError) {
      // CredentialsSignin is the type for a failed authorize()
      return error.type === "CredentialsSignin"
        ? "Invalid email or password."
        : "Something went wrong.";
    }
    throw error; // re-throw the redirect Next.js uses internally
  }
}

signIn/signOut redirect by throwing

On success they throw a special NEXT_REDIRECT to perform the navigation. Never wrap them in a try/catch that swallows everything — re-throw anything that isn't an AuthError, or the redirect won't happen.

docs.title: On the server — await auth()
docs.text:
The canonical, secure way to read the session is auth() in any server context. It reads and verifies the HttpOnly cookie server-side — exactly the model this guide recommends.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { auth } from "@/auth";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";

export default async function Dashboard() {
  const session = await auth();
  if (!session?.user) redirect("/login");

  return <h1>Welcome, {session.user.name}</h1>;
}

It works the same in a Route Handler and a Server Action:

// app/api/profile/route.ts (Route Handler)
import { auth } from "@/auth";
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export const GET = auth((req) => {
  if (!req.auth) return NextResponse.json({ error: "Unauthorized" }, { status: 401 });
  return NextResponse.json({ user: req.auth.user });
});

// a Server Action
"use server";
import { auth } from "@/auth";

export async function updateProfile(formData: FormData) {
  const session = await auth();
  if (!session?.user) throw new Error("Not authenticated"); // re-check, always
  // …mutate using session.user.id…
}

docs.title: On the client — useSession + <SessionProvider>
docs.text:
When a Client Component needs the session (e.g. to show an avatar), wrap the app in <SessionProvider> and read it with useSession. The provider fetches the session from /api/auth/session over HTTP.

// app/providers.tsx
"use client";
import { SessionProvider } from "next-auth/react";

export function Providers({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return <SessionProvider>{children}</SessionProvider>;
}

// app/layout.tsx — keep "use client" out of the layout; just render the provider
import { Providers } from "./providers";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <Providers>{children}</Providers>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

// app/components/user-menu.tsx
"use client";
import { useSession, signIn, signOut } from "next-auth/react";

export function UserMenu() {
  const { data: session, status } = useSession();

  if (status === "loading") return <span>Loading…</span>;
  if (!session) return <button onClick={() => signIn("github")}>Sign in</button>;

  return (
    <div>
      <span>{session.user?.name}</span>
      <button onClick={() => signOut()}>Sign out</button>
    </div>
  );
}

useSession is for UX, never authorization

This is the same rule as the React chapter's "client-side authorization is UX, not security". useSession data comes from the client and can be faked by a user editing your bundle. Use it to render an avatar or hide a menu item — never to decide whether a mutation or data read is allowed. That decision belongs to auth() on the server.

Prefer the server when you can

In the App Router you often don't need <SessionProvider> at all: read the session with auth() in a Server Component and pass only the fields you need down as props. Reach for useSession only for genuinely client-driven UI that must react to sign-in/out without a navigation.

docs.title: Typing the session (roles, ids)
docs.text:
Augment the Auth.js types so session.user.role and session.user.id are typed everywhere. Populate them in the jwt/session callbacks.

// types/next-auth.d.ts
import { DefaultSession } from "next-auth";

declare module "next-auth" {
  interface Session {
    user: { id: string; role: "user" | "admin" } & DefaultSession["user"];
  }
  interface User {
    role: "user" | "admin";
  }
}

// auth.ts — copy role/id through the callbacks (JWT strategy shown)
callbacks: {
  async jwt({ token, user }) {
    if (user) token.role = user.role; // runs on sign-in; user is the DB/authorize result
    return token;
  },
  async session({ session, token }) {
    session.user.id = token.sub!;          // sub is the user id
    session.user.role = token.role as "user" | "admin";
    return session;
  },
},

docs.title: 5. Protecting routes
docs.text: There are two layers, and you need both.

docs.title: Layer 1 — Middleware (optimistic, fast)
docs.text: Middleware runs at the edge before the request hits a route, so it's the cheapest place to bounce obviously-unauthenticated traffic and avoid rendering a protected page at all. Either the minimal re-export or the authorized callback from §2 handles this.

docs.title: Layer 2 — Per-page server-side checks in the DAL (authoritative)
docs.text:
Middleware alone is NOT sufficient — this is the critical point

Next.js's own guidance is explicit: do not rely on middleware as your only auth check. Reasons:

  • Middleware runs an optimistic check (it typically reads/decodes the session cookie) — it is convenient, not a guarantee. A 2025 middleware-bypass CVE (CVE-2025-29927) let crafted headers skip middleware entirely. If middleware were your only gate, every protected page was exposed.
  • Middleware doesn't cover every entry point uniformly — Server Actions, Route Handlers, and data reads can be hit directly.
  • The secure pattern is defense in depth: middleware for UX/redirects, plus an authoritative check close to the data.

Treat middleware as a redirect convenience and put the real check in your Data Access Layer.

The recommended structure is a centralized DAL (Data Access Layer) that verifies the session and is the only path to sensitive data. Every Server Component / Action calls it; the check can't be skipped.

// lib/dal.ts
import "server-only"; // build error if this is ever imported into client code
import { cache } from "react";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { auth } from "@/auth";

// `cache` dedupes this within a single render pass.
export const verifySession = cache(async () => {
  const session = await auth();
  if (!session?.user) redirect("/login");
  return { userId: session.user.id, role: session.user.role };
});

export async function getCurrentUserData() {
  const { userId } = await verifySession();        // authoritative gate
  return prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: userId } });
}

// app/dashboard/page.tsx — the page can't render data without passing the gate
import { getCurrentUserData } from "@/lib/dal";

export default async function Dashboard() {
  const user = await getCurrentUserData(); // redirects if not authed
  return <h1>Welcome, {user?.name}</h1>;
}

Why import \"server-only\" matters here

Just like the React/SSR chapter's guard against leaking server modules to the browser, marking the DAL server-only means an accidental client import fails the build instead of shipping your DB query and auth logic to the browser.

docs.title: Role-based access
docs.text:
Layer role checks on top of verifySession — fail closed (deny by default):

// lib/dal.ts (continued)
export async function requireRole(role: "admin") {
  const session = await verifySession();
  if (session.role !== role) redirect("/403"); // or notFound()
  return session;
}

// app/admin/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { requireRole } from "@/lib/dal";

export default async function AdminPage() {
  await requireRole("admin"); // server-side, authoritative
  return <AdminDashboard />;
}

// guarding a Server Action by role
"use server";
import { requireRole } from "@/lib/dal";

export async function deleteUser(id: string) {
  await requireRole("admin"); // re-checked here too — actions are a separate entry point
  await prisma.user.delete({ where: { id } });
}

You can also hide the admin link in the UI with useSession/session.user.role, but remember that's cosmetic — the requireRole calls above are what actually enforce it. This is the same two-place model (route-level + component-level, both backed by a server check) as the React authorization section.

docs.title: 6. Session strategies: JWT vs database
docs.text:
Auth.js stores the session one of two ways. Set it with session.strategy.

// auth.ts
session: { strategy: "jwt" }       // default for OAuth-only; required for Credentials
// or
session: { strategy: "database" }  // requires an adapter (Prisma/Drizzle/…)
JWT (default) Database Where session lives Encrypted JWT inside the HttpOnly cookie Row in your DB; cookie holds only an opaque session id Needs an adapter? No Yes (Prisma, Drizzle, etc.) Read cost Decrypt cookie — no DB hit DB lookup per request Revoke a session instantly? ❌ Hard — valid until expiry (same limitation as stateless JWTs in the React chapter) ✅ Delete the row → session dead immediately Edge-friendly ✅ Yes (no DB) ⚠️ Needs DB access from the edge Works with Credentials provider ✅ (forced) ❌ Not supported 
// Database strategy needs an adapter:
import { PrismaAdapter } from "@auth/prisma-adapter";
import { prisma } from "@/lib/prisma";

export const { handlers, auth, signIn, signOut } = NextAuth({
  adapter: PrismaAdapter(prisma),
  session: { strategy: "database" },
  providers: [GitHub],
});

JWT sessions can't be force-revoked — keep them short

With the JWT strategy, "log out everywhere" and "ban this user now" don't take effect until the token expires. Mirror the React chapter's advice: keep session.maxAge modest, and if you need instant revocation (admin lockout, password reset, breach response), use the database strategy or maintain a server-side denylist checked in the jwt/session callback.

How to choose

  • OAuth-only app, want edge speed, no instant-revoke requirement → JWT (the default).
  • Need instant revocation, an audit trail, or to query active sessions → database.
  • Using the Credentials provider → you're on JWT whether you like it or not; add a denylist if you need revocation.

docs.title: 7. Alternatives
docs.text:
Auth.js is the open-source default, but it isn't the only fit.

Option Best for Trade-off Auth.js (NextAuth) Most Next.js apps; you want self-hosted sessions + many providers You own the user table, emails, and UI Clerk B2C/SaaS that wants to ship fast Hosted (vendor + pricing), but prebuilt components, orgs/multi-tenancy, MFA, and Next middleware helpers out of the box Lucia / your own Full control, learning, unusual requirements You implement sessions, cookies, CSRF, rotation yourself — most work, most responsibility 
// Clerk — guarding is declarative; middleware + components are provided
// middleware.ts
import { clerkMiddleware, createRouteMatcher } from "@clerk/nextjs/server";

const isProtected = createRouteMatcher(["/dashboard(.*)", "/admin(.*)"]);

export default clerkMiddleware(async (auth, req) => {
  if (isProtected(req)) await auth.protect(); // redirects if signed out
});

// Clerk — server-side check in a Server Component (still the authoritative layer)
import { auth } from "@clerk/nextjs/server";

export default async function Page() {
  const { userId } = await auth();
  if (!userId) redirect("/sign-in");
  // …
}

On Lucia

Lucia is well known as a lightweight, type-safe session library, but the maintainer has been winding it down as a library, repositioning it as a learning resource for implementing sessions yourself. If you want the "own your auth" path today, treat Lucia as a reference for the patterns (session table, cookie handling, validation) and pair it with your own code or a maintained primitive like Oslo. For most teams, Auth.js or Clerk is the lower-risk choice.

When to build vs buy (same call as the React chapter)

Buy when you need MFA, SSO, compliance, or social login soon — auth is not where you want hand-rolled bugs. Auth.js is the middle ground: open-source and self-hosted, but it still handles the dangerous parts (OAuth, CSRF, cookie flags) for you. Roll your own only with simple requirements and the appetite to own rotation and revocation.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description auth.ts Central config; NextAuth() returns handlers/auth/signIn/signOut app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts One-line re-export of handlers — wires all auth endpoints middleware.ts export { auth as middleware } — optimistic, edge gate (UX, not the boundary) Split config (auth.config.ts) Edge-safe slice so middleware avoids Node-only adapters/Credentials OAuth providers GitHub/Google/… — server-side code exchange, HttpOnly cookie out Credentials provider Email/password; forces JWT, you hash + rate-limit, return null on any failure await auth() Authoritative session read on the server (Components, Actions, Handlers) useSession + <SessionProvider> Client session for UX only — never authorization DAL + verifySession Per-page/-action server check close to the data — the real security layer requireRole Fail-closed role gate, re-checked in every entry point JWT vs database session JWT = edge-fast, no DB, hard to revoke · DB = revocable, needs adapter AUTH_SECRET Mandatory signing/encryption key; server-only Alternatives Clerk (hosted, fast), Lucia/own (full control, most work)

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Auth.js — Documentation
  • Auth.js — Migrating to v5
  • Auth.js — Session strategies
  • Next.js — Authentication guide
  • Next.js — Data Access Layer & why middleware isn't enough
  • CVE-2025-29927 — Next.js middleware bypass
  • Clerk — Next.js quickstart

docs.title: Middleware & Edge
docs.text:
Middleware is code that runs before a request finishes routing—after the request hits your deployment but before it reaches a page, layout, or Route Handler. It's your one chance to inspect, redirect, rewrite, or decorate every matching request in a single place: auth gates, locale redirects, A/B routing, geo-personalization, and security headers all live here.

In Next.js, middleware runs on the Edge runtime by default—a lightweight, Web-standard environment distributed close to users. That makes it fast, but it also constrains what you can do (no Node.js APIs, a limited package surface). This chapter covers the middleware.ts API end to end, the common use cases, the correct way to use middleware for auth (a lightweight optimistic check only—never your real authorization), the Edge runtime and its trade-offs, and the static next.config networking primitives (redirects, rewrites, headers) that handle the cases where you don't need code at all.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/routing/middleware · https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/middleware

docs.title: 1. middleware.ts basics
docs.text:
Create a single middleware.ts (or .js) file at the root of your project—next to app/, or inside src/ if you use that layout. Next.js automatically detects it; there is no import or registration step. You get one middleware file per project.

// middleware.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

// Runs before every matching request (see `config.matcher` below)
export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  console.log("Incoming:", request.method, request.nextUrl.pathname);
  return NextResponse.next(); // continue to the intended route
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*"], // only run for these paths
};

The file exports two things:

  • middleware — the function that runs per request. It can be sync or async and receives a NextRequest.
  • config (optional but recommended) — configuration, most importantly the matcher that scopes which requests trigger it.

docs.title: NextRequest
docs.text:
NextRequest extends the Web Request with Next.js conveniences:

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  // Parsed, mutable URL — prefer this over building URLs by hand
  const { pathname, searchParams } = request.nextUrl;

  // Typed cookie helpers (read here; write on the response — see §4)
  const session = request.cookies.get("session")?.value;

  // Standard headers
  const ua = request.headers.get("user-agent");

  // Geo & IP are provided by the host (e.g. Vercel) at the edge
  const country = request.headers.get("x-vercel-ip-country"); // "US", "DE", …

  return NextResponse.next();
}

request.geo / request.ip moved to headers

In older Next.js, NextRequest exposed request.geo and request.ip. In Next.js 15 these were removed; on Vercel, read the equivalent values from request headers (x-vercel-ip-country, x-vercel-ip-city, x-forwarded-for, …) or use @vercel/functions' geolocation(request) / ipAddress(request) helpers. Other hosts inject their own headers.

docs.title: 2. The response: next, redirect, rewrite
docs.text: A middleware function returns a NextResponse (or a plain Response, or nothing—which is treated as "continue"). The three workhorse factories on NextResponse decide what happens to the request.

docs.title: NextResponse.next() — continue
docs.text:
Let the request proceed to its intended destination. Return this when you only want to observe the request or attach something (a header, a cookie) on the way through.

return NextResponse.next();

You can also pass request-header overrides so the downstream route sees a modified request (covered in §4).

docs.title: NextResponse.redirect() — change the URL the user sees
docs.text:
Sends a real HTTP redirect (307 by default). The browser's address bar changes to the new URL.

// Bounce unauthenticated users to /login
return NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/login", request.url));

Always build URLs with new URL(path, request.url)

Passing a bare string like "/login" is not a valid absolute URL for a redirect. new URL("/login", request.url) resolves it against the current request's origin, so it works in dev, preview, and production without hardcoding a domain.

docs.title: NextResponse.rewrite() — change what's served, not the URL
docs.text:
Serves the content of a different path while the browser keeps showing the original URL. The user never sees the rewrite; it's invisible proxying.

// User sees /blog, but we serve the content at /cms/blog internally
return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL("/cms/blog", request.url));
Method Browser URL changes? HTTP status Typical use next() No (continues) Observe / attach headers & cookies redirect() Yes (new URL shown) 307 / 308 Auth gate, moved pages, locale switch rewrite() No (original URL kept) 200 (of target) Proxying, A/B, masking internal paths

docs.title: 3. The config.matcher
docs.text:
Without a matcher, middleware runs on every request—including static assets, images, and _next internals—which is wasteful and slow. The matcher scopes it to exactly the paths you care about. It's statically analyzed at build time, so it must be a literal value (not computed at runtime).

export const config = {
  // A single path, or an array of paths
  matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*", "/account/:path*"],
};

Matcher syntax (powered by path-to-regexp):

  • /about — exact path.
  • /dashboard/:path* — /dashboard and everything beneath it.
  • /users/:id — a single dynamic segment.
  • Full regex via a named group: /((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*).

A very common pattern is "run on everything except static assets and image optimization":

export const config = {
  matcher: [
    /*
     * Match all request paths except:
     *  - /api            (API routes handle their own logic)
     *  - /_next/static   (static files)
     *  - /_next/image    (image optimization)
     *  - /favicon.ico, /robots.txt, /sitemap.xml, common image extensions
     */
    "/((?!api|_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico|robots.txt|sitemap.xml|.*\\.(?:png|jpg|jpeg|gif|svg|webp|ico)$).*)",
  ],
};

For finer control, a matcher entry can be an object that also conditions on method, headers, or query params:

export const config = {
  matcher: [
    {
      source: "/dashboard/:path*",
      // Skip prefetch requests (Next.js sends these for <Link> hover prefetch)
      missing: [{ type: "header", key: "next-router-prefetch" }],
    },
  ],
};

Matcher first, guard clauses second

Even with a matcher, start your function with early return NextResponse.next() guards for paths you don't want to touch. The matcher narrows which requests run the function; in-function checks keep the logic readable and let you short-circuit cheaply. Middleware runs on the hot path of every matching request, so keep it lean.

docs.title: 4. Reading & setting cookies and headers
docs.text: Cookies and headers are read from the request and written on the response. Mixing these up is the most common middleware mistake.

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text:
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  // READ from the incoming request
  const theme = request.cookies.get("theme")?.value ?? "light";
  const all = request.cookies.getAll();
  const hasSession = request.cookies.has("session");

  const response = NextResponse.next();

  // WRITE on the outgoing response (Set-Cookie)
  response.cookies.set("theme", theme, {
    httpOnly: true,
    secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === "production",
    sameSite: "lax",
    path: "/",
    maxAge: 60 * 60 * 24 * 365, // 1 year
  });

  // Delete a cookie
  // response.cookies.delete("stale-flag");

  return response;
}

docs.title: Response headers
docs.text:
Attach headers (e.g. for security or tracing) to what the client receives:

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const response = NextResponse.next();
  response.headers.set("x-request-id", crypto.randomUUID());
  response.headers.set("X-Frame-Options", "DENY");
  return response;
}

docs.title: Request headers (passed into the route)
docs.text:
To make the downstream page/Route Handler see a modified request—say, to forward a derived value—clone the request headers and pass them through NextResponse.next({ request }):

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const requestHeaders = new Headers(request.headers);
  requestHeaders.set("x-user-locale", "en-US"); // visible to the route via headers()
  requestHeaders.set("x-pathname", request.nextUrl.pathname);

  return NextResponse.next({
    request: { headers: requestHeaders },
  });
}

The route can then read it server-side:

// app/some-page/page.tsx
import { headers } from "next/headers";

export default async function Page() {
  const locale = (await headers()).get("x-user-locale"); // "en-US"
  return <p>Locale: {locale}</p>;
}

Request headers vs response headers

requestHeaders (set via next({ request })) are sent forward to your route—the browser never sees them. response.headers.set(...) are sent back to the browser. Use the former to pass derived data inward, the latter for security/caching headers outward.

docs.title: Auth gate (lightweight check)
docs.text:
The canonical example: redirect unauthenticated users away from protected sections. Note this is an optimistic check only—§6 explains why.

// middleware.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

const PROTECTED = ["/dashboard", "/account", "/settings"];

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const { pathname } = request.nextUrl;
  const isProtected = PROTECTED.some((p) => pathname.startsWith(p));
  const hasSession = request.cookies.has("session");

  if (isProtected && !hasSession) {
    const url = new URL("/login", request.url);
    url.searchParams.set("from", pathname); // remember where they wanted to go
    return NextResponse.redirect(url);
  }

  // Already logged in? Keep them off the auth pages.
  if (pathname.startsWith("/login") && hasSession) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/dashboard", request.url));
  }

  return NextResponse.next();
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*", "/account/:path*", "/settings/:path*", "/login"],
};

docs.title: i18n locale redirect
docs.text:
Detect the user's preferred locale and redirect to the localized path when none is present in the URL.

// middleware.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

const LOCALES = ["en", "de", "fr"] as const;
const DEFAULT_LOCALE = "en";

function getLocale(request: NextRequest): string {
  // 1. Prefer a previously chosen locale cookie
  const cookieLocale = request.cookies.get("NEXT_LOCALE")?.value;
  if (cookieLocale && LOCALES.includes(cookieLocale as (typeof LOCALES)[number])) {
    return cookieLocale;
  }
  // 2. Fall back to Accept-Language (first matching locale)
  const accept = request.headers.get("accept-language") ?? "";
  const preferred = accept.split(",")[0]?.split("-")[0];
  return LOCALES.includes(preferred as (typeof LOCALES)[number])
    ? (preferred as string)
    : DEFAULT_LOCALE;
}

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const { pathname } = request.nextUrl;

  // Skip if the path already has a supported locale prefix
  const hasLocale = LOCALES.some(
    (l) => pathname === `/${l}` || pathname.startsWith(`/${l}/`)
  );
  if (hasLocale) return NextResponse.next();

  const locale = getLocale(request);
  const url = new URL(`/${locale}${pathname}`, request.url);
  return NextResponse.redirect(url);
}

export const config = {
  // Skip internals & files (anything with a dot)
  matcher: ["/((?!api|_next|.*\\..*).*)"],
};

docs.title: A/B testing (rewrite to a variant)
docs.text:
Assign a sticky bucket via cookie, then rewrite so each user consistently sees one variant—at the same URL.

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  if (request.nextUrl.pathname !== "/") return NextResponse.next();

  let bucket = request.cookies.get("ab-bucket")?.value;
  if (!bucket) {
    bucket = Math.random() < 0.5 ? "a" : "b"; // assign once
  }

  // Serve /home-a or /home-b, but the URL stays "/"
  const response = NextResponse.rewrite(
    new URL(`/home-${bucket}`, request.url)
  );
  response.cookies.set("ab-bucket", bucket, { maxAge: 60 * 60 * 24 * 30 });
  return response;
}

export const config = { matcher: ["/"] };

docs.title: Geo-personalization
docs.text:
Route or redirect based on the visitor's country (provided by the host at the edge).

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const country = request.headers.get("x-vercel-ip-country") ?? "US";

  // Send EU visitors to the EU storefront
  if (["DE", "FR", "ES", "IT"].includes(country)) {
    if (!request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/eu")) {
      return NextResponse.rewrite(
        new URL(`/eu${request.nextUrl.pathname}`, request.url)
      );
    }
  }
  return NextResponse.next();
}

docs.title: 6. Using middleware for auth correctly
docs.text:
Middleware is a tempting place to put authorization because it runs before everything. It is the right place for a fast, optimistic gate—and the wrong place to be your only line of defense.

Don't rely on middleware alone for auth

Treat middleware as an optimistic check only: read the session cookie's presence and redirect obvious cases (e.g. no cookie → /login) for a fast UX. Do not make it your sole authorization layer. Per Next.js guidance, perform the real authentication and authorization close to the data—in your Data Access Layer (DAL), Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers—where you can verify the session and check permissions before reading or mutating data. A middleware-only check can be bypassed and can read stale or unverified state, and middleware on the Edge often can't safely do the heavy work (DB lookups, full token verification) anyway. Defense in depth: optimistic gate at the edge, authoritative check at the data layer.

Why this split:

  • Middleware runs on the Edge with tight CPU/time budgets and a limited package surface—doing a database round-trip or a heavy crypto verification on every request is slow and often unsupported.
  • A cookie's presence is not proof of a valid session. Cookies can be stale, forged, or revoked. The middleware check is a UX optimization (skip rendering a protected page just to bounce the user), not a security boundary.
  • The data layer is the real boundary. Whatever actually reads or writes user data must verify identity and permissions itself, so a request that slips past middleware (or comes from a non-browser client) still can't access protected data.
// middleware.ts — OPTIMISTIC gate only: just check the cookie exists
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const hasSession = request.cookies.has("session");
  if (!hasSession && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/dashboard")) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/login", request.url));
  }
  return NextResponse.next();
}

export const config = { matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*"] };

// lib/dal.ts — AUTHORITATIVE check, run wherever data is accessed
import "server-only";
import { cookies } from "next/headers";
import { cache } from "react";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { decrypt } from "@/lib/session"; // verify & decrypt the session token

// `cache` dedupes this within a single render pass
export const verifySession = cache(async () => {
  const token = (await cookies()).get("session")?.value;
  const session = await decrypt(token); // throws/returns null if invalid or expired

  if (!session?.userId) {
    redirect("/login"); // not authenticated
  }
  return { userId: session.userId, role: session.role };
});

// Authorize a specific action close to the data
export async function getUserOrders() {
  const { userId } = await verifySession(); // real check, every time
  return db.order.findMany({ where: { userId } });
}

The page just calls the DAL; the verification happens there, not in middleware:

// app/dashboard/page.tsx
import { getUserOrders } from "@/lib/dal";

export default async function Dashboard() {
  const orders = await getUserOrders(); // verifies session + scopes to the user
  return <OrdersList orders={orders} />;
}

Where each layer belongs

  • Middleware → optimistic redirect on missing/obvious cases. Fast, runs everywhere.
  • DAL / Server Components / Server Actions / Route Handlers → verify the session and check authorization every time data is read or mutated. This is the real gate.

docs.title: What it is
docs.text:
The Edge runtime is a lightweight JavaScript environment based on Web standard APIs (the same APIs as Service Workers / Cloudflare Workers), not Node.js. Middleware runs here by default, and you can opt individual Route Handlers / pages into it. Code is deployed to many locations and runs geographically close to the user, with near-instant cold starts.

  • ✅ Very fast cold starts, globally distributed, ideal for latency-sensitive cross-cutting logic (redirects, header rewriting, optimistic auth, geo).
  • ❌ A constrained environment: limited APIs, smaller deployment size limits, short execution budgets.

docs.title: Constraints — no Node.js APIs
docs.text:
The Edge runtime exposes Web APIs (fetch, Request/Response, Headers, URL, crypto.subtle, TextEncoder, structuredClone, …) but not the Node.js standard library:

// ❌ These do NOT work in the Edge runtime
import fs from "node:fs";          // no filesystem
import path from "node:path";      // no node:path
import crypto from "node:crypto";  // no node:crypto (use Web Crypto: globalThis.crypto)
import net from "node:net";        // no raw TCP sockets

// ✅ Web-standard equivalents that DO work
const id = crypto.randomUUID();             // Web Crypto
const hash = await crypto.subtle.digest(/* … */);
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com");

Practical consequences:

  • No native database drivers that rely on TCP sockets (e.g. the classic pg driver). Use HTTP-based / serverless-driver clients (Neon, PlanetScale, Upstash, Turso) if you must hit a DB at the edge—though for middleware you usually shouldn't.
  • Many npm packages won't run if they import Node built-ins. Crypto/JWT libraries often ship Edge-compatible builds (e.g. jose works on the Edge; some others don't).
  • process.env is available for build-time-inlined env vars, but dynamic Node-only globals are not.

docs.title: When to opt into the Node.js runtime
docs.text:
If middleware genuinely needs Node APIs or an incompatible package, Next.js 15 lets you run middleware on the Node.js runtime (stabilized for middleware in 15.x). Opt in via the runtime flag in config:

// middleware.ts
export const config = {
  matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*"],
  runtime: "nodejs", // run middleware on Node.js instead of the Edge
};

For Route Handlers and pages, the runtime is chosen per file via the segment export (Node.js is the default there):

// app/api/report/route.ts
export const runtime = "nodejs"; // default — full Node APIs, DB drivers, big libs
// export const runtime = "edge"; // opt this handler into the Edge runtime instead

Default runtimes differ by context

Middleware defaults to the Edge runtime; Route Handlers and pages default to the Node.js runtime. Reach for the Edge when you want global, low-latency execution and only need Web APIs; stay on / switch to Node.js when you need the full Node standard library, native drivers, or heavier packages. Don't opt into the Edge "for speed" if your code needs Node—measure first.

docs.title: 8. next.config networking: redirects, rewrites, headers
docs.text:
Not everything needs middleware. For static, declarative URL handling, next.config.ts offers three async functions. They run as part of routing without your own code on the hot path, so prefer them when the rule is fixed (doesn't depend on cookies, geo, or runtime logic).

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  async redirects() {
    return [
      // Permanent move (308): /old-blog/:slug → /blog/:slug
      { source: "/old-blog/:slug", destination: "/blog/:slug", permanent: true },
      // Temporary (307)
      { source: "/promo", destination: "/sales/summer", permanent: false },
      // Conditional on a header / cookie / query
      {
        source: "/legacy",
        has: [{ type: "cookie", key: "beta", value: "true" }],
        destination: "/new",
        permanent: false,
      },
    ];
  },

  async rewrites() {
    return [
      // Proxy /api/v1/* to an external API (URL stays /api/v1/* for the client)
      { source: "/api/v1/:path*", destination: "https://api.example.com/:path*" },
      // Mask an internal docs path
      { source: "/help/:slug*", destination: "/cms/help/:slug*" },
    ];
  },

  async headers() {
    return [
      {
        // Apply security headers to every route
        source: "/:path*",
        headers: [
          { key: "X-Frame-Options", value: "DENY" },
          { key: "X-Content-Type-Options", value: "nosniff" },
          { key: "Referrer-Policy", value: "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" },
        ],
      },
      {
        // Long-cache static assets
        source: "/assets/:path*",
        headers: [
          { key: "Cache-Control", value: "public, max-age=31536000, immutable" },
        ],
      },
    ];
  },
};

export default nextConfig;
  • redirects — declarative HTTP redirects. permanent: true → 308, false → 307. Supports path params, wildcards (:path*), and has/missing conditions on headers, cookies, host, or query.
  • rewrites — declarative proxying: serve a different (possibly external) destination while keeping the user's URL. Same matching power as redirects.
  • headers — attach response headers to matching routes (security headers, caching, CORS).

docs.title: next.config networking vs middleware
docs.text:
next.config (redirects/rewrites/headers) Middleware Logic Static / declarative (path patterns + has/missing) Dynamic — arbitrary code per request Inputs URL, host, headers, cookies, query (pattern match) Anything: parse cookies, call APIs, geo, randomize Performance No user code on the path; very cheap Runs your function on every matching request Use when The rule is fixed and known at build time The decision depends on runtime logic

Reach for config first

If a rule can be expressed as a static pattern—"these old URLs 308 to new ones", "add these security headers everywhere", "proxy /api/v1/* to an external host"—put it in next.config. Use middleware only when the decision needs real per-request logic (auth state, locale detection, A/B bucketing, geo). It's cheaper and harder to get wrong.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description middleware.ts One root-level file; runs before matching requests reach a route NextRequest Web Request + nextUrl, typed cookies, headers NextResponse.next() Continue to the route (optionally attach headers/cookies) NextResponse.redirect() Change the URL the browser shows (307/308) NextResponse.rewrite() Serve a different path; browser URL stays the same config.matcher Statically-analyzed scope: which paths trigger middleware Read cookies/headers From the request (request.cookies / request.headers) Write cookies/headers On the response (response.cookies.set / response.headers.set) next({ request: { headers } }) Forward modified headers into the route (read via headers()) Use cases Auth gate, i18n locale redirect, A/B (rewrite), geo-personalization Auth in middleware Optimistic check only — real authz lives in the DAL/server Edge runtime Web-standard APIs, global, fast cold start; no Node.js APIs Node runtime for middleware config.runtime = "nodejs" (Next 15) when you need Node/native pkgs redirects / rewrites / headers Static, declarative networking in next.config — prefer over middleware when the rule is fixed

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Middleware
  • Next.js — middleware.ts API reference & matcher
  • Next.js — Authentication (DAL, optimistic vs authoritative checks)
  • Next.js — Edge & Node.js runtimes
  • Next.js — redirects · rewrites · headers
  • Edge Runtime — supported APIs

docs.title: State Management in Next.js
docs.text:
"State management" in a React SPA usually means one big question: which library holds all my data? In the App Router that question mostly dissolves. With React Server Components, the server already holds your data and renders it to HTML, so a huge category of "state" — the server's data — never needs to live in a client store at all. What remains is genuinely client-side: ephemeral UI, the URL, and a cache of server data you fetch from the client.

This chapter sorts state into the few buckets that actually exist in an App Router app, then shows the right tool for each: the URL for shareable state, Context/Zustand for client-only UI state (with the per-request provider pattern that keeps server renders safe), and TanStack Query / SWR for caching server data on the client. It closes with the patterns for handing initial server data across the server/client boundary.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/rendering · https://tanstack.com/query/latest · https://zustand.docs.pmnd.rs

docs.title: 1. Server state vs client state
docs.text:
The first and most important move is to stop treating all "state" as one thing. In an App Router app there are really two species, and they want different tools.

Server state is data that lives somewhere else — a database, a CMS, a third-party API — and your UI only borrows a copy. It's the post list, the current user's profile, the product catalog. Its source of truth is the server. Your job is to fetch it, display it, and keep the copy reasonably fresh.

Client state is data that is born in the browser and only matters there: is this dropdown open, what's typed in this input, which tab is active, is the sidebar collapsed. It has no server source of truth.

Server state                      Client state
-----------------------------     -----------------------------
source of truth = server          source of truth = browser
posts, user, catalog, settings    modal open?, form draft, tab
needs fetching + freshness        needs nothing — it just is

docs.title: Why RSC means less client state
docs.text:
In a classic SPA, server state gets dragged into client state: you useEffect + fetch, drop the result into useState or Redux, then manage loading flags, error flags, and refetching by hand. That's the bulk of what state libraries were doing — managing a cache of server data.

React Server Components remove most of that. A Server Component can await your data directly and render it to HTML. There's no loading flag, no client store, no hydration of that data into a reducer — it's just rendered output.

// app/posts/page.tsx — a Server Component
// The data is "state" in the abstract sense, but it never enters a client store.
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export default async function PostsPage() {
  const posts = await db.post.findMany({ take: 20 }); // server state, rendered server-side
  return (
    <ul>
      {posts.map((p) => (
        <li key={p.id}>{p.title}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

So before reaching for any state tool, ask where the source of truth is:

Question Use Is it server data displayed on first load? Server Component — await it, no store Should it be shareable / bookmarkable / survive refresh? The URL (searchParams) Is it data fetched from the client (after interaction, polling, infinite scroll)? TanStack Query / SWR Is it client-only UI state for one component? useState Is it client-only UI state shared across many components? Context (rarely changing) or Zustand (frequently changing)

Reach for a global client store last, not first

The most common App Router mistake migrating from an SPA is recreating a giant Redux/Zustand store full of server data. Most of that data should be fetched on the server (RSC) or cached by TanStack Query — both of which handle freshness for you. A global client store should hold only genuinely client-owned state.

docs.title: 2. The URL as state
docs.text: A surprising amount of "state" should live in the URL query string, not in a JS variable: the current search term, active filters, sort order, page number, the open tab. Putting it in the URL gives you four things a useState can't: it's shareable (paste the link, get the same view), bookmarkable, survives a refresh, and works with the back/forward buttons.

docs.title: Reading searchParams
docs.text:
On the server, a page receives searchParams as a prop (a Promise in Next.js 15). Because it's a prop the Server Component reads, changing the URL re-runs the server render with the new value — no client fetching required.

// app/products/page.tsx — Server Component reads the URL
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export default async function ProductsPage({
  searchParams,
}: {
  searchParams: Promise<{ q?: string; sort?: string }>;
}) {
  const { q = "", sort = "newest" } = await searchParams; // Next.js 15: async
  const products = await db.product.findMany({
    where: q ? { name: { contains: q } } : undefined,
    orderBy: sort === "price" ? { price: "asc" } : { createdAt: "desc" },
  });
  return <ProductGrid products={products} />;
}

On the client, read the same values with useSearchParams:

"use client";
import { useSearchParams } from "next/navigation";

export function SortLabel() {
  const params = useSearchParams();
  return <span>Sorted by: {params.get("sort") ?? "newest"}</span>;
}

docs.title: Writing to the URL
docs.text:
