A client component updates the URL by building a new query string and pushing it. Wrap URLSearchParams so you only change the keys you mean to:

// app/products/SearchBox.tsx
"use client";
import { usePathname, useRouter, useSearchParams } from "next/navigation";
import { useCallback } from "react";

export function SearchBox() {
  const router = useRouter();
  const pathname = usePathname();
  const searchParams = useSearchParams();

  const setQuery = useCallback(
    (q: string) => {
      const params = new URLSearchParams(searchParams.toString());
      if (q) params.set("q", q);
      else params.delete("q");
      router.replace(`${pathname}?${params.toString()}`); // replace = no history spam
    },
    [router, pathname, searchParams],
  );

  return (
    <input
      defaultValue={searchParams.get("q") ?? ""}
      onChange={(e) => setQuery(e.target.value)}
      placeholder="Search products…"
    />
  );
}

When the page above is a Server Component reading searchParams, router.replace triggers a fresh server render with the new query — the product list re-filters on the server.

replace vs push for transient state

Use router.replace for state that changes rapidly (a search box, a slider): it updates the URL without adding a history entry, so the back button doesn't have to click through every keystroke. Use router.push for navigations the user should be able to go back from (changing pages, applying a distinct filter set). Debounce rapid writes so you're not pushing on every keypress.

docs.title: The nuqs pattern: URL state with a useState-like API
docs.text:
Hand-rolling URLSearchParams parsing, type coercion, and defaults for every filter gets tedious. nuqs is a small library that gives you a useState-style hook backed by the URL, with parsers for numbers, booleans, arrays, and more — and it works in both the App and Pages routers.

npm install nuqs

nuqs needs an adapter at the root so it knows how to talk to the router:

// app/layout.tsx
import { NuqsAdapter } from "nuqs/adapters/next/app";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <NuqsAdapter>{children}</NuqsAdapter>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

Now state reads and writes look just like useState, but the value lives in ?q=…&page=…:

// app/products/Filters.tsx
"use client";
import { useQueryState, parseAsInteger, parseAsString } from "nuqs";

export function Filters() {
  const [q, setQ] = useQueryState("q", parseAsString.withDefault(""));
  const [page, setPage] = useQueryState(
    "page",
    parseAsInteger.withDefault(1),
  );

  return (
    <div>
      <input value={q} onChange={(e) => setQ(e.target.value || null)} />
      <button onClick={() => setPage((p) => p + 1)}>Next page (now {page})</button>
    </div>
  );
}

nuqs handles serialization, parsing, defaults (a value equal to its default is removed from the URL to keep it clean), and batching multiple updates into one navigation. Setting a value to null removes the key.

docs.title: Shallow routing
docs.text:
By default, changing searchParams in the App Router re-runs the matching Server Component (it's a navigation). Sometimes you want to change the URL without re-rendering on the server — for example, syncing a purely client-side tab to the URL for shareability while a TanStack Query result drives the actual content.

nuqs exposes this directly with shallow (default true = client-only update):

"use client";
import { useQueryState } from "nuqs";

export function Tabs() {
  // shallow: true (the default) updates the URL on the client only — no server round-trip
  const [tab, setTab] = useQueryState("tab", { defaultValue: "overview" });
  // …render based on `tab`, no Server Component re-render
}

export function ServerSyncedFilter() {
  // shallow: false forces a server round-trip so a Server Component re-reads searchParams
  const [q, setQ] = useQueryState("q", { shallow: false, defaultValue: "" });
}

Shallow updates don't re-run the server

With a shallow URL update, your Server Component does not re-render, so any server-side searchParams logic won't run. Choose shallow: false when a Server Component must react to the change; keep the default shallow: true when the URL is just a bookmark for client-driven UI.

docs.title: 3. Client state: Context and Zustand in the App Router
docs.text: For state that genuinely belongs to the browser and is shared across components — a theme, a shopping-cart drawer, a multi-step wizard — you need a client store. The catch in the App Router is that providers and stores are client concepts, and the server renders your tree too. Getting the boundary right is the whole game.

docs.title: Context with the "use client" provider pattern
docs.text:
React Context requires a component that calls createContext/useContext, which only works in Client Components. But your root layout is a Server Component, and you want it to stay that way. The pattern is to extract the provider into its own "use client" file and render it from the (server) layout, passing children through.

// app/theme-provider.tsx
"use client";
import { createContext, useContext, useState } from "react";

type Theme = "light" | "dark";
const ThemeContext = createContext<{
  theme: Theme;
  toggle: () => void;
} | null>(null);

export function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState<Theme>("light");
  const toggle = () => setTheme((t) => (t === "light" ? "dark" : "light"));
  return (
    <ThemeContext.Provider value={{ theme, toggle }}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext.Provider>
  );
}

export function useTheme() {
  const ctx = useContext(ThemeContext);
  if (!ctx) throw new Error("useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider");
  return ctx;
}

// app/layout.tsx — stays a Server Component
import { ThemeProvider } from "./theme-provider";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        {/* children passed through the client provider stay Server Components */}
        <ThemeProvider>{children}</ThemeProvider>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

The key insight: {children} passed into a Client Component are still rendered on the server. Wrapping your tree in a client provider does not turn the whole tree into client code — only the provider component itself is a Client Component.

Context is great for low-frequency shared state (theme, locale, the current user object). For state that updates often, every consumer of the context re-renders on every change, which gets expensive — that's where Zustand shines.

docs.title: Zustand v5 — the per-request store
docs.text:
Zustand is a tiny, hook-based store with no provider required in a pure SPA. You'd normally create the store once at module scope and import it anywhere:

// ⚠️ This SPA-style pattern is UNSAFE on the server — see the warning below
import { create } from "zustand";

export const useCounterStore = create<{ count: number; inc: () => void }>(
  (set) => ({ count: 0, inc: () => set((s) => s.count + 1) }),
);

Never use a module-level store on the server

A module-level store is created once per server process and shared across every request. On the server that means one user's state can leak into another user's render — the same store instance is reused for concurrent requests. It also can't be initialized with per-request data (like the logged-in user). In the App Router you must create the store per request, behind a provider, so each render gets its own isolated instance.

The fix is to create the store inside a "use client" provider, hold the instance in a useRef (so it's created exactly once per component instance), and read it through Context. Define the store as a factory, not a singleton:

// app/stores/counter-store.ts
import { createStore } from "zustand/vanilla";

export type CounterState = { count: number };
export type CounterActions = {
  increment: () => void;
  setCount: (count: number) => void;
};
export type CounterStore = CounterState & CounterActions;

// A factory — call it to get a fresh store. `initState` lets the server seed it.
export const createCounterStore = (
  initState: CounterState = { count: 0 },
) =>
  createStore<CounterStore>()((set) => ({
    ...initState,
    increment: () => set((state) => ({ count: state.count + 1 })),
    setCount: (count) => set({ count }),
  }));

// app/stores/counter-store-provider.tsx
"use client";
import { createContext, useContext, useRef, type ReactNode } from "react";
import { useStore } from "zustand";
import {
  createCounterStore,
  type CounterStore,
  type CounterState,
} from "./counter-store";

type CounterStoreApi = ReturnType<typeof createCounterStore>;
const CounterStoreContext = createContext<CounterStoreApi | null>(null);

export function CounterStoreProvider({
  children,
  initialState,
}: {
  children: ReactNode;
  initialState?: CounterState;
}) {
  // useRef ensures the store is created exactly ONCE per component instance,
  // and a new instance per request on the server.
  const storeRef = useRef<CounterStoreApi | null>(null);
  if (storeRef.current === null) {
    storeRef.current = createCounterStore(initialState);
  }
  return (
    <CounterStoreContext.Provider value={storeRef.current}>
      {children}
    </CounterStoreContext.Provider>
  );
}

// Selector hook — `selector` keeps re-renders scoped to the slice you use.
export function useCounterStore<T>(selector: (store: CounterStore) => T): T {
  const store = useContext(CounterStoreContext);
  if (!store) {
    throw new Error("useCounterStore must be used within CounterStoreProvider");
  }
  return useStore(store, selector);
}

Wrap the relevant part of the tree — and seed it with server data if you have it:

// app/dashboard/layout.tsx — a Server Component
import { CounterStoreProvider } from "@/app/stores/counter-store-provider";
import { getInitialCount } from "@/lib/data";

export default async function DashboardLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  const count = await getInitialCount(); // per-request server data
  return (
    <CounterStoreProvider initialState={{ count }}>
      {children}
    </CounterStoreProvider>
  );
}

docs.title: Selecting state with useShallow
docs.text:
The hook above takes a selector, so a component only re-renders when its slice changes. But if a selector returns a new object or array each call (e.g. picking multiple fields), the default referential (Object.is) equality sees a new reference every time and re-renders on every store change. Zustand v5's useShallow compares the selected object shallowly to prevent that:

"use client";
import { useShallow } from "zustand/react/shallow";
import { useCounterStore } from "@/app/stores/counter-store-provider";

export function CounterControls() {
  // Selecting multiple values returns a NEW object each render.
  // useShallow does a shallow compare so this only re-renders when count/increment change.
  const { count, increment } = useCounterStore(
    useShallow((s) => ({ count: s.count, increment: s.increment })),
  );

  return <button onClick={increment}>Count: {count}</button>;
}

Selector discipline keeps Zustand fast

Select the smallest slice each component needs. A single-value selector ((s) => s.count) needs no useShallow because it returns a primitive. Reach for useShallow only when a selector returns a fresh object/array. Selecting the whole store (useCounterStore((s) => s)) re-renders on every change — avoid it.

docs.title: 4. Caching server data on the client: TanStack Query & SWR
docs.text: RSC handles data on first render. But some data is fetched from the client: results that update after a user interaction, infinite scroll, polling/live data, optimistic mutations, or any page where re-running a Server Component would be too coarse. For that, you want a client-side cache of server state — and that's exactly what TanStack Query and SWR are. They give you caching, deduplication, background refetching, stale-while-revalidate, and request states (loading/error/data) so you stop hand-rolling useEffect + useState.

docs.title: Provider setup (TanStack Query v5)
docs.text:
QueryClientProvider is a Client Component, so it follows the same "use client" provider pattern. Critically, you must create the QueryClient so that it isn't shared across requests on the server but is stable across re-renders on the client:

// app/get-query-client.ts
import {
  isServer,
  QueryClient,
  defaultShouldDehydrateQuery,
} from "@tanstack/react-query";

function makeQueryClient() {
  return new QueryClient({
    defaultOptions: {
      queries: {
        // With SSR, set staleTime > 0 so data isn't refetched on the client immediately
        staleTime: 60 * 1000,
        // gcTime (v5; renamed from cacheTime) — how long unused cache is kept before GC
        gcTime: 5 * 60 * 1000,
      },
      dehydrate: {
        // Also dehydrate pending queries so streamed/prefetched data hydrates correctly
        shouldDehydrateQuery: (query) =>
          defaultShouldDehydrateQuery(query) ||
          query.state.status === "pending",
      },
    },
  });
}

let browserQueryClient: QueryClient | undefined;

export function getQueryClient() {
  if (isServer) {
    // Server: ALWAYS a fresh client per request (no cross-request leakage)
    return makeQueryClient();
  }
  // Browser: reuse a single client so React state survives Suspense/re-renders
  if (!browserQueryClient) browserQueryClient = makeQueryClient();
  return browserQueryClient;
}

// app/providers.tsx
"use client";
import { QueryClientProvider } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { getQueryClient } from "./get-query-client";

export function Providers({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  // NB: call getQueryClient() during render, NOT in useState's initializer with a new client,
  // and do not wrap in useMemo — getQueryClient handles the singleton-per-environment logic.
  const queryClient = getQueryClient();
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>{children}</QueryClientProvider>
  );
}

// app/layout.tsx
import { Providers } from "./providers";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <Providers>{children}</Providers>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

One QueryClient per server request, one per browser tab

On the server, a module-level QueryClient would be shared by every concurrent request — the same data-leak hazard as a module-level Zustand store. getQueryClient returns a fresh client on the server every call but memoizes a single client in the browser (where one tab = one user). Never put new QueryClient() at module scope.

docs.title: Using a query
docs.text:
// app/posts/PostList.tsx
"use client";
import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

async function fetchPosts() {
  const res = await fetch("/api/posts");
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to load posts");
  return res.json() as Promise<{ id: string; title: string }[]>;
}

export function PostList() {
  const { data, isPending, isError } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["posts"],
    queryFn: fetchPosts,
  });

  if (isPending) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  if (isError) return <p>Something went wrong.</p>;
  return (
    <ul>
      {data.map((p) => (
        <li key={p.id}>{p.title}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

docs.title: Prefetch on the server, hydrate on the client (HydrationBoundary)
docs.text:
The best of both worlds: fetch the initial data on the server (no client loading spinner, SEO-friendly) and hand it to TanStack Query so the same useQuery finds it already cached. You prefetch into a server-side QueryClient, dehydrate it, and wrap the client subtree in HydrationBoundary.

// app/posts/page.tsx — Server Component
import {
  dehydrate,
  HydrationBoundary,
} from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { getQueryClient } from "@/app/get-query-client";
import { getPosts } from "@/lib/data";
import { PostList } from "./PostList";

export default async function PostsPage() {
  const queryClient = getQueryClient();

  // Prefetch on the server using the SAME queryKey the client uses.
  // (Call the data function directly — no need to HTTP-fetch your own API.)
  await queryClient.prefetchQuery({
    queryKey: ["posts"],
    queryFn: getPosts,
  });

  return (
    // dehydrate() serializes the cache; HydrationBoundary rehydrates it on the client
    <HydrationBoundary state={dehydrate(queryClient)}>
      <PostList />
    </HydrationBoundary>
  );
}

The client PostList uses useQuery({ queryKey: ["posts"], queryFn: fetchPosts }). Because the cache was hydrated with that exact queryKey, it renders the data immediately — no loading flash — and only refetches in the background once the data goes stale (per staleTime). The query keys must match exactly for hydration to connect.

staleTime is what prevents a double fetch

If staleTime is 0 (the default), hydrated data is considered stale instantly and TanStack Query refetches it on mount — you prefetched on the server and fetched again on the client. Set a staleTime (e.g. 30–60s) so the freshly-prefetched data is trusted for a while. This is the single most common SSR-hydration mistake.

docs.title: SWR, briefly
docs.text:
SWR (also from Vercel) is the lighter-weight alternative with the same stale-while-revalidate philosophy. Setup mirrors TanStack Query — a client provider and a hook — and you seed server data via fallback (SWR's equivalent of hydration):

// app/posts/Posts.tsx
"use client";
import useSWR, { SWRConfig } from "swr";

const fetcher = (url: string) => fetch(url).then((r) => r.json());

function PostList() {
  const { data, isLoading } = useSWR("/api/posts", fetcher);
  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  return <ul>{data.map((p: any) => <li key={p.id}>{p.title}</li>)}</ul>;
}

// A Server Component renders <SWRConfig value={{ fallback: { "/api/posts": posts } }}>
// to seed the cache, and PostList reads it without a client fetch.
export function Posts({ fallback }: { fallback: Record<string, unknown> }) {
  return (
    <SWRConfig value={{ fallback }}>
      <PostList />
    </SWRConfig>
  );
}

Do you even need a client cache?

For data shown on first load that rarely changes, plain RSC (await in a Server Component) is simpler and ships less JS. Reach for TanStack Query / SWR when the client drives fetching: pagination, infinite scroll, polling, dependent queries, optimistic updates, or shared cached data across many components that mutate frequently.

docs.title: 5. Crossing the server/client boundary
docs.text: The recurring theme above — seeding a store, hydrating a query cache — is one pattern: fetch on the server, pass the initial value to a Client Component as a prop. Worth stating its rules directly.

docs.title: Pass initial data as a serializable prop
docs.text:
A Server Component fetches and passes the result down. The Client Component takes it as initialData and owns it from there.

// app/cart/page.tsx — Server Component
import { getCart } from "@/lib/data";
import { CartWidget } from "./CartWidget";

export default async function CartPage() {
  const cart = await getCart(); // server fetch
  return <CartWidget initialItems={cart.items} />; // hand off across the boundary
}

// app/cart/CartWidget.tsx
"use client";
import { useState } from "react";

type Item = { id: string; name: string };

export function CartWidget({ initialItems }: { initialItems: Item[] }) {
  // Seed client state from the server once; client owns it afterward.
  const [items, setItems] = useState(initialItems);
  // …add/remove interactively without a round-trip
  return <div>{items.length} items</div>;
}

Only serializable props cross the boundary

Props passed from a Server to a Client Component must be serializable: plain objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, null, and (with RSC's superset of JSON) Date, Map, Set, and Promises. You cannot pass functions, class instances, or DB connections. If you need behavior on the client, define it in client code; pass only the data.

docs.title: Choosing the hand-off mechanism
docs.text:
The three sections above are really three flavors of the same boundary crossing — pick by what owns the data afterward:

Initial server data lands in… Mechanism When Local component state initialItems prop → useState(initialItems) One component owns it; no refetching A Zustand store <Provider initialState={…}> → useRef factory Shared client state seeded from the server A query cache prefetchQuery → dehydrate → <HydrationBoundary> Server data the client will refetch/keep fresh The URL render with ?key=value already set Shareable, bookmarkable, refresh-proof

Don't double-fetch across the boundary

The classic mistake is fetching in a Server Component to render initial HTML and fetching the same thing again in a Client Component on mount. Either render it server-side and pass it as a prop, or hydrate the client cache (so the client treats the server's copy as fresh via staleTime/fallback). Pick one path for each piece of data.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Server state Data owned by the server (DB/CMS/API); prefer fetching it in a Server Component Client state Browser-born UI state (open/closed, drafts, tabs); needs a client tool RSC → less client state Server Components render data directly, so most "state" never enters a client store URL as state searchParams for shareable/bookmarkable/refresh-proof state nuqs useState-like hooks backed by the URL, with parsers and defaults Shallow routing shallow: true updates the URL without re-running the Server Component Context provider pattern Put providers in a "use client" file; render from the (server) layout Zustand per-request store A factory + useRef provider — never a module-level singleton on the server useShallow Shallow-compares multi-field selectors to avoid extra re-renders (Zustand v5) TanStack Query / SWR Client-side cache of server state for client-driven fetching getQueryClient Fresh QueryClient per server request, one memoized client in the browser HydrationBoundary Prefetch on the server, dehydrate, hydrate the client cache (set staleTime!) Boundary hand-off Fetch on the server, pass serializable initial data to a Client Component

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Rendering & the server/client boundary
  • TanStack Query v5 — Advanced SSR (App Router)
  • Zustand — Setup with Next.js
  • nuqs — Type-safe search params state
  • SWR — Next.js App Router

docs.title: Testing Next.js Apps
docs.text:
Testing a Next.js app is mostly testing React — but the App Router adds wrinkles that plain-React tooling doesn't cover out of the box: components that run on the server, the next/navigation and next/headers modules that only work inside a request, Route Handlers that are just exported functions, and Server Actions that look like ordinary async functions but really cross the network. This chapter covers the Next.js-specific angle on each.

Read the React testing chapter first

This chapter assumes you know the general testing toolkit — the testing pyramid, Vitest setup, React Testing Library queries (getByRole/findBy*), user-event, MSW handler basics, and Playwright fundamentals. All of that lives in React Testing and Debugging and is not repeated here. We build on it and focus only on what's different in Next.js.

docs.title: 1. The Lay of the Land
docs.text:
Next.js code falls into a few buckets, and each is tested a little differently:

What you're testing Best tool Why Pure logic / utilities Vitest No DOM, no framework — same as anywhere Client Components ("use client") Vitest + RTL Render in jsdom like ordinary React Async Server Components E2E (Playwright) RTL can't fully render them yet (see below) Route Handlers (route.ts) Vitest Call the exported GET/POST with a Request Server Actions ("use server") Vitest Call the function directly with mocked deps Whole user flows Playwright / Cypress Real browser, real server, real RSC

The single biggest Next.js testing decision is the line between unit/component tests (fast, in jsdom, with mocks) and E2E tests (slow, in a real browser, against a real build). Server-rendered behavior — Server Components, streaming, the actual data fetching — is most reliably verified by E2E. Logic and Client Components are unit-tested.

Async Server Components aren't fully supported by RTL yet

React Testing Library renders into jsdom, which has no React Server Components runtime. An async Server Component returns a promise that RTL can't render the way React does on the server, and Next-specific server APIs (fetch caching, cookies(), headers()) don't run there. The official guidance is:

  • For async Server Components, prefer end-to-end tests (Playwright) that exercise the real rendered output.
  • Or extract and unit-test the logic separately — pull data fetching, mapping, and formatting into plain async functions you can call directly, and keep the component a thin shell. You then test the functions with Vitest and the rendered result with E2E.

Synchronous Server Components (no await, no server-only APIs) can often be rendered by RTL because they're just functions returning JSX — but treat that as a convenience, not a guarantee.

docs.title: 2. Setting Up Vitest for Next.js
docs.text:
The Vitest + RTL + jsdom setup is the same as in the React chapter, with two Next.js additions: the React plugin must understand JSX/TSX in your app/ files, and you'll want path aliases (@/) to resolve. Next.js's docs use Vitest with @vitejs/plugin-react.

npm install -D vitest @vitejs/plugin-react jsdom \
  @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event @testing-library/jest-dom \
  vite-tsconfig-paths

// vitest.config.ts
import { defineConfig } from "vitest/config";
import react from "@vitejs/plugin-react";
import tsconfigPaths from "vite-tsconfig-paths";

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [tsconfigPaths(), react()], // tsconfigPaths resolves your "@/*" aliases
  test: {
    globals: true,
    environment: "jsdom",
    setupFiles: ["./vitest.setup.ts"],
  },
});

// vitest.setup.ts
import "@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest";

// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "test": "vitest",
    "test:run": "vitest run"
  }
}

Why not Jest?

Next.js ships first-class config for both Jest (next/jest, which wires up SWC, CSS modules, and next/font mocks automatically) and Vitest. This chapter uses Vitest to stay consistent with the rest of the guide; if your team prefers Jest, run npm install -D jest jest-environment-jsdom @testing-library/jest-dom and use the next/jest transformer — the tests themselves are nearly identical.

Don't put server-API tests in the jsdom environment

Route Handler and Server Action tests don't need a DOM and may import server-only modules. Either keep them in the default config (they ignore jsdom) or give them the node environment with a per-file directive:

// @vitest-environment node

docs.title: 3. Testing Client Components
docs.text:
A "use client" component is ordinary React — render it with RTL exactly as the React chapter describes. The only Next.js-specific issue is that Client Components often import from next/navigation and next/link, which throw outside a Next runtime. You mock them.

// components/SearchBox.tsx
"use client";

import { useRouter, useSearchParams } from "next/navigation";
import { useState } from "react";

export default function SearchBox() {
  const router = useRouter();
  const params = useSearchParams();
  const [q, setQ] = useState(params.get("q") ?? "");

  return (
    <form
      onSubmit={(e) => {
        e.preventDefault();
        router.push(`/search?q=${encodeURIComponent(q)}`);
      }}
    >
      <label htmlFor="q">Search</label>
      <input id="q" value={q} onChange={(e) => setQ(e.target.value)} />
      <button type="submit">Go</button>
    </form>
  );
}

docs.title: Mocking next/navigation
docs.text:
useRouter, usePathname, and useSearchParams all come from next/navigation. Replace the module with vi.mock and hand back controllable stand-ins. Note that useSearchParams must return a real URLSearchParams (or ReadonlyURLSearchParams) so .get() works.

// components/SearchBox.test.tsx
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import userEvent from "@testing-library/user-event";
import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import SearchBox from "./SearchBox";

const push = vi.fn();

vi.mock("next/navigation", () => ({
  useRouter: () => ({ push, replace: vi.fn(), refresh: vi.fn(), prefetch: vi.fn() }),
  usePathname: () => "/search",
  useSearchParams: () => new URLSearchParams("q=react"),
}));

beforeEach(() => vi.clearAllMocks());

describe("SearchBox", () => {
  it("prefills from the URL and pushes a new query on submit", async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<SearchBox />);

    const input = screen.getByLabelText(/search/i);
    expect(input).toHaveValue("react"); // came from useSearchParams

    await user.clear(input);
    await user.type(input, "next.js");
    await user.click(screen.getByRole("button", { name: /go/i }));

    expect(push).toHaveBeenCalledWith("/search?q=next.js");
  });
});

Mock once, override per test

If you need different return values across tests, expose the mocked functions and reassign them with vi.mocked(...):

import { usePathname } from "next/navigation";

vi.mock("next/navigation", () => ({
  usePathname: vi.fn(),
  useRouter: () => ({ push: vi.fn() }),
  useSearchParams: () => new URLSearchParams(),
}));

it("highlights the active link", () => {
  vi.mocked(usePathname).mockReturnValue("/dashboard");
  // ...render and assert
});

<Link> usually just works

next/link renders a plain <a> in tests, so you rarely need to mock it. If a component calls router.push via a <Link>, query the anchor by role (getByRole("link", { name: ... })) and assert its href instead of spying on the router.

docs.title: Mocking next/headers
docs.text:
cookies() and headers() from next/headers only run on the server. If a Client Component's child path or a shared helper imports them in a unit test, mock the module:

vi.mock("next/headers", () => ({
  cookies: () => ({
    get: (name: string) => (name === "theme" ? { value: "dark" } : undefined),
    getAll: () => [{ name: "theme", value: "dark" }],
    has: (name: string) => name === "theme",
  }),
  headers: () => new Headers({ "x-forwarded-for": "127.0.0.1" }),
}));

cookies() and headers() are async in Next.js 15

In Next.js 15 these APIs became asynchronous — you await cookies() / await headers() in app code. When you mock them, make the mock match: either return the object directly and await a non-promise (which is fine), or return Promise.resolve(...) to mirror real usage precisely. Prefer mirroring real usage so your test catches a missing await.

docs.title: 4. Testing Route Handlers
docs.text:
A Route Handler (app/api/.../route.ts) exports functions named after HTTP verbs. The beauty of this is that a handler is just a function — you import the exported GET or POST and call it with a standard Request. No server needs to be running.

// app/api/users/route.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

const db = { users: [{ id: 1, name: "Ada Lovelace" }] };

export async function GET(request: Request) {
  const { searchParams } = new URL(request.url);
  const q = searchParams.get("q")?.toLowerCase();
  const users = q
    ? db.users.filter((u) => u.name.toLowerCase().includes(q))
    : db.users;
  return NextResponse.json({ users });
}

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const body = (await request.json()) as { name?: string };
  if (!body.name) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "name is required" }, { status: 400 });
  }
  const user = { id: db.users.length + 1, name: body.name };
  db.users.push(user);
  return NextResponse.json(user, { status: 201 });
}

// app/api/users/route.test.ts
// @vitest-environment node
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { GET, POST } from "./route";

describe("GET /api/users", () => {
  it("returns all users", async () => {
    const res = await GET(new Request("http://localhost/api/users"));
    expect(res.status).toBe(200);
    expect(await res.json()).toEqual({ users: [{ id: 1, name: "Ada Lovelace" }] });
  });

  it("filters by the q query param", async () => {
    const res = await GET(new Request("http://localhost/api/users?q=ada"));
    const { users } = await res.json();
    expect(users).toHaveLength(1);
  });
});

describe("POST /api/users", () => {
  it("creates a user", async () => {
    const req = new Request("http://localhost/api/users", {
      method: "POST",
      headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
      body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Grace Hopper" }),
    });
    const res = await POST(req);
    expect(res.status).toBe(201);
    expect(await res.json()).toMatchObject({ name: "Grace Hopper" });
  });

  it("400s when name is missing", async () => {
    const req = new Request("http://localhost/api/users", {
      method: "POST",
      body: JSON.stringify({}),
    });
    const res = await POST(req);
    expect(res.status).toBe(400);
  });
});

NextResponse is a real Response

NextResponse.json(...) extends the web Response, so res.status, await res.json(), and res.headers.get(...) all work in the test with no special handling.

docs.title: Dynamic segments and the context argument
docs.text:
A dynamic route like app/api/users/[id]/route.ts receives a second context argument whose params you must supply. In Next.js 15, params is a Promise, so wrap it.

// app/api/users/[id]/route.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function GET(
  _request: Request,
  { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }
) {
  const { id } = await params;
  return NextResponse.json({ id: Number(id) });
}

// app/api/users/[id]/route.test.ts
// @vitest-environment node
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { GET } from "./route";

it("reads the dynamic id param", async () => {
  const res = await GET(new Request("http://localhost/api/users/42"), {
    params: Promise.resolve({ id: "42" }), // params is a Promise in Next 15
  });
  expect(await res.json()).toEqual({ id: 42 });
});

Need NextRequest, cookies, or headers?

If your handler uses request.cookies or request.nextUrl, construct a NextRequest (from next/server) instead of a plain Request, or set headers on the Request you pass in:

import { NextRequest } from "next/server";
const req = new NextRequest("http://localhost/api/me", {
  headers: { cookie: "session=abc123" },
});

docs.title: 5. Testing Server Actions
docs.text:
A Server Action ("use server") is, from a test's point of view, an exported async function. You import it and call it directly, mocking the things it touches — the database, revalidatePath, redirect, and auth helpers. You are testing the action's logic, not the React form integration (that's an E2E concern).

// app/actions.ts
"use server";

import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { z } from "zod";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { getCurrentUser } from "@/lib/auth";

const schema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1, "Title is required"),
});

export async function createPost(_prev: unknown, formData: FormData) {
  const user = await getCurrentUser();
  if (!user) return { error: "Unauthorized" };

  const parsed = schema.safeParse({ title: formData.get("title") });
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { error: parsed.error.issues[0].message };
  }

  const post = await db.post.create({
    data: { title: parsed.data.title, authorId: user.id },
  });

  revalidatePath("/posts");
  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`);
}

The action mixes pure validation with three side-effecting dependencies. Mock each so the test stays isolated and you can assert how the action used them.

// app/actions.test.ts
// @vitest-environment node
import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { getCurrentUser } from "@/lib/auth";
import { createPost } from "./actions";

vi.mock("next/cache", () => ({ revalidatePath: vi.fn() }));
vi.mock("next/navigation", () => ({
  // redirect throws internally in Next; a no-op mock is enough for unit tests
  redirect: vi.fn(),
}));
vi.mock("@/lib/auth", () => ({ getCurrentUser: vi.fn() }));
vi.mock("@/lib/db", () => ({
  db: { post: { create: vi.fn() } },
}));

function form(fields: Record<string, string>) {
  const fd = new FormData();
  for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(fields)) fd.set(k, v);
  return fd;
}

beforeEach(() => vi.clearAllMocks());

describe("createPost", () => {
  it("rejects unauthenticated users", async () => {
    vi.mocked(getCurrentUser).mockResolvedValue(null);

    const result = await createPost(null, form({ title: "Hello" }));

    expect(result).toEqual({ error: "Unauthorized" });
    expect(db.post.create).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
  });

  it("returns a validation error for an empty title", async () => {
    vi.mocked(getCurrentUser).mockResolvedValue({ id: "u1" });

    const result = await createPost(null, form({ title: "" }));

    expect(result).toEqual({ error: "Title is required" });
    expect(db.post.create).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
  });

  it("creates the post, revalidates, and redirects", async () => {
    vi.mocked(getCurrentUser).mockResolvedValue({ id: "u1" });
    vi.mocked(db.post.create).mockResolvedValue({ id: 7, title: "Hello" });

    await createPost(null, form({ title: "Hello" }));

    expect(db.post.create).toHaveBeenCalledWith({
      data: { title: "Hello", authorId: "u1" },
    });
    expect(revalidatePath).toHaveBeenCalledWith("/posts");
    expect(redirect).toHaveBeenCalledWith("/posts/7");
  });
});

redirect() throws by design

In real Next.js, redirect() works by throwing a special NEXT_REDIRECT error that the framework catches. In a unit test that's noise, so mock it to a no-op (as above) and assert it was called. If you test the un-mocked action, wrap the call in expect(...).rejects or a try/catch and inspect the thrown digest — but mocking is simpler and tests your intent more directly.

Keep actions thin and testable

The easiest Server Actions to test are the ones that delegate: validate input, call a well-defined data-access function, revalidate, redirect. Push real database logic into a lib/ data layer so you can mock it cleanly here and test it separately. This is the same data-access-layer pattern from the Database Integration chapter.

docs.title: 6. API Mocking with MSW
docs.text:
When a Client Component (or a SWR/TanStack Query hook) fetches from your own API or a third party, mock the network with MSW v2 instead of stubbing fetch. The handler setup is identical to the React chapter — same http/HttpResponse API, same setupServer for Node — so this section only highlights the Next.js specifics.

// test/handlers.ts
import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw";

export const handlers = [
  http.get("https://api.example.com/posts", () =>
    HttpResponse.json([{ id: 1, title: "Hello from MSW" }])
  ),
];

// test/server.ts
import { setupServer } from "msw/node";
import { handlers } from "./handlers";

export const server = setupServer(...handlers);

// vitest.setup.ts
import "@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest";
import { beforeAll, afterEach, afterAll } from "vitest";
import { server } from "./test/server";

beforeAll(() => server.listen({ onUnhandledRequest: "error" }));
afterEach(() => server.resetHandlers());
afterAll(() => server.close());

Use absolute URLs in handlers for server-side fetches

In jsdom/Node there is no origin, so a relative fetch("/api/posts") has nothing to resolve against. For Client-Component tests, configure a base URL or use absolute handler patterns. For data fetched inside Server Components, MSW-in-Node can intercept those server fetch calls too — but because async Server Components are awkward to render in jsdom (Section 1), it's usually cleaner to assert that behavior with Playwright against the real server, mocking upstream APIs with page.route.

MSW shines for third-party APIs

The highest-value MSW use in Next.js is mocking external services (Stripe, a CMS, an auth provider) in component and integration tests, so you don't hit real endpoints. For your own Route Handlers, prefer calling the exported function directly (Section 4) — it's faster and doesn't need interception.

docs.title: 7. End-to-End Testing with Playwright
docs.text:
E2E is where Next.js testing gets its real confidence: a Playwright run drives a real browser against a real production build, so Server Components, streaming, caching, Server Actions, and middleware all execute for real. This is the recommended way to test async Server Components and full user flows.

npm init playwright@latest

The key Next.js detail is the webServer block: build and start the app the way production does (next build && next start), not next dev. The dev server is slower, behaves differently around caching, and isn't what you ship.

// playwright.config.ts
import { defineConfig, devices } from "@playwright/test";

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: "./e2e",
  use: { baseURL: "http://localhost:3000", trace: "on-first-retry" },
  webServer: {
    command: "npm run build && npm run start", // next build && next start
    url: "http://localhost:3000",
    reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI,
    timeout: 120_000, // a production build can take a while on first run
  },
  projects: [{ name: "chromium", use: { ...devices["Desktop Chrome"] } }],
});

Speed up local iteration

A full next build before every run is slow while developing tests. Locally you can point webServer.command at npm run dev and switch to the build command in CI via an env check. Just remember dev-mode caching differs from production, so the build-based config is the source of truth.

A test exercising a Server-Component page plus a Server Action — the exact things unit tests can't cover:

// e2e/posts.spec.ts
import { test, expect } from "@playwright/test";

test("renders server-fetched posts", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/posts");
  // This HTML was rendered on the server from a real data fetch.
  await expect(page.getByRole("heading", { name: /posts/i })).toBeVisible();
  await expect(page.getByRole("listitem")).not.toHaveCount(0);
});

test("creates a post via a Server Action", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/posts/new");
  await page.getByLabel(/title/i).fill("My first post");
  await page.getByRole("button", { name: /create/i }).click();

  // The action redirected to the new post's page.
  await expect(page).toHaveURL(/\/posts\/\d+/);
  await expect(page.getByRole("heading", { name: "My first post" })).toBeVisible();
});

docs.title: Mocking upstream APIs and faking auth
docs.text:
Use page.route to stub the external services your server calls, and seed an authenticated session by setting a cookie before navigating (the exact mechanism depends on your auth setup — see the Authentication chapter).

test("shows weather from a mocked upstream API", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.route("https://api.weather.com/**", (route) =>
    route.fulfill({ json: { tempC: 21 } })
  );
  await page.goto("/weather");
  await expect(page.getByText("21°C")).toBeVisible();
});

test("dashboard requires a session", async ({ page, context }) => {
  await context.addCookies([
    { name: "session", value: "test-token", url: "http://localhost:3000" },
  ]);
  await page.goto("/dashboard");
  await expect(page.getByRole("heading", { name: /dashboard/i })).toBeVisible();
});

Reuse a logged-in state with storage state

Logging in through the UI in every test is slow. Do it once in a Playwright setup project (or global setup), save context.storageState() to a file, and load it via use: { storageState: "e2e/.auth/user.json" }. The Playwright auth docs cover this pattern in full — it's the standard way to test authenticated Next.js apps.

docs.title: 8. Cypress (Alternative)
docs.text:
Cypress is a solid alternative to Playwright for Next.js E2E, with its signature time-travel runner. The setup mirrors any app: point it at your running server and write specs. As with Playwright, run against a production build for trustworthy results.

npm install -D cypress
npx cypress open

// cypress/e2e/posts.cy.ts
describe("posts", () => {
  it("creates a post", () => {
    cy.visit("/posts/new");
    cy.findByLabelText(/title/i).type("My first post");
    cy.findByRole("button", { name: /create/i }).click();

    cy.url().should("match", /\/posts\/\d+/);
    cy.contains("My first post").should("be.visible");
  });
});

You start the server yourself before running Cypress (Cypress has no built-in webServer like Playwright); the start-server-and-test package is the common helper:

// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "e2e": "start-server-and-test 'npm run build && npm run start' http://localhost:3000 'cypress run'"
  }
}

Component testing in Cypress

Cypress also has a Component Testing mode that can mount Next.js Client Components in a real browser. It's a middle ground between RTL and full E2E, but for Next.js most teams stick with Vitest + RTL for components and reserve Cypress/Playwright for flows. See the comparison table in the React Testing chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Pick the tool by what runs where. Logic and Client Components → Vitest + RTL; Route Handlers and Server Actions → call the exported function directly; Server Components and full flows → Playwright.
  • Async Server Components aren't fully supported by RTL — prefer E2E for them, or extract their logic into plain functions you can unit-test and keep the component thin.
  • Mock next/navigation (useRouter/usePathname/useSearchParams) and next/headers (cookies/headers) with vi.mock for Client Component tests; remember cookies()/headers() are async in Next 15.
  • Route Handlers are just functions — import GET/POST, pass a Request, assert on the returned Response. Dynamic params is a Promise in Next 15.
  • Server Actions are just functions too — call them with a FormData and mock db, revalidatePath, redirect, and auth; remember redirect() throws by design.
  • MSW v2 mocks the network for Client Components and third-party APIs; for your own API, calling the handler directly is faster.
  • Playwright is the recommended E2E tool — configure webServer to run next build && next start so tests hit a real production build; Cypress is the established alternative.
  • For all the general testing fundamentals (queries, user-event, the testing pyramid, debugging, error boundaries), see React Testing and Debugging.

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Testing overview: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/testing
  • Next.js — Setting up Vitest: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/testing/vitest
  • Next.js — Setting up Jest: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/testing/jest
  • Next.js — Setting up Playwright: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/testing/playwright
  • Next.js — Setting up Cypress: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/testing/cypress
  • Vitest documentation: https://vitest.dev/
  • Testing Library — React: https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/intro/
  • Mock Service Worker (MSW): https://mswjs.io/
  • Playwright — Authentication guide: https://playwright.dev/docs/auth
  • Playwright — Network mocking: https://playwright.dev/docs/mock
  • start-server-and-test: https://github.com/bahmutov/start-server-and-test

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
Building a Next.js app is only half the job — it has to run somewhere. Next.js is built by Vercel, so a zero-config push-to-deploy on Vercel is the smoothest path, but the framework is deliberately portable: the same app runs on any Node server, inside a Docker container, or behind your own reverse proxy. The trade-off is what you have to wire up yourself — ISR caching, image optimization, and observability all come free on Vercel and become your responsibility when you self-host.

This chapter covers deploying to Vercel, the standalone build output, self-hosting with a Node server and a production Dockerfile, how environment variables behave (build-time vs runtime, the NEXT_PUBLIC_ exposure rule), CDN/caching considerations, and monitoring/analytics. Examples target Next.js 15 with the App Router and TypeScript.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/deploying

docs.title: 1. Deploying to Vercel
docs.text: Vercel is the reference platform for Next.js. It detects the framework, runs the build, and maps your app's features onto its infrastructure automatically — there is no adapter, plugin, or config file to write for a standard app.

docs.title: What "zero-config" actually does
docs.text:
When you connect a Next.js repo, Vercel infers everything from package.json and the framework:

Your code Vercel maps it to Static routes / SSG / ISR pages Static files on the global CDN async Server Components / SSR routes Serverless (or Edge) Functions Route Handlers (route.ts) Serverless / Edge Functions next/image The built-in Image Optimization service middleware.ts Runs at the edge, before the cache revalidate / revalidateTag Managed ISR + the Data Cache

You do not run next start yourself on Vercel — there is no long-lived server. Each dynamic render and Route Handler is an on-demand function invocation.

docs.title: Connecting a repository
docs.text:
The normal flow is Git-driven:

  1. Push your project to GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.
  2. In the Vercel dashboard, Add New → Project and import the repo.
  3. Vercel auto-detects "Next.js" as the framework preset; the default build settings are correct for almost all apps.
  4. Add environment variables (below), then Deploy.

From then on, every push to the production branch (usually main) triggers a production deployment, and every push to any other branch or pull request triggers a preview deployment.

You can also deploy from the terminal with the Vercel CLI, which is handy for one-off deploys and debugging the build locally:

npm i -g vercel

vercel          # creates a preview deployment from the current directory
vercel --prod   # promotes to production
vercel env pull # download project env vars into a local .env.local

docs.title: Environment variables on Vercel
docs.text:
Set these under Project → Settings → Environment Variables. Each variable is scoped to one or more environments:

  • Production — used for production deployments.
  • Preview — used for preview (branch/PR) deployments.
  • Development — pulled down by vercel env pull for local next dev.
# Example values set in the dashboard (never commit real secrets)
DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@host:5432/app   # Production + Preview
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=https://example.com          # Production only
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_...                      # Production only

NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables are inlined into the browser bundle

Any variable prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ is embedded into the client JavaScript at build time and is fully visible to anyone who opens DevTools. Never put secrets (API keys, database URLs, tokens) behind that prefix. See section 5.

docs.title: Preview deployments
docs.text:
Every branch and pull request gets its own immutable URL (e.g. my-app-git-feature-x-team.vercel.app). This is the single most useful Vercel feature for teams:

  • Reviewers click a live URL instead of pulling the branch.
  • Each preview is fully isolated, with its own Preview environment variables.
  • The Vercel GitHub app comments the preview URL on the PR automatically.

Because previews use the Preview env scope, point them at a staging database — not production — so a test PR can't mutate live data.

docs.title: Build & function settings
docs.text:
The defaults rarely need changing, but the knobs exist:

  • Build command / Output directory / Install command — override only for monorepos or custom toolchains. For a monorepo, set the Root Directory to the app's subfolder.
  • Node.js version — set in Project Settings or pinned via the engines field in package.json.
  • Function region & runtime — choose a region close to your database to cut latency; pick the runtime per route with export const runtime = "edge" | "nodejs".
  • Function max duration — extend the execution limit for long-running Route Handlers.

A small amount can be committed as vercel.json so it lives in the repo:

{
  "$schema": "https://openapi.vercel.sh/vercel.json",
  "regions": ["iad1"],
  "functions": {
    "app/api/report/route.ts": { "maxDuration": 60 }
  },
  "crons": [{ "path": "/api/cron/cleanup", "schedule": "0 3 * * *" }]
}

docs.title: 2. The build output
docs.text:
Running the production build compiles your app and prints a per-route breakdown:

next build

Route (app)                      Size     First Load JS
┌ ○ /                            1.2 kB         92 kB
├ ● /blog/[slug]                 0.8 kB         91 kB
├ ƒ /dashboard                   2.4 kB         96 kB
└ ƒ /api/posts                   0 B             0 B

○  (Static)    prerendered as static content
●  (SSG)       prerendered using generateStaticParams
ƒ  (Dynamic)   server-rendered on demand

Read this output before every deploy — it tells you exactly which routes are static (cheap, CDN-served) and which are dynamic (a function invocation per request). A route you expected to be static showing up as ƒ (Dynamic) usually means you accidentally used cookies(), headers(), or a no-store fetch in it.

The build artifacts land in .next/. By default this includes the full node_modules, which is fine for Vercel but heavy for a container.

docs.title: output: 'standalone'
docs.text:
For self-hosting, set output: 'standalone' in your config. Next.js then traces exactly which files (including the minimal subset of node_modules) the server actually needs and copies them into .next/standalone/. This is the key to small, fast Docker images.

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  output: "standalone",
};

export default nextConfig;

After next build, .next/standalone/ contains a self-contained app with its own minimal server.js. You start it with node server.js — no next start, and you don't need to ship the whole node_modules tree.

Standalone doesn't copy public/ or static assets

The standalone output deliberately excludes public/ and .next/static/, because on many platforms those are served by a CDN, not the Node process. When you self-host the whole thing yourself you must copy them in manually — the Dockerfile in section 4 does exactly that.

docs.title: 3. Self-hosting on a Node server
docs.text:
Next.js doesn't need Vercel. Any environment that can run Node can run a Next.js app with two commands:

next build     # produce the production build
next start     # start the production server (default: port 3000)

// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start -p 3000"
  }
}

next start is a long-lived Node process that serves static assets, renders dynamic routes, runs Route Handlers, handles ISR regeneration, and performs image optimization — everything Vercel splits across services, in one process. Keep it alive with a process manager (pm2, systemd) and run multiple instances behind a load balancer to use all CPU cores and survive restarts.

output: 'export' is not the same thing

output: 'export' produces a pure static site (HTML/CSS/JS only) you can drop on any static host. But it disables everything that needs a server: SSR, ISR, Route Handlers, Server Actions, Middleware, and on-demand image optimization. Use it only for fully static sites; for everything else use next start or the standalone server.

docs.title: Reverse proxy notes
docs.text:
In production you almost always put Nginx (or Caddy/a cloud load balancer) in front of the Node process to terminate TLS, serve as a single entry point, and optionally cache static assets:

# /etc/nginx/conf.d/nextjs.conf
server {
  listen 443 ssl http2;
  server_name example.com;

  # ... ssl_certificate / ssl_certificate_key ...

  location / {
    proxy_pass         http://127.0.0.1:3000;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header   Host              $host;
    proxy_set_header   X-Real-IP         $remote_addr;
    proxy_set_header   X-Forwarded-For   $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
    proxy_set_header   X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;   # so Next.js knows it's HTTPS
    proxy_set_header   X-Forwarded-Host  $host;
  }

  # Long-cache immutable build assets
  location /_next/static/ {
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;
    add_header Cache-Control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable";
  }
}

Key points: forward X-Forwarded-* headers so request-host/protocol detection works, don't buffer responses if you rely on streaming (proxy_buffering off; for streamed routes), and let /_next/static/ (content-hashed, immutable) be cached aggressively.

docs.title: ISR & caching when self-hosting
docs.text:
This is the part that surprises people. ISR works out of the box with next start, but the default cache is on the local filesystem of that single instance. With more than one instance (a typical horizontally-scaled or containerized deploy) that breaks down:

  • Instance A regenerates a page; instance B still serves the stale copy.
  • On platforms with ephemeral disks (most container hosts), the cache is lost on every redeploy or restart, forcing full regeneration.

The fix is a shared cache handler backed by something all instances share — Redis, S3, etc. Point Next.js at a custom handler in your config:

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  output: "standalone",
  cacheHandler: require.resolve("./cache-handler.js"),
  // disable the in-memory LRU when using an external store
  cacheMaxMemorySize: 0,
};

export default nextConfig;

// cache-handler.js — a minimal Redis-backed CacheHandler
// Implements the interface Next.js expects: get / set / revalidateTag.
const { createClient } = require("redis");

const client = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });
client.connect().catch(console.error);

module.exports = class CacheHandler {
  constructor(options) {
    this.options = options;
  }

  async get(key) {
    const value = await client.get(`nextcache:${key}`);
    return value ? JSON.parse(value) : null;
  }

  async set(key, data, ctx) {
    const entry = { value: data, lastModified: Date.now(), tags: ctx.tags };
    await client.set(`nextcache:${key}`, JSON.stringify(entry));
    // Maintain a tag → keys index so revalidateTag can find entries
    for (const tag of ctx.tags ?? []) {
      await client.sAdd(`nextcache:tag:${tag}`, key);
    }
  }

  async revalidateTag(tags) {
    const tagList = Array.isArray(tags) ? tags : [tags];
    for (const tag of tagList) {
      const keys = await client.sMembers(`nextcache:tag:${tag}`);
      if (keys.length) {
        await client.del(keys.map((k) => `nextcache:${k}`));
        await client.del(`nextcache:tag:${tag}`);
      }
    }
  }
};

With this in place, all instances read and write the same cache, regeneration is shared, and revalidatePath/revalidateTag invalidate consistently across the fleet. (For common stores you can use the community @neshca/cache-handler package instead of hand-rolling this.)

Image optimization when self-hosting

next/image optimizes images on the fly using sharp — install it (npm i sharp) for the standalone server, or it falls back to a slower path. If a CDN already handles image resizing, you can set images.unoptimized: true or configure a custom loader to skip the Node-side work.

docs.title: 4. Self-hosting with Docker
docs.text:
A production Dockerfile uses the standalone output and a multi-stage build so the final image contains only the runtime, not the full toolchain or node_modules. This typically produces an image in the low hundreds of MB rather than over a gigabyte.

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1

# 1) Install dependencies only when lockfile changes
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm ci

# 2) Build the app (requires output: 'standalone' in next.config.ts)
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

# 3) Minimal runtime image
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1

# Run as an unprivileged user
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs \
 && adduser  --system --uid 1001 nextjs

# Copy the standalone server, then the assets it does NOT include
COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"

# The standalone build emits its own server.js entrypoint
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# Build and run
docker build -t my-next-app .
docker run -p 3000:3000 --env-file .env.production my-next-app

Notes that trip people up:

  • The two COPY lines for public and .next/static are mandatory — the standalone output omits them (see section 2).
  • Pass runtime secrets with --env-file / -e at docker run, not baked into the image. Only NEXT_PUBLIC_ values that exist at build time are inlined; anything read on the server at runtime should be injected when the container starts.
  • Set HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 so the server listens on all interfaces inside the container.
  • For ISR across replicas, combine this with the shared cache handler from section 3.

A docker-compose.yml ties the app to its cache/database for local production parity:

services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
    env_file: .env.production
    depends_on: [redis]
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ["6379:6379"]

docs.title: 5. Environment variables in depth
docs.text: Next.js loads .env files automatically and distinguishes server-only variables from browser-exposed ones.

docs.title: .env file loading order
docs.text:
Next.js reads these files, with later ones overriding earlier ones (and .env.local always overriding except in test):

File Loaded when Commit to git? .env always ✅ defaults / non-secrets .env.local always (except next test) ❌ never — real secrets .env.development next dev ✅ .env.production next build / next start ✅ non-secrets only 
# .env.local (gitignored)
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/dev
AUTH_SECRET=super-secret-value
NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000

Always add .env*.local to .gitignore (create-next-app does this for you).

docs.title: Server-only vs NEXT_PUBLIC_
docs.text:
• A variable without the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix is available only in server code — Server Components, Route Handlers, Server Actions, middleware.ts, and next.config. If you read it in a Client Component it is undefined.
  • A variable with NEXT_PUBLIC_ is inlined into the JavaScript bundle so the browser can read it.
// Server Component / Route Handler — both work
const db = process.env.DATABASE_URL;             // server-only secret ✅
const url = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL;    // also available ✅

// Client Component ("use client")
const url = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL;    // ✅ inlined at build
const db  = process.env.DATABASE_URL;            // ❌ undefined in the browser

NEXT_PUBLIC_ is permanent, public, and build-time

NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables are replaced with their literal value during next build. Two consequences: (1) they are visible to every user — never use the prefix for secrets; (2) changing one requires a rebuild, not just a restart, because the old value is already baked into the bundle. Treat them as compile-time constants.

docs.title: Build-time vs runtime
docs.text:
This distinction is the source of most "it works locally but not in prod" bugs:

  • Build-time — every NEXT_PUBLIC_ value and anything read while statically generating a page is fixed at next build. In a Docker image these are baked in.
  • Runtime — plain (non-public) process.env.* reads inside dynamic server code are evaluated on each request in the running process, so you can change them by restarting with new values (no rebuild).

To force a value to be read at runtime instead of inlined at build, read it inside a dynamic code path. The App Router also exposes a connection() / dynamic API to opt a render out of static generation when it depends on runtime env. The practical rule: inject runtime secrets when the container starts; only build-time public config goes in the image.

docs.title: 6. Caching & CDN considerations
docs.text:
Performance and cost both come down to serving as much as possible from a CDN and invoking functions as little as possible.

  • Static & ISR pages are cacheable files — put a CDN in front of your deployment (Vercel does this automatically; self-hosters use Cloudflare, CloudFront, or the proxy cache). This is what lets a static route absorb unlimited traffic for near-zero cost.
  • Immutable assets — everything under /_next/static/ is content-hashed, so it's safe to serve with Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable.
  • Set cache behavior intentionally with Cache-Control headers via next.config (or the proxy):
// next.config.ts — example header rules
const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  async headers() {
    return [
      {
        source: "/_next/static/:path*",
        headers: [
          { key: "Cache-Control", value: "public, max-age=31536000, immutable" },
        ],
      },
    ];
  },
};
  • ISR is stale-while-revalidate at the edge — the CDN serves the cached page instantly and Next.js regenerates in the background. On self-host this only works correctly with a shared cache handler (section 3).
  • Don't cache personalized responses — routes that read cookies()/headers() are dynamic; make sure your proxy/CDN doesn't cache them across users.
  • Middleware runs before the cache — it's the right place for redirects and auth gating, but heavy work there is paid on every request, even for cached pages.

docs.title: 7. Monitoring & analytics
docs.text: A deployed app needs observability — to catch errors, watch real-user performance, and trace slow requests.

docs.title: Vercel Analytics & Speed Insights
docs.text:
On Vercel, two first-party, privacy-friendly tools drop in with a single component each:

  • Analytics — page-view and visitor metrics (no cookies, GDPR-friendly).
  • Speed Insights — real-user Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) measured in the field, not a lab.
npm i @vercel/analytics @vercel/speed-insights

// app/layout.tsx
import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/next";
import { SpeedInsights } from "@vercel/speed-insights/next";

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        {children}
        <Analytics />
        <SpeedInsights />
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

docs.title: OpenTelemetry
docs.text:
For traces and metrics that work anywhere (Vercel or self-hosted), Next.js has built-in OpenTelemetry support. It auto-instruments rendering and data fetching so you can export spans to Datadog, Honeycomb, Grafana, Jaeger, etc.

npm i @vercel/otel

// instrumentation.ts — at the project root; runs once on server startup
import { registerOTel } from "@vercel/otel";

export function register() {
  registerOTel({ serviceName: "my-next-app" });
}

The register() export in instrumentation.ts is Next.js's official startup hook — useful for OTel, error reporters (e.g. Sentry's init), and other one-time server setup.

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
• Server-side console.log/console.error from Server Components, Route Handlers, and Server Actions go to the server process — captured by Vercel's runtime logs, or by docker logs/your log aggregator when self-hosting. They never reach the browser.
  • Prefer structured (JSON) logging (e.g. pino) in production so logs are queryable in your aggregator.
  • Pair an error-tracking service (Sentry, etc.) with instrumentation.ts and an app/error.tsx / global-error.tsx boundary so client-side and rendering errors are reported, not just lost.
// app/global-error.tsx — top-level error boundary ("use client")
"use client";

export default function GlobalError({
  error,
  reset,
}: {
  error: Error & { digest?: string };
  reset: () => void;
}) {
  // report(error)  ← send to Sentry / your tracker here
  return (
    <html>
      <body>
        <h2>Something went wrong</h2>
        <button onClick={reset}>Try again</button>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Topic 🧠 Key point Vercel Zero-config: static → CDN, dynamic → functions, no next start Preview deployments Every branch/PR gets an isolated URL + its own Preview env vars Build output next build shows which routes are Static (○/●) vs Dynamic (ƒ) output: 'standalone' Traces minimal files into .next/standalone/ for small Docker images next start Long-lived Node server; run multiple instances behind a load balancer output: 'export' Pure static; disables SSR/ISR/Route Handlers/Actions/Middleware Dockerfile Multi-stage + standalone; must also copy public/ and .next/static/ ISR self-hosted Default cache is per-instance/ephemeral → use a shared cache handler Reverse proxy Forward X-Forwarded-*; long-cache /_next/static/; mind streaming NEXT_PUBLIC_ Inlined into the browser bundle at build — never a secret, needs rebuild Build vs runtime env Public/static values baked at build; server secrets injected at runtime CDN/caching Static + ISR served from edge; /_next/static/ is immutable Analytics @vercel/analytics + @vercel/speed-insights for field Core Web Vitals OpenTelemetry instrumentation.ts + @vercel/otel for traces anywhere Logging Server logs go to the runtime/docker logs; use structured logging

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js Docs — Deploying
  • Next.js Docs — Self-Hosting
  • Next.js Docs — Environment Variables
  • Next.js Docs — output: 'standalone'
  • Next.js Docs — Custom Cache Handler
  • Next.js Docs — OpenTelemetry
  • Vercel — Analytics & Speed Insights

docs.title: Routing in Next.js
docs.text:
The App Router is file-system based: the folders you create inside app/ are your URL structure, and a handful of reserved file names (page, layout, loading, error, …) decide what each segment renders. There is no central route config to keep in sync—the directory tree is the routing table.

This chapter covers how URLs map to files, how layouts nest, the full range of dynamic and advanced route conventions (route groups, parallel and intercepting routes), the navigation APIs, and the special files that give each segment loading, error, and not-found UI. Throughout, the stack is Next.js 15 + TypeScript + React 18/19, App Router only.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/routing

docs.title: 1. File-based routing in app/
docs.text:
A route is created when a folder contains a page.tsx. The folder path (relative to app/) becomes the URL path; the page.tsx inside it makes that path publicly reachable.

app/
├── layout.tsx          → required root layout (wraps every page)
├── page.tsx            → /
├── about/
│   └── page.tsx        → /about
└── blog/
    ├── page.tsx        → /blog
    └── [slug]/
        └── page.tsx    → /blog/:slug

A folder without a page.tsx is just a path segment used for organisation—it doesn't become a navigable URL on its own. Files you co-locate next to a page.tsx (components, styles, tests, utilities) are not routable; only the reserved file names are special.

Reserved file names at a glance

File Role page.tsx The unique UI of a route (makes the segment public) layout.tsx Shared shell that wraps a segment and its children template.tsx Like a layout, but re-mounts on every navigation loading.tsx Suspense fallback for the segment error.tsx Error boundary for the segment (Client Component) not-found.tsx UI for notFound() / unmatched routes route.ts An API endpoint (Route Handler) instead of UI

docs.title: page.tsx — the leaf UI
docs.text:
A page.tsx exports a default React component. It's a Server Component by default and receives params and searchParams as props.

// app/page.tsx — the home page, rendered at "/"
export default function HomePage() {
  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Welcome</h1>
      <p>This is the root route.</p>
    </main>
  );
}

// app/dashboard/page.tsx — a Server Component reading the URL's query string
export default async function DashboardPage({
  searchParams,
}: {
  searchParams: Promise<{ tab?: string }>;
}) {
  const { tab = "overview" } = await searchParams; // Next.js 15: searchParams is async
  return <h1>Dashboard — {tab}</h1>;
}

params and searchParams are Promises in Next.js 15

In Next.js 15, params and searchParams (and cookies()/headers()) are async and must be awaited in Server Components, or unwrapped with use() in Client Components. In Next.js 14 they were plain synchronous objects. Every example here uses the v15 async form.

docs.title: layout.tsx — the shared shell
docs.text:
A layout.tsx wraps the page.tsx in its segment and everything nested below it. It receives a children prop—the active page or child layout—and must render it. The root layout (app/layout.tsx) is required, must render <html> and <body>, and is the only place those tags live.

// app/layout.tsx — the required root layout
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "My App",
  description: "Built with the Next.js App Router",
};

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <header>Global header</header>
        {children}
        <footer>Global footer</footer>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

Layouts preserve state across navigation

When you navigate between routes that share a layout, the layout does not re-render or unmount—its DOM and React state are preserved, and only the changing children swap out. That's why scroll position in a persistent sidebar or the contents of an <input> in a layout survive page changes. (Use template.tsx when you need the opposite.)

docs.title: 2. Nested routes & nested layouts
docs.text:
Nesting folders nests both the URL and the layouts. Each segment can have its own layout.tsx, and Next.js composes them from the root inward—the root layout wraps the section layout, which wraps the page.

app/
├── layout.tsx              → RootLayout
└── dashboard/
    ├── layout.tsx          → DashboardLayout (sidebar, tabs…)
    ├── page.tsx            → /dashboard
    ├── settings/
    │   └── page.tsx        → /dashboard/settings
    └── analytics/
        └── page.tsx        → /dashboard/analytics

For /dashboard/settings, the rendered tree is:

RootLayout
  └── DashboardLayout
        └── SettingsPage

// app/dashboard/layout.tsx — wraps every route under /dashboard
import Link from "next/link";
import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export default function DashboardLayout({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <div className="dashboard">
      <nav>
        <Link href="/dashboard">Home</Link>
        <Link href="/dashboard/settings">Settings</Link>
        <Link href="/dashboard/analytics">Analytics</Link>
      </nav>
      <section>{children}</section>
    </div>
  );
}

Layouts nest; pages don't

A layout always wraps its descendants, so deeper layouts stack. Pages are leaves—only one page.tsx renders for a given URL, at the deepest matched segment.

docs.title: 3. Dynamic routes
docs.text: When part of a URL is data (a post slug, a user id), wrap the folder name in square brackets. The bracketed name becomes a key on params.

docs.title: [id] — a single dynamic segment
docs.text:
app/users/[id]/page.tsx   → /users/1, /users/2, /users/anything

// app/users/[id]/page.tsx
export default async function UserPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}) {
  const { id } = await params; // e.g. "42" for /users/42
  const user = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/users/${id}`).then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return <h1>{user.name}</h1>;
}

Multiple dynamic segments in one path each get their own key:

app/[category]/[product]/page.tsx   → /shoes/runner  →  { category, product }

// app/[category]/[product]/page.tsx
export default async function ProductPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ category: string; product: string }>;
}) {
  const { category, product } = await params;
  return <h1>{category} / {product}</h1>;
}

docs.title: [...slug] — catch-all segments
docs.text:
A catch-all matches one or more path segments and gives you an array. It does not match the parent route with no segments.

app/docs/[...slug]/page.tsx
  /docs/a          → { slug: ["a"] }
  /docs/a/b/c      → { slug: ["a", "b", "c"] }
  /docs            → ❌ not matched (needs at least one segment)

// app/docs/[...slug]/page.tsx
export default async function DocsPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string[] }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // always an array, length >= 1
  return <h1>Docs path: {slug.join(" / ")}</h1>;
}

docs.title: [[...slug]] — optional catch-all
docs.text:
Double brackets make the catch-all optional: it also matches the parent route, where slug is then undefined. Use this when one component should handle both /shop and /shop/any/depth.

app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.tsx
  /shop            → { slug: undefined }
  /shop/sale       → { slug: ["sale"] }
  /shop/men/shoes  → { slug: ["men", "shoes"] }

// app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.tsx
export default async function ShopPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug?: string[] }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // undefined at /shop
  if (!slug) return <h1>Shop home</h1>;
  return <h1>Filtering by: {slug.join(", ")}</h1>;
}

Catch-all vs optional catch-all

Convention Matches parent (no segment)? params value [...slug] ❌ No string[] (≥ 1) [[...slug]] ✅ Yes string[] or undefined

docs.title: Pre-rendering dynamic routes with generateStaticParams
docs.text:
To statically generate dynamic routes at build time (SSG), export generateStaticParams—it's the App Router replacement for the legacy getStaticPaths.

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const posts: { slug: string }[] = await fetch(
    "https://api.example.com/posts"
  ).then((r) => r.json());
  return posts.map((post) => ({ slug: post.slug })); // one entry per pre-rendered page
}

export default async function BlogPost({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const post = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${slug}`).then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{post.title}</h1>
      <p>{post.body}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

docs.title: 4. Route groups (group)
docs.text:
Wrapping a folder name in parentheses creates a route group. The folder name is used purely for organisation and is stripped from the URL. Route groups let you:

  1. Group routes by feature/team without affecting the path.
  2. Apply a different layout to a subset of routes at the same URL level.
  3. Split one app into multiple root layouts.
app/
├── (marketing)/
│   ├── layout.tsx       → marketing layout
│   ├── page.tsx         → /          (NOT /marketing)
│   └── about/page.tsx   → /about
└── (shop)/
    ├── layout.tsx       → shop layout
