    ├── cart/page.tsx    → /cart
    └── checkout/page.tsx→ /checkout

Both groups sit at the URL root, but each gets its own layout.tsx. A visitor on /about gets the marketing layout; on /cart they get the shop layout—even though neither "marketing" nor "shop" appears in the URL.

Two root layouts → full page reload between them

You can give each group its own root layout (each rendering <html>/<body>) by omitting a shared app/layout.tsx. But navigating between two different root layouts triggers a full page reload, not a client-side transition. Also avoid creating two routes that resolve to the same URL across groups (e.g. (a)/page.tsx and (b)/page.tsx both at /)—that's a build error.

docs.title: 5. Parallel routes @slot
docs.text:
Parallel routes let a single layout render multiple pages in the same view simultaneously, each in its own slot. A slot is a folder prefixed with @. Slots are passed to the layout as named props (the @ folder name), alongside the usual children.

app/dashboard/
├── layout.tsx
├── page.tsx          → the implicit "children" slot
├── @team/
│   └── page.tsx      → the "team" slot
└── @analytics/
    └── page.tsx      → the "analytics" slot

// app/dashboard/layout.tsx — slots arrive as named props
import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export default function DashboardLayout({
  children,
  team,
  analytics,
}: {
  children: ReactNode;
  team: ReactNode;
  analytics: ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <div className="grid">
      <section>{children}</section>
      <aside>{team}</aside>
      <aside>{analytics}</aside>
    </div>
  );
}

Like (group) folders, @slot folders do not add anything to the URL—@team/page.tsx is not reachable at /dashboard/team. Each slot can have its own loading.tsx and error.tsx, so the sections stream and fail independently.

docs.title: default.tsx for unmatched slots
docs.text:
When you navigate to a URL that matches one slot but not another, the unmatched slot falls back to its default.tsx (and to the currently-rendered content on soft navigation). Providing default.tsx avoids a 404 on hard reload.

// app/dashboard/@team/default.tsx — fallback when this slot has no match
export default function Default() {
  return null; // render nothing for the team slot
}

What parallel routes are for

Dashboards with independently-loading panels, modals (a slot that overlays content), and conditional UI (e.g. render @login vs @dashboard based on auth) all map naturally onto parallel routes.

docs.title: 6. Intercepting routes (.), (..)
docs.text:
Intercepting routes let you load a route's content within the current layout instead of navigating away to it—most famously to show a route as a modal when reached via in-app navigation, while a hard refresh or direct visit shows the full standalone page.

The convention mirrors relative imports, using parentheses:

Convention Intercepts (.) the same level (..) one level up (..)(..) two levels up (...) from the root app

A common pattern is a photo gallery where clicking a thumbnail opens the photo in a modal, but /photos/3 loaded directly renders the full page. Combine an intercepting route with a parallel @modal slot:

app/
├── layout.tsx
├── @modal/
│   ├── default.tsx
│   └── (.)photos/
│       └── [id]/page.tsx   → intercepts /photos/[id] → shows a modal
├── photos/
│   └── [id]/page.tsx       → the full standalone page
└── page.tsx                → the gallery with <Link>s to /photos/[id]

// app/@modal/(.)photos/[id]/page.tsx — shown when /photos/[id] is reached via <Link>
import { Modal } from "@/components/modal";

export default async function PhotoModal({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}) {
  const { id } = await params;
  return (
    <Modal>
      <img src={`/photos/${id}.jpg`} alt={`Photo ${id}`} />
    </Modal>
  );
}

// app/layout.tsx — the @modal slot renders alongside children
import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
  modal,
}: {
  children: ReactNode;
  modal: ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        {children}
        {modal}
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

Soft vs hard navigation

The interception only happens on soft (client-side) navigation—a <Link> click from inside the app. A hard navigation (typing the URL, refreshing, or arriving from outside) bypasses the interceptor and renders the real photos/[id]/page.tsx. That's exactly the modal-vs-full-page behaviour you want.

docs.title: 7. Navigation
docs.text: Navigation between routes is client-side by default: Next.js prefetches and swaps only the changed segments, preserving shared layouts. There are declarative (<Link>) and imperative (useRouter) APIs, plus server-side redirect, and hooks to read the current URL.

docs.title: <Link> — the primary navigation primitive
docs.text:
<Link> renders an <a> but performs a client-side transition and prefetches the target in the background when it enters the viewport. It works in both Server and Client Components.

// Works in a Server Component — no "use client" needed
import Link from "next/link";

export default function Nav() {
  return (
    <nav>
      <Link href="/">Home</Link>
      <Link href="/blog">Blog</Link>
      <Link href="/users/42">User 42</Link>
      {/* Object form with query string */}
      <Link href={{ pathname: "/search", query: { q: "next" } }}>Search</Link>
      <Link href="/dashboard" prefetch={false}>
        Dashboard (no prefetch)
      </Link>
    </nav>
  );
}

docs.title: useRouter — imperative navigation (Client Component)
docs.text:
For programmatic navigation (after a form submit, a timeout, etc.), use useRouter from next/navigation (not the legacy next/router). This is a hook, so its component must be a Client Component.

"use client"; // required: useRouter is a hook
import { useRouter } from "next/navigation";

export default function LoginForm() {
  const router = useRouter();

  async function onSubmit(formData: FormData) {
    await fetch("/api/login", { method: "POST", body: formData });
    router.push("/dashboard");   // navigate, adds a history entry
    // router.replace("/dashboard"); // navigate without adding history
    // router.back();                // history back
    // router.refresh();             // re-fetch server data for the current route
  }

  return <form action={onSubmit}>{/* fields */}</form>;
}

next/navigation, not next/router

In the App Router, all navigation hooks (useRouter, usePathname, useSearchParams, useParams) come from next/navigation. The Pages Router's next/router does not work in app/.

docs.title: redirect — server-side redirect
docs.text:
redirect() (from next/navigation) is for Server Components, Server Actions, and Route Handlers. It throws to interrupt rendering, so call it outside try/catch blocks and after any work that must complete first.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx — redirect on the server before rendering
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { getSession } from "@/lib/auth";

export default async function DashboardPage() {
  const session = await getSession();
  if (!session) {
    redirect("/login"); // 307 redirect; nothing below runs
  }
  return <h1>Hello, {session.user.name}</h1>;
}

Use permanentRedirect() for a 308 (permanent) instead of a 307.

docs.title: usePathname & useSearchParams — reading the URL (Client Component)
docs.text:
Both are hooks from next/navigation, so they require "use client". usePathname returns the current path string; useSearchParams returns a read-only URLSearchParams.

"use client"; // required: both are hooks
import { usePathname, useSearchParams } from "next/navigation";
import Link from "next/link";

export default function ActiveLink({ href, label }: { href: string; label: string }) {
  const pathname = useSearchParamsAware(href);
  return (
    <Link href={href} aria-current={pathname ? "page" : undefined}>
      {label}
    </Link>
  );
}

function useSearchParamsAware(href: string) {
  const pathname = usePathname();             // e.g. "/blog"
  const searchParams = useSearchParams();     // e.g. ?page=2
  const page = searchParams.get("page");      // "2" | null
  return pathname === href && !page;
}

useSearchParams needs a Suspense boundary

A Client Component calling useSearchParams should be wrapped in <Suspense>. Without it, the route opts out of static rendering and is forced fully dynamic during the build. To read query params on the server instead, use the searchParams prop on page.tsx—no hook required.

Updating the URL without a navigation

To change query params in place (filters, tabs) you typically build a new query string and call router.replace(\?\${params.toString()}`). Libraries likenuqswrap this pattern with typed,useState`-like ergonomics for "URL as state."

docs.title: 8. Special files
docs.text: Each route segment can declare reserved files that Next.js wires into the React tree automatically—loading fallbacks, error boundaries, and not-found UI—without you writing the Suspense/error-boundary plumbing.

docs.title: loading.tsx — instant loading UI
docs.text:
Adding loading.tsx wraps the segment's page.tsx in a <Suspense> boundary for you. While the page's async work resolves, the fallback shows immediately and the rest of the already-rendered shell (the layout) stays put.

// app/dashboard/loading.tsx — shown while DashboardPage's data loads
export default function Loading() {
  return <p>Loading dashboard…</p>; // a skeleton goes here in real apps
}

docs.title: error.tsx — segment error boundary (Client Component)
docs.text:
error.tsx is a React error boundary for its segment. It must be a Client Component ("use client") and receives the error plus a reset() function to retry rendering the segment.

// app/dashboard/error.tsx
"use client"; // required: error boundaries are Client Components
import { useEffect } from "react";

export default function Error({
  error,
  reset,
}: {
  error: Error & { digest?: string };
  reset: () => void;
}) {
  useEffect(() => {
    console.error(error); // report to your monitoring service
  }, [error]);

  return (
    <div role="alert">
      <h2>Something went wrong.</h2>
      <button onClick={() => reset()}>Try again</button>
    </div>
  );
}

error.tsx does not catch the layout in its own segment

An error.tsx catches errors thrown by its segment's page and nested children—but not the layout.tsx of the same segment (the error boundary lives inside that layout). To catch a segment's own layout errors, add an error.tsx one level up. For the root layout, use the special app/global-error.tsx, which replaces the entire page (and must render its own <html>/<body>).

docs.title: not-found.tsx — 404 UI
docs.text:
not-found.tsx renders when you call notFound() from a segment, and the root app/not-found.tsx also handles any unmatched URL. notFound(), like redirect(), throws to halt rendering.

// app/users/[id]/page.tsx — trigger the nearest not-found boundary
import { notFound } from "next/navigation";

export default async function UserPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const user = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/users/${id}`).then((r) =>
    r.ok ? r.json() : null
  );
  if (!user) notFound(); // renders the nearest not-found.tsx, sends a 404
  return <h1>{user.name}</h1>;
}

// app/not-found.tsx — root 404 page
import Link from "next/link";

export default function NotFound() {
  return (
    <div>
      <h2>Page not found</h2>
      <Link href="/">Go home</Link>
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: template.tsx — a layout that re-mounts
docs.text:
template.tsx is like layout.tsx—it wraps children—but it creates a new instance on every navigation: DOM is recreated, state resets, and effects re-run. Reach for it when you specifically want a fresh start per route, e.g. enter animations or per-page analytics that should fire on each visit.

// app/template.tsx — re-runs effects and resets state on each navigation
"use client"; // common, since templates usually animate/track on mount
import { useEffect } from "react";
import type { ReactNode } from "react";

export default function Template({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  useEffect(() => {
    // fires on EVERY navigation into a route using this template
  }, []);
  return <div className="page-transition">{children}</div>;
}

layout.tsx vs template.tsx

layout.tsx template.tsx State across navigation Preserved Reset DOM across navigation Reused Re-created Effects on navigation Run once Run every time Use when Persistent nav/sidebars (the default) Per-visit animations/logging

docs.title: Route segment config
docs.text:
Each page.tsx, layout.tsx, or route.ts can export config constants that control how the segment is rendered, cached, and run. They are static exports (not function calls) read at build time.

// app/products/page.tsx — segment-level configuration
export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"; // "auto" | "force-dynamic" | "error" | "force-static"
export const revalidate = 3600;          // ISR window in seconds (or false / 0)
export const dynamicParams = true;       // allow params not in generateStaticParams
export const fetchCache = "auto";        // override default fetch caching
export const runtime = "nodejs";         // "nodejs" (default) | "edge"
export const preferredRegion = "auto";   // deployment region hint

export default async function Products() {
  const products = await fetch("https://api.example.com/products").then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return <ul>{products.map((p: { id: string; name: string }) => <li key={p.id}>{p.name}</li>)}</ul>;
}

The two you'll reach for most

dynamic and revalidate are the everyday levers. export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" opts a route fully into SSR (re-rendered per request); export const revalidate = N turns a static route into ISR (regenerated at most every N seconds). Leave the rest at their defaults until you have a specific reason. See the Data Fetching & Caching chapter for how these interact with the cache.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Convention 🧠 What it does page.tsx Makes a segment a public route (Server Component by default) layout.tsx Shared shell wrapping a segment + children; state preserved Nested folders Nest both URL and layouts (root → section → page) [id] Single dynamic segment → params.id (string) [...slug] Catch-all → params.slug (string[], ≥ 1) [[...slug]] Optional catch-all → string[] or undefined (group) Route group: organise/share layouts, no URL segment @slot Parallel route rendered as a named layout prop, no URL segment (.) (..) (...) Intercepting routes (e.g. modals on soft navigation) <Link> Declarative client-side navigation + prefetch (any component) useRouter Imperative navigation — Client Component (next/navigation) redirect() / notFound() Server-side control flow (throw to interrupt render) usePathname / useSearchParams Read the URL — Client Component hooks loading.tsx Auto Suspense fallback for the segment error.tsx Segment error boundary — Client Component not-found.tsx 404 UI (root file also catches unmatched URLs) template.tsx Layout that re-mounts per navigation Segment config dynamic, revalidate, runtime, … per segment

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Routing Fundamentals
  • Next.js — Pages & Layouts
  • Next.js — Dynamic Routes
  • Next.js — Parallel & Intercepting Routes
  • Next.js — Linking & Navigating
  • Next.js — Route Segment Config

docs.title: Rendering: Server & Client Components
docs.text:
The App Router is built on React Server Components (RSC). The single biggest mental shift coming from plain React or the Pages Router is this: components run on the server by default, and you opt into the client, not the other way around. Getting the server/client split right is what determines how much JavaScript ships, how fast the page paints, and whether your data access stays safely on the server.

This chapter covers the RSC model, when each kind of component is appropriate, the "use client" boundary and the composition patterns that keep it small, how Next.js decides between static and dynamic rendering, streaming with <Suspense>, and the Node.js vs Edge runtime choice.

📚 For more: https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components · https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components

docs.title: 1. The React Server Components model
docs.text:
React Server Components are components that render only on the server and never ship their code to the browser. They were designed by the React team and are surfaced to most developers through a framework — in our case, the Next.js App Router.

A useful way to picture it: there are now two kinds of React components living in one tree.

  • Server Components run during the request (or at build time). They can be async, talk to a database, read files, and use secrets. Their rendered output — not their source — is sent to the browser as a special serialized description of the UI (the "RSC payload").
  • Client Components are the React you already know. They are server-rendered to HTML for the first paint, then their JavaScript is sent to the browser and hydrated so they can hold state and respond to events.
Request ──▶ Server renders the whole tree
            ├─ Server Components → RSC payload (description of UI, no JS)
            └─ Client Components → initial HTML + their JS bundle
                                   ↓
Browser ──▶ paints HTML, then hydrates only the Client Components

The RSC payload is React's serialized output, not HTML. The browser uses it to reconcile the tree and to slot Client Components into the right places. On navigation, Next.js fetches a fresh RSC payload for the new route instead of a full HTML document, which is why client-side navigation stays fast while still re-running server code.

RSC ≠ SSR

They are related but distinct. SSR turns a React tree into an HTML string on each request (it has existed for years). RSC is a component type that runs only on the server and ships zero JS for that component. In the App Router you get both: Server Components produce no client JS, and the whole tree is server-rendered to HTML for the first paint. See the React SSR & SSG chapter for the SSR/SSG/ISR fundamentals this builds on.

docs.title: Server Components are the default
docs.text:
In the App Router, every component is a Server Component unless the file (or one it's imported from) has "use client". There is no directive for "this is a server component" — server is simply the default.

// app/products/page.tsx — a Server Component (no directive needed)
// This code, the DB call, and any secrets never reach the browser.
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export default async function ProductsPage() {
  const products = await db.product.findMany({ take: 20 }); // direct data access

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Products</h1>
      <ul>
        {products.map((p) => (
          <li key={p.id}>{p.name}</li>
        ))}
      </ul>
    </main>
  );
}

What Server Components can do that Client Components can't:

  • Be async and await data directly — no useEffect, no getServerSideProps.
  • Access server-only resources: the database, the filesystem (fs), environment secrets, internal services.
  • Keep large dependencies (markdown parsers, date libraries, SDKs) on the server so they never inflate the client bundle.

What they cannot do:

  • Use state or lifecycle: useState, useReducer, useEffect, useRef for DOM, etc.
  • Attach event handlers (onClick, onChange, onSubmit).
  • Use browser-only APIs (window, document, localStorage, navigator).
  • Use most React Context (useContext) or hooks that depend on the above.

docs.title: Client Components opt in with "use client"
docs.text:
When a component needs interactivity, mark its file with "use client" as the very first line.

// app/components/Counter.tsx
"use client";
import { useState } from "react";

export default function Counter({ start }: { start: number }) {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(start);
  return (
    <button onClick={() => setCount((n) => n + 1)}>
      Clicked {count} times
    </button>
  );
}

Reach for a Client Component when you need any of: useState/useReducer, useEffect/lifecycle, event handlers, browser APIs, React Context consumers/providers, or class components, and most third-party libraries that use those internally (animation, charts, form libraries, anything touching window).

docs.title: Choosing — a quick reference
docs.text:
You need to… Component type Fetch data / query the DB Server Use secrets / server-only env vars Server Keep a heavy dependency off the client Server Read cookies() / headers() Server Use useState, useEffect, useRef Client Handle events (onClick, onChange) Client Use window / localStorage / navigator Client Consume/provide React Context Client

Default to Server, opt into Client at the leaves

Start everything as a Server Component. Only convert the smallest interactive piece to a Client Component. A "page" is almost always a Server Component that renders a few small client islands (a like button, a search box, a menu toggle).

docs.title: 3. The "use client" boundary
docs.text:
"use client" doesn't just mark one component — it marks the boundary between the server-only world and the client bundle. Everything imported from a "use client" file (and everything it imports) becomes part of the client bundle.

app/page.tsx                (Server)
└─ "use client" Header.tsx  ← boundary starts here
   ├─ Nav.tsx               (Client — imported by Header, no directive needed)
   └─ SearchBox.tsx         (Client — imported by Header)

You only write "use client" once, at the top of the boundary. Files imported into the client subtree are automatically Client Components — adding the directive to every file is redundant but harmless.

Push the boundary down the tree

Marking a layout.tsx or a big page "use client" drags its entire subtree into the client bundle and forfeits the benefits of Server Components. Keep "use client" on the smallest leaf that actually needs it.

docs.title: Common mistake: importing a Server Component into a Client Component
docs.text:
This is the single most common RSC mistake. Once a file is a Client Component, anything it imports is bundled and run on the client — so a Server Component imported there loses its server-ness (and will error the moment it does something server-only like await db…).

// ❌ Wrong: importing a Server Component into a Client Component
"use client";
import { useState } from "react";
import ServerData from "./ServerData"; // an async Server Component

export default function Panel() {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setOpen((o) => !o)}>Toggle</button>
      {open && <ServerData />} {/* ServerData is now treated as client code */}
    </div>
  );
}

The fix is composition: pass the Server Component in from a server parent, as children or a prop, instead of importing it.

docs.title: The fix: pass Server Components as children / props
docs.text:
A Client Component can render Server Components it receives as props — it just can't import them. The Server Component is rendered on the server; the Client Component only ever receives its already-rendered output and drops it into a "hole" in its own tree.

// app/components/Panel.tsx
"use client";
import { useState, type ReactNode } from "react";

export default function Panel({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setOpen((o) => !o)}>Toggle</button>
      {open && children} {/* rendered on the server, just placed here */}
    </div>
  );
}

// app/page.tsx — a Server Component composes the two
import Panel from "./components/Panel";
import ServerData from "./components/ServerData"; // async Server Component

export default function Page() {
  return (
    <Panel>
      <ServerData /> {/* stays a Server Component: rendered on the server */}
    </Panel>
  );
}

This is the interleaving pattern: server → client → server again, alternating down the tree. The rule is simply who imports whom. A Server Component may import and render Client Components; a Client Component may only receive Server Components as props/children, never import them.

docs.title: Only serializable props cross the boundary
docs.text:
Props passed from a Server Component to a Client Component are serialized into the RSC payload, so they must be serializable.

// app/page.tsx (Server)
import Counter from "./components/Counter";

export default async function Page() {
  const start = await getInitialCount();

  return (
    <>
      {/* ✅ OK: numbers, strings, booleans, arrays, plain objects, */}
      {/*        null, Dates, Maps/Sets, Promises, and JSX (as children) */}
      <Counter start={start} />

      {/* ❌ Not OK: functions, class instances, and other non-serializable values */}
      {/* <Counter onChange={() => save()} /> */}
    </>
  );
}
  • ✅ Allowed: primitives, plain objects/arrays, Date, Map, Set, Promise (React can pass a promise and you use() it on the client), and JSX/Server Components passed as children.
  • ❌ Not allowed: functions/event handlers, class instances, symbols, anything with methods or non-plain prototypes.

If a Client Component needs to trigger server work, don't pass a function down — call a Server Action ("use server"), covered in the Server Actions chapter, or hit a Route Handler.

The server-only and client-only guards

To make accidental cross-boundary imports fail at build time instead of leaking, mark modules explicitly:

// lib/db.ts — must never reach the browser
import "server-only"; // build error if imported into a Client Component

npm i server-only client-only. Use server-only for anything with secrets or a DB/fs dependency; use client-only for code that touches window.

docs.title: "use client" doesn't mean "no SSR"
docs.text: A common misread: Client Components still render to HTML on the server for the first paint — "use client" only means this component's JS is also sent to the browser and hydrated. It does not turn off server-side rendering, and it does not make the component a client-only/CSR component. (For genuinely client-only rendering, see next/dynamic with ssr: false in the Optimizations chapter.)

docs.title: 4. Static vs Dynamic rendering
docs.text:
Independently of the server/client split, each route is rendered in one of two modes:

  • Static rendering (SSG) — the route is rendered at build time (or in the background during revalidation) and served as cached HTML. This is the default.
  • Dynamic rendering (SSR) — the route is rendered on each request, because it depends on request-time information.

Next.js chooses automatically: a route stays static until it uses something that can't be known at build time, at which point it becomes dynamic.

docs.title: What triggers dynamic rendering
docs.text:
A route opts into dynamic rendering when it uses any Dynamic API or an uncached request:

  • cookies(), headers(), draftMode() from next/headers.
  • The searchParams prop of a page (it depends on the actual URL).
  • An uncached fetch — fetch(url, { cache: "no-store" }), or in Next.js 15 simply a fetch you didn't opt into caching.
  • An explicit route-segment config forcing it (below).
// app/dashboard/page.tsx — DYNAMIC: reads a request-time cookie
import { cookies } from "next/headers";

export default async function Dashboard() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies(); // Next.js 15: cookies() is async
  const theme = cookieStore.get("theme")?.value ?? "light";
  return <main data-theme={theme}>Welcome back</main>;
}

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx — STATIC: no dynamic APIs, cached data
export default async function Post({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // Next.js 15: params is async
  const post = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${slug}`, {
    cache: "force-cache",
  }).then((r) => r.json());
  return <article>{post.title}</article>;
}

docs.title: Forcing the mode with route-segment config
docs.text:
You can override the heuristic per route segment:

// at the top of a page.tsx / layout.tsx / route.ts
export const dynamic = "force-dynamic";  // always render per request (SSR)
// export const dynamic = "force-static"; // always static; Dynamic APIs return defaults
// export const revalidate = 60;          // ISR: static, refreshed at most every 60s
// export const dynamic = "error";        // build error if anything makes it dynamic

Caching defaults changed in Next.js 15

In Next.js 14, fetch was cached by default (routes leaned static). In Next.js 15, fetch is uncached by default, so a route with a plain fetch renders dynamically unless you opt in with cache: "force-cache" or next: { revalidate }. Be explicit about caching rather than relying on the version default — see the Data Fetching & Caching chapter for the full model.

You don't choose one mode for the whole app

Static vs dynamic is per route, and with streaming you can keep a static shell while rendering a dynamic region inside <Suspense> (the Partial Prerendering direction). Default to static; go dynamic only where the data is genuinely per-request.

docs.title: 5. Streaming & <Suspense>
docs.text:
The App Router can stream a response: send the static shell of the page immediately, then flush slower parts as their data resolves — instead of blocking the entire response on the slowest query. This improves TTFB and lets users see and interact with the fast parts first.

Wrap a slow async Server Component in <Suspense> with a fallback. React sends the fallback in the initial HTML, then streams the real content in when it's ready and swaps it in.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx
import { Suspense } from "react";
import RevenueChart from "./RevenueChart"; // slow async Server Component
import Greeting from "./Greeting";         // fast

export default function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <main>
      <Greeting /> {/* renders immediately */}
      <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading revenue…</p>}>
        <RevenueChart /> {/* streamed in when its data resolves */}
      </Suspense>
    </main>
  );
}

// app/dashboard/RevenueChart.tsx — its slow await doesn't block the rest of the page
export default async function RevenueChart() {
  const data = await getRevenue(); // e.g. a 2s query
  return <Chart data={data} />;
}

Each <Suspense> boundary streams independently, so you can have several slow regions resolve in parallel without any one blocking the others.

docs.title: loading.tsx — automatic Suspense for a whole segment
docs.text:
Dropping a loading.tsx file into a route segment wraps that segment's page in a Suspense boundary for you, giving the whole route an instant loading UI on navigation.

// app/dashboard/loading.tsx
export default function Loading() {
  return <p>Loading dashboard…</p>; // shown instantly while the segment renders
}

Streaming and dynamic rendering work together

A route can serve a static shell instantly and stream a dynamic region (one that reads cookies() or does a no-store fetch) inside a <Suspense>. That's the essence of Partial Prerendering: cached shell + streamed dynamic islands. See the SSR & SSG chapter for how this maps onto the broader rendering spectrum.

docs.title: 6. Runtimes: Node.js vs Edge
docs.text:
Your server code (Server Components, Route Handlers, Server Actions, Middleware) runs in one of two runtimes, and you can choose per route segment.

// at the top of a page.tsx / layout.tsx / route.ts
export const runtime = "nodejs"; // default
// export const runtime = "edge";
Node.js runtime (default) Edge runtime APIs Full Node.js APIs Web-standard APIs only (fetch, Request, Response, Web Crypto) Native modules / DB drivers ✅ Supported ❌ Many TCP-based DB drivers won't run Cold start Larger Near-zero Distribution Regional Deployed close to the user (edge network) Best for Data fetching, ORMs, heavy libraries, most app code Auth checks, redirects, lightweight personalization, geolocation
  • Node.js is the default and the right choice for the vast majority of work: anything using a database driver, an ORM (Prisma/Drizzle), the filesystem, or a substantial dependency.
  • Edge runs in a constrained, Web-standard environment with very fast cold starts and runs geographically close to the user. It's ideal for Middleware (which runs on Edge), auth gating, redirects, and lightweight per-user logic — but it can't use Node-only APIs or most traditional database drivers.

Edge is constrained — verify your dependencies

The Edge runtime is not Node.js. Code that imports fs, crypto (the Node module, not Web Crypto), native addons, or a TCP database client will fail there. Use Edge deliberately for small, latency-sensitive routes; keep your data-heavy routes on Node.js. The Middleware & Edge chapter covers the constraints in depth.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text:
🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Server Component Default in the App Router; runs server-only, can be async, ships zero JS Client Component Opt in with "use client"; SSR'd for first paint, then hydrated for interactivity "use client" Marks the boundary; everything imported below it joins the client bundle Composition Pass Server Components into Client Components as children/props, never import them Serializable props Only primitives, plain objects, Dates, Promises, JSX cross the boundary — no functions server-only / client-only Build-time guards against leaking server code to the client (and vice-versa) Static rendering Default; rendered at build time and cached (SSG) Dynamic rendering Per-request (SSR); triggered by cookies()/headers(), searchParams, uncached fetch export const dynamic / revalidate Route-segment config to force static/dynamic/ISR Streaming + <Suspense> Send the shell first, stream slow regions in as data resolves loading.tsx Automatic Suspense fallback for a whole route segment Node.js runtime Default; full Node APIs, DB drivers, heavy libraries Edge runtime Web-standard only; near-zero cold start, close to the user, constrained

The mistakes to avoid

  • Importing a Server Component into a Client Component → pass it as children/props instead.
  • Using useState/useEffect/onClick/window in a Server Component → it will error; add "use client" to the smallest leaf.
  • Passing a function as a prop across the boundary → use a Server Action or Route Handler.
  • Marking a layout/page "use client" → bloats the bundle; push the directive down the tree.
  • Forgetting cache/revalidate → surprising stale-or-always-dynamic behavior after the 14→15 default flip.

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Server and Client Components
  • Next.js — Partial Prerendering
  • Next.js — Runtimes (Node.js vs Edge)
  • React — Server Components
  • React — "use client" directive
  • React — <Suspense>

docs.title: Data Fetching & Caching
docs.text:
Data fetching in the App Router is deceptively simple — you await data right inside a Server Component — but the caching behind it is where most of the surprises (and the performance) live. Next.js layers four distinct caches between your fetch call and the user's screen, and the defaults for those caches changed significantly in Next.js 15.

This chapter covers fetching in Server Components, the full four-cache model, every flavour of revalidation, static generation with generateStaticParams and ISR, the newer unstable_cache / 'use cache' directions, when to drop down to client-side fetching with SWR or TanStack Query, and how to handle errors and missing data gracefully.

📚 Builds on React SSR & SSG and the Rendering chapter. For the canonical reference: nextjs.org/docs — Caching.

docs.title: 1. Fetching in Server Components
docs.text:
In the App Router, the default data-fetching mechanism is async/await directly inside a Server Component. There is no getServerSideProps, no getStaticProps, no hook — the component itself is async and you await the data it needs.

// app/posts/page.tsx — a Server Component (no "use client")
type Post = { id: string; title: string; excerpt: string };

export default async function PostsPage() {
  const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/posts");
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to load posts");
  const posts: Post[] = await res.json();

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Posts</h1>
      <ul>
        {posts.map((p) => (
          <li key={p.id}>
            <h2>{p.title}</h2>
            <p>{p.excerpt}</p>
          </li>
        ))}
      </ul>
    </main>
  );
}

Because this runs only on the server, you can also skip HTTP entirely and call your database, ORM, or internal services directly — credentials and query code never reach the browser.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export default async function Dashboard() {
  const stats = await db.metric.findMany({ take: 20 }); // direct DB access
  return <StatsTable rows={stats} />;
}

docs.title: Parallel vs. sequential fetching
docs.text:
Each await is a potential waterfall. If two requests don't depend on each other, fire them in parallel with Promise.all instead of awaiting one after the other.

// ❌ Sequential — total time = artist time + albums time (a waterfall)
const artist = await getArtist(id);
const albums = await getAlbums(id);

// ✅ Parallel — total time = max(artist, albums)
const [artist, albums] = await Promise.all([getArtist(id), getAlbums(id)]);

For independent slow regions, prefer streaming: render each in its own <Suspense> boundary so the page shell flushes immediately and each region streams in when ready (covered in the Rendering chapter, and again under error handling below).

Fetch where the data is used

You don't need to lift fetching to the top of the tree and thread props down. Fetch in the component that actually needs the data — even if two sibling components request the same URL, Request Memoization (next section) collapses them into one call. Co-locating fetches keeps components self-contained.

docs.title: 2. The Next.js caching model
docs.text:
Next.js sits four caches between your code and the response. Understanding which one is doing what is the key to predictable behaviour:

Cache What it stores Where Lifetime Cleared by Request Memoization Return value of fetch (and React.cache) Server A single render pass Automatically, when the render finishes Data Cache Result of data fetches Server (persistent) Across requests & deploys revalidate, revalidateTag, revalidatePath Full Route Cache Rendered HTML + RSC payload of a route Server (persistent) Across requests, until revalidated Revalidating its data, redeploy Router Cache RSC payload of visited routes Client (in-memory) Session / a short time router.refresh(), navigation, server action revalidation

They run in roughly that order: a fetch first checks memoization (same render), then the Data Cache (persistent), and the rendered output lands in the Full Route Cache (server) and Router Cache (client).

docs.title: 2.1 Request Memoization
docs.text:
During a single render pass, if you call fetch with the same URL and options more than once, Next.js runs it only once and replays the result to every caller. This is React's deduplication, not a persistent cache — it's discarded as soon as the render completes.

// Both of these run during the same request → fetch executes ONCE
async function getUser(id: string) {
  const res = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/users/${id}`);
  return res.json();
}

// Called in the layout...
const user = await getUser("42");
// ...and again in a deeply nested component — no second network call.
const sameUser = await getUser("42");

Memoization only applies to GET fetch calls within the React render. For non-fetch work (a DB query you want deduped within a render), wrap it in React's cache:

import { cache } from "react";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

// Deduped per-request, just like fetch memoization
export const getProject = cache(async (id: string) => {
  return db.project.findUnique({ where: { id } });
});

docs.title: 2.2 The Data Cache
docs.text:
The Data Cache is a persistent server-side store that survives across requests and even deployments. This is the cache whose default flipped in Next.js 15.

Next.js 15: fetch is NOT cached by default

This is the single most important change to internalize.

  • Next.js 14 and earlier: fetch() defaulted to cache: 'force-cache' — results were cached in the Data Cache indefinitely unless you opted out. People were frequently surprised by stale data.
  • Next.js 15: fetch() defaults to cache: 'no-store' — it is not cached, and the request runs every time. You must opt in to caching.

The same uncached-by-default shift applies to GET Route Handlers and to client-side navigations (the Router Cache — see §2.4). Always set caching explicitly rather than relying on whatever the installed version's default happens to be.

// Next.js 15 defaults & how to opt back into caching:

// (default) Not cached — runs on every request → dynamic / SSR-like
await fetch("https://api.example.com/feed");
await fetch("https://api.example.com/feed", { cache: "no-store" }); // explicit, same thing

// Cached indefinitely in the Data Cache → static / SSG-like
await fetch("https://api.example.com/page", { cache: "force-cache" });

// Cached, but revalidated in the background at most every 60s → ISR
await fetch("https://api.example.com/products", { next: { revalidate: 60 } });

// Cached AND tagged for targeted on-demand invalidation
await fetch("https://api.example.com/products", {
  next: { tags: ["products"], revalidate: 3600 },
});

Reading cookies()/headers() forces dynamic

Even with force-cache fetches, calling a dynamic API like await cookies(), await headers(), await draftMode(), or reading searchParams opts the route into dynamic rendering. The Data Cache entry can still be reused, but the route's HTML won't be statically frozen. (In Next.js 15 these are all async — await them.)

docs.title: 2.3 The Full Route Cache
docs.text:
When a route renders without any dynamic input, Next.js caches its rendered output (the HTML and the RSC payload) at build time into the Full Route Cache. Subsequent requests are served from this cache with no re-render — this is what makes a route "static."

A route is statically rendered (and thus Full-Route-Cached) unless it:

  • uses a dynamic function (cookies(), headers(), connection(), uncached searchParams), or
  • contains an uncached fetch (the Next.js 15 default), or
  • sets export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" (or revalidate = 0).
// app/about/page.tsx — fully static, lands in the Full Route Cache at build
export default function About() {
  return <article>Static marketing copy, served from cache.</article>;
}

// Force a route to render dynamically on every request (opt out of the Full Route Cache)
export const dynamic = "force-dynamic";
// Or force it static and error if anything dynamic is used:
export const dynamic = "force-static";

The Full Route Cache is invalidated when the underlying Data Cache entries are revalidated, or on redeploy.

docs.title: 2.4 The Router Cache (client-side)
docs.text:
The Router Cache lives in the browser. As the user navigates with <Link> or router.push, Next.js stores the RSC payload of visited segments so that going back (or revisiting) is instant — no server round-trip.

Next.js 15: client navigations are not cached by default

In Next.js 14, the Router Cache held page segments for a default staleTime of 30 seconds even for dynamic pages, which led to users seeing stale data after navigating. In Next.js 15, the default staleTime for dynamically-rendered pages is 0 — pages are refetched on navigation by default. (Statically-rendered pages still cache for ~5 minutes.) You can tune this explicitly:

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  experimental: {
    staleTimes: {
      dynamic: 30, // cache dynamic pages in the Router Cache for 30s
      static: 180, // and static pages for 180s
    },
  },
};

export default nextConfig;

The Router Cache is cleared by router.refresh(), by a Server Action that calls revalidatePath/revalidateTag, or on a full page reload.

docs.title: Putting the four together
docs.text:
fetch(url)
  │
  ├─▶ Request Memoization   (same render only — dedupes duplicate calls)
  │
  ├─▶ Data Cache            (persistent — survives requests/deploys; opt-in in v15)
  │
  ▼
render route
  │
  ├─▶ Full Route Cache      (server — cached HTML/RSC for static routes)
  │
  ▼
client navigation
  │
  └─▶ Router Cache          (browser — instant back/forward; v15 default 0 for dynamic)

docs.title: 3. Revalidation
docs.text: Revalidation is how cached data becomes fresh again without a full rebuild. There are two models: time-based and on-demand.

docs.title: 3.1 Time-based revalidation
docs.text:
Set a revalidate window and Next.js serves the cached value, then regenerates it in the background after the window elapses — the stale-while-revalidate pattern. The first visitor after expiry gets the stale page instantly; the next visitor gets fresh content.

// Per-fetch — only this request revalidates on its own clock
await fetch("https://api.example.com/products", { next: { revalidate: 60 } });

// Per-segment — applies to the whole route (and is the floor for fetches in it)
// app/products/page.tsx
export const revalidate = 3600; // seconds

export default async function Products() {
  const products = await fetch("https://api.example.com/products", {
    cache: "force-cache",
  }).then((r) => r.json());
  return <ProductList products={products} />;
}

How segment vs. fetch revalidate interact

The lowest revalidate wins for the route's freshness. A segment revalidate = 3600 with a fetch at revalidate: 60 means that fetch refreshes every 60s, but the route's Full Route Cache follows the segment value. Setting export const revalidate = 0 makes the route fully dynamic.

docs.title: 3.2 On-demand revalidation
docs.text:
When data changes on an event (a CMS publish, a successful mutation) rather than on a schedule, invalidate exactly what changed with revalidatePath or revalidateTag. Both come from next/cache and run on the server only — in a Route Handler or a Server Action.

// app/api/revalidate/route.ts — e.g. called by a CMS webhook
import { revalidatePath, revalidateTag } from "next/cache";
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const secret = new URL(request.url).searchParams.get("secret");
  if (secret !== process.env.REVALIDATE_SECRET) {
    return NextResponse.json({ message: "Invalid token" }, { status: 401 });
  }

  const { path, tag } = await request.json();
  if (path) revalidatePath(path); // refresh a specific route, e.g. "/products"
  if (tag) revalidateTag(tag);     // refresh every fetch tagged with this tag

  return NextResponse.json({ revalidated: true, now: Date.now() });
}
  • revalidateTag(tag) — purges every Data Cache entry tagged with tag. Tag your fetches (next: { tags: ["products"] }) and you can invalidate a whole logical group from anywhere. This is the most precise tool.
  • revalidatePath(path) — purges the cached data and route for a given path. Pass a route pattern with a type for dynamic segments: revalidatePath("/blog/[slug]", "page").
// Inside a Server Action: revalidate right after a mutation
"use server";
import { revalidateTag } from "next/cache";

export async function createProduct(data: FormData) {
  await db.product.create({ data: parse(data) });
  revalidateTag("products"); // any page listing products is now stale → refreshes
}

Tag, then invalidate by tag

Reaching for revalidatePath everywhere couples your cache logic to your URL structure. Prefer tagging fetches by the data they return (tags: ["products"], tags: [product:${id}]) and invalidating by tag — it's decoupled from routing and far easier to reason about as the app grows.

docs.title: 4.1 generateStaticParams (SSG for dynamic routes)
docs.text:
To pre-render a set of dynamic routes at build time (one page per blog post, per product, etc.), export generateStaticParams. It's the App Router replacement for the Pages Router's getStaticPaths.

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx
type Post = { slug: string; title: string; body: string };

// 1. Which slugs to pre-render at build time
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const posts: Post[] = await fetch("https://api.example.com/posts").then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return posts.map((post) => ({ slug: post.slug }));
}

// 2. Render each slug (runs per-slug at build, async params in v15)
export default async function BlogPost({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // Next.js 15: params is a Promise
  const post: Post = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${slug}`, {
    cache: "force-cache",
  }).then((r) => r.json());

  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{post.title}</h1>
      <p>{post.body}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

params & searchParams are async in Next.js 15

In Next.js 15, params, searchParams, cookies(), and headers() are Promises — await them (or read with use() in a Client Component). In Next.js 14 they were synchronous. All examples here use the v15 async form.

docs.title: 4.2 dynamicParams — handling the long tail
docs.text:
By default dynamicParams = true: a slug not returned by generateStaticParams is rendered on first request and then cached (on-demand ISR). Set it to false to return a 404 for any unlisted slug.

export const dynamicParams = false; // unlisted slugs → 404 instead of on-demand render

This lets you pre-build only your most popular pages and generate the rest lazily — keep the generateStaticParams list bounded and let dynamicParams cover the tail.

docs.title: 4.3 Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)
docs.text:
ISR = static pages that regenerate in the background. Add revalidate to a statically-generated route and you have it:

// app/products/[id]/page.tsx
export const revalidate = 600; // regenerate at most every 10 minutes

export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const ids: string[] = await getTopProductIds();
  return ids.map((id) => ({ id }));
}

export default async function ProductPage({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ id: string }>;
}) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const product = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/products/${id}`, {
    next: { revalidate: 600, tags: [`product:${id}`] },
  }).then((r) => r.json());
  return <ProductDetail product={product} />;
}

You get CDN speed plus data that stays reasonably fresh. Combine the time-based revalidate with tag-based on-demand invalidation (revalidateTag(product:${id})) so a product edit refreshes instantly and a daily window catches anything that slipped through.

docs.title: 4.4 unstable_cache for non-fetch data
docs.text:
fetch caching only covers HTTP. To put a direct DB query (or any async function) into the Data Cache with the same revalidate/tags semantics, wrap it in unstable_cache.

// lib/products.ts
import { unstable_cache } from "next/cache";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export const getCachedProducts = unstable_cache(
  async (category: string) => db.product.findMany({ where: { category } }),
  ["products-by-category"],          // key prefix parts
  { revalidate: 3600, tags: ["products"] }
);

// Usage in a Server Component:
const electronics = await getCachedProducts("electronics");

The function arguments are automatically folded into the cache key, so getCachedProducts("books") and getCachedProducts("toys") are cached separately. revalidateTag("products") invalidates them all.

unstable_cache arguments & closures

unstable_cache keys on the arguments you pass and the key array — not on variables captured from an outer scope. Always pass anything that affects the result as an argument, or you'll get cache collisions. (It also can't read dynamic data like cookies() inside.)

docs.title: 4.5 The 'use cache' direction
docs.text:
Next.js is moving toward an explicit, composable caching directive: 'use cache'. Placed at the top of a file, a function, or a component, it marks that scope as cacheable, replacing the implicit-defaults model and eventually unstable_cache. It pairs with cacheLife (named revalidation profiles) and cacheTag.

// A cached async function (Next 15 canary / 16 — behind a flag today)
import { unstable_cacheLife as cacheLife, unstable_cacheTag as cacheTag } from "next/cache";

async function getProducts(category: string) {
  "use cache";
  cacheLife("hours");      // named profile: revalidate ~hourly
  cacheTag("products");    // invalidate via revalidateTag("products")
  return db.product.findMany({ where: { category } });
}

Enable it in config while it stabilizes:

// next.config.ts
const nextConfig = {
  experimental: { useCache: true }, // or dynamicIO, depending on release
};
export default nextConfig;

Still stabilizing — pin your version

As of mid-2026, 'use cache', cacheLife, and cacheTag are experimental and gated behind a flag (useCache/dynamicIO); the API surface is still shifting between releases. For production today, prefer explicit fetch options and unstable_cache. Treat 'use cache' as the clearly-signposted future direction and adopt it once it's stable in your pinned Next.js version.

docs.title: 5. Client-side fetching: when to reach for SWR / TanStack Query
docs.text:
Server Components handle the initial render. Reach for client-side fetching when the data is genuinely client-driven and doesn't belong on the server:

  • Data that changes rapidly and needs polling or focus refetching (live dashboards, presence).
  • User-specific, highly interactive data behind authentication where SEO is irrelevant.
  • Infinite scroll / pagination driven by user interaction.
  • Anything needing optimistic updates with automatic rollback on the client.

For one-off client fetches, plain fetch in an event handler is fine. For caching, deduping, revalidation, and retries on the client, use a library.

docs.title: 5.1 SWR
docs.text:
Lightweight, hook-first, from Vercel. The name is the pattern: stale-while-revalidate.

"use client";
import useSWR from "swr";

const fetcher = (url: string) => fetch(url).then((r) => r.json());

export default function Profile() {
  const { data, error, isLoading } = useSWR("/api/me", fetcher, {
    refreshInterval: 30_000, // poll every 30s
  });

  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  if (error) return <p>Failed to load.</p>;
  return <p>Welcome, {data.name}</p>;
}

docs.title: 5.2 TanStack Query (v5)
docs.text:
More powerful (mutations, infinite queries, fine-grained cache control). TanStack Query v5 changed several APIs — note the single-object signatures and the renamed flags below.

First, a provider (a Client Component) mounted in your root layout:

// app/providers.tsx
"use client";
import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { useState } from "react";

export default function Providers({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  // One client per browser session; useState avoids re-creating it on render
  const [queryClient] = useState(() => new QueryClient());
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>{children}</QueryClientProvider>
  );
}

// app/layout.tsx
import Providers from "./providers";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <Providers>{children}</Providers>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

Querying — note v5's object form and isPending (v4's isLoading was renamed; isLoading now means "pending and fetching"):

"use client";
import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

type Todo = { id: string; title: string };

async function fetchTodos(): Promise<Todo[]> {
  const res = await fetch("/api/todos");
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Network error");
  return res.json();
}

export default function Todos() {
  const { data, error, isPending } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["todos"],
    queryFn: fetchTodos,
    staleTime: 60_000,
  });

  if (isPending) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  if (error) return <p>Error: {error.message}</p>;
  return (
    <ul>
      {data.map((t) => (
        <li key={t.id}>{t.title}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

Mutations — v5 also takes a single object, and the cache-callback signatures are object-based:

"use client";
import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";

export default function AddTodo() {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  const mutation = useMutation({
    mutationFn: (title: string) =>
      fetch("/api/todos", {
        method: "POST",
        headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
        body: JSON.stringify({ title }),
      }).then((r) => r.json()),
    onSuccess: () => {
      // v5: invalidateQueries takes a filters object
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["todos"] });
    },
  });

  return (
    <button onClick={() => mutation.mutate("New todo")} disabled={mutation.isPending}>
      {mutation.isPending ? "Adding…" : "Add"}
    </button>
  );
}

Server-render, then hydrate (best of both)

You can prefetch on the server and hand the cache to TanStack Query so the client mounts already-populated — no loading flash, no double fetch. Prefetch into a QueryClient on the server, then wrap the tree in <HydrationBoundary state={dehydrate(queryClient)}>. This pattern (v5's HydrationBoundary, replacing v4's Hydrate) is the recommended way to combine RSC data loading with client-side caching.

Don't reach for a client library by default

In the App Router, most data should be fetched in Server Components — it ships less JS and is simpler. Add SWR/TanStack Query only for the genuinely client-interactive cases above. Fetching everything on the client re-introduces the waterfalls and SEO gaps the App Router was designed to remove. (See the State Management chapter for combining server data with client state.)

docs.title: 6.1 error.tsx — segment error boundaries
docs.text:
An error.tsx file wraps its route segment in a React Error Boundary. When a Server (or Client) Component in that segment throws, the boundary renders instead of the page. It must be a Client Component and receives the error plus a reset function to retry the render.

// app/dashboard/error.tsx
"use client";
import { useEffect } from "react";

export default function Error({
  error,
  reset,
}: {
  error: Error & { digest?: string };
  reset: () => void;
}) {
  useEffect(() => {
    // Log to your monitoring service
    console.error(error);
  }, [error]);

  return (
    <div role="alert">
      <h2>Something went wrong.</h2>
      <button onClick={() => reset()}>Try again</button>
    </div>
  );
}
  • error.tsx catches errors in its segment and below, but not in the segment's own layout.tsx (the layout sits above the boundary). To catch layout errors, put an error.tsx in the parent segment.
  • For the root layout itself, use app/global-error.tsx (it must render its own <html> and <body>).
  • In production the actual error message is hidden from the client for security; you get a digest you can correlate with server logs.

docs.title: 6.2 not-found.tsx and notFound()
docs.text:
Call notFound() from a Server Component to render the nearest not-found.tsx and send a 404 status. This is the idiomatic way to handle "this record doesn't exist."

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx
import { notFound } from "next/navigation";

export default async function BlogPost({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const res = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${slug}`);

  if (res.status === 404) notFound(); // throws → renders not-found.tsx, 404 status
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to load post"); // → error.tsx

  const post = await res.json();
  return <article>{post.title}</article>;
}

// app/blog/[slug]/not-found.tsx
import Link from "next/link";

export default function NotFound() {
  return (
    <div>
      <h2>Post not found</h2>
      <Link href="/blog">Back to all posts</Link>
    </div>
  );
}

Pair loading.tsx + error.tsx + Suspense for streaming

Wrap slow fetches in <Suspense> and give the segment a loading.tsx; pair it with error.tsx and notFound(). The result: an instant skeleton, content streamed in as it resolves, a clean 404 for missing data, and a friendly fallback for unexpected failures — all without blocking the rest of the page.

// app/products/page.tsx — streaming a slow region with graceful states
import { Suspense } from "react";

export default function ProductsPage() {
  return (
    <section>
      <h1>Products</h1>
      <Suspense fallback={<ProductsSkeleton />}>
        <ProductList /> {/* async; may notFound() or throw → caught by not-found/error.tsx */}
      </Suspense>
    </section>
  );
}

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text:
🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Server Component fetch async/await (or direct DB) inside the component; no getServerSideProps Request Memoization Dedupes identical fetch/cache() calls within one render Data Cache Persistent server cache for fetch results; opt-in in Next 15 Full Route Cache Server cache of rendered static route HTML/RSC Router Cache Client cache of visited routes; dynamic default staleTime is 0 in Next 15 cache: 'force-cache' Opt a fetch back into the Data Cache (was the v14 default) next: { revalidate } Time-based ISR — stale-while-revalidate revalidateTag / revalidatePath On-demand invalidation; prefer tags generateStaticParams Pre-render dynamic routes at build (replaces getStaticPaths) dynamicParams true = render unlisted slugs on demand; false = 404 unstable_cache Put non-fetch (DB) results in the Data Cache 'use cache' Experimental future direction; cacheLife + cacheTag SWR / TanStack Query v5 Client-side caching for interactive/polling data error.tsx / notFound() Segment error boundary; idiomatic 404 handling

The one rule to remember

In Next.js 15, nothing is cached unless you ask for it — fetch, GET Route Handlers, and client navigations are all uncached by default. Set cache/revalidate/tags explicitly, tag your data, and invalidate by tag. Default to the server; reach for client libraries only for genuinely interactive data.

docs.text:

  • Next.js Blog — Next.js 15 (caching defaults)

docs.title: Server Actions & Mutations
docs.text:
Reading data in the App Router is just await inside a Server Component. Writing data—creating a post, updating a profile, deleting a row—is the job of Server Actions: async functions that are guaranteed to run on the server, callable directly from your components (including straight from a <form>) without you hand-writing an API route, a fetch, or any request plumbing.

This chapter covers what Server Actions are and how the "use server" directive works, wiring them to forms with progressive enhancement, managing form state and UX with React 19's useActionState / useFormStatus / useOptimistic, validating input with Zod and returning typed errors, finishing a mutation with revalidation and redirect, the security rules that make actions safe, and calling actions outside of forms from Client Components.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/data-fetching/server-actions-and-mutations · https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-actions

docs.title: 1. What are Server Actions?
docs.text:
A Server Action is an async function marked with the "use server" directive. Next.js compiles it into a secure RPC endpoint: when a Client Component "calls" the action, React serializes the arguments, POSTs them to the server, runs the real function there, and serializes the result back. Your client code never sees the function body, the database client, or any secret it touches—only the call.

Three things make them distinct from a Route Handler:

  • No endpoint to write or name — there's no URL, no route.ts, no fetch. You import the function and call it (or hand it to a form's action prop).
  • They work without JavaScript — when used as a form action, the form submits over plain HTTP and the action still runs (see progressive enhancement below).
  • They compose with the cache — an action can call revalidatePath / revalidateTag and redirect to update the UI after a write, all in one round trip.

docs.title: The "use server" directive
docs.text:
There are two ways to mark code as a Server Action. File level — put "use server" at the very top of a module, and every export becomes a Server Action:

// app/actions.ts
"use server";

import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export async function createPost(title: string, body: string) {
  return db.post.create({ data: { title, body } });
}

export async function deletePost(id: string) {
  await db.post.delete({ where: { id } });
}

Inline — put "use server" as the first statement inside an async function, which is handy for small actions defined right in a Server Component:

// app/page.tsx (a Server Component)
export default function Page() {
  async function create(formData: FormData) {
    "use server";
    await db.post.create({ data: { title: String(formData.get("title")) } });
  }

  return (
    <form action={create}>
      <input name="title" />
      <button type="submit">Create</button>
    </form>
  );
}

\"use server\" is not \"use client\"

They are unrelated despite the rhyme. "use client" marks a component boundary that ships to the browser. "use server" marks a function as a server-only RPC endpoint that is exposed to the network. Putting "use server" at the top of a Client Component file is a mistake—a Client Component imports actions from a separate "use server" module instead.

docs.title: A typical organization
docs.text:
A common, scalable pattern is to keep actions in dedicated files (often colocated near the feature, or under a lib//actions/ folder) so both Server and Client Components can import them:

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";

import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";

export async function toggleLike(postId: string) {
  // ...mutation...
  revalidatePath(`/posts/${postId}`);
}

docs.title: 2. Forms & progressive enhancement
docs.text:
The headline use of Server Actions is the <form action={...}> prop. Pass an action directly, and Next.js wires the form to it. The action receives a single FormData argument:

// app/posts/new/page.tsx — a Server Component
import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";

export default function NewPostPage() {
  async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
    "use server";
    const title = String(formData.get("title") ?? "");
    const body = String(formData.get("body") ?? "");

    const post = await db.post.create({ data: { title, body } });

    revalidatePath("/posts");        // refresh the list
    redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`);   // go to the new post
  }

  return (
    <form action={createPost}>
      <input name="title" placeholder="Title" required />
      <textarea name="body" placeholder="Write something…" />
      <button type="submit">Publish</button>
    </form>
  );
}

Note the inputs use the native name attribute—that's how values land in FormData. No useState, no onChange, no controlled inputs required.

docs.title: Progressive enhancement
docs.text: Because the form's action is a real server endpoint, the form works before (or without) JavaScript. If the user submits while the page's JS is still downloading—or if JS is disabled entirely—the browser performs a normal HTML form POST and the action still runs. Once React hydrates, the same form upgrades to a client-side submission (no full-page reload, with pending state and optimistic UI available). You get the resilience of classic server-rendered forms and the polish of an SPA from the same code.

docs.title: Passing extra arguments with .bind
docs.text:
Forms only carry their fields. To pass additional data (an id, a context value) to an action, use .bind—the bound arguments are sent securely as part of the action call, not as hidden form fields the client can tamper with as easily:

// Bind the post id; formData is appended as the last argument
import { updatePost } from "./actions";

export function EditForm({ id }: { id: string }) {
  const updatePostWithId = updatePost.bind(null, id);
  return (
    <form action={updatePostWithId}>
      <input name="title" />
      <button type="submit">Save</button>
    </form>
  );
}

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";
export async function updatePost(id: string, formData: FormData) {
  // id came from .bind, formData from the form
}

Server vs client form handling

With Server Actions you reach for name + FormData and let the platform submit the form. This is the opposite of the controlled-input + fetch pattern you'd use against a Route Handler. Both are valid—Server Actions are usually less code and give you progressive enhancement for free.

docs.title: 3. Form state & UX
docs.text: Raw <form action> works, but real apps need to show errors, disable the button while submitting, and feel instant. React 19 (which ships with Next.js 15) provides three hooks for exactly this.

docs.title: useActionState — state + the action in one
docs.text:
useActionState wraps an action so it can return a value (validation errors, a success message) that React stores as state and re-renders with. It's the backbone of form UX.

React 19 renamed useFormState → useActionState

In React 18 / Next.js 14 this hook lived in react-dom and was called useFormState. In React 19 it moved to react and was renamed useActionState, gaining a third element in its return tuple: the pending flag. Use useActionState for all new code; useFormState is deprecated.

The action's signature changes: it now receives the previous state as its first argument and FormData as its second.

// app/posts/new-form.tsx
"use client";

import { useActionState } from "react";
import { createPost, type FormState } from "./actions";

const initialState: FormState = { ok: false, errors: {} };

export function NewPostForm() {
  const [state, formAction, isPending] = useActionState(createPost, initialState);

  return (
    <form action={formAction}>
      <input name="title" aria-describedby="title-error" />
      {state.errors?.title && (
        <p id="title-error" role="alert">
          {state.errors.title}
        </p>
      )}

      <textarea name="body" />
      {state.errors?.body && <p role="alert">{state.errors.body}</p>}

      <button type="submit" disabled={isPending}>
        {isPending ? "Publishing…" : "Publish"}
      </button>

      {state.ok && <p>Saved!</p>}
    </form>
  );
}

The matching action receives prevState then formData and returns the next state:

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";

export type FormState = {
  ok: boolean;
  errors?: { title?: string; body?: string };
};

export async function createPost(
  _prevState: FormState,
  formData: FormData
): Promise<FormState> {
  const title = String(formData.get("title") ?? "");
  if (!title) return { ok: false, errors: { title: "Title is required" } };

  await db.post.create({ data: { title } });
  return { ok: true };
}

docs.title: useFormStatus — pending state for a submit button
docs.text:
useFormStatus (from react-dom) reads the status of the nearest parent <form>. Its main use is a reusable submit button that disables and relabels itself while the form is in flight—without the parent needing to pass a prop down.

// app/components/submit-button.tsx
"use client";

import { useFormStatus } from "react-dom";

export function SubmitButton({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const { pending } = useFormStatus();
  return (
    <button type="submit" disabled={pending}>
      {pending ? "Submitting…" : children}
    </button>
  );
}

useFormStatus must live inside the form

The hook reads context from the surrounding <form>, so the component that calls it has to be rendered as a child of that form, not the component that renders the form. If you already have isPending from useActionState, prefer that for the same form; reach for useFormStatus when you want a self-contained button component that any form can drop in.

docs.title: useOptimistic — instant UI before the server responds
docs.text:
useOptimistic lets you show the expected result immediately, then reconcile with the real server state when the action finishes (or roll back automatically if it fails). Perfect for likes, todos, and chat messages.

// app/todos/todo-list.tsx
"use client";

import { useOptimistic } from "react";
import { addTodo } from "./actions";

type Todo = { id: string; text: string; pending?: boolean };

export function TodoList({ todos }: { todos: Todo[] }) {
  const [optimisticTodos, addOptimistic] = useOptimistic(
    todos,
    (current, newText: string) => [
      ...current,
      { id: crypto.randomUUID(), text: newText, pending: true },
    ]
  );

  return (
    <>
      <ul>
        {optimisticTodos.map((t) => (
          <li key={t.id} style={{ opacity: t.pending ? 0.5 : 1 }}>
            {t.text}
          </li>
        ))}
      </ul>

      <form
        action={async (formData: FormData) => {
          const text = String(formData.get("text") ?? "");
          addOptimistic(text);     // update UI instantly
          await addTodo(text);     // then run the real mutation
        }}
      >
        <input name="text" />
        <SubmitButton>Add</SubmitButton>
      </form>
    </>
  );
}

When addTodo resolves and the page revalidates, optimisticTodos is replaced by the authoritative todos prop. If the action throws, React discards the optimistic entry automatically.

docs.title: 4. Validation & typed errors
docs.text:
Never trust the client. FormData is just strings the browser sent—anyone can craft a request that bypasses your required attributes and HTML5 constraints. Validate every input inside the action with a schema library. Zod pairs especially well with useActionState because it produces structured, field-level errors you can return as typed state.

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";

import { z } from "zod";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";

const PostSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1, "Title is required").max(120, "Title is too long"),
  body: z.string().min(10, "Body must be at least 10 characters"),
});

// The shape our form state takes — strongly typed for the client to consume
export type FormState = {
  ok: boolean;
  message?: string;
  // Per-field error arrays, keyed by the schema's field names
  errors?: Partial<Record<keyof z.infer<typeof PostSchema>, string[]>>;
};

export async function createPost(
  _prevState: FormState,
  formData: FormData
): Promise<FormState> {
  const parsed = PostSchema.safeParse({
    title: formData.get("title"),
    body: formData.get("body"),
  });

  if (!parsed.success) {
    // flatten() gives you { fieldErrors: { title: [...], body: [...] } }
    return {
      ok: false,
      message: "Please fix the errors below.",
      errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors,
    };
  }

  await db.post.create({ data: parsed.data }); // parsed.data is fully typed

  revalidatePath("/posts");
  redirect("/posts");
}

On the client, the state.errors object is typed, so reading state.errors?.title is checked at compile time:

"use client";
import { useActionState } from "react";
import { createPost, type FormState } from "./actions";

const initial: FormState = { ok: false };

export function PostForm() {
  const [state, action, pending] = useActionState(createPost, initial);

  return (
    <form action={action}>
      <input name="title" />
      {state.errors?.title?.map((e) => (
        <p key={e} role="alert">{e}</p>
      ))}

      <textarea name="body" />
      {state.errors?.body?.map((e) => (
        <p key={e} role="alert">{e}</p>
      ))}

      {state.message && <p>{state.message}</p>}
      <button disabled={pending}>Save</button>
    </form>
  );
}

Validate once, on the server

Client-side validation (required, type="email", a Zod check before submit) is a UX nicety—it gives faster feedback. The server-side validation inside the action is the one that actually protects your data. If you only validate one place, validate on the server. safeParse keeps you in control of the error shape; parse throws, which surfaces as an unhandled action error instead of friendly field messages.

docs.title: 5. After a mutation: revalidation & redirect
docs.text: A write usually invalidates something you've already cached or rendered. Next.js gives you two cache-busting functions and a navigation helper, all callable from inside an action.

docs.title: revalidatePath and revalidateTag
docs.text:
After mutating, tell Next.js which cached data is now stale so the next render fetches fresh data:

"use server";
import { revalidatePath, revalidateTag } from "next/cache";

export async function publishPost(id: string) {
  await db.post.update({ where: { id }, data: { published: true } });

  revalidatePath("/posts");          // re-render the /posts route
  revalidatePath(`/posts/${id}`);    // and this specific post's page
  revalidateTag("posts");            // and every fetch tagged "posts"
}
  • revalidatePath(path) clears the cache for a specific route (or a layout/page). Use it when you know exactly which URL displays the changed data.
  • revalidateTag(tag) clears every cached fetch that was tagged with that string (fetch(url, { next: { tags: ["posts"] } })). Use it when the same data appears across many routes.

Without revalidation, the user would submit successfully but keep seeing the old, cached UI.

docs.title: redirect after a write
docs.text:
To send the user somewhere after a successful mutation, call redirect from next/navigation:

"use server";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";

export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
  const post = await db.post.create({ data: { title: String(formData.get("title")) } });
  revalidatePath("/posts");
  redirect(`/posts/${post.id}`); // must come AFTER revalidation
}

redirect throws on purpose

redirect() works by throwing a special internal signal that Next.js catches, so code after it never runs, and you should not call it inside a try/catch that swallows errors (it would catch the redirect signal and break navigation). Call redirect after your mutation and revalidation, at the end of the action. If you catch errors around a DB call, put redirect outside that try/catch. The same applies to notFound().

docs.title: Returning errors instead of throwing
docs.text: When you're using useActionState, you typically return an error state rather than redirect-ing, so the form can display field messages and stay on the page. Reserve redirect for the success path (or for navigation that should happen regardless, like sending an unauthenticated user to /login).

docs.title: 6. Security
docs.text:
This is the most important section. Because a Server Action compiles to a publicly reachable POST endpoint, you must treat it with the same suspicion as any API route. The convenience of "just call a function" can hide the fact that anyone on the internet can invoke it with any arguments.

Server Actions are public endpoints — secure every one

A "use server" function is exposed to the network. An attacker can call it directly with forged arguments, skipping your form, your .bind values, and your client-side checks entirely.
