Every action must authenticate and authorize on its own. Never assume an action can only be reached "from the right page" or "by the right user"—there is no such guarantee.

docs.title: Always authenticate and authorize inside the action
docs.text:
Do the auth check at the top of the action body, not in a wrapper component or middleware you hope ran:

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";

import { auth } from "@/lib/auth";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";
import { z } from "zod";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";

const UpdateSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  title: z.string().min(1).max(120),
});

export async function updatePost(_prev: unknown, formData: FormData) {
  // 1. AUTHENTICATE — who is calling?
  const session = await auth();
  if (!session?.user) redirect("/login");

  // 2. VALIDATE — never trust the payload
  const parsed = UpdateSchema.safeParse({
    id: formData.get("id"),
    title: formData.get("title"),
  });
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { ok: false, errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors };
  }

  // 3. AUTHORIZE — is THIS user allowed to touch THIS resource?
  const post = await db.post.findUnique({ where: { id: parsed.data.id } });
  if (!post) return { ok: false, message: "Not found" };
  if (post.authorId !== session.user.id) {
    return { ok: false, message: "Forbidden" }; // ownership check
  }

  // 4. Only now perform the mutation
  await db.post.update({
    where: { id: parsed.data.id },
    data: { title: parsed.data.title },
  });

  return { ok: true };
}

Never trust client input — including hidden fields and .bind args

Treat every value from FormData (and even arguments passed via .bind) as attacker-controlled. A hidden <input name="role" value="admin"> or a bound userId can be swapped by anyone replaying the request. Re-derive identity from the server-side session, validate the shape with a schema, and check ownership/permissions against the database. Authorization based on the resource's actual owner—not a value the client sent—is what stops one user from editing another's data.

docs.title: Other hardening notes
docs.text:
• Don't leak internals in error messages. Return generic messages to the client; log the real error server-side.
  • Rate-limit sensitive actions (login, password reset, anything that sends email or money) the same way you would an API route.
  • CSRF — Server Actions only accept POST and Next.js enforces same-origin checks (the Origin/Host comparison, configurable via serverActions.allowedOrigins), which mitigates CSRF for the common case—but this does not replace your own auth checks.
  • Use a Data Access Layer. Centralize auth + ownership checks in functions your actions call, so the rules live in one audited place rather than copy-pasted into every action.
  • Dead-code elimination is not security. An unused/unreferenced action can still be present in the deployed bundle's action manifest; assume any "use server" export is reachable.

docs.title: 7. Calling actions from Client Components
docs.text: Forms are the common case, but Server Actions are just functions—you can call them from event handlers, effects, or anywhere in a Client Component. The catch: a call that updates the UI should be wrapped so React knows it's a transition.

docs.title: Non-form usage with startTransition
docs.text:
Outside a <form action>, wrap the action call in startTransition. This keeps the UI responsive (the pending state is tracked, the page stays interactive) and is the supported way to invoke an action imperatively:

// app/posts/like-button.tsx
"use client";

import { useTransition } from "react";
import { likePost } from "./actions";

export function LikeButton({ postId }: { postId: string }) {
  const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();

  return (
    <button
      disabled={isPending}
      onClick={() =>
        startTransition(async () => {
          await likePost(postId); // a Server Action, called like a normal function
        })
      }
    >
      {isPending ? "…" : "♥ Like"}
    </button>
  );
}

// app/posts/actions.ts
"use server";
import { auth } from "@/lib/auth";
import { revalidatePath } from "next/cache";

export async function likePost(postId: string) {
  const session = await auth();
  if (!session?.user) throw new Error("Unauthorized"); // still authenticate!
  await db.like.create({ data: { postId, userId: session.user.id } });
  revalidatePath(`/posts/${postId}`);
}

docs.title: Importing actions into Client Components
docs.text:
A Client Component cannot define a "use server" action (its file is "use client"), but it can import one from a separate "use server" module and call it. This is the standard pattern: actions live in actions.ts, and any component—server or client—imports them.

"use client";
import { deletePost } from "./actions"; // imported from a "use server" file

export function DeleteButton({ id }: { id: string }) {
  return <button onClick={() => deletePost(id)}>Delete</button>;
}

Reading the return value with useActionState vs startTransition

If you call an action imperatively and need its returned value (e.g. an error to display), useActionState is still the cleanest path—pass its formAction to a <form> or invoke it inside startTransition. For fire-and-forget mutations that just revalidate, a plain startTransition call is enough. Either way, don't await an action directly in render or an effect without a transition—you'll lose pending tracking and risk waterfalls.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Server Action async function marked "use server"; compiles to a secure RPC endpoint "use server" (file) Top of a module — every export becomes an action "use server" (inline) First line in an async function — that function becomes an action <form action={fn}> Wire an action to a form; receives FormData; works without JS Progressive enhancement Form submits over plain HTTP pre-hydration, upgrades to client submit after .bind(null, arg) Pass extra arguments to an action securely (not as form fields) useActionState React 19 hook: action returns state (errors/success) + pending flag useFormStatus react-dom hook read inside a form for a self-contained submit button useOptimistic Show the expected result instantly, reconcile/rollback on completion Zod + safeParse Validate FormData in the action; return typed fieldErrors revalidatePath Bust the cache for a specific route after a write revalidateTag Bust every fetch tagged with a string after a write redirect Navigate after a mutation; throws (keep it out of try/catch) Auth in the action Authenticate + authorize inside every action — they're public endpoints Never trust input Re-derive identity from the session; validate; check ownership in the DB startTransition Call an action imperatively from a Client Component, keeping pending state

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — Server Actions & Mutations
  • Next.js — Security & Server Actions (Sebastian Markbåge)
  • React — useActionState
  • React — useFormStatus
  • React — useOptimistic
  • Zod — Documentation

docs.title: Route Handlers (API)
docs.text:
A Route Handler is how you build an HTTP API inside the App Router. You create a route.ts file and export functions named after HTTP verbs (GET, POST, …); Next.js wires each one up as an endpoint. Handlers are built on the Web Platform Request/Response APIs, so the same code you'd write for a service worker, Deno, or Cloudflare Workers mostly carries over—Next.js just adds a couple of ergonomic helpers (NextRequest/NextResponse).

This is the App Router's replacement for pages/api/*. The mental model is different too: because Server Components can read your database directly and Server Actions handle form mutations, you no longer reach for an API route for every server interaction. Route Handlers are for things that genuinely need a public HTTP endpoint—webhooks, third-party callbacks, OAuth, public/mobile APIs, file downloads, and streaming.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/routing/route-handlers

docs.title: 1. route.ts basics
docs.text:
A Route Handler lives at app/<segment>/route.ts and exports one function per HTTP method it supports. The file takes the place of page.tsx for that segment—you can't have both page.tsx and route.ts in the same folder, because both claim the same URL.

// app/api/health/route.ts → responds at GET /api/health
export async function GET() {
  return Response.json({ status: "ok", time: Date.now() });
}

You're not limited to an api/ folder—that's just a convention. app/sitemap-data/route.ts answers at /sitemap-data. The api/ prefix is purely organizational (and keeps API URLs visually distinct from pages).

docs.title: Supported HTTP methods
docs.text:
You can export any of these named functions. Each receives the incoming request and returns a Response (or a Promise<Response>):

// app/api/items/route.ts
export async function GET(request: Request) { /* read */ }
export async function POST(request: Request) { /* create */ }
export async function PUT(request: Request) { /* replace */ }
export async function PATCH(request: Request) { /* partial update */ }
export async function DELETE(request: Request) { /* remove */ }
export async function HEAD(request: Request) { /* headers only */ }
export async function OPTIONS(request: Request) { /* preflight / capabilities */ }
  • If a method isn't exported, Next.js automatically returns 405 Method Not Allowed with a correct Allow header.
  • HEAD is handled automatically if you export GET (it runs GET and strips the body), but you can export it explicitly to optimize.
  • OPTIONS is auto-generated for the methods you export, though you'll often write your own for CORS preflight (see §5).

Verbs are uppercase named exports

They must be the exact uppercase verb names. export async function get() won't be picked up. They can be async or sync; returning a Promise<Response> is the norm since you usually await data.

docs.title: What a handler returns
docs.text:
A handler must resolve to a Web Response. You have three common ways to build one:

// 1. The native Response constructor — full control over body + init
return new Response("Hello", { status: 200, headers: { "X-Custom": "1" } });

// 2. Response.json() — a static helper that sets Content-Type for you (Web standard)
return Response.json({ ok: true }, { status: 201 });

// 3. NextResponse — Next's subclass, adds cookies(), redirect(), rewrite(), next()
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
return NextResponse.json({ ok: true }, { status: 201 });

Response.json() and NextResponse.json() produce the same JSON response; reach for NextResponse when you need its extras (cookie mutation, redirects, rewrites).

docs.title: 2. NextRequest/NextResponse vs Request/Response
docs.text: Everything in a Route Handler is plain Web Request/Response by default. next/server exports two subclasses that add Next-specific conveniences—you opt in by typing your parameter as NextRequest and/or returning NextResponse.

docs.title: NextRequest — extends Request
docs.text:
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  request.nextUrl.pathname;           // parsed URL (no manual `new URL(request.url)`)
  request.nextUrl.searchParams;       // a URLSearchParams you can read directly
  request.cookies.get("session");     // typed cookie helpers (get/getAll/has)
  request.headers.get("user-agent");  // standard Headers (same as native Request)
}

Without NextRequest, you'd reconstruct the URL yourself: const { searchParams } = new URL(request.url). Both work—NextRequest is just less boilerplate.

docs.title: NextResponse — extends Response
docs.text:
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

// JSON (typed via a generic)
NextResponse.json<{ id: string }>({ id: "1" }, { status: 201 });

// Redirect (must be an absolute URL)
NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/login", request.url));

// Set cookies on the response
const res = NextResponse.json({ ok: true });
res.cookies.set("token", "abc", { httpOnly: true, secure: true, path: "/" });
return res;

When to use which

Use bare Request/Response for simple, portable handlers (and to keep the Web-standard mental model). Reach for NextRequest/NextResponse when you want nextUrl, the cookie helpers, or redirect/rewrite—i.e. the things plain Web APIs make awkward. You can mix them: accept a NextRequest and return a plain Response, or vice versa.

docs.title: Dynamic route segments (async params)
docs.text:
Dynamic segments work exactly like pages. app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts receives a second argument with params. In Next.js 15, params is a Promise and must be awaited.

// app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts → /api/posts/123
export async function GET(
  request: Request,
  { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }
) {
  const { id } = await params;            // Next 15: params is async
  const post = await db.post.findUnique({ where: { id } });
  if (!post) return new Response("Not found", { status: 404 });
  return Response.json(post);
}

Catch-all segments give you a string array—app/api/files/[...path]/route.ts yields params: Promise<{ path: string[] }> (so /api/files/a/b/c → ["a", "b", "c"]).

params is a Promise in Next 15

In Next.js 14 params was a plain object. In 15 it's a Promise (aligning with the async-request-APIs change). Always destructure with await params. The same applies to cookies() and headers() from next/headers.

docs.title: Query / search params
docs.text:
Read them off nextUrl.searchParams (a standard URLSearchParams):

// /api/search?q=next&page=2&tag=ssr&tag=rsc
export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  const sp = request.nextUrl.searchParams;
  const q = sp.get("q") ?? "";          // "next"
  const page = Number(sp.get("page") ?? "1");  // 2
  const tags = sp.getAll("tag");        // ["ssr", "rsc"] — repeated keys
  const has = sp.has("q");              // true

  const results = await search(q, { page, tags });
  return Response.json(results);
}

With a plain Request, do const { searchParams } = new URL(request.url) instead.

Reading searchParams makes the route dynamic

Touching the query string opts the handler out of static rendering (there's nothing to statically cache—the answer depends on the URL). That's usually what you want for a search endpoint.

docs.title: JSON body
docs.text:
request.json() parses the body. It throws on malformed JSON, so guard it:

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  let body: unknown;
  try {
    body = await request.json();
  } catch {
    return Response.json({ error: "Invalid JSON" }, { status: 400 });
  }

  // Validate before trusting it (Zod recommended — never trust the client)
  const parsed = CreatePostSchema.safeParse(body);
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return Response.json({ errors: parsed.error.flatten() }, { status: 422 });
  }

  const created = await db.post.create({ data: parsed.data });
  return Response.json(created, { status: 201 });
}

A Zod schema for the example above:

import { z } from "zod";

const CreatePostSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1),
  body: z.string().min(1),
  published: z.boolean().default(false),
});

A request body can only be read once

request.json(), request.text(), request.formData(), and request.arrayBuffer() all consume the stream. Call exactly one. If you need the raw bytes and the parsed value (e.g. webhook signature verification, §5), read the raw form first and parse from that.

docs.title: Form data (and file uploads)
docs.text:
For application/x-www-form-urlencoded or multipart/form-data bodies, use request.formData():

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const form = await request.formData();

  const email = form.get("email");      // string | File | null
  const avatar = form.get("avatar");    // a File for <input type="file">

  if (avatar instanceof File) {
    const bytes = Buffer.from(await avatar.arrayBuffer());
    await saveToStorage(avatar.name, bytes);
  }

  return Response.json({ email });
}

form.get() returns string | File | null; form.getAll("key") returns every value for a repeated field.

docs.title: Cookies
docs.text:
Two ways, both valid:

import { cookies } from "next/headers";

export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  // (a) From the request object (NextRequest)
  const sessionA = request.cookies.get("session")?.value;

  // (b) From next/headers — async in Next 15, works across the App Router
  const cookieStore = await cookies();
  const sessionB = cookieStore.get("session")?.value;

  return Response.json({ session: sessionA ?? sessionB });
}

To set a cookie, mutate the response (you cannot set a request cookie):

const res = NextResponse.json({ ok: true });
res.cookies.set("theme", "dark", {
  httpOnly: true,
  secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === "production",
  sameSite: "lax",
  path: "/",
  maxAge: 60 * 60 * 24 * 7, // one week
});
return res;

docs.title: Headers
docs.text:
import { headers } from "next/headers";

export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  // (a) Off the request
  const auth = request.headers.get("authorization");

  // (b) From next/headers (read-only, async in Next 15)
  const h = await headers();
  const ua = h.get("user-agent");

  if (!auth?.startsWith("Bearer ")) {
    return new Response("Unauthorized", { status: 401 });
  }
  return Response.json({ ua });
}

Reading cookies/headers forces dynamic rendering

Like searchParams, calling cookies() or headers() (or reading request.cookies) marks the handler dynamic—it can't be cached because the response depends on the incoming request.

docs.title: 4. Caching & dynamic behavior
docs.text: This is the part that trips people up most, and it changed between major versions.

docs.title: GET handlers are uncached by default in Next.js 15
docs.text:
In Next.js 14, a GET Route Handler with no dynamic inputs was cached at build time by default (it behaved like a static asset). In Next.js 15, that default flipped: Route Handlers are not cached by default—every GET runs per request unless you opt in.

Next.js 14 Next.js 15 GET with no dynamic inputs Cached (static) by default Uncached (dynamic) by default POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE Always dynamic Always dynamic Reading searchParams/cookies/headers Forces dynamic Forces dynamic 
// Next.js 15: this runs on every request by default
export async function GET() {
  const data = await getExpensiveData();
  return Response.json(data);
}

docs.title: Opting back into caching
docs.text:
Use the route segment config exports to control behavior explicitly. They're plain export const values Next.js reads at build time:

// app/api/feed/route.ts

// Force this GET to be statically cached (build-time / shared)
export const dynamic = "force-static";

// …or revalidate the cached response at most every 60s (ISR for the endpoint)
export const revalidate = 60;

export async function GET() {
  const posts = await db.post.findMany();
  return Response.json(posts);
}

Key segment-config options for handlers:

Export Effect export const dynamic = "force-static" Cache the GET response; dynamic functions return empty/default values export const dynamic = "force-dynamic" Never cache; run per request (the Next 15 default for handlers) export const dynamic = "auto" Default — Next.js decides based on what you use export const revalidate = 60 Time-based revalidation (seconds) for a cached handler export const runtime = "edge" Run on the Edge runtime instead of Node.js export const fetchCache = "..." Override the default caching of fetch calls inside the handler

You can also control caching at the data layer instead of the whole route—per-fetch options still apply inside a handler:

export async function GET() {
  // This specific fetch is cached & revalidated, independent of the route
  const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/rates", {
    next: { revalidate: 3600, tags: ["rates"] },
  });
  return Response.json(await res.json());
}

…and invalidate it on demand from another handler with revalidateTag("rates").

Be explicit

Because the default changed across versions—and is the opposite of what fetch does inside Server Components—don't rely on it. State dynamic/revalidate (or per-fetch cache/revalidate) intentionally so the behavior is obvious to the next reader and stable across upgrades.

docs.title: CORS
docs.text:
By default a Route Handler only answers same-origin requests in a browser. To allow cross-origin calls, set CORS headers yourself—and handle the OPTIONS preflight:

// app/api/public/route.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

const ALLOWED_ORIGIN = "https://app.example.com";

const corsHeaders = {
  "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": ALLOWED_ORIGIN,
  "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "GET, POST, OPTIONS",
  "Access-Control-Allow-Headers": "Content-Type, Authorization",
  "Access-Control-Max-Age": "86400",
};

// Browsers send a preflight OPTIONS for non-simple requests
export async function OPTIONS() {
  return new NextResponse(null, { status: 204, headers: corsHeaders });
}

export async function GET() {
  return NextResponse.json({ ok: true }, { headers: corsHeaders });
}

CORS for all routes belongs in middleware or next.config

Per-route headers are fine for one endpoint. To apply CORS across many routes, set them once in middleware.ts or via the headers() config in next.config.ts. Avoid Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for authenticated APIs—pin it to known origins.

docs.title: Streaming responses
docs.text:
Return a ReadableStream to stream a response incrementally—ideal for LLM token streams, long-running jobs, or large exports. The browser/consumer receives bytes as they're produced instead of waiting for the whole body.

// app/api/stream/route.ts
export async function GET() {
  const encoder = new TextEncoder();

  const stream = new ReadableStream({
    async start(controller) {
      for (let i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
        controller.enqueue(encoder.encode(`chunk ${i}\n`));
        await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 500));
      }
      controller.close();
    },
  });

  return new Response(stream, {
    headers: { "Content-Type": "text/plain; charset=utf-8" },
  });
}

For Server-Sent Events (a long-lived text/event-stream the browser consumes via EventSource), the shape is the same—just format each chunk as data: ...\n\n and set the SSE content type:

return new Response(stream, {
  headers: {
    "Content-Type": "text/event-stream",
    "Cache-Control": "no-cache, no-transform",
    Connection: "keep-alive",
  },
});

AI streaming

For LLM apps, the Vercel AI SDK (ai package) wraps this pattern—result.toDataStreamResponse() returns exactly such a streaming Response from a Route Handler, and a matching client hook consumes it. Under the hood it's the ReadableStream shown above.

docs.title: Webhooks (raw body + signature verification)
docs.text:
Webhooks (Stripe, GitHub, Clerk, etc.) sign the request so you can prove it really came from them. The signature is computed over the exact raw bytes, so you must verify against request.text()/arrayBuffer() before parsing—request.json() would re-serialize and could change the bytes, breaking the signature.

// app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import Stripe from "stripe";

const stripe = new Stripe(process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY!);
const webhookSecret = process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET!;

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  // 1. Read the RAW body — do not call request.json() first
  const rawBody = await request.text();
  const signature = request.headers.get("stripe-signature");

  if (!signature) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Missing signature" }, { status: 400 });
  }

  // 2. Verify the signature against the raw bytes
  let event: Stripe.Event;
  try {
    event = stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(rawBody, signature, webhookSecret);
  } catch (err) {
    return NextResponse.json({ error: "Invalid signature" }, { status: 400 });
  }

  // 3. Now it's safe to act on the (verified) event
  switch (event.type) {
    case "checkout.session.completed":
      await fulfillOrder(event.data.object);
      break;
    // …other event types
  }

  // 4. Return 2xx quickly so the provider doesn't retry
  return NextResponse.json({ received: true });
}

Webhook gotchas

  • Read the raw body first. Calling request.json() consumes the stream and reshapes the bytes—signature verification will fail. In the App Router you get the raw body simply via request.text() (no bodyParser config needed, unlike the old Pages Router).
  • Return 2xx fast. Providers retry on non-2xx or slow responses, which can double-process events. Acknowledge first, then do heavy work (queue it, or use after() from next/server).
  • Make handlers idempotent. Retries and at-least-once delivery mean the same event can arrive twice—key off the event ID.

docs.title: 6. Route Handlers vs Server Actions — when to use which
docs.text:
Both run on the server and both can mutate data, so it's easy to confuse them. The distinction: a Server Action is an RPC-style function you call from your own React UI; a Route Handler is a real, addressable HTTP endpoint anyone can call.

// Server Action — "use server"; invoked from a form/component, no URL
"use server";
export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
  const title = formData.get("title");
  await db.post.create({ data: { title: String(title) } });
}

// Route Handler — a URL (POST /api/posts) callable by anything that speaks HTTP
export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const body = await request.json();
  const post = await db.post.create({ data: body });
  return Response.json(post, { status: 201 });
}

docs.title: How they differ
docs.text: Server Action Route Handler Invocation Called like a function from your React app (form action, useActionState, event handler) A public URL — fetch/curl/any HTTP client Has a stable URL? No (internal RPC endpoint, obfuscated id) Yes — you design the path & method Progressive enhancement Yes — forms work before JS hydrates No — needs a client to make the request External callers No (only your app) Yes — webhooks, mobile apps, third parties HTTP semantics Hidden (always a POST under the hood) Full control — status codes, headers, methods, CORS Return type Serializable JS value A Response (any body/status/headers) Typical use Form submits & mutations from your UI Public/3rd-party APIs, webhooks, OAuth callbacks, downloads, streaming

docs.title: Rules of thumb
docs.text:
Pick the right tool

  • Mutation triggered by your own UI? Use a Server Action—less boilerplate, progressive enhancement, built-in pending/optimistic state via useActionState/useFormStatus/useOptimistic, and easy revalidatePath/revalidateTag after.
  • Reading data for your own UI? Often you need neither—fetch directly in a Server Component (await db…). Skip the network hop.
  • Something external must call you over HTTP? Use a Route Handler: webhooks, OAuth/payment callbacks, public REST/JSON APIs, mobile/native clients, file downloads/uploads, RSS/sitemaps, and streaming responses (SSE, AI token streams).
  • You need precise HTTP control (custom status codes, Cache-Control, content negotiation, CORS): Route Handler.

In short: Server Actions for your app's own mutations; Route Handlers when the caller isn't your React app or when you need true HTTP control. They coexist happily—most apps use both.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description route.ts App Router API file; exports functions named after HTTP verbs GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE Uppercase named exports; missing verbs → automatic 405 Request/Response Web-standard primitives every handler is built on NextRequest Request + nextUrl, typed cookies, helpers NextResponse Response + json, redirect, rewrite, cookie mutation params (async) Dynamic segments; a Promise in Next 15 — await params nextUrl.searchParams Read query params (URLSearchParams); forces dynamic request.json() / formData() / text() Parse the body — read once only cookies() / headers() Async request APIs (Next 15); reading them forces dynamic Caching default GET is uncached by default in Next 15 (cached in 14) dynamic / revalidate Segment config to opt into static caching / ISR for a handler CORS Set headers + handle OPTIONS preflight (per-route or in middleware) Streaming Return a ReadableStream for SSE / AI token streams Webhooks Verify signature against the raw request.text() before parsing Server Action vs Route Handler Internal RPC for your UI vs a real HTTP endpoint for external callers

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js Docs — Route Handlers
  • Next.js Docs — Route Segment Config
  • Next.js Docs — Caching
  • Next.js Docs — Server Actions & Mutations
  • MDN — Request · MDN — Response · MDN — ReadableStream
  • Stripe — Verifying webhook signatures

docs.title: Styling & Fonts
docs.text:
The styling options in Next.js are the same ones you already know from React — CSS Modules, global CSS, Sass, Tailwind, and CSS-in-JS — but the App Router changes the rules. Because components render on the server by default (React Server Components), anything that depends on a browser runtime has to declare itself, and a runtime CSS-in-JS library can't just be dropped in. On the upside, Next.js builds in things you'd otherwise wire up by hand: first-class CSS Modules and Sass support, a Tailwind-ready PostCSS pipeline, and next/font, which optimizes web fonts and eliminates font-driven layout shift automatically.

This chapter assumes you've read the React Styling Techniques chapter — the fundamentals of CSS Modules, Tailwind, and CSS-in-JS carry over unchanged. Here we focus on the Next.js 15 App Router angle: where each approach plugs into the framework, what the server/client boundary means for styling, and how to load fonts the Next.js way.

📚 Stack: Next.js 15, App Router, TypeScript. Scaffold with npx create-next-app@latest my-app --typescript --app.

docs.title: 1. CSS in Next.js
docs.text: Next.js has a built-in CSS pipeline (powered by PostCSS and Lightning CSS / its bundler), so .css and .module.css files just work — no loader configuration. There are three flavors: global CSS, CSS Modules, and Sass.

docs.title: Global CSS
docs.text:
Global stylesheets apply everywhere and aren't scoped. Use them for resets, CSS custom properties (design tokens), base typography, and body-level rules. In the App Router, import your global CSS once into the root layout (app/layout.tsx):

// app/layout.tsx
import "./globals.css";

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

/* app/globals.css */
:root {
  --color-primary: #2563eb;
  --color-text: #111827;
  --radius: 6px;
}

* {
  box-sizing: border-box;
  margin: 0;
}

body {
  font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;
  color: var(--color-text);
}

Where global CSS can be imported

In the App Router you can import a global (non-module) .css file from any layout, page, or component — not just the root layout, unlike the old Pages Router which restricted global imports to pages/_app.tsx. That said, importing it in the root layout is the convention: it guarantees the styles load on every route and keeps the import in one obvious place.

docs.title: CSS Modules
docs.text:
A CSS Module is a regular CSS file named *.module.css whose class names are locally scoped — at build time each class is hashed to a unique name so it can never collide. This is the React behavior you already know, and it's enabled out of the box in Next.js. Crucially, CSS Modules work in Server Components: they're resolved entirely at build time and ship zero JavaScript, so there's no "use client" requirement.

/* app/components/Button.module.css */
.btn {
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: var(--radius);
  cursor: pointer;
}
.primary {
  background: var(--color-primary);
  color: #fff;
}

// app/components/Button.tsx — a Server Component, no "use client" needed
import styles from "./Button.module.css";
import clsx from "clsx";

export default function Button({
  variant = "primary",
  children,
}: {
  variant?: "primary" | "ghost";
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return <button className={clsx(styles.btn, styles[variant])}>{children}</button>;
}

CSS Modules are the safe default in the App Router

Because they're zero-runtime and server-compatible, CSS Modules are the lowest-friction "real CSS" option in Next.js. You get full CSS power (pseudo-classes, media queries, keyframes), guaranteed scope isolation, and no impact on the client bundle. The tiny clsx helper handles conditional classes, exactly as in plain React.

docs.title: Sass
docs.text:
Next.js supports .scss/.sass natively — install sass and the framework wires up the loader. Both global Sass and Sass Modules (*.module.scss) are supported.

npm install --save-dev sass

// app/components/Card.module.scss
$radius: 8px;

.card {
  border: 1px solid #e5e7eb;
  border-radius: $radius;
  padding: 16px;

  &__title {
    font-weight: 700;
  }

  &--featured {
    border-color: var(--color-primary);
  }
}

// app/components/Card.tsx
import styles from "./Card.module.scss";

export default function Card({ title }: { title: string }) {
  return (
    <div className={styles.card}>
      <h3 className={styles.__title}>{title}</h3>
    </div>
  );
}

You can configure Sass globally via next.config.ts — for example to register load paths so @use can resolve shared partials without long relative paths:

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";
import path from "node:path";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  sassOptions: {
    includePaths: [path.join(process.cwd(), "styles")],
  },
};

export default nextConfig;

Modern Sass syntax

Dart Sass deprecated @import in favor of the module system (@use / @forward). New projects should reach for @use "variables" as vars; rather than @import. The deprecation warnings you may see come from Sass itself, not Next.js.

docs.title: 2. Tailwind CSS
docs.text:
Tailwind CSS is the most common styling choice for new Next.js apps — create-next-app even offers to set it up for you. It's utility-first: you compose small classes (flex, pt-4, bg-blue-600) in your markup, and the build emits only the CSS you actually use. Tailwind is zero-runtime and works in Server Components without "use client", which makes it a natural fit for the App Router.

The setup differs by major version. Tailwind v4 (the current default) is CSS-first and configured through a PostCSS plugin; v3.4 uses the older tailwind.config.js + directives model.

Tailwind v4 (recommended)Tailwind v3.4

Install Tailwind and its PostCSS plugin:

npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss

Register the plugin in PostCSS. Next.js auto-detects postcss.config.mjs:

// postcss.config.mjs
const config = {
  plugins: {
    "@tailwindcss/postcss": {},
  },
};

export default config;

A single import replaces the old @tailwind base/components/utilities directives:

/* app/globals.css */
@import "tailwindcss";

That's it — there's no JS config file and no content array by default; v4 auto-detects your source files. Import globals.css in the root layout (as in §1) and you're done.

The v3 flow uses three dev dependencies and an init step:

npm install -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init -p

Point the content array at your App Router source so the JIT engine knows where to scan for classes:

// tailwind.config.js
/** @type {import('tailwindcss').Config} */
export default {
  content: [
    "./app/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}",
    "./components/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}",
  ],
  theme: { extend: {} },
  plugins: [],
};

/* app/globals.css */
@tailwind base;
@tailwind components;
@tailwind utilities;

v4 changed the PostCSS package

In Tailwind v3 the PostCSS plugin was the tailwindcss package itself. In v4 the PostCSS integration moved to a separate @tailwindcss/postcss package, and the v3 tailwind.config.js is replaced by CSS-based configuration. Mixing the two (e.g. v4's import with a v3 PostCSS setup) is a common source of "Tailwind classes aren't applying" bugs. (Tailwind v4 also ships a first-party Vite plugin, but in a Next.js project you use the PostCSS plugin, since Next.js owns the bundler.)

docs.title: Using utilities and customizing the theme
docs.text:
Utilities, responsive prefixes (md:), and state prefixes (hover:, focus:, dark:) behave exactly as in any Tailwind project:

// app/components/Hero.tsx — a Server Component
export default function Hero() {
  return (
    <section className="mx-auto max-w-2xl p-8 text-center">
      <h1 className="mb-2 text-3xl font-bold text-gray-900 dark:text-white">
        Build fast
      </h1>
      <button className="mt-4 rounded-md bg-blue-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-700">
        Get started
      </button>
    </section>
  );
}

In v4, you customize design tokens in CSS with @theme; in v3 you extend tailwind.config.js:

v4 — CSS @themev3 — tailwind.config.js 
@import "tailwindcss";

@theme {
  --color-brand: #2563eb;
  --font-display: "Inter", sans-serif;
}

These tokens generate utilities automatically (bg-brand, font-display).

export default {
  content: ["./app/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}"],
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: { brand: "#2563eb" },
      fontFamily: { display: ["Inter", "sans-serif"] },
    },
  },
  plugins: [],
};

For conditional and merge-aware class composition, the clsx + tailwind-merge pairing is standard (see the React chapter). And as you'll see in §5, Tailwind utilities combine perfectly with next/font CSS variables.

docs.title: 3. CSS-in-JS with the App Router
docs.text:
This is where Next.js diverges most sharply from a plain React app. Runtime CSS-in-JS libraries like styled-components and Emotion rely on React Context and generate styles in the browser — both of which assume a client runtime. In the App Router, components are Server Components by default, and Server Components have no client runtime, no Context, and no useState. So a runtime CSS-in-JS library cannot be used directly in a Server Component.

Two things are needed to make it work:

  1. The components that use CSS-in-JS must be Client Components ("use client").
  2. A style registry must collect the styles rendered on the server and inject them into the initial HTML, so the first paint isn't unstyled. Next.js exposes the useServerInsertedHTML hook for exactly this.

docs.title: The style registry pattern (styled-components)
docs.text:
npm install styled-components

Create a client-side registry component. It buffers the styles styled-components generates during SSR and flushes them into <head> via useServerInsertedHTML:

// app/lib/styled-components-registry.tsx
"use client";

import { useState } from "react";
import { useServerInsertedHTML } from "next/navigation";
import { ServerStyleSheet, StyleSheetManager } from "styled-components";

export default function StyledComponentsRegistry({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  // Create the stylesheet only once, lazily, per render.
  const [styledComponentsStyleSheet] = useState(() => new ServerStyleSheet());

  useServerInsertedHTML(() => {
    const styles = styledComponentsStyleSheet.getStyleElement();
    styledComponentsStyleSheet.instance.clearTag();
    return <>{styles}</>;
  });

  // On the client, render children as-is (the browser takes over).
  if (typeof window !== "undefined") return <>{children}</>;

  return (
    <StyleSheetManager sheet={styledComponentsStyleSheet.instance}>
      {children}
    </StyleSheetManager>
  );
}

Wrap the app with the registry in the root layout. The registry is a Client Component, but it can wrap Server Component children — the boundary stays at the registry:

// app/layout.tsx
import StyledComponentsRegistry from "./lib/styled-components-registry";

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <StyledComponentsRegistry>{children}</StyledComponentsRegistry>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

You'll also want SWC's styled-components transform on for stable class names and SSR correctness:

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  compiler: {
    styledComponents: true,
  },
};

export default nextConfig;

Now any Client Component can use styled-components normally:

// app/components/FancyButton.tsx
"use client";

import styled from "styled-components";

const Button = styled.button<{ $primary?: boolean }>`
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  border: none;
  cursor: pointer;
  background: ${(p) => (p.$primary ? "#2563eb" : "#e5e7eb")};
  color: ${(p) => (p.$primary ? "white" : "#111827")};
`;

export default function FancyButton() {
  return <Button $primary>Click me</Button>;
}

Emotion follows the same shape

Emotion needs an equivalent registry built on its @emotion/cache + CacheProvider, again flushed through useServerInsertedHTML. The mechanics differ but the pattern is identical: a "use client" registry that injects server-collected styles. As with styled-components, every component that calls Emotion's css prop or styled API must itself be a Client Component.

RSC limitations — CSS-in-JS opts you out of the server

The catch is fundamental, not cosmetic: runtime CSS-in-JS does not work in Server Components, so adopting it pushes a "use client" boundary across your styled UI and ships those components (plus the library runtime) to the browser. That undercuts a core App Router benefit — shipping less JavaScript. There's also a per-render serialization cost in the browser, the same one called out in the React chapter.

Prefer zero-runtime styling in the App Router

For new App Router projects, the Next.js docs (and the broader ecosystem) recommend zero-runtime styling: CSS Modules, Tailwind, or a compile-time CSS-in-JS library that emits static CSS — Pigment CSS, vanilla-extract, or Linaria. These work in Server Components, ship no styling runtime, and keep the client bundle small. Reach for styled-components / Emotion mainly when you're migrating an existing app or already standardized on them (e.g. a heavy MUI codebase).

docs.title: 4. Fonts with next/font
docs.text:
Custom web fonts are a classic source of two problems: a network request to Google's servers (a privacy and latency cost) and layout shift when the web font swaps in for the fallback (hurting CLS). The built-in next/font module solves both.

What next/font does for you

  • Self-hosts the font — at build time Google Fonts files are downloaded and served from your own deployment. No request ever goes to fonts.googleapis.com at runtime (better privacy, no extra DNS/connection round-trips).
  • Zero layout shift — it computes the font's metrics and applies a matched size-adjust on the fallback, so the swap doesn't reflow text (improving CLS).
  • Self-optimizes — fonts are preloaded and scoped automatically; no manual <link rel="preload">.

docs.title: Google fonts
docs.text:
Import the font from next/font/google, call it as a function, and apply the generated className. Do this in a layout (commonly the root) so it's loaded once:

// app/layout.tsx
import { Inter } from "next/font/google";
import "./globals.css";

const inter = Inter({
  subsets: ["latin"],
  display: "swap",
});

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en" className={inter.className}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

Variable fonts need no weight array

Inter is a variable font, so you don't list weights — the whole axis ships in one file. For a non-variable font you must specify the weights (and styles) you use, which keeps the download small:

import { Roboto } from "next/font/google";

const roboto = Roboto({
  weight: ["400", "700"],
  style: ["normal", "italic"],
  subsets: ["latin"],
  display: "swap",
});

docs.title: CSS variables (and pairing fonts with Tailwind)
docs.text:
Instead of (or alongside) className, expose the font as a CSS custom property with the variable option. This lets you reference it from CSS or wire it into a Tailwind theme — and use multiple fonts cleanly (e.g. a body font and a display font):

// app/layout.tsx
import { Inter, Roboto_Mono } from "next/font/google";

const inter = Inter({
  subsets: ["latin"],
  variable: "--font-sans",
  display: "swap",
});

const robotoMono = Roboto_Mono({
  subsets: ["latin"],
  variable: "--font-mono",
  display: "swap",
});

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en" className={`${inter.variable} ${robotoMono.variable}`}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

Now --font-sans and --font-mono are available everywhere. Reference them in CSS:

/* app/globals.css */
body {
  font-family: var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif;
}
code,
pre {
  font-family: var(--font-mono), monospace;
}

Or register them in Tailwind so they drive font-sans / font-mono utilities:

Tailwind v4 (@theme)Tailwind v3 (config) 
@import "tailwindcss";

@theme {
  --font-sans: var(--font-sans);
  --font-mono: var(--font-mono);
}

export default {
  content: ["./app/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx,mdx}"],
  theme: {
    extend: {
      fontFamily: {
        sans: ["var(--font-sans)"],
        mono: ["var(--font-mono)"],
      },
    },
  },
};

docs.title: Local fonts
docs.text:
For fonts you ship yourself (brand fonts, licensed files), use next/font/local and point at the files in your project. The same optimization and zero-shift benefits apply:

// app/layout.tsx
import localFont from "next/font/local";

const brand = localFont({
  src: [
    { path: "./fonts/Brand-Regular.woff2", weight: "400", style: "normal" },
    { path: "./fonts/Brand-Bold.woff2", weight: "700", style: "normal" },
  ],
  variable: "--font-brand",
  display: "swap",
});

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  return (
    <html lang="en" className={brand.variable}>
      <body>{children}</body>
    </html>
  );
}

Call the font loader at module scope

A next/font loader must be called in the top-level module scope with literal arguments — not inside a component, a loop, or with values computed at runtime. Next.js evaluates these calls at build time to download and inline the font; a dynamic call can't be analyzed and will error. Define each font once per module (typically in a layout) and reuse its className/variable.

docs.title: Choosing an approach
docs.text:
Approach Works in Server Components? Runtime cost Notes for Next.js Global CSS ✅ None Resets, tokens; import once in the root layout CSS Modules ✅ None Scoped, zero-runtime — the safe default Sass / Sass Modules ✅ None Install sass; global or *.module.scss Tailwind CSS ✅ None (build-time JIT) v4 uses @tailwindcss/postcss + @import "tailwindcss" styled-components / Emotion ❌ (client-only) Medium (runtime) Needs a "use client" registry; opts components out of RSC Pigment / vanilla-extract / Linaria ✅ None (compiled) Zero-runtime CSS-in-JS; recommended if you want the css ergonomics

TL;DR for the App Router

  • Default to zero-runtime: CSS Modules, Sass Modules, or Tailwind — all work in Server Components and ship no styling JS.
  • Want CSS-in-JS ergonomics? Prefer a compile-time library (Pigment CSS, vanilla-extract) over runtime styled-components/Emotion.
  • Migrating an existing styled-components/Emotion app? Use the style registry pattern and accept the "use client" boundary.
  • For fonts, always use next/font — self-hosting, preloading, and zero layout shift come for free; expose a variable to combine fonts and feed Tailwind.

docs.title: 📚 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js — CSS
  • Next.js — Sass
  • Next.js — Tailwind CSS
  • Next.js — CSS-in-JS
  • Next.js — Font Optimization (next/font)
  • Tailwind CSS — Next.js install guide
  • Pigment CSS · vanilla-extract

✅ Best Practice: Reach for the zero-runtime options first — CSS Modules, Sass, or Tailwind — since they run in Server Components and keep your client bundle lean. Only adopt runtime CSS-in-JS behind a style registry when a project already depends on it, and prefer compile-time CSS-in-JS otherwise. Load every web font through next/font so self-hosting, preloading, and zero layout shift are handled for you.

docs.title: Optimizations (Image, Metadata/SEO, Scripts)
docs.text:
Next.js ships a set of built-in optimizations that you'd otherwise wire up by hand: an image pipeline that resizes and reformats on demand, a typed Metadata API that produces correct SEO and social-share tags, file conventions for sitemaps and robots.txt, controlled loading of third-party scripts, and code-splitting via lazy components. The throughline is the same one from the rendering chapter—ship less to the client, and ship it at the right moment—because that is what moves Core Web Vitals.

This chapter is App Router-first, targeting Next.js 15 with TypeScript. Everything here is the framework's default toolkit; you reach for these on essentially every production app.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing

docs.title: 1. Images with next/image
docs.text: <img> is easy to get wrong: oversized files, no responsive variants, no modern formats, and layout shift while the image loads. The next/image component fixes all of those by optimizing images on demand—resizing to the requested dimensions, serving modern formats (AVIF/WebP), lazy-loading off-screen images, and reserving space to prevent layout shift.

docs.title: Basic usage with a local import
docs.text:
For images in your project, import them. Next.js reads the intrinsic width/height at build time, so it can reserve the correct space automatically (no width/height props needed) and even generate a blur placeholder.

// app/page.tsx
import Image from "next/image";
import hero from "@/public/hero.jpg"; // a real file in /public or anywhere importable

export default function Home() {
  return (
    <Image
      src={hero}
      alt="A mountain range at sunrise"
      placeholder="blur" // auto blur-up using a generated low-res preview
      // width/height inferred from the imported file
    />
  );
}

For remote images (a URL string), Next.js can't know the dimensions, so you must provide width and height (the intrinsic aspect ratio, not a CSS size) or use fill:

<Image
  src="https://cdn.example.com/photos/123.jpg"
  alt="Product photo"
  width={800}
  height={600} // sets the aspect ratio; CSS can still scale it responsively
/>

width/height are the aspect ratio, not the display size

The width and height props tell Next.js the image's proportions so it can reserve space and avoid layout shift. Control the rendered size with CSS (e.g. className="w-full h-auto"). Setting width={800} does not force the image to render at 800px.

docs.title: Responsive images and sizes
docs.text:
By default next/image is lazy and generates a srcset of candidate widths. The browser picks the right one—but only if you tell it how wide the image will be on screen via the sizes prop. Without sizes, a full-width responsive image may download a desktop-sized file on a phone.

import Image from "next/image";
import banner from "@/public/banner.jpg";

export default function ResponsiveBanner() {
  return (
    <Image
      src={banner}
      alt="Seasonal sale banner"
      // The image is full-viewport on mobile, half on tablet, a third on desktop.
      sizes="(max-width: 640px) 100vw, (max-width: 1024px) 50vw, 33vw"
      className="w-full h-auto"
    />
  );
}

sizes is a media-query list read left to right; the first match wins, and the final value is the fallback. Next.js uses it to generate an appropriate srcset so the browser fetches the smallest sufficient variant.

sizes is mandatory whenever you use fill or a fluid width

If the image scales with the viewport (most responsive layouts), set sizes. The default (100vw) is correct for true full-bleed images but wasteful for anything narrower.

docs.title: fill for unknown-dimension containers
docs.text:
When you don't know the intrinsic size—or you want the image to cover a styled box (hero sections, card thumbnails, art-directed crops)—use fill. The image absolutely positions itself to fill the nearest positioned ancestor, so the parent must be position: relative (or absolute/fixed) and have a size.

import Image from "next/image";

export default function Card() {
  return (
    <div className="relative aspect-video w-full overflow-hidden rounded-lg">
      <Image
        src="https://cdn.example.com/cover.jpg"
        alt="Article cover"
        fill
        sizes="(max-width: 768px) 100vw, 50vw"
        className="object-cover" // control cropping with object-fit
      />
    </div>
  );
}

With fill, omit width/height and use object-fit (object-cover/object-contain) plus object-position to control cropping.

docs.title: priority for the LCP image
docs.text:
Off-screen images lazy-load, which is what you want—except for the largest image above the fold, which is usually your Largest Contentful Paint element. Mark it priority to preload it (and skip lazy-loading), improving LCP.

<Image src={hero} alt="..." priority sizes="100vw" />

Use priority sparingly

priority adds a <link rel="preload">, competing for early bandwidth. Apply it to the one LCP image per page (typically the hero), not to every image. Next.js warns in dev if a large above-the-fold image lacks priority.

docs.title: Remote images and next.config
docs.text:
By design, Next.js will only optimize remote images from hosts you explicitly allow—otherwise your image endpoint could be abused as an open proxy. Configure allowed hosts with remotePatterns:

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  images: {
    remotePatterns: [
      {
        protocol: "https",
        hostname: "cdn.example.com",
        pathname: "/photos/**", // optional path scoping
      },
      {
        protocol: "https",
        hostname: "*.supabase.co", // wildcard subdomains are allowed in hostname
      },
    ],
    // Opt into format negotiation; AVIF is smaller but slower to encode.
    formats: ["image/avif", "image/webp"],
  },
};

export default nextConfig;

remotePatterns replaced domains

Older guides use images.domains: [...]. That option is deprecated in favor of remotePatterns, which is more precise (protocol, port, pathname) and therefore safer. Prefer remotePatterns in new code.

docs.title: Modern formats and quality
docs.text:
Next.js negotiates the best format the browser accepts. With formats: ["image/avif", "image/webp"], modern browsers get AVIF (smallest), others fall back to WebP, then the original. Tune compression per-image with quality (default 75):

<Image src={hero} alt="..." quality={90} priority sizes="100vw" />

When to skip optimization

For SVGs, tiny icons, or images you don't control the pipeline for, you can pass unoptimized (per-image or globally). On a fully static export (output: "export") the default optimizer isn't available—use unoptimized or a custom loader pointing at an external CDN.

docs.title: 2. The Metadata API
docs.text: Correct <head> tags drive SEO and social-share previews. Instead of manually rendering <title>/<meta> (and fighting duplicates across nested layouts), the App Router gives you a typed Metadata API: export a metadata object or a generateMetadata function from a layout.tsx or page.tsx, and Next.js renders the tags for you—deduplicated and merged down the route tree.

docs.title: Static metadata
docs.text:
For tags known at build time, export a metadata object typed as Metadata:

// app/about/page.tsx
import type { Metadata } from "next";

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "About Us",
  description: "Who we are and what we build.",
  keywords: ["company", "team", "mission"],
  alternates: { canonical: "/about" },
};

export default function AboutPage() {
  return <h1>About Us</h1>;
}

Metadata merges down the tree: a root layout sets site-wide defaults, and each page overrides or extends them. The title field supports a template so child titles get a consistent suffix:

// app/layout.tsx
import type { Metadata } from "next";

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: {
    default: "Acme Inc", // used when a page sets no title
    template: "%s · Acme Inc", // "About Us" → "About Us · Acme Inc"
  },
  description: "Tools for building on the web.",
};

docs.title: metadataBase for absolute URLs
docs.text:
Open Graph and Twitter card URLs must be absolute when scrapers fetch them. Set metadataBase once (usually in the root layout) and then use relative paths everywhere—Next.js resolves them against the base.

// app/layout.tsx
export const metadata: Metadata = {
  metadataBase: new URL(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "https://acme.example.com"
  ),
  openGraph: { images: ["/og.png"] }, // resolved to https://acme.example.com/og.png
};

Forgetting metadataBase

Without metadataBase, relative OG/Twitter image paths resolve to localhost in dev and may be dropped or broken in production. Set it once at the root; everything below inherits it.

docs.title: Open Graph and Twitter cards
docs.text:
Social previews come from openGraph and twitter fields. Provide a title, description, and a 1200×630 image for rich link previews:

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx (excerpt)
export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: "Shipping faster with the App Router",
  openGraph: {
    title: "Shipping faster with the App Router",
    description: "How we cut our build times in half.",
    url: "/blog/app-router-speed",
    siteName: "Acme Blog",
    type: "article",
    images: [
      {
        url: "/blog/app-router-speed/og.png", // resolved via metadataBase
        width: 1200,
        height: 630,
        alt: "App Router performance",
      },
    ],
  },
  twitter: {
    card: "summary_large_image",
    title: "Shipping faster with the App Router",
    description: "How we cut our build times in half.",
    images: ["/blog/app-router-speed/og.png"],
  },
};

docs.title: Dynamic metadata with generateMetadata
docs.text:
When tags depend on data (a blog post's title, a product's name), export an async generateMetadata function instead of a static object. It receives the same params/searchParams as the page.

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import { notFound } from "next/navigation";

type Props = { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> };

export async function generateMetadata({ params }: Props): Promise<Metadata> {
  const { slug } = await params; // Next.js 15: params is a Promise
  const post = await getPost(slug);
  if (!post) return {}; // page itself will handle notFound()

  return {
    title: post.title,
    description: post.excerpt,
    openGraph: {
      title: post.title,
      description: post.excerpt,
      type: "article",
      publishedTime: post.date,
      images: [{ url: post.coverImage, width: 1200, height: 630 }],
    },
  };
}

export default async function BlogPost({ params }: Props) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const post = await getPost(slug);
  if (!post) notFound();
  return <article>{/* ... */}</article>;
}

Data fetches are deduplicated

Calling getPost(slug) in both generateMetadata and the page looks wasteful, but fetch requests are memoized per request, so the same call runs once. For non-fetch data, wrap the loader in React's cache() to get the same deduplication.

metadata and "use client" don't mix

The Metadata API only works in Server Components. You can't export metadata/generateMetadata from a file marked "use client". Keep metadata in the page.tsx/layout.tsx (which stay server components) and push interactivity into child client components.

docs.title: 3. File-based metadata
docs.text: Some metadata is better expressed as files than as exports. Next.js recognizes special filenames in app/ and turns them into the right routes and tags. These can be static assets or dynamic .ts files that generate content.

docs.title: sitemap.ts
docs.text:
A sitemap.ts (or .xml) in app/ produces /sitemap.xml. Export a default function returning a typed MetadataRoute.Sitemap:

// app/sitemap.ts
import type { MetadataRoute } from "next";

export default async function sitemap(): Promise<MetadataRoute.Sitemap> {
  const baseUrl = "https://acme.example.com";

  // Dynamic entries from your data source
  const posts = await getAllPosts();
  const postEntries: MetadataRoute.Sitemap = posts.map((post) => ({
    url: `${baseUrl}/blog/${post.slug}`,
    lastModified: post.updatedAt,
    changeFrequency: "weekly",
    priority: 0.7,
  }));

  return [
    { url: baseUrl, lastModified: new Date(), changeFrequency: "daily", priority: 1 },
    { url: `${baseUrl}/about`, changeFrequency: "monthly", priority: 0.5 },
    ...postEntries,
  ];
}

docs.title: robots.ts
docs.text:
Similarly, robots.ts generates /robots.txt:

// app/robots.ts
import type { MetadataRoute } from "next";

export default function robots(): MetadataRoute.Robots {
  return {
    rules: [
      { userAgent: "*", allow: "/", disallow: ["/admin", "/api"] },
    ],
    sitemap: "https://acme.example.com/sitemap.xml",
  };
}

docs.title: opengraph-image (and twitter-image)
docs.text:
Drop an opengraph-image.png (or .jpg) into any route segment and Next.js wires up the correct og:image meta tags automatically—no manual URLs. Even better, generate the image dynamically with opengraph-image.tsx using next/og's ImageResponse, which renders JSX to a PNG at the edge:

// app/blog/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx
import { ImageResponse } from "next/og";

export const runtime = "edge";
export const alt = "Blog post cover";
export const size = { width: 1200, height: 630 };
export const contentType = "image/png";

export default async function Image({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) {
  const post = await getPost(params.slug);

  return new ImageResponse(
    (
      <div
        style={{
          width: "100%",
          height: "100%",
          display: "flex",
          flexDirection: "column",
          justifyContent: "center",
          padding: 80,
          background: "linear-gradient(135deg, #0f172a, #1e293b)",
          color: "white",
          fontSize: 64,
        }}
      >
        <div style={{ fontSize: 28, opacity: 0.7 }}>Acme Blog</div>
        <div style={{ fontWeight: 700 }}>{post?.title ?? "Untitled"}</div>
      </div>
    ),
    { ...size }
  );
}

Other file conventions

The same pattern covers favicon.ico, icon.png/icon.tsx, apple-icon.png, and manifest.ts (a web app manifest). Place them in app/; Next.js emits the matching <link>/<meta> tags. A static opengraph-image.png in a segment is the zero-code option; the .tsx version is for per-page, data-driven cards.

docs.title: 4. Scripts with next/script
docs.text:
Third-party scripts (analytics, chat widgets, payment SDKs) are a common performance drag. The next/script component lets you load them with an explicit strategy so they don't block your page from becoming interactive, and it deduplicates a script even if multiple components request it.

// app/layout.tsx
import Script from "next/script";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        {children}

        {/* Analytics: load once the page is idle, never blocking interactivity */}
        <Script
          src="https://example.com/analytics.js"
          strategy="afterInteractive"
        />
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

The strategy prop controls when the script loads:

Strategy When it loads Use for beforeInteractive Before any hydration, injected into the initial HTML Critical scripts needed before the page runs (rare): bot detection, consent managers. Must live in the root layout. afterInteractive (default) Right after the page becomes interactive Most third-party scripts: analytics, tag managers. lazyOnload During browser idle time, after everything else Low-priority widgets: chat, social embeds, support tools. worker (experimental) In a web worker via Partytown Offloading heavy third-party JS off the main thread.

You can also run inline code and react to load events:

<Script id="show-banner" strategy="afterInteractive">
  {`window.dataLayer = window.dataLayer || []; dataLayer.push({ ready: true });`}
</Script>

<Script
  src="https://maps.example.com/sdk.js"
  strategy="lazyOnload"
  onLoad={() => console.log("Maps SDK ready")} // requires "use client" parent
  onError={(e) => console.error("Maps SDK failed", e)}
/>

Inline scripts need an id

Give inline <Script> blocks a unique id so Next.js can track and deduplicate them. onLoad/onError/onReady are client callbacks, so the file using them must be a Client Component.

docs.title: 5. Lazy loading with next/dynamic
docs.text:
Code-splitting keeps the initial bundle small by loading heavy components only when needed. next/dynamic is a wrapper around React.lazy + Suspense tailored to Next.js. Use it for components that are large, rarely shown, or below the fold (charts, rich editors, modals, maps).

// app/dashboard/page.tsx
import dynamic from "next/dynamic";

// HeavyChart and its dependencies are split into a separate chunk,
// fetched only when this component actually renders.
const HeavyChart = dynamic(() => import("@/components/HeavyChart"), {
  loading: () => <p>Loading chart…</p>, // shown while the chunk downloads
});

export default function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <section>
      <h1>Dashboard</h1>
      <HeavyChart />
    </section>
  );
}

docs.title: Disabling SSR with ssr: false
docs.text:
Some components only work in the browser—they touch window, use a non-SSR-safe library, or are purely interactive. Skip server rendering with ssr: false so they render only on the client (avoiding hydration mismatches and server-side crashes).

"use client";
import dynamic from "next/dynamic";

// A map widget that reads `window` and isn't SSR-safe.
const Map = dynamic(() => import("@/components/Map"), {
  ssr: false,
  loading: () => <div className="h-96 animate-pulse bg-gray-200" />,
});

export default function LocationPicker() {
  return <Map />;
}

ssr: false requires a Client Component in Next.js 15

In Next.js 15, calling dynamic(..., { ssr: false }) inside a Server Component is not allowed—it throws a build error. Move the dynamic() call into a file marked "use client" (as above). Without ssr: false, next/dynamic works fine in Server Components.

Server Components don't need next/dynamic to split code

RSCs are already streamed and code-split by the framework. Reach for next/dynamic mainly to lazy-load Client Components, to attach a loading fallback, or to opt out of SSR. For deferring server work, prefer <Suspense> with streaming (see the rendering chapter).

docs.title: 6. Performance: bundle analysis & Core Web Vitals
docs.text: Optimization is measurement-driven. Two habits matter most: knowing what's in your JavaScript bundle, and watching the field metrics Google ranks on.

docs.title: Bundle analysis with @next/bundle-analyzer
docs.text:
The analyzer produces an interactive treemap of your bundles, exposing the heavy dependencies worth lazy-loading, replacing, or removing.

npm install --save-dev @next/bundle-analyzer

// next.config.ts
import type { NextConfig } from "next";
import bundleAnalyzer from "@next/bundle-analyzer";

const withBundleAnalyzer = bundleAnalyzer({
  enabled: process.env.ANALYZE === "true",
});

const nextConfig: NextConfig = {
  // ...your config
};

export default withBundleAnalyzer(nextConfig);

# Build with analysis enabled; opens treemaps in your browser
ANALYZE=true npm run build

What to look for

A few common wins: a large date/utility library imported wholesale (switch to per-function imports or a lighter alternative), an editor/charting lib loaded eagerly (lazy-load it), duplicated dependencies across chunks, and big client components that could be Server Components. The treemap makes the offenders obvious.

docs.title: Core Web Vitals (including INP)
docs.text:
The three metrics Google reports as Core Web Vitals:

  • LCP — Largest Contentful Paint (loading): time to render the largest above-the-fold element. Levers: next/image with priority on the LCP image, SSR/SSG so real content paints early, next/font to avoid font-swap delays.
  • INP — Interaction to Next Paint (responsiveness): replaced FID as a Core Web Vital in March 2024. It measures the latency of all interactions across the visit, capturing slow event handlers and long tasks—not just the first input. Levers: ship less client JS (Server Components), code-split with next/dynamic, and avoid expensive synchronous work in event handlers.
  • CLS — Cumulative Layout Shift (visual stability): how much the layout jumps. Levers: always size images/embeds (next/image reserves space), reserve space for ads/widgets, and use next/font to prevent font-driven reflow.

Why INP rewards the App Router

INP is dominated by main-thread work and hydration cost. Because Server Components send zero JavaScript for non-interactive UI, an App Router app naturally ships a smaller client bundle—directly improving INP. Pushing "use client" to the smallest leaves and lazy-loading heavy interactive widgets compounds the win.

docs.title: Measuring vitals in your app
docs.text:
For lab data, use Lighthouse (Chrome DevTools) and next build's per-route bundle report. For real-user (field) data, report vitals from a Client Component with useReportWebVitals, then send them to your analytics endpoint:

// app/_components/web-vitals.tsx
"use client";
import { useReportWebVitals } from "next/web-vitals";

export function WebVitals() {
  useReportWebVitals((metric) => {
    // metric.name is "LCP" | "INP" | "CLS" | "FCP" | "TTFB"
    navigator.sendBeacon("/api/vitals", JSON.stringify(metric));
  });
  return null;
}

// app/layout.tsx (mount it once)
import { WebVitals } from "./_components/web-vitals";

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <WebVitals />
        {children}
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

On Vercel, use Speed Insights

If you deploy to Vercel, @vercel/speed-insights collects field Core Web Vitals (including INP) with one component—no custom endpoint needed. Either way, optimize against field data: lab scores don't always reflect what real users on real devices experience.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
