        <button onClick={decrementAsync}>DecrementAsync</button>
        {/* direct use 2 */}
        <button onClick={resetCount} className="text-red-400">Reset Count</button>
      </div>
    </>
  );
};

export default App;

docs.title: 4. Selectors & Optimization
docs.text: Efficient state selection and optimization are key to Zustand’s performance. Unlike Context API, Zustand only re-renders components that use the part of the state that actually changes.

docs.title: 🔹 Selecting Specific State Slices
docs.text:
Zustand lets you “select” only the part of the state your component needs. This reduces unnecessary re-renders and improves performance.

Example usage:

  • const count = useCounterStore((state) => state.count);
  • const increase = useCounterStore((state) => state.increase);

💡 Best Practice: Always select specific slices of state instead of the entire store.

docs.title: 🔹 Avoiding Unnecessary Re-renders
docs.text:
If you destructure the entire store like this:

const { count, increase, decrease } = useCounterStore();

The component will re-render whenever any property in the store changes.

✅ Instead, subscribe only to what’s needed:

const count = useCounterStore((state) => state.count);

This ensures that your component only re-renders when count changes.

docs.title: 🔹 Using shallow Comparison for Optimization
docs.text:
When selecting multiple values, Zustand provides a shallow comparison helper to prevent re-renders when selected values haven’t actually changed.

Zustand v5 changed this API

Passing shallow as the second argument to the store hook was removed in Zustand v5. Instead, wrap your selector with useShallow imported from zustand/react/shallow.

You import the helper like this:

import { useShallow } from 'zustand/react/shallow';

Then use it like:

const { count, increase } = useCounterStore(
  useShallow((state) => ({ count: state.count, increase: state.increase }))
);

This ensures re-renders only happen if either count or increase changes — not when other parts of the store update.

docs.title: 5. Middlewares
docs.text: Zustand provides powerful middlewares that extend its functionality — such as persistence, debugging, and immutable state updates. You can easily combine multiple middlewares for more advanced setups.

docs.title: 🔹 persist — Saving State to Storage
docs.text:
The persist middleware allows your store to automatically save and rehydrate state from localStorage, sessionStorage, or custom storage.

Use Case: Keeping user preferences, cart data, or session info persistent between page reloads.

Key Points:

  • Import from zustand/middleware
  • Wrap your store definition with persist
  • Define a unique key for storage

Features:

  • Works with both sync and async storages
  • Allows migration, versioning, and partial persistence
  • Can blacklist or whitelist certain parts of the state

docs.title: 🔹 devtools — Debugging with Redux DevTools
docs.text:
The devtools middleware enables time-travel debugging and inspection via the popular Redux DevTools browser extension.

Use Case: Monitoring and debugging state changes easily during development.

Key Points:

  • Import from zustand/middleware
  • Wrap your store with devtools
  • Add a name for your store in the DevTools tab

Features:

  • Shows every action and its payload
  • Allows stepping through past state changes
  • Works seamlessly with other middlewares like persist

💡 Tip: Use devtools only in development mode to avoid performance overhead in production.

docs.title: 🔹 immer — Simplifying Immutable Updates
docs.text:
Zustand supports using Immer to simplify complex state mutations while keeping immutability intact.

Use Case: When your state has deeply nested objects and you want cleaner update logic.

Key Points:

  • Import from zustand/middleware/immer
  • Write “mutable-looking” code that produces immutable updates under the hood

Example Concept: Instead of:

set((state) => ({
  user: { ...state.user, profile: { ...state.user.profile, name: "John" } }
}));

You can write:

set((state) => {
  state.user.profile.name = "John";
});

docs.title: 🔹 Combining persist, devtools, and immer
docs.text:
Middlewares compose by wrapping. A common order is devtools(persist(immer(...))):

import { create } from "zustand";
import { devtools, persist } from "zustand/middleware";
import { immer } from "zustand/middleware/immer";

type CartState = {
  items: { id: string; qty: number }[];
  addItem: (id: string) => void;
  clear: () => void;
};

export const useCartStore = create<CartState>()(
  devtools(
    persist(
      immer((set) => ({
        items: [],
        // immer lets us "mutate" draft state directly
        addItem: (id) =>
          set((state) => {
            const existing = state.items.find((i) => i.id === id);
            if (existing) existing.qty += 1;
            else state.items.push({ id, qty: 1 });
          }),
        clear: () =>
          set((state) => {
            state.items = [];
          }),
      })),
      { name: "cart-storage" } // persist: localStorage key
    ),
    { name: "CartStore" } // devtools: label in Redux DevTools
  )
);

docs.title: 🔹 create()
docs.text:
• Core function that creates a Zustand store.
  • Returns a custom hook you can use anywhere in React.
  • Example: const useStore = create((set) => ({ count: 0 }))

docs.title: 🔹 set()
docs.text:
• Function provided by Zustand to update the store.
  • Works similar to React’s setState(), but at a global level.

docs.title: 🔹 Async Actions
docs.text: You can define async actions directly in the store — no need for extra middleware like Redux Thunk.

docs.title: 7. Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep the store minimal — only include truly shared or global state.
  • Group related logic into separate stores (e.g., authStore, cartStore).
  • Use selectors (like (state) => state.value) for performance.
  • Avoid destructuring the entire store to prevent re-renders.
  • Use TypeScript interfaces or types to define store structure clearly.
  • Access store outside React only when required (e.g., debugging, utility functions).

docs.title: 📦 1. Context API and useContext Hook
docs.text:
✅ What is Context API? React Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without having to pass props manually at every level. It’s useful for global data like themes, authentication, or user preferences.

✅ When to Use Context API

  • When multiple components need access to the same state.
  • When props are deeply nested and cause prop drilling.
  • For app-wide settings (theme, locale, auth, etc.)
import React, { createContext, useContext, useState, useMemo } from 'react';

const ThemeContext = createContext();

✅ Providing and Consuming Context

✅ Optimizing Context with useMemo

const ThemeProvider = ({ children }) => {
  const [darkMode, setDarkMode] = useState(false);

  const toggleTheme = () => setDarkMode(prev => !prev);

  // Memoize the value so consumers don't re-render unnecessarily
  const value = useMemo(() => ({ darkMode, toggleTheme }), [darkMode]);

  return <ThemeContext.Provider value={value}>{children}</ThemeContext.Provider>;
};

✅ Using useContext Hook

const ThemeToggle = () => {
  const { darkMode, toggleTheme } = useContext(ThemeContext);
  return (
    <div>
      <p>Current theme: {darkMode ? 'Dark' : 'Light'}</p>
      <button onClick={toggleTheme}>Toggle Theme</button>
    </div>
  );
};

const HomePage = () => {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider>
      <h2>Welcome to the App</h2>
      <ThemeToggle />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
};

docs.title: 🔄 2. When to Use Context API vs Other Solutions
docs.text:
• Small-Scale vs Large-Scale Applications

    • Context is great for small-to-medium apps.
    • For large apps with deeply interdependent state, use Redux or Zustand.

  • When Context API is Sufficient

    • User authentication
    • Theme switching
    • Language/localization

  • When to Use Redux or Others

    • Complex business logic
    • Large team with modular state slices
    • Time-travel debugging or middleware

  • Performance Considerations

    • Context causes re-render for all consumers when the value changes.
    • Optimize with useMemo or split context into smaller providers.

docs.title: 🔃 3. Prop Drilling vs Context API
docs.text:
• What is Prop Drilling?

    • Passing props through multiple layers just to reach a deeply nested component.

  • How Context API Solves It

    • Use context to provide values to deeply nested components without passing props manually.

  • Alternatives to Context API

    • Component Composition
    • Render Props
    • State management libraries

  • Best Practices

    • Avoid large monolithic contexts
    • Split concerns: e.g. AuthContext, ThemeContext, etc.
    • Memoize context values to prevent unnecessary re-renders

docs.title: React Styling Techniques
docs.text:
React is unopinionated about how you style your components — it just renders DOM. That freedom means there are many valid approaches, from plain CSS files to runtime CSS-in-JS to utility-first frameworks to full component libraries. This chapter walks through each major option with modern, runnable examples (React 18/19 functional components), and ends with a decision table to help you pick.

📚 The examples assume a Vite + React project. Create one with npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts.

docs.title: Inline Styles
docs.text:
In React, inline styles are passed as a JavaScript object to the style prop. Property names are camelCased (backgroundColor, not background-color), and values are usually strings. Numeric values for length properties get px appended automatically.

function Badge() {
  return (
    <span
      style={{
        backgroundColor: '#2563eb',
        color: 'white',
        padding: '4px 8px',
        borderRadius: 6,        // becomes 6px
        fontWeight: 600,
      }}
    >
      New
    </span>
  );
}

docs.title: Handling Dynamic Styles
docs.text:
Because the style is just an object, you can compute it from props or state — this is where inline styles shine.

function ProgressBar({ percent }) {
  return (
    <div style={{ width: '100%', background: '#e5e7eb', borderRadius: 8 }}>
      <div
        style={{
          width: `${percent}%`,
          height: 8,
          background: percent < 30 ? '#dc2626' : '#16a34a',
          borderRadius: 8,
          transition: 'width 0.3s ease',
        }}
      />
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: Pros and Cons
docs.text:
Inline styles at a glance

Pros: scoped to the element, trivially dynamic, no extra build tooling, great for one-off computed values.

Cons: no pseudo-classes (:hover, :focus), no media queries, no keyframes, no @supports. Styles can't be cached or reused, and re-creating the object on every render can hurt performance in hot paths. Use inline styles for dynamic values, not as your primary styling strategy.

docs.title: External CSS Stylesheets
docs.text:
The simplest "real" approach: write a .css file and import it. With Vite (or CRA), the bundler injects it into the page.

/* Button.css */
.btn {
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 6px;
  cursor: pointer;
}
.btn--primary {
  background: #2563eb;
  color: #fff;
}

import './Button.css';

function Button({ children }) {
  return <button className="btn btn--primary">{children}</button>;
}

Global scope

Plain CSS imports are global. A class named .btn defined in one file applies everywhere on the page. As projects grow, name collisions become a real risk — which is exactly why naming conventions and CSS Modules exist.

docs.title: Structuring CSS for Large Projects
docs.text:
A common pattern is to colocate a component's CSS with its component file, plus a few global files:

src/
├── styles/
│   ├── reset.css        # normalize/reset
│   ├── variables.css    # CSS custom properties (design tokens)
│   └── global.css       # base typography, body, etc.
└── components/
    ├── Button/
    │   ├── Button.jsx
    │   └── Button.css
    └── Card/
        ├── Card.jsx
        └── Card.css

Using CSS custom properties as design tokens keeps things consistent:

/* variables.css */
:root {
  --color-primary: #2563eb;
  --color-text: #111827;
  --space-2: 8px;
  --radius: 6px;
}

docs.title: Naming Conventions (BEM, SMACSS)
docs.text:
BEM (Block, Element, Modifier) is the most popular convention. It encodes the component structure into the class name so styles stay flat and predictable:

.block            /* the component         → .card        */
.block__element   /* a part of it          → .card__title */
.block--modifier  /* a variant/state       → .card--featured */

.card { border: 1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius: var(--radius); }
.card__title { font-weight: 700; }
.card__body { color: #4b5563; }
.card--featured { border-color: var(--color-primary); }

function Card({ featured, title, children }) {
  return (
    <div className={`card ${featured ? 'card--featured' : ''}`}>
      <h3 className="card__title">{title}</h3>
      <div className="card__body">{children}</div>
    </div>
  );
}

SMACSS (Scalable and Modular Architecture for CSS) is less about naming and more about categorizing rules into five buckets — Base, Layout (l-), Module, State (is-active, is-hidden), and Theme. It pairs well with BEM rather than competing with it.

Tip

Conventions like BEM are a discipline, not a tool — nothing enforces them. If you want the compiler to guarantee no collisions, reach for CSS Modules.

docs.title: What Are CSS Modules?
docs.text: A CSS Module is a regular CSS file whose class names are locally scoped by default. At build time, each class is renamed to a unique hash (e.g. .btn → .Button_btn__a1b2c), so it can never collide with a class of the same name elsewhere. Vite supports them out of the box — just name the file *.module.css.

docs.title: Using CSS Modules in React
docs.text:
/* Button.module.css */
.btn {
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border-radius: 6px;
}
.primary {
  background: #2563eb;
  color: white;
}

import styles from './Button.module.css';

function Button({ children }) {
  // styles.btn is the hashed, locally-scoped class string
  return <button className={`${styles.btn} ${styles.primary}`}>{children}</button>;
}

For conditional classes, the tiny clsx library is the standard helper:

import clsx from 'clsx';
import styles from './Button.module.css';

function Button({ variant = 'primary', disabled, children }) {
  return (
    <button
      className={clsx(styles.btn, styles[variant], { [styles.disabled]: disabled })}
      disabled={disabled}
    >
      {children}
    </button>
  );
}

docs.title: Scope Isolation and Benefits
docs.text:
Why CSS Modules?

  • Guaranteed isolation — class names are hashed, so no global leakage and no naming wars.
  • Plain CSS — full power of CSS (pseudo-classes, media queries, keyframes) with zero new syntax to learn.
  • Dead-code friendly — unused classes are easy to spot since they're imported by name.
  • Zero runtime — it's all resolved at build time, so there's no performance cost at render.

They're an excellent default when you like writing real CSS but want safety. Composition is supported via the composes keyword.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: styled-components lets you write actual CSS inside template literals and attach it to a component. Each styled component generates a unique class name, so styles are automatically scoped. It's the archetypal CSS-in-JS library.

docs.title: Installing and Setting Up
docs.text:
npm install styled-components

For TypeScript, the types ship with the package as of v6 (no separate @types package needed).

docs.title: Writing Styled Components
docs.text:
import styled from 'styled-components';

const Button = styled.button`
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border: none;
  border-radius: 6px;
  background: #2563eb;
  color: white;
  cursor: pointer;

  &:hover {
    background: #1d4ed8;
  }
`;

function App() {
  return <Button>Click me</Button>;
}

The & refers to the generated class itself — so nesting, pseudo-classes, and media queries all work as in Sass.

docs.title: Using Props to Modify Styles Dynamically
docs.text:
Interpolated functions receive the component's props, letting styles react to data.

v6 transient props

In styled-components v6, props used only for styling should be prefixed with $ (transient props). This stops them from leaking onto the underlying DOM node as invalid HTML attributes.

import styled, { css } from 'styled-components';

const Button = styled.button<{ $variant?: 'primary' | 'ghost'; $size?: 'sm' | 'md' }>`
  border-radius: 6px;
  cursor: pointer;
  padding: ${(props) => (props.$size === 'sm' ? '4px 10px' : '8px 16px')};

  ${(props) =>
    props.$variant === 'ghost'
      ? css`
          background: transparent;
          color: #2563eb;
          border: 1px solid #2563eb;
        `
      : css`
          background: #2563eb;
          color: white;
          border: none;
        `}
`;

function App() {
  return (
    <>
      <Button $variant="primary">Primary</Button>
      <Button $variant="ghost" $size="sm">Ghost</Button>
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: Theming with Styled Components
docs.text:
Wrap your app in a ThemeProvider and the theme becomes available in every styled component's interpolations.

import styled, { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components';

const theme = {
  colors: { primary: '#2563eb', text: '#111827' },
  radius: '6px',
};

const Card = styled.div`
  color: ${(props) => props.theme.colors.text};
  border: 1px solid ${(props) => props.theme.colors.primary};
  border-radius: ${(props) => props.theme.radius};
  padding: 16px;
`;

function App() {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      <Card>Themed card</Card>
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
}

Type the theme

In TypeScript, augment the library's DefaultTheme so props.theme is fully typed: 

// styled.d.ts
import 'styled-components';
declare module 'styled-components' {
  export interface DefaultTheme {
    colors: { primary: string; text: string };
    radius: string;
  }
}

docs.title: Global Styles
docs.text:
Use createGlobalStyle for resets and base styles. Render it once near the root.

import { createGlobalStyle } from 'styled-components';

const GlobalStyle = createGlobalStyle`
  * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; }
  body {
    font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;
    color: ${(props) => props.theme.colors.text};
  }
`;

function Root() {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      <GlobalStyle />
      <App />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
Styled-components best practices

  • Define styled components at module scope, never inside render — defining them in render recreates the component on every render, breaking memoization and causing DOM remounts.
  • Use transient props ($) for styling-only props (v6).
  • Keep a single theme source of truth and type it.
  • Use the Babel/SWC plugin for better debugging class names and dead-code elimination.
  • Be aware of the runtime cost: styles are computed in the browser. For very large or render-heavy apps, a zero-runtime option (CSS Modules, Tailwind, or a compile-time CSS-in-JS lib) may perform better.

docs.title: What Is Tailwind CSS?
docs.text: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first framework. Instead of writing custom CSS, you compose small single-purpose classes (flex, pt-4, text-center, bg-blue-600) directly in your markup. It generates only the CSS you actually use, so the final bundle stays tiny.

docs.title: Installing Tailwind in a Vite Project
docs.text:
Tailwind's setup differs by major version. Both are shown below.

Tailwind v4 (recommended)Tailwind v3.4+

v4 ships a first-party Vite plugin and removes the JS config file in favor of CSS-based configuration.

npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/vite

// vite.config.js
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
import tailwindcss from '@tailwindcss/vite';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react(), tailwindcss()],
});

/* src/index.css — a single import replaces the old @tailwind directives */
@import "tailwindcss";

npm install -D tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer
npx tailwindcss init -p

// tailwind.config.js — content tells Tailwind where to scan for classes
export default {
  content: ['./index.html', './src/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'],
  theme: { extend: {} },
  plugins: [],
};

/* src/index.css */
@tailwind base;
@tailwind components;
@tailwind utilities;

Make sure your CSS entry file is imported in main.jsx:

import './index.css';

docs.title: Using Utility Classes for Styling
docs.text:
function Card({ title, children }) {
  return (
    <div className="max-w-sm rounded-lg border border-gray-200 p-4 shadow-sm">
      <h3 className="mb-2 text-lg font-bold text-gray-900">{title}</h3>
      <p className="text-gray-600">{children}</p>
      <button className="mt-4 rounded-md bg-blue-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-700">
        Action
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}

Responsive design and states are prefixes: md:flex, hover:bg-blue-700, dark:bg-gray-800, focus:ring-2.

For conditional classes, combine clsx with tailwind-merge (which resolves conflicting utilities like p-2 p-4):

import { twMerge } from 'tailwind-merge';
import clsx from 'clsx';

const cn = (...inputs) => twMerge(clsx(inputs));

function Button({ active, className, children }) {
  return (
    <button className={cn('rounded px-4 py-2', active && 'bg-blue-600 text-white', className)}>
      {children}
    </button>
  );
}

docs.title: Customizing Tailwind Configurations
Performance: The Built-in JIT Engine
docs.text:
PurgeCSS is no longer needed

Modern Tailwind (v3.4+ and v4) uses a built-in JIT (Just-In-Time) engine. It scans your source files and generates only the classes you actually use, on demand. There is no separate purge step and no purge config — the old PurgeCSS workflow from Tailwind v1/v2 is obsolete. Production bundles are typically just a few kilobytes of CSS. In v3 you point the engine at your files via the content array; in v4 detection is largely automatic.

docs.title: Tailwind vs Traditional CSS
docs.text:
Trade-offs

In favor: rapid development, no context-switching to a .css file, no naming decisions, consistent design tokens, tiny production CSS, styles colocated with markup.

Against: verbose className strings ("class soup"), a learning curve for the utility vocabulary, and markup that mixes structure with styling. Extract repeated patterns into components (not @apply-heavy CSS) to keep it manageable.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Emotion is a flexible, high-performance CSS-in-JS library. It offers two APIs: a styled API nearly identical to styled-components (@emotion/styled), and a css prop API (@emotion/react) that lets you attach styles inline without creating a named component. Emotion is the styling engine under the hood of MUI v5/v6.

docs.title: Setting Up Emotion in React
docs.text:
npm install @emotion/react @emotion/styled

To use the css prop with the automatic JSX runtime in a Vite project, add the Emotion preset to the React plugin:

// vite.config.js
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react({ jsxImportSource: '@emotion/react' })],
});

docs.title: Writing CSS with Emotion
docs.text:
The css prop — define styles inline as an object or template literal:

/** @jsxImportSource @emotion/react */
import { css } from '@emotion/react';

const card = css`
  padding: 16px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  border: 1px solid #e5e7eb;
  &:hover { border-color: #2563eb; }
`;

function Card() {
  return <div css={card}>Hello from Emotion</div>;
}

The styled API — identical ergonomics to styled-components:

import styled from '@emotion/styled';

const Button = styled.button<{ primary?: boolean }>`
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  background: ${(props) => (props.primary ? '#2563eb' : '#e5e7eb')};
  color: ${(props) => (props.primary ? 'white' : '#111827')};
`;

docs.title: Theming with Emotion
docs.text:
import { ThemeProvider, css } from '@emotion/react';

const theme = { colors: { primary: '#2563eb' } };

function Title() {
  return (
    <h1 css={(t) => css`color: ${t.colors.primary};`}>Themed</h1>
  );
}

function App() {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      <Title />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
}

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
Emotion performance

  • Emotion caches serialized styles, so repeated identical styles are computed once.
  • Like all runtime CSS-in-JS, there is a per-render cost; define static styles outside the component to avoid re-serializing them every render.
  • For SSR, Emotion provides extraction utilities so critical CSS ships in the initial HTML.
  • Emotion vs styled-components: very similar feature sets. Emotion's css prop is more flexible (no need to name every styled element) and it's the default engine for MUI — making it a natural choice in MUI-based projects.

docs.title: 5. Bootstrap & Material UI
docs.text: Sometimes you don't want to build a design system from scratch — you want ready-made, accessible components. That's what UI libraries provide.

docs.title: Installing and Using Bootstrap in React
docs.text:
Don't use plain Bootstrap's jQuery plugins in React. Use React Bootstrap, which reimplements Bootstrap components as real React components.

npm install react-bootstrap bootstrap

// import the stylesheet once, e.g. in main.jsx
import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css';

import { Button, Card } from 'react-bootstrap';

function Profile() {
  return (
    <Card style={{ width: '18rem' }}>
      <Card.Body>
        <Card.Title>Jane Doe</Card.Title>
        <Card.Text>Frontend developer.</Card.Text>
        <Button variant="primary">Follow</Button>
      </Card.Body>
    </Card>
  );
}

docs.title: Using Material UI Components
docs.text:
Material UI (MUI) is a comprehensive React component library implementing Google's Material Design. The current major versions are v5/v6, both built on Emotion by default.

npm install @mui/material @emotion/react @emotion/styled

import { Button, Card, CardContent, Typography } from '@mui/material';

function Profile() {
  return (
    <Card sx={{ maxWidth: 320 }}>
      <CardContent>
        <Typography variant="h6">Jane Doe</Typography>
        <Typography color="text.secondary">Frontend developer.</Typography>
        <Button variant="contained" sx={{ mt: 2 }}>Follow</Button>
      </CardContent>
    </Card>
  );
}

The sx prop is MUI's shorthand styling system — it accepts theme-aware values (mt: 2 → 16px from the spacing scale) and is the idiomatic way to do one-off overrides.

docs.title: Customizing Themes
docs.text:
MUI uses a central theme via ThemeProvider and createTheme:

import { createTheme, ThemeProvider } from '@mui/material/styles';

const theme = createTheme({
  palette: {
    mode: 'light',
    primary: { main: '#2563eb' },
  },
  shape: { borderRadius: 8 },
});

function App() {
  return (
    <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
      {/* every MUI component now reads this theme */}
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
}

Bootstrap is customized either by overriding CSS variables (Bootstrap 5 exposes many --bs-* custom properties) or by recompiling its Sass with your own variable values before importing.

docs.title: Performance Considerations for UI Libraries
docs.text:
Bundle size

Component libraries add weight. To keep bundles lean:

  • Import only what you use — import Button from '@mui/material/Button' (named imports from the package root are tree-shaken in modern bundlers, but path imports are the safest guarantee).
  • With Bootstrap, import individual components from react-bootstrap rather than the whole library.
  • MUI's runtime CSS-in-JS (Emotion) has a render cost; for static-heavy pages consider its zero-runtime Pigment CSS option (used in MUI's newer offerings) or extract critical CSS for SSR.

docs.title: When to Use a Component Library vs Custom Styling
docs.text:
Decision guidance

Reach for a component library (MUI, Bootstrap) when:

  • You need to ship fast (dashboards, internal tools, MVPs, admin panels).
  • You want accessibility, keyboard support, and complex widgets (date pickers, autocomplete, data grids) for free.
  • A consistent, conventional look is acceptable or desired.

Prefer custom styling (CSS Modules, Tailwind, CSS-in-JS) when:

  • You have a bespoke / branded design that would fight a library's defaults.
  • Bundle size and full control matter more than speed of delivery.
  • You want to build your own design system.

A common hybrid: a headless library (Radix UI, Headless UI, React Aria) for behavior + accessibility, styled with Tailwind for full visual control. This gives you the best of both worlds.

docs.title: Comparison & Decision Table
docs.text: Approach Type Scope isolation Dynamic styles Runtime cost Best for Inline styles JS object Per-element ✅ Excellent Low (object churn) One-off computed values, quick prototypes External CSS + BEM Plain CSS ❌ Global (by convention) ⚠️ via class toggling None Simple sites, full CSS power, no build extras CSS Modules Compiled CSS ✅ Auto (hashed) ⚠️ via class toggling None Real CSS with guaranteed safety — a great default styled-components CSS-in-JS ✅ Auto ✅ Excellent (props/theme) Medium (runtime) Component-scoped dynamic styling, theming Emotion CSS-in-JS ✅ Auto ✅ Excellent Medium (cached) Same as above; css prop flexibility, MUI projects Tailwind CSS Utility classes ✅ (atomic) ⚠️ via conditional classes None (build-time JIT) Rapid dev, design consistency, tiny CSS bundle Bootstrap / MUI Component library ✅ ✅ (sx, props, theme) Medium–High Fast delivery, accessibility, prebuilt widgets

docs.title: Quick recommendations
docs.text:
TL;DR

  • Just starting / want plain CSS safely → CSS Modules.
  • Move fast with a consistent design + small bundle → Tailwind CSS.
  • Heavily dynamic, theme-driven component styling → styled-components or Emotion.
  • Need accessible, prebuilt components yesterday → MUI (or React Bootstrap).
  • Bespoke design + accessibility → headless components + Tailwind.

There's no single "right" answer — most production apps mix two or three of these (e.g. Tailwind for layout + MUI for complex widgets). Choose based on team familiarity, design requirements, and performance budget.

docs.title: 📚 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• CSS Modules
  • styled-components docs
  • Tailwind CSS docs
  • Emotion docs
  • Material UI docs
  • React Bootstrap docs

✅ Best Practice: Pick one primary styling strategy per project and stay consistent. Use inline styles only for genuinely dynamic values, scope everything (CSS Modules, CSS-in-JS, or atomic utilities) to avoid global leakage, and lean on a component library when accessibility and speed outweigh the need for a fully custom look.

docs.title: React SSR & SSG
docs.text:
Rendering strategy decides where and when your HTML is generated: in the browser, on a server at request time, or ahead of time at build. Server-Side Rendering (SSR) and Static Site Generation (SSG) move work off the user's device, producing real HTML that ships fast and is fully crawlable—two things plain client-side React struggles with.

This chapter starts with the concepts (CSR / SSR / SSG / ISR), then goes deep on Next.js, which is the dominant React framework for all of these. The modern App Router (Next.js 13.4+, default in 14/15) is presented as the primary approach, with the legacy Pages Router APIs covered afterward as the older model you'll still meet in existing codebases.

📚 For more: https://nextjs.org/docs · https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components

docs.title: 1. Introduction to SSR, SSG, CSR & ISR
docs.text: A React app is ultimately HTML + JavaScript. The rendering strategy is the answer to: who turns my components into HTML, and at what moment?

docs.title: CSR — Client-Side Rendering
docs.text:
The browser downloads a near-empty HTML shell plus a JavaScript bundle, then React builds the DOM on the user's device. This is the classic SPA model (Vite + React, Create React App).

<!-- What a crawler / slow device first receives with CSR -->
<div id="root"></div>
<script src="/bundle.js"></script>
  • ✅ Cheap to host (static files + an API), great for highly interactive, auth-gated dashboards where SEO is irrelevant.
  • ❌ Blank screen until JS downloads, parses, and runs; weaker SEO; performance scales with the user's device.

docs.title: SSR — Server-Side Rendering
docs.text:
The server runs your React components on every request and returns fully-formed HTML. The browser shows content immediately, then React hydrates it to make it interactive.

  • ✅ Personalized, always-fresh content (per-user dashboards, request-time data); good SEO.
  • ❌ Server compute on every request → higher cost and latency than serving a static file; needs a running server/serverless runtime.

docs.title: SSG — Static Site Generation
docs.text:
HTML is generated once at build time and served from a CDN. Every visitor gets the same pre-built file.

  • ✅ Fastest possible delivery (a file on a CDN), cheapest hosting, excellent SEO.
  • ❌ Data is frozen at build time; rebuilding a huge site can be slow.

docs.title: ISR — Incremental Static Regeneration
docs.text:
SSG's pragmatic upgrade: pages are static but regenerate in the background on a schedule (or on demand) without a full rebuild. You get CDN speed with data that stays reasonably fresh—ideal for blogs, docs, and product catalogs that change occasionally.

These are per-route, not per-app

In modern Next.js you don't pick one strategy for the whole site. Each route chooses its own rendering mode, and you can even mix static and dynamic regions inside a single page using streaming. Think of CSR/SSR/SSG/ISR as a spectrum, not four separate buckets.

docs.title: Side-by-side
docs.text: Strategy HTML generated Data freshness Hosting cost Best for CSR In the browser Live (client fetch) Lowest (static + API) Auth-only apps, internal dashboards SSR Per request on the server Always fresh Higher (compute/request) Personalized, request-time data SSG Once at build Frozen at build Lowest (CDN) Marketing, docs, rarely-changing content ISR At build, refreshed in background Stale-then-fresh Low (CDN + occasional compute) Blogs, catalogs, content that changes sometimes

docs.title: SEO, performance & Core Web Vitals
docs.text:
The whole reason these strategies exist is that what the server sends first matters.

  • SEO — Crawlers do execute JavaScript, but rendering it is slower, sometimes deferred, and unreliable for social-share/Open Graph previews. Pre-rendered HTML (SSR/SSG/ISR) gives crawlers complete content immediately.
  • Performance — Pre-rendered HTML produces meaningful content before any app JS runs, and SSG/ISR can be cached at the CDN edge.
  • Core Web Vitals — The metrics Google measures map directly onto rendering choices:
    • LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): SSR/SSG paint real content fast; CSR delays it behind the JS bundle.
    • INP (Interaction to Next Paint, the metric that replaced FID in 2024): hurt by large hydration bundles—shipping less client JS (see Server Components) helps directly.
    • CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): pre-rendering exact markup, including sized images, avoids reflow.

Less JavaScript is the throughline

Across SEO and Core Web Vitals, the recurring win is shipping less JavaScript to the client. React Server Components (below) make that the default rather than an optimization you bolt on later.

docs.title: Improved SEO with pre-rendering
docs.text: Search engines and social scrapers receive the actual <h1>, copy, and <meta>/Open Graph tags in the initial response—no JS execution required. This is the single biggest reason content-driven sites adopt SSR/SSG.

docs.title: Faster initial page load
docs.text: The user sees real content as soon as the HTML arrives, instead of staring at a spinner while a bundle downloads and executes. This improves perceived performance and LCP, especially on slow networks and low-end devices.

docs.title: Caching & performance optimization
docs.text: Static and ISR pages live on a CDN as plain files—served from an edge location near the user, infinitely scalable, and cheap. SSR responses can also be cached (full-page or fragment) to absorb traffic spikes.

docs.title: Reduced client-side JavaScript
docs.text:
With React Server Components, components that only render data never ship to the browser at all. Only genuinely interactive ("use client") components are hydrated. Less JS → faster parse/execute → better INP and lower bandwidth.

SSR is not automatically faster end-to-end

SSR adds server work on every request and a hydration step on the client. A poorly-cached SSR page with a heavy data fetch can have a worse TTFB than CSR. SSR wins on first contentful paint and SEO, not necessarily on total cost. Prefer SSG/ISR whenever the data doesn't truly need to be per-request.

docs.title: 3. The modern approach: Next.js App Router
What is Next.js?
docs.text: Next.js is a React framework (by Vercel) that provides file-system routing, server rendering, data fetching, bundling, image optimization, and serverless deployment out of the box. As of v13.4 the App Router (the app/ directory) is stable and is the default and recommended model in Next.js 14 and 15. It is built on React Server Components (RSC).

docs.title: Setting up a project
docs.text:
npx create-next-app@latest my-app --typescript --app
cd my-app
npm run dev

This scaffolds the app/ directory with TypeScript. Key conventions:

File Purpose app/layout.tsx Shared shell (required root layout, wraps <html>/<body>) app/page.tsx UI for the / route app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx Dynamic route /blog/:slug app/loading.tsx Streaming fallback shown while a segment loads app/error.tsx Error boundary for a segment ("use client") app/api/.../route.ts A Route Handler (API endpoint)

docs.title: Server Components are the default
docs.text:
In the App Router, every component is a Server Component unless you opt out. Server Components run only on the server, can be async, can await data directly, and send zero JavaScript to the browser.

// app/page.tsx — a Server Component (no "use client" directive)
// Runs on the server. The DB call, the secret, and this code never reach the browser.
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export default async function HomePage() {
  const posts = await db.post.findMany({ take: 10 }); // direct data access

  return (
    <main>
      <h1>Latest posts</h1>
      <ul>
        {posts.map((p) => (
          <li key={p.id}>{p.title}</li>
        ))}
      </ul>
    </main>
  );
}

There's no getServerSideProps here—fetching is just await inside the component. This is the headline difference from the Pages Router.

docs.title: Data fetching & caching with fetch
docs.text:
Next.js extends the native fetch with caching controls. The cache option you choose is what makes a route SSR vs SSG vs ISR.

// SSR: re-fetch on every request (dynamic, request-time data)
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/feed", { cache: "no-store" });

// SSG: fetched once at build and cached indefinitely (static)
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/page", { cache: "force-cache" });

// ISR: cached, but revalidated in the background at most every 60 seconds
const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/products", {
  next: { revalidate: 60 },
});

Caching defaults changed in Next.js 15

In Next.js 14, fetch was cached by default. In Next.js 15, the defaults flipped to uncached (fetch and route segments are dynamic unless you opt in), which is far less surprising. Always set cache/revalidate explicitly rather than relying on the version's default. You can also tag fetches with next: { tags: ["products"] } for targeted on-demand invalidation.

docs.title: The "use client" boundary
docs.text:
When a component needs interactivity—state, effects, event handlers, browser-only APIs—mark it with "use client" at the top of the file. Everything imported into a client component becomes part of the client bundle.

// app/components/LikeButton.tsx
"use client";
import { useState } from "react";

export default function LikeButton({ initial }: { initial: number }) {
  const [likes, setLikes] = useState(initial);
  return <button onClick={() => setLikes((n) => n + 1)}>♥ {likes}</button>;
}

A Server Component can render a Client Component and pass it serializable props (and even pass Server Components as children):

// Server Component composing a Client Component
import LikeButton from "./components/LikeButton";

export default async function Post({ id }: { id: string }) {
  const post = await getPost(id); // server-only
  return (
    <article>
      <h2>{post.title}</h2>
      <LikeButton initial={post.likes} /> {/* interactive island */}
    </article>
  );
}

Server/client boundary rules

  • You cannot pass functions, class instances, or Dates-as-behaviour from a Server Component to a Client Component—only serializable props.
  • You cannot use useState/useEffect/onClick/window in a Server Component.
  • You cannot import a server-only module (DB client, fs, secrets) into a Client Component. Mark such modules with import "server-only" so an accidental import fails the build instead of leaking to the browser.
  • Push "use client" down the tree to the smallest interactive leaf. Marking a layout "use client" drags its whole subtree into the client bundle.

docs.title: Streaming with <Suspense> and loading.tsx
docs.text:
The App Router can stream HTML: send the shell immediately and flush slower parts as they resolve. Wrap a slow Server Component in <Suspense> to show a fallback without blocking the rest of the page.

// app/dashboard/page.tsx
import { Suspense } from "react";
import Stats from "./Stats";        // slow async Server Component
import Sidebar from "./Sidebar";    // fast

export default function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <div>
      <Sidebar />
      <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading stats…</p>}>
        <Stats /> {/* streamed in when its data resolves */}
      </Suspense>
    </div>
  );
}

Adding an app/<segment>/loading.tsx file gives the whole route segment an instant loading UI automatically (it wraps the segment in a Suspense boundary for you).

// app/dashboard/loading.tsx
export default function Loading() {
  return <p>Loading dashboard…</p>;
}

docs.title: 4. SSG & ISR with the App Router
Static generation by default
docs.text: If a route has no dynamic data sources (no cookies(), headers(), no-store fetches, or dynamic functions), Next.js statically generates it at build time automatically. No special export needed.

docs.title: generateStaticParams for dynamic SSG
docs.text:
To statically pre-render a set of dynamic routes (e.g. one page per blog post), export generateStaticParams. It's the App Router replacement for getStaticPaths.

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx

// 1. Tell Next.js which slugs to pre-render at build time
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const posts = await fetch("https://api.example.com/posts").then((r) => r.json());
  return posts.map((post: { slug: string }) => ({ slug: post.slug }));
}

// 2. Render each one (this Server Component runs per slug at build)
export default async function BlogPost({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // Next.js 15: params is async
  const post = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts/${slug}`).then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{post.title}</h1>
      <p>{post.body}</p>
    </article>
  );
}

params and searchParams are async in Next.js 15

In Next.js 15, params, searchParams, cookies(), and headers() are Promises and must be awaited (or read with use()). In Next.js 14 they were synchronous objects. The examples here use the v15 async form.

docs.title: Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)
docs.text:
Add a segment-level revalidate (or a per-fetch revalidate) to make a static page refresh in the background.

// app/products/page.tsx
export const revalidate = 3600; // regenerate at most once per hour

export default async function Products() {
  const products = await fetch("https://api.example.com/products").then((r) =>
    r.json()
  );
  return <ProductList products={products} />;
}

How it works: the first request after the window serves the existing (stale) page instantly, then triggers a background regeneration so the next visitor gets fresh HTML—the stale-while-revalidate pattern.

For unknown slugs, dynamicParams (default true) lets routes not listed in generateStaticParams be generated on first request and then cached—on-demand ISR.

docs.title: On-demand revalidation
docs.text:
Instead of (or alongside) time-based revalidation, invalidate exactly when data changes—e.g. from a CMS webhook in a Route Handler:

// app/api/revalidate/route.ts
import { revalidatePath, revalidateTag } from "next/cache";
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const { path, tag } = await request.json();
  if (path) revalidatePath(path);       // refresh a specific route
  if (tag) revalidateTag(tag);          // refresh all fetches with this tag
  return NextResponse.json({ revalidated: true });
}

docs.title: unstable_cache for non-fetch data
docs.text:
fetch caching only covers HTTP calls. To cache a direct DB query or any async function with the same revalidate/tag semantics, wrap it:

import { unstable_cache } from "next/cache";
import { db } from "@/lib/db";

export const getCachedProducts = unstable_cache(
  async () => db.product.findMany(),
  ["products-list"],                 // cache key parts
  { revalidate: 3600, tags: ["products"] }
);

docs.title: Mixing static and dynamic on one page
docs.text:
You don't have to choose per page. Render the static shell statically and stream the dynamic part inside <Suspense>—the page is cached, but the personalized region (which reads cookies()/no-store) is rendered dynamically and streamed in. This partial approach (formalized as Partial Prerendering, experimental in 14/15) is the App Router's answer to "SSG + client-side rendering for the dynamic bits."

Best practices for static sites

  • Default to static; opt into dynamic only where data is truly per-request.
  • Use revalidate for content that changes on a schedule; use tags + on-demand revalidation for content that changes on events (CMS publish).
  • Keep generateStaticParams lists bounded; use dynamicParams for the long tail.
  • Set <meta>/Open Graph via the metadata export or generateMetadata so pre-rendered pages are share- and SEO-ready.

docs.title: Route Handlers (App Router)
docs.text:
In the App Router, API endpoints are Route Handlers: a route.ts file exporting functions named after HTTP verbs. They use the Web Request/Response APIs.

// app/api/posts/route.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";

export async function GET() {
  const posts = await db.post.findMany();
  return NextResponse.json(posts);
}

export async function POST(request: Request) {
  const body = await request.json();
  const created = await db.post.create({ data: body });
  return NextResponse.json(created, { status: 201 });
}

Dynamic segments work here too—app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts receives params:

// app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts
export async function GET(
  _request: Request,
  { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }
) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const post = await db.post.findUnique({ where: { id } });
  if (!post) return new Response("Not found", { status: 404 });
  return Response.json(post);
}

Do you even need an API route?

Server Components can query your database directly, and Server Actions ("use server" functions) handle mutations from forms without a hand-written endpoint. Reserve Route Handlers for things that genuinely need an HTTP endpoint: webhooks, third-party callbacks, public APIs, or non-React clients (mobile apps).

docs.title: Serverless functions & runtimes
docs.text:
On platforms like Vercel, each Route Handler (and SSR render) is deployed as a serverless function—it spins up on demand, scales automatically, and you pay per invocation. You can pick the runtime per route:

// Run on the lightweight Edge runtime (fast cold starts, geographically distributed)
export const runtime = "edge"; // or "nodejs" (default, full Node APIs)
  • Node.js runtime — full Node APIs, database drivers, larger libraries. Default.
  • Edge runtime — a limited Web-standard environment with near-zero cold start, great for auth checks, redirects, and lightweight personalization close to the user.

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text:
middleware.ts at the project root runs before a request reaches a route—ideal for auth gating, redirects, A/B routing, and header rewriting. It runs on the Edge runtime.

// middleware.ts
import { NextResponse } from "next/server";
import type { NextRequest } from "next/server";

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const token = request.cookies.get("session")?.value;
  if (!token && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith("/dashboard")) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL("/login", request.url));
  }
  return NextResponse.next();
}

export const config = { matcher: ["/dashboard/:path*"] };

docs.title: Fetching from your own API
docs.text:
A Server Component can call your Route Handler over HTTP, but it's often simpler (and skips a network hop) to call the shared data function directly. From the client, fetch the Route Handler as you would any API:

"use client";
import { useState } from "react";

export default function NewPostForm() {
  const [title, setTitle] = useState("");
  async function submit() {
    await fetch("/api/posts", {
      method: "POST",
      headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
      body: JSON.stringify({ title }),
    });
  }
  return (
    <>
      <input value={title} onChange={(e) => setTitle(e.target.value)} />
      <button onClick={submit}>Create</button>
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: Deployment considerations
docs.text:
• Vercel is zero-config for Next.js (SSR/ISR/Route Handlers become serverless/edge functions automatically). Other hosts (Netlify, AWS, Cloudflare) have adapters; self-hosting uses next start behind Node, or output: "export" for a pure-static build (no SSR/ISR/Route Handlers).
  • Cold starts affect serverless functions; the Edge runtime mitigates this for latency-sensitive routes.
  • Secrets belong in server-only env vars; only NEXT_PUBLIC_-prefixed vars are exposed to the browser.
  • CDN caching of ISR/static output is what gives you scale—make sure cache headers and revalidation are configured intentionally.

docs.title: 6. The legacy Pages Router
docs.text:
Older model — know it, but prefer the App Router for new code

The pages/ directory predates Server Components. It's still supported and you'll find it across existing projects, but new Next.js apps should use the App Router. The data-fetching functions below do not exist in the App Router—they're replaced by async components, fetch caching, generateStaticParams, and revalidate.

docs.title: SSR with getServerSideProps
docs.text:
Runs on the server on every request; its return value becomes the page's props.

// pages/dashboard.tsx
import type { GetServerSideProps } from "next";

export const getServerSideProps: GetServerSideProps = async (context) => {
  const res = await fetch(`https://api.example.com/user/${context.params?.id}`);
  const user = await res.json();
  return { props: { user } }; // can also return { notFound: true } / { redirect } 
};

export default function Dashboard({ user }: { user: { name: string } }) {
  return <h1>Welcome, {user.name}</h1>;
}

docs.title: SSG with getStaticProps + getStaticPaths
docs.text:
getStaticProps runs at build time; getStaticPaths enumerates dynamic routes to pre-render. Adding revalidate to getStaticProps enables ISR in the Pages Router.

// pages/blog/[slug].tsx
import type { GetStaticPaths, GetStaticProps } from "next";

export const getStaticPaths: GetStaticPaths = async () => {
  const posts = await fetch("https://api.example.com/posts").then((r) => r.json());
  return {
    paths: posts.map((p: { slug: string }) => ({ params: { slug: p.slug } })),
    fallback: "blocking", // generate unlisted slugs on first request (on-demand ISR)
  };
};

export const getStaticProps: GetStaticProps = async ({ params }) => {
  const post = await fetch(
    `https://api.example.com/posts/${params?.slug}`
  ).then((r) => r.json());
  return {
    props: { post },
    revalidate: 60, // ISR: rebuild this page at most once per minute
  };
};

export default function BlogPost({ post }: { post: { title: string } }) {
  return <h1>{post.title}</h1>;
}

docs.title: API routes (pages/api)
docs.text:
A file in pages/api/ exporting a default handler becomes an endpoint—the precursor to Route Handlers.

// pages/api/hello.ts
import type { NextApiRequest, NextApiResponse } from "next";

export default function handler(req: NextApiRequest, res: NextApiResponse) {
  res.status(200).json({ message: "Hello from a serverless function" });
}

docs.title: App Router ↔ Pages Router cheat sheet
docs.text: Goal Pages Router (legacy) App Router (modern) SSR (per-request) getServerSideProps async component + fetch(..., { cache: "no-store" }) SSG (build-time) getStaticProps async component + fetch(..., { cache: "force-cache" }) (default for static routes) Dynamic SSG paths getStaticPaths generateStaticParams ISR revalidate in getStaticProps export const revalidate / per-fetch revalidate API endpoint pages/api/* (default handler) app/api/*/route.ts (verb exports) Mutations from a form API route + client fetch Server Actions ("use server")

docs.title: 7. Hydration & common pitfalls
docs.text: Hydration is the step where React attaches event listeners to the server-rendered HTML and "takes over" the static markup, turning it interactive. The DOM the client produces on first render must match what the server sent.

docs.title: Hydration mismatch
docs.text:
If the server-rendered HTML and the first client render differ, React throws a hydration mismatch and may discard and re-render that subtree. Common causes:

// ❌ Mismatch: server and client compute different values
function Clock() {
  return <span>{new Date().toLocaleTimeString()}</span>; // differs server vs client
}

// ❌ Mismatch: window/localStorage exist only on the client
function Theme() {
  return <div>{localStorage.getItem("theme")}</div>; // crashes/differs on server
}

// ✅ Render the stable value first, then update after mount
"use client";
import { useState, useEffect } from "react";

function Clock() {
  const [time, setTime] = useState<string | null>(null);
  useEffect(() => setTime(new Date().toLocaleTimeString()), []);
  return <span>{time ?? "—"}</span>; // same output on server & first client render
}

Typical mismatch triggers to watch for: Date.now()/Math.random(), reading window/localStorage during render, locale/timezone-dependent formatting, and invalid HTML nesting (e.g. a <div> inside a <p>). For unavoidable client-only content, suppressHydrationWarning (on a single element) or a mounted-flag pattern are escape hatches.

docs.title: Server/client boundary mistakes
docs.text:
• Importing a Server Component into a Client Component to render it directly (instead, pass it as children/props).
  • Adding "use client" too high in the tree, ballooning the client bundle.
  • Using useState/useEffect/event handlers in a Server Component (the build will error).
  • Leaking secrets by importing server-only modules into client code—guard with import "server-only".

docs.title: Other gotchas
docs.text:
• Forgetting cache/revalidate and being surprised by stale or always-dynamic pages (especially across the Next.js 14→15 default change).
  • Calling browser APIs at module scope in code that also runs on the server.
  • Double data fetching when you fetch in a Server Component and re-fetch the same thing on the client—lift it to the server.

docs.title: 8. Beyond Next.js (briefly)
docs.text:
Next.js dominates, but it isn't the only option:

  • Vite SSR — Vite ships a low-level SSR API (renderToString / renderToPipeableStream on the server, hydrateRoot on the client). Maximum control, but you assemble routing, data loading, and caching yourself. Good when you need a custom setup.
  • React Router v7 (framework mode) — the merger of Remix into React Router. Provides SSR, nested routes with loader/action data APIs, and streaming as a framework, as an alternative to Next.js.
  • Remix — the same lineage, web-standards-first (built on Request/Response), now largely converging into React Router v7.
  • Astro — content-focused; renders React (and other frameworks) as static HTML with selective hydration ("islands"), excellent for content sites.

The common foundation

All of these build on the same React primitives: renderToPipeableStream/renderToReadableStream (streaming SSR), hydrateRoot (client takeover), and increasingly React Server Components. Frameworks differ mainly in ergonomics—routing conventions, data-loading APIs, and caching—not in the underlying rendering mechanism.

docs.title: 📚 Summary
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description CSR Browser renders React from a JS bundle; weakest SEO/LCP, cheapest hosting SSR HTML built per request on the server; fresh + SEO-friendly, higher cost SSG HTML built once at build, served from CDN; fastest + cheapest, frozen data ISR Static pages regenerated in the background (time-based or on-demand) Hydration Client attaches interactivity to server HTML; output must match exactly "use client" Opt a component into the client bundle (state, effects, events) Server Component Default in App Router; runs server-only, async, ships zero JS fetch cache/revalidate Per-request caching that selects SSR vs SSG vs ISR generateStaticParams App Router: enumerate dynamic routes to pre-render (replaces getStaticPaths) revalidate / unstable_cache App Router ISR: time-based + non-fetch caching revalidatePath / revalidateTag On-demand cache invalidation (e.g. CMS webhooks) Route Handler (route.ts) App Router API endpoint using Web Request/Response Server Actions ("use server") Server mutations from forms without a hand-written endpoint Middleware Edge code before a route — auth, redirects, rewrites getServerSideProps Legacy Pages Router SSR getStaticProps / getStaticPaths Legacy Pages Router SSG (+ revalidate for ISR) pages/api/* Legacy Pages Router API routes

docs.title: 📌 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Next.js Docs — App Router
  • Next.js — Caching & Revalidating
  • React — Server Components
  • web.dev — Core Web Vitals
  • React Router v7 (framework mode)

docs.title: React Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)
docs.text:
A Progressive Web App is a regular web app enhanced with browser capabilities that let it behave like a native app: it can be installed to the home screen, work offline, load instantly from a cache, and receive push notifications—while still being just HTML, CSS, and JavaScript served over HTTPS.

This chapter covers what PWAs are, the three building blocks (Service Worker, Web App Manifest, and HTTPS), and how to add them to a React app. The recommended, modern path for a Vite React app is the vite-plugin-pwa plugin (which wraps Google's Workbox), so we lead with that. We also walk through the raw Service Worker API so you understand what the tooling generates under the hood.

📚 For more: web.dev/explore/progressive-web-apps · Vite PWA Docs · MDN: Progressive Web Apps

docs.title: Definition and Characteristics
docs.text:
A PWA is a web application that uses modern web platform features to deliver an app-like experience. There is no single API called "PWA"—the term describes a set of best practices and three technical requirements.

The defining characteristics:

  • Progressive — works for every user regardless of browser, then layers on enhancements where supported.
  • Responsive — adapts to any screen: phone, tablet, desktop.
  • Connectivity-independent — works offline or on flaky networks via a Service Worker cache.
  • App-like — feels like a native app: standalone window, no browser chrome, smooth navigation.
  • Installable — can be added to the home screen / app launcher without an app store.
  • Re-engageable — can send push notifications even when the tab is closed.
  • Linkable — shareable via a URL, no installation required to try it.
  • Safe — served over HTTPS so requests can't be tampered with.

The three hard technical requirements for installability are:

  1. HTTPS (or localhost during development).
  2. A registered Service Worker with a fetch handler.
  3. A Web App Manifest with the required fields (name, icons, start_url, display).

PWAs are not a framework

There is nothing React-specific about a PWA. The Service Worker, manifest, and Cache API are browser platform features. React just renders the UI; the PWA layer wraps the built output. That is also why tooling like vite-plugin-pwa lives in the build pipeline, not in your component tree.

docs.title: Benefits of Building PWAs
docs.text:
• Instant repeat loads — pre-cached assets load from disk, not the network.
  • Offline resilience — the app shell renders even with no connection.
  • No app store friction — install straight from the browser; updates ship instantly on deploy.
  • One codebase — the same app runs on web, mobile, and desktop installs.
  • Smaller footprint — a PWA install is typically a fraction of an equivalent native binary.
  • Re-engagement — push notifications bring users back without an app store presence.

Platform limitations

PWA support is strong on Chromium browsers and Android. On iOS/Safari, support has historically lagged: install ("Add to Home Screen") is manual, and Web Push on iOS requires the app to be installed to the home screen (Safari 16.4+). Always treat PWA features as progressive enhancements and feature-detect before using them.

docs.title: What is a Service Worker?
docs.text:
A Service Worker is a JavaScript file the browser runs in a separate background thread, independent of your page. It acts as a programmable network proxy: it can intercept every outgoing request from pages within its scope and decide how to respond—from the network, from a cache, or with a generated response.

Key properties that shape how you write one:

  • No DOM access — it cannot touch window or document. It communicates with pages via postMessage.
  • Event-driven & terminable — the browser can kill an idle Service Worker and restart it on the next event, so it keeps no in-memory state between events. Persist data in the Cache API or IndexedDB.
  • HTTPS only — required (except on localhost), because a proxy that could be MITM'd would be a security hole.
  • Scope-bound — it can only control pages under its scope (see below).

docs.title: Registering a Service Worker in React
docs.text:
Registration is done from your app code (not inside the worker). Register after load so it doesn't compete with critical rendering, and feature-detect first:

// src/registerSW.ts
export function registerServiceWorker() {
  if (!('serviceWorker' in navigator)) {
    console.warn('Service workers are not supported in this browser.');
    return;
  }

  window.addEventListener('load', async () => {
    try {
      const registration = await navigator.serviceWorker.register('/sw.js', {
        scope: '/',
      });
      console.log('SW registered with scope:', registration.scope);
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('SW registration failed:', err);
    }
  });
}

// src/main.tsx
import { StrictMode } from 'react';
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
import App from './App';
import { registerServiceWorker } from './registerSW';

createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render(
  <StrictMode>
    <App />
  </StrictMode>
);

registerServiceWorker();

Create React App's service worker is deprecated

Older tutorials reference CRA's src/serviceWorkerRegistration.ts and service-worker.js. Create React App itself is deprecated (the React team now recommends Vite or a framework like Next.js), and CRA's offline-first service worker was opt-out/legacy. Do not start a new PWA from CRA. Use vite-plugin-pwa (Vite) or Serwist/next-pwa (Next.js).

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Scope determines which URLs a Service Worker controls. By default, the scope is the directory the worker file is served from. A worker at /sw.js controls the whole origin (/); a worker at /app/sw.js would only control /app/....

This is why the Service Worker file must sit at the root of your site (or you must send the Service-Worker-Allowed header to widen scope). Build tools place it correctly for you.

/sw.js          → scope "/"      controls everything
/admin/sw.js    → scope "/admin" controls only /admin/*

docs.title: Lifecycle: install → activate → fetch
docs.text:
A Service Worker moves through a well-defined lifecycle. Understanding it is the key to avoiding "stale app" bugs.

// sw.js (raw API — what Workbox generates for you)
const CACHE_NAME = 'app-shell-v1';
const APP_SHELL = ['/', '/index.html', '/offline.html', '/styles.css'];

// 1) INSTALL — fired once when a new SW is downloaded.
//    Pre-cache the app shell here.
self.addEventListener('install', (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.open(CACHE_NAME).then((cache) => cache.addAll(APP_SHELL))
  );
  // Activate this SW immediately instead of waiting (use with care).
  self.skipWaiting();
});

// 2) ACTIVATE — fired when the SW takes control.
//    Clean up old caches from previous versions here.
self.addEventListener('activate', (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.keys().then((keys) =>
      Promise.all(
        keys.filter((k) => k !== CACHE_NAME).map((k) => caches.delete(k))
      )
    )
  );
  // Start controlling open pages without a reload.
  self.clients.claim();
});

// 3) FETCH — fired for every network request in scope.
self.addEventListener('fetch', (event) => {
  event.respondWith(
    caches.match(event.request).then((cached) => cached ?? fetch(event.request))
  );
});

The lifecycle states in order:

  1. Installing — install event runs; pre-cache assets in event.waitUntil().
  2. Installed / Waiting — a new worker waits until all tabs using the old worker are closed, unless you call skipWaiting().
  3. Activating — activate event runs; this is where you delete outdated caches.
  4. Activated — now intercepting fetch. Existing tabs are controlled only after a reload, unless you call clients.claim().
  5. Redundant — superseded or failed; discarded.

skipWaiting() + clients.claim() can break in-flight pages

Forcing an immediate takeover means a page that already loaded the old JS chunks may suddenly request new hashed chunks that no longer exist, causing runtime errors. The safer pattern is a "new version available" prompt that asks the user to reload. vite-plugin-pwa gives you this with the autoUpdate / prompt strategies (shown later).

docs.title: 3. The Web App Manifest
docs.text:
The Web App Manifest is a JSON file that tells the browser how your app should appear when installed: its name, icons, colors, and window mode. Link it from your HTML <head>:

<!-- index.html -->
<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest" />
<meta name="theme-color" content="#0f172a" />

// public/manifest.webmanifest
{
  "name": "Acme Tasks",
  "short_name": "Tasks",
  "description": "Plan and track your work, online or offline.",
  "start_url": "/",
  "scope": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#0f172a",
  "theme_color": "#0f172a",
  "icons": [
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-192.png",
      "sizes": "192x192",
      "type": "image/png"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icons/icon-512.png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "type": "image/png"
    },
    {
      "src": "/icons/maskable-512.png",
      "sizes": "512x512",
      "type": "image/png",
      "purpose": "maskable"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Icons
docs.text:
• Provide at least 192×192 and 512×512 PNGs—these are the minimum for installability.
  • Add a "purpose": "maskable" icon so Android can crop it into its adaptive shapes (circle, squircle) without clipping content. Keep important art inside the central "safe zone."
  • Use Maskable.app to preview maskable icons; use a PWA asset generator to produce all sizes from one source.

docs.title: Display modes
docs.text:
The display field controls how much browser UI is shown:

Mode Appearance fullscreen No browser UI at all; uses the whole screen (games, kiosks). standalone Looks like a native app: own window, no address bar. Most common. minimal-ui Standalone plus a minimal set of navigation controls (back/reload). browser A normal browser tab (effectively "not installed").

docs.title: Install prompt and beforeinstallprompt
docs.text:
Chromium browsers fire a beforeinstallprompt event when the app meets installability criteria. You can capture it, show your own "Install" button, and trigger the prompt on a user gesture:

// src/hooks/useInstallPrompt.ts
import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';

interface BeforeInstallPromptEvent extends Event {
  prompt: () => Promise<void>;
  userChoice: Promise<{ outcome: 'accepted' | 'dismissed' }>;
}

export function useInstallPrompt() {
  const [promptEvent, setPromptEvent] = useState<BeforeInstallPromptEvent | null>(null);
  const [installed, setInstalled] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    const onBeforeInstall = (e: Event) => {
      e.preventDefault(); // stop the default mini-infobar
      setPromptEvent(e as BeforeInstallPromptEvent);
    };
    const onInstalled = () => {
      setInstalled(true);
      setPromptEvent(null);
    };

    window.addEventListener('beforeinstallprompt', onBeforeInstall);
    window.addEventListener('appinstalled', onInstalled);
    return () => {
      window.removeEventListener('beforeinstallprompt', onBeforeInstall);
      window.removeEventListener('appinstalled', onInstalled);
    };
  }, []);

  const install = async () => {
    if (!promptEvent) return;
    await promptEvent.prompt();
    await promptEvent.userChoice;
    setPromptEvent(null); // a prompt event can only be used once
  };

  return { canInstall: !!promptEvent && !installed, install };
}

// Usage in a component
function InstallButton() {
  const { canInstall, install } = useInstallPrompt();
  if (!canInstall) return null;
  return <button onClick={install}>Install app</button>;
}

beforeinstallprompt is Chromium-only

Safari/Firefox do not fire this event. On iOS, installation is the manual Share → Add to Home Screen flow. Detect standalone mode with window.matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)').matches (or navigator.standalone on iOS) and show platform-appropriate instructions.

docs.title: 4. The Recommended Path: vite-plugin-pwa
docs.text: For a Vite + React app, vite-plugin-pwa generates the Service Worker (via Workbox), injects the manifest, wires up precaching of your build output, and handles update flows—so you rarely hand-write a worker.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
npm install -D vite-plugin-pwa

// vite.config.ts
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
import { VitePWA } from 'vite-plugin-pwa';

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [
    react(),
    VitePWA({
      registerType: 'prompt', // 'autoUpdate' to update silently on next load
      injectRegister: 'auto', // injects the SW registration for you
      workbox: {
        // Precache the built assets (JS/CSS/HTML) automatically.
        globPatterns: ['**/*.{js,css,html,ico,png,svg,woff2}'],
        navigateFallback: '/index.html', // SPA offline fallback
      },
      manifest: {
        name: 'Acme Tasks',
        short_name: 'Tasks',
        start_url: '/',
        display: 'standalone',
        background_color: '#0f172a',
        theme_color: '#0f172a',
        icons: [
          { src: '/icons/icon-192.png', sizes: '192x192', type: 'image/png' },
          { src: '/icons/icon-512.png', sizes: '512x512', type: 'image/png' },
          {
            src: '/icons/maskable-512.png',
            sizes: '512x512',
            type: 'image/png',
            purpose: 'maskable',
          },
        ],
      },
      devOptions: { enabled: true }, // test the SW during `vite dev`
    }),
  ],
});

docs.title: Handling updates with the virtual module
docs.text:
The plugin exposes a virtual module, virtual:pwa-register/react, with a hook that reports update/offline-ready state:

// src/components/ReloadPrompt.tsx
import { useRegisterSW } from 'virtual:pwa-register/react';

export default function ReloadPrompt() {
  const {
    offlineReady: [offlineReady, setOfflineReady],
    needRefresh: [needRefresh, setNeedRefresh],
    updateServiceWorker,
  } = useRegisterSW();

  const close = () => {
    setOfflineReady(false);
    setNeedRefresh(false);
  };

  if (!offlineReady && !needRefresh) return null;

  return (
    <div role="alert" className="pwa-toast">
      <span>
        {offlineReady ? 'App ready to work offline.' : 'A new version is available.'}
      </span>
      {needRefresh && (
        <button onClick={() => updateServiceWorker(true)}>Reload</button>
      )}
      <button onClick={close}>Close</button>
    </div>
  );
}

Customizing the generated worker

generateSW (the default) builds the worker from config—great for static caching. When you need custom logic (push handlers, background sync, your own routes), switch to strategies: 'injectManifest' and write a src/sw.ts. Workbox injects the precache manifest into your file, and you keep full control. Next.js users get the same model via Serwist (the maintained successor to next-pwa).

docs.title: 5. Offline Support
docs.text: Offline support comes from the Service Worker's fetch handler plus the Cache API, a key-value store of Request/Response pairs that persists across sessions.

docs.title: Caching assets for offline access
docs.text:
Pre-cache the app shell (the minimal HTML/CSS/JS to render the UI) at install time. With vite-plugin-pwa this is automatic via globPatterns. With the raw API, you call cache.addAll() in the install event (see §2).

// Inspecting the Cache API directly (works in DevTools console too)
const cache = await caches.open('app-shell-v1');
await cache.add('/index.html');           // fetch + store
await cache.put('/data', new Response('{}')); // store a custom response
const hit = await cache.match('/index.html'); // read back

docs.title: Implementing an offline fallback page
docs.text:
When both the network and cache miss, serve a friendly fallback instead of the browser's dino error. Pre-cache an offline.html, then return it for failed navigations:

// sw.js — offline fallback for page navigations
const OFFLINE_URL = '/offline.html';

self.addEventListener('fetch', (event) => {
  // Only handle top-level navigations (HTML documents).
  if (event.request.mode === 'navigate') {
    event.respondWith(
      fetch(event.request).catch(async () => {
        const cache = await caches.open('app-shell-v1');
        return (await cache.match(OFFLINE_URL)) ?? Response.error();
      })
    );
  }
});

In vite-plugin-pwa, navigateFallback: '/index.html' handles SPA routes; for a dedicated offline page, add it to globPatterns/additionalManifestEntries and reference it in your worker logic.

docs.title: Handling offline requests
docs.text:
For API/data requests you have two jobs: serve something useful when offline, and avoid caching things you shouldn't.

  • Detect offline state in React with the navigator.onLine flag and the online/offline events, and reflect it in the UI:
// src/hooks/useOnlineStatus.ts
import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';

export function useOnlineStatus() {
  const [online, setOnline] = useState(navigator.onLine);
  useEffect(() => {
    const up = () => setOnline(true);
    const down = () => setOnline(false);
    window.addEventListener('online', up);
    window.addEventListener('offline', down);
    return () => {
      window.removeEventListener('online', up);
      window.removeEventListener('offline', down);
    };
  }, []);
  return online;
}

Never blindly cache POST/PUT/DELETE responses

The Cache API is for GET requests. Mutating requests should not be served from cache; instead queue them for Background Sync (see §6). Also avoid caching authenticated/personalized responses unless you scope and invalidate them carefully.

docs.title: 6. Caching Strategies and Background Sync
docs.text: A caching strategy is the policy your fetch handler uses to decide between cache and network. Choose per resource type.

docs.title: The main strategies
Background Sync for deferred requests
docs.text:
Background Sync lets you defer an action made while offline and have the browser retry it automatically once connectivity returns—even if the user has navigated away. You register a sync tag from the page; the Service Worker handles the matching sync event.

// Page: queue a request when the network fails
async function submitWithSync(payload: unknown) {
  await saveToOutbox(payload); // store in IndexedDB
  const reg = await navigator.serviceWorker.ready;
  if ('sync' in reg) {
    await (reg as any).sync.register('outbox-sync');
  } else {
    await flushOutbox(); // fallback: try immediately
  }
}

// sw.js: drain the queue when the browser fires 'sync'
self.addEventListener('sync', (event) => {
  if (event.tag === 'outbox-sync') {
    event.waitUntil(flushOutbox()); // re-attempt each queued request
  }
});

Use Workbox's Background Sync queue

Hand-rolling an IndexedDB outbox is error-prone. Workbox's BackgroundSyncPlugin provides a battle-tested queue with automatic replay. The Background Sync API is Chromium-only; design a graceful fallback (retry on online) for other browsers.

docs.title: 7. Web Push Notifications
docs.text:
Push notifications re-engage users even when your app isn't open. They combine three APIs:

  • Notification API — displays the notification (also works in the foreground).
  • Push API — lets a server send a message to the Service Worker via a browser push service.
  • Web Push protocol + VAPID — how your server authenticates pushes to the browser's push service.

docs.title: The flow at a glance
docs.text:
1. The app asks the user for notification permission.
  2. The app subscribes to push, getting a PushSubscription (an endpoint URL + keys).
  3. The app sends that subscription to your server to store.
  4. Your server sends a push (signed with your VAPID private key) to the subscription endpoint.
  5. The browser wakes the Service Worker with a push event; the worker calls showNotification.

docs.title: VAPID keys
docs.text:
VAPID (Voluntary Application Server Identification) is a public/private key pair that identifies your server to the push service. The public key goes in the browser subscription; the private key stays on your server and signs each push.

# Generate a VAPID key pair with the web-push CLI (Node)
npx web-push generate-vapid-keys
# → Public Key:  BJ...   (ship to the client)
# → Private Key: 7q...   (keep secret on the server)

docs.title: Requesting permission and subscribing (client)
docs.text:
// src/push/subscribe.ts
const VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY = import.meta.env.VITE_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY as string;

// Push subscriptions need the key as a Uint8Array.
function urlBase64ToUint8Array(base64: string) {
  const padding = '='.repeat((4 - (base64.length % 4)) % 4);
  const raw = atob((base64 + padding).replace(/-/g, '+').replace(/_/g, '/'));
  return Uint8Array.from([...raw].map((c) => c.charCodeAt(0)));
}

export async function subscribeToPush() {
