  if (!('serviceWorker' in navigator) || !('PushManager' in window)) {
    throw new Error('Push is not supported in this browser.');
  }

  const permission = await Notification.requestPermission();
  if (permission !== 'granted') throw new Error('Notification permission denied.');

  const registration = await navigator.serviceWorker.ready;

  const subscription = await registration.pushManager.subscribe({
    userVisibleOnly: true, // required by browsers: every push must show UI
    applicationServerKey: urlBase64ToUint8Array(VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY),
  });

  // Persist the subscription on your server.
  await fetch('/api/push/subscribe', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    body: JSON.stringify(subscription),
  });

  return subscription;
}

Always ask permission from a user gesture

Browsers penalize sites that request notification permission on page load—many now block it entirely. Trigger requestPermission() from a click (e.g. an "Enable notifications" button) and explain the value first.

docs.title: Handling push events in the Service Worker
docs.text:
The push event delivers the server's payload; render it with showNotification. Handle clicks with notificationclick to focus or open your app:

// sw.js (or src/sw.ts with injectManifest)
self.addEventListener('push', (event) => {
  const data = event.data?.json() ?? {};
  const title = data.title ?? 'New notification';
  const options = {
    body: data.body ?? '',
    icon: '/icons/icon-192.png',
    badge: '/icons/badge-72.png',
    data: { url: data.url ?? '/' },
  };
  event.waitUntil(self.registration.showNotification(title, options));
});

self.addEventListener('notificationclick', (event) => {
  event.notification.close();
  const url = event.notification.data?.url ?? '/';
  event.waitUntil(
    self.clients.matchAll({ type: 'window' }).then((clients) => {
      const existing = clients.find((c) => c.url === url);
      if (existing) return existing.focus();
      return self.clients.openWindow(url);
    })
  );
});

docs.title: Sending push notifications from a server
docs.text:
Use the web-push library, which implements the Web Push protocol and VAPID signing for you:

// server/push.js (Node / Express)
import webpush from 'web-push';

webpush.setVapidDetails(
  'mailto:admin@example.com',
  process.env.VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY,
  process.env.VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY
);

export async function sendPush(subscription, payload) {
  try {
    await webpush.sendNotification(
      subscription,
      JSON.stringify(payload) // becomes event.data in the SW
    );
  } catch (err) {
    // 404/410 means the subscription expired — delete it from your DB.
    if (err.statusCode === 404 || err.statusCode === 410) {
      await deleteSubscription(subscription.endpoint);
    } else {
      throw err;
    }
  }
}

// Example route
app.post('/api/notify', async (req, res) => {
  const subs = await getAllSubscriptions();
  await Promise.allSettled(
    subs.map((s) => sendPush(s, { title: 'Hello', body: 'A new update!', url: '/' }))
  );
  res.json({ sent: subs.length });
});

Prune dead subscriptions

Push services return 404/410 when a subscription is no longer valid (user uninstalled, cleared data, or it expired). Delete those from your database on send to keep your subscription list clean and your sends efficient.

docs.title: 8. Testing and Debugging PWAs
docs.text:
• Lighthouse (Chrome DevTools → Lighthouse) audits installability and best practices.
  • Application panel (DevTools) inspects the manifest, registered Service Workers, the Cache Storage, and IndexedDB.
  • Use "Update on reload" and "Bypass for network" in the Service Workers section while developing to avoid serving stale workers.
  • Toggle Offline in the Network panel to test offline behavior and fallbacks.
  • vite-plugin-pwa's devOptions.enabled lets you exercise the worker during vite dev.

The #1 PWA gotcha: stale Service Workers

A cached, never-updating Service Worker is the most common PWA frustration. Always version caches, clean up old ones in activate, and ship an explicit update flow (the ReloadPrompt pattern from §4). When in doubt, "Unregister" in the Application panel and hard-reload.

docs.title: 📚 Additional Resources
docs.text:
• Vite PWA Plugin Docs
  • Workbox Documentation
  • Serwist (Next.js PWA)
  • MDN: Service Worker API
  • MDN: Web App Manifest
  • MDN: Push API
  • web.dev: Learn PWA

docs.title: 🧭 PWA Core Concepts at a Glance
docs.text: 🔹 Concept 🧠 Description Service Worker Background script that proxies network requests; enables offline, caching, and push. Web App Manifest JSON describing name, icons, colors, and display mode for installation. Scope The URL subtree a Service Worker controls; defined by where the SW file is served. Lifecycle install → installed/waiting → activate → activated (fetch) → redundant. Cache API Persistent store of Request/Response pairs used for offline assets. beforeinstallprompt Chromium event you capture to show a custom install button. Caching strategies Cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate, network-only, cache-only. Background Sync Defers failed/offline requests and retries them when connectivity returns. Push API + VAPID Server-to-SW messaging; VAPID keys authenticate your server to the push service. Notification API Displays notifications from a push event (or in the foreground). vite-plugin-pwa Recommended Vite plugin wrapping Workbox to generate the SW and manifest. Serwist / next-pwa The equivalent tooling for Next.js apps.

docs.title: React Performance Optimization
docs.text:
React is fast by default. Most apps never need any of the techniques in this chapter, and reaching for them too early usually makes code harder to read without making it faster. The golden rule of performance work is:

Measure first, optimize second.

This chapter covers the tools React gives you to control re-rendering and bundle size — React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, React.lazy, code splitting, virtualization, and the concurrent features (useTransition, useDeferredValue). It also covers the React Compiler, which (in React 19) auto-memoizes your components and changes how often you should reach for manual memoization at all.

Premature optimization is the enemy

Wrapping everything in useMemo/useCallback/React.memo is a common anti-pattern. Each adds CPU and memory overhead (a comparison + a cached value), and clutters the code. Only optimize a path after you have profiled it and confirmed it is a real bottleneck. Read When NOT to use them below.

docs.title: What is React.memo?
docs.text:
By default, when a component re-renders, all of its children re-render too — even if their props did not change. React.memo is a higher-order component that memoizes the rendered output of a component. React skips re-rendering it when its props are shallowly equal to the previous props.

import { memo } from "react";

interface AvatarProps {
  name: string;
  src: string;
}

// Re-renders only when `name` or `src` change identity.
const Avatar = memo(function Avatar({ name, src }: AvatarProps) {
  console.log("Avatar rendered:", name);
  return <img src={src} alt={name} className="h-10 w-10 rounded-full" />;
});

export default Avatar;

docs.title: When to Use React.memo
docs.text:
React.memo pays off when all of these are true:

  • The component re-renders often with the same props.
  • The component is expensive to render (big subtree, heavy markup, costly calculations).
  • Its props are stable (primitives, or memoized objects/functions/callbacks).

A classic case: a parent holds frequently-changing state (a text input, a timer) but renders an expensive child that does not depend on that state.

import { memo, useState } from "react";

const ExpensiveList = memo(function ExpensiveList({ items }: { items: string[] }) {
  console.log("ExpensiveList rendered"); // does NOT log on every keystroke
  return (
    <ul>
      {items.map((item) => (
        <li key={item}>{item}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
});

function Page() {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState("");
  const [items] = useState(() => ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"]);

  return (
    <div>
      <input value={query} onChange={(e) => setQuery(e.target.value)} />
      {/* `items` keeps the same reference, so memo skips the re-render */}
      <ExpensiveList items={items} />
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: Preventing Unnecessary Re-renders
docs.text:
React.memo only stops a re-render if the props are referentially stable. The most common reason memo "doesn't work" is passing a new object, array, or function on every render:

// ❌ memo is useless here — a brand-new function is created every render,
//    so `onClick` is never shallow-equal to the previous one.
<Avatar name="Ada" src="/ada.png" onClick={() => doThing()} />

// ✅ Pair memo with useCallback / useMemo to keep references stable.
const handleClick = useCallback(() => doThing(), []);
<Avatar name="Ada" src="/ada.png" onClick={handleClick} />

memo and useCallback/useMemo are a team

React.memo checks props; useCallback/useMemo keep those props referentially stable across renders. Using memo without stabilizing object/function props usually does nothing.

docs.title: Custom Comparison Function
docs.text:
React.memo accepts a second argument — a comparison function (prevProps, nextProps) => boolean. Return true to skip the render (props are "equal"), false to re-render. This is the opposite polarity of class shouldComponentUpdate.

interface UserCardProps {
  user: { id: number; name: string };
  theme: string;
}

const UserCard = memo(
  function UserCard({ user }: UserCardProps) {
    return <div>{user.name}</div>;
  },
  (prev, next) => prev.user.id === next.user.id // re-render only when id changes
);

Use custom comparison sparingly

A custom comparator runs on every render and is easy to get wrong (forget a prop and you ship stale UI). It also tempts deep comparisons, which can be slower than just re-rendering. Prefer fixing the data flow (stable references, smaller props) over a hand-written comparator.

docs.title: Limitations of React.memo
docs.text:
• It performs a shallow comparison only. New object/array/function identities defeat it.
  • It does not prevent re-renders caused by useState/useReducer inside the component, or by a useContext value changing.
  • It adds overhead: a props comparison plus memory to cache the result. For cheap components this is a net loss.
  • It is not a semantic guarantee — React may still choose to re-render. Treat it as a performance hint, never as a way to skip side effects.

docs.title: 2. useCallback & useMemo Hooks
docs.text:
Both hooks cache a value between renders so its identity stays stable as long as the dependencies don't change.

  • useMemo(fn, deps) caches the return value of fn.
  • useCallback(fn, deps) caches the function itself. useCallback(fn, deps) is exactly useMemo(() => fn, deps).

docs.title: How useMemo Optimizes Computation-heavy Operations
docs.text:
Use useMemo to avoid re-running an expensive calculation on every render when its inputs haven't changed.

import { useMemo, useState } from "react";

function ProductTable({ products }: { products: Product[] }) {
  const [filter, setFilter] = useState("");

  // Only re-sorts/filters when `products` or `filter` change,
  // not when some unrelated state elsewhere triggers a render.
  const visible = useMemo(() => {
    console.log("recomputing visible products");
    return products
      .filter((p) => p.name.toLowerCase().includes(filter.toLowerCase()))
      .sort((a, b) => a.price - b.price);
  }, [products, filter]);

  return (
    <>
      <input value={filter} onChange={(e) => setFilter(e.target.value)} />
      <ul>{visible.map((p) => <li key={p.id}>{p.name}</li>)}</ul>
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: How useCallback Prevents Unnecessary Function Recreation
docs.text:
Functions are recreated on every render. That's usually fine — but it matters when the function is:

  1. passed as a prop to a React.memo-wrapped child, or
  2. listed in another hook's dependency array (e.g. a useEffect that would otherwise re-run every render).
import { memo, useCallback, useState } from "react";

const Button = memo(function Button({ onClick, label }: { onClick: () => void; label: string }) {
  console.log("Button rendered:", label);
  return <button onClick={onClick}>{label}</button>;
});

function Toolbar() {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  // Same function identity across renders → memoized Button won't re-render.
  const increment = useCallback(() => setCount((c) => c + 1), []);

  return (
    <div>
      <span>{count}</span>
      <Button onClick={increment} label="Increment" />
    </div>
  );
}

Use the updater form to shrink dependencies

setCount((c) => c + 1) lets the dependency array stay []. If you wrote setCount(count + 1) you'd need [count], recreating the callback on every change and defeating the point.

docs.title: Practical Examples Together
docs.text:
A typical "real" use: a memoized child receiving both a derived list and a stable handler.

function Dashboard({ rows }: { rows: Row[] }) {
  const [selectedId, setSelectedId] = useState<number | null>(null);

  const totals = useMemo(
    () => rows.reduce((sum, r) => sum + r.amount, 0),
    [rows]
  );

  const handleSelect = useCallback((id: number) => setSelectedId(id), []);

  return <MemoizedTable rows={rows} total={totals} onSelect={handleSelect} />;
}

docs.title: When NOT to use useMemo and useCallback
docs.text:
This is the part people skip. Most useMemo/useCallback calls in real codebases are unnecessary and slightly harmful.

Skip them when:

  • The calculation is cheap (string concat, simple arithmetic, mapping a short array). The memo machinery costs more than the work it saves.
  • The value/function is not passed to a memoized child and not a hook dependency. Stabilizing identity buys you nothing.
  • You're memoizing a value used only in JSX of the same component (it re-renders anyway).
// ❌ Pointless — the work is trivial and `full` isn't a dependency anywhere.
const full = useMemo(() => `${first} ${last}`, [first, last]);

// ✅ Just compute it.
const full = `${first} ${last}`;

The mental model

useMemo/useCallback are caches. A cache only helps if (a) recomputing is expensive, or (b) reference identity is load-bearing. If neither is true, you're paying for a cache you never read.

React Compiler may make these obsolete

If you adopt the React Compiler (see §7), you should stop adding useMemo/useCallback by hand for performance — the compiler inserts the right memoization automatically. The hooks remain valid React; you just rarely need to write them yourself.

docs.title: What is Lazy Loading?
docs.text: Lazy loading means deferring the download of code (or data, or images) until it is actually needed. Instead of shipping one giant JavaScript bundle on first load, you split it and fetch pieces on demand. This shrinks the initial bundle, improving time-to-interactive.

docs.title: React.lazy for Component Splitting
docs.text:
React.lazy takes a function that returns a dynamic import() and gives you back a component you render normally. The bundler creates a separate chunk for that module.

import { lazy, Suspense } from "react";

// `SettingsPanel` is NOT in the main bundle — it's fetched when first rendered.
const SettingsPanel = lazy(() => import("./SettingsPanel"));

function App() {
  const [showSettings, setShowSettings] = useState(false);
  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setShowSettings(true)}>Open settings</button>
      <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
        {showSettings && <SettingsPanel />}
      </Suspense>
    </div>
  );
}

React.lazy needs a default export

The imported module must export default the component. For a named export, re-map it inside the import:

const Chart = lazy(() =>
  import("./charts").then((m) => ({ default: m.Chart }))
);

docs.title: Using <Suspense> for Fallback UI
docs.text:
A lazy component must be rendered inside a <Suspense> boundary. While the chunk is loading, React shows the fallback; once it arrives, it swaps in the real UI. One boundary can wrap many lazy components, and boundaries can nest for granular loading states.

<Suspense fallback={<PageSkeleton />}>
  <Header />
  <Suspense fallback={<ChartSpinner />}>
    <LazyChart />
  </Suspense>
</Suspense>

docs.title: Best Practices for Lazy Loading
docs.text:
• Split at natural boundaries: routes, modals, tabs, heavy widgets (rich text editors, charts, maps) the user may never open.
  • Don't over-split: dozens of tiny chunks add HTTP overhead and waterfalls. Split things that are large or rarely used.
  • Give meaningful fallbacks: skeletons that match layout prevent layout shift better than a bare spinner.
  • Handle load failures with an Error Boundary around <Suspense> (a chunk can fail on flaky networks / after a redeploy).
  • Consider preloading on intent — e.g. start the import on onMouseEnter of the button that reveals it, so the chunk is ready by click time.
// Preload pattern: kick off the import early, render the lazy component later.
const importPanel = () => import("./SettingsPanel");
const SettingsPanel = lazy(importPanel);
// ...
<button onMouseEnter={importPanel} onClick={open}>Open settings</button>

docs.title: Introduction to Code Splitting
docs.text:
Code splitting is the bundler-level mechanism behind lazy loading. Tools like Vite, webpack, and Rspack see a dynamic import("...") and emit that module as a separate chunk that loads at runtime. React.lazy is just the React-aware wrapper around this.

// Dynamic import returns a Promise — works for any module, not just components.
async function exportToPdf(data: Report) {
  const { generatePdf } = await import("./pdf"); // heavy lib stays out of main bundle
  return generatePdf(data);
}

docs.title: Route-based Code Splitting with React Router
docs.text:
The highest-value split is per route: a user landing on / shouldn't download the code for /admin/reports. With React Router v6.4+ (the data router API):

import { lazy, Suspense } from "react";
import { createBrowserRouter, RouterProvider } from "react-router-dom";

const Home = lazy(() => import("./routes/Home"));
const Dashboard = lazy(() => import("./routes/Dashboard"));
const Settings = lazy(() => import("./routes/Settings"));

const router = createBrowserRouter([
  {
    path: "/",
    element: (
      <Suspense fallback={<PageSpinner />}>
        <Home />
      </Suspense>
    ),
  },
  {
    path: "/dashboard",
    element: (
      <Suspense fallback={<PageSpinner />}>
        <Dashboard />
      </Suspense>
    ),
  },
  {
    path: "/settings",
    element: (
      <Suspense fallback={<PageSpinner />}>
        <Settings />
      </Suspense>
    ),
  },
]);

export default function App() {
  return <RouterProvider router={router} />;
}

Route splitting with lazy route config

React Router also supports a per-route lazy property that loads the route module (component and its loader/action) on demand, which keeps loaders out of the main bundle too:

{
  path: "/dashboard",
  lazy: () => import("./routes/Dashboard"), // module exports `Component`, `loader`, etc.
}

docs.title: Performance Benefits of Code Splitting
docs.text:
• Smaller initial bundle → faster first paint and time-to-interactive.
  • Pay-as-you-go: users download only the code for screens they visit.
  • Better caching: a change to one route invalidates only that route's chunk, not the whole app.
  • Parallelism: the browser can fetch route chunks alongside data.

Watch for request waterfalls

Naïve splitting can create a chain: load route chunk → then component renders → then it starts its data fetch. Prefer router data loaders (React Router) or framework conventions (Next.js) that start data fetching in parallel with the chunk download, and preload the next route on link hover.

docs.title: 5. Avoiding Unnecessary Re-renders
docs.text: Memoization treats symptoms. Often the better fix is structuring state and components so the re-render never happens. To do that, you need to know why React re-renders.

docs.title: Understanding React's Reconciliation Process
docs.text:
When state changes, React re-renders the component and, by default, all of its descendants. Rendering produces a new virtual tree; React then reconciles it against the previous tree (a "diff") and applies the minimal set of real DOM mutations.

Key facts that drive most performance decisions:

  • A component re-renders when: its state changes, its parent re-renders, or a context it consumes changes.
  • A component re-rendering does not mean the DOM changes — reconciliation may find nothing to update. But the render function (and its child renders) still runs, which can be the expensive part.
  • Re-rendering is top-down. Props being "the same value" does not stop it unless the child is wrapped in React.memo.

docs.title: a) Stable references
docs.text:
Don't create new objects/arrays/functions inline when they're passed to memoized children or used as hook dependencies. Hoist constants out of the component, or memoize them.

// ❌ new array + new style object every render
<Chart data={[1, 2, 3]} style={{ height: 300 }} />

// ✅ stable references
const CHART_DATA = [1, 2, 3];            // module scope
const CHART_STYLE = { height: 300 };
<Chart data={CHART_DATA} style={CHART_STYLE} />

docs.title: b) State colocation
docs.text:
Keep state as close as possible to where it's used. If only one small component needs a piece of state, don't lift it to a big parent — that would re-render the whole subtree on every change. Move the state into the component that uses it.

// ❌ `hover` lives in Parent, so Parent + ALL children re-render on hover.
function Parent() {
  const [hover, setHover] = useState(false);
  return (
    <>
      <HugeList />
      <Tooltip visible={hover} onEnter={() => setHover(true)} />
    </>
  );
}

// ✅ Move hover state into the only component that needs it.
function Parent() {
  return (
    <>
      <HugeList />
      <HoverableTooltip />
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: c) "Lifting state down" / push state into children
docs.text:
The mirror image of "lifting state up": when frequently-changing state has crept up to a parent that renders expensive siblings, extract a small component that owns the state, so only it re-renders.

// The expensive <Report /> re-renders on every keystroke because `text` lives here.
function Page() {
  const [text, setText] = useState("");
  return (
    <>
      <input value={text} onChange={(e) => setText(e.target.value)} />
      <Report /> {/* expensive, doesn't use `text` */}
    </>
  );
}

// ✅ Isolate the input + its state. <Report /> now never re-renders on typing.
function SearchBox() {
  const [text, setText] = useState("");
  return <input value={text} onChange={(e) => setText(e.target.value)} />;
}
function Page() {
  return (
    <>
      <SearchBox />
      <Report />
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: d) Pass children as props (the "slot" trick)
docs.text:
A component that re-renders does not re-render children passed to it from above — those elements were created by the parent and keep their identity.

// <ExpensiveChild /> is created in App, so Ticker re-rendering won't re-render it.
function Ticker({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [n, setN] = useState(0);
  useEffect(() => {
    const id = setInterval(() => setN((x) => x + 1), 1000);
    return () => clearInterval(id);
  }, []);
  return <div>{n}{children}</div>;
}

function App() {
  return (
    <Ticker>
      <ExpensiveChild />
    </Ticker>
  );
}

docs.title: e) Context splitting
docs.text:
Every consumer of a context re-renders when any part of that context value changes. Two fixes:

  • Split contexts by update frequency: a rarely-changing AuthContext and a fast-changing CursorContext should be separate providers, so cursor updates don't re-render auth consumers.
  • Memoize the provider value so it isn't a new object every render.
  • Separate state and dispatch: put dispatch (stable) in one context and the changing state in another, so components that only dispatch never re-render.
const StateCtx = createContext<State | null>(null);
const DispatchCtx = createContext<Dispatch | null>(null);

function Provider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initial);
  // dispatch is referentially stable; only StateCtx consumers re-render on state change.
  return (
    <DispatchCtx.Provider value={dispatch}>
      <StateCtx.Provider value={state}>{children}</StateCtx.Provider>
    </DispatchCtx.Provider>
  );
}

docs.title: f) Correct, stable keys
docs.text:
In lists, key tells React which item is which across renders. Bad keys cause extra DOM work, lost state, and bugs.

// ❌ Index keys break when the list reorders/inserts — React reuses the wrong DOM nodes,
//    so input focus, animations, and component state get mismatched.
{items.map((item, i) => <Row key={i} item={item} />)}

// ✅ Stable, unique id from the data.
{items.map((item) => <Row key={item.id} item={item} />)}

Never use Math.random() (or a fresh id) as a key

A key that changes every render forces React to unmount and remount the element each time — the opposite of optimization, and it destroys component state.

docs.title: Class-Component Equivalents (for legacy code)
docs.text:
The functional techniques above replace the older class APIs you'll still see in existing code:

  • shouldComponentUpdate(nextProps, nextState) — return false to skip the update. The class-era ancestor of React.memo's comparator (note the inverted return value).
  • React.PureComponent — a base class that implements shouldComponentUpdate with a shallow props/state comparison. It is the class equivalent of wrapping a function component in React.memo.
import { PureComponent } from "react";

class Row extends PureComponent<{ item: Item }> {
  render() {
    return <li>{this.props.item.name}</li>; // skips render when item is shallow-equal
  }
}

docs.title: What is Virtualization?
docs.text: Rendering 10,000 rows means 10,000 DOM nodes — slow to mount, heavy on memory, janky to scroll. Virtualization (a.k.a. windowing) renders only the handful of items currently visible in the viewport (plus a small overscan buffer) and recycles them as you scroll. The list appears full-height via a spacer, but only ~20 rows exist in the DOM at any moment.

docs.title: Choosing a Library
docs.text:
Don't build this by hand. The current recommendations:

Library Notes @tanstack/react-virtual Modern, headless (you render the markup), tiny, hook-based, great TS support, supports dynamic/variable sizes. A strong default for new code. react-window Small, battle-tested, component-based API. Good when you want a simple drop-in for fixed/variable-size lists and grids. react-virtualized The older, heavier predecessor of react-window (same author). More features (tables, autosizers) but a larger bundle. Prefer react-window/@tanstack/react-virtual for new projects.

docs.title: Example: @tanstack/react-virtual
docs.text:
import { useRef } from "react";
import { useVirtualizer } from "@tanstack/react-virtual";

function VirtualList({ rows }: { rows: string[] }) {
  const parentRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);

  const virtualizer = useVirtualizer({
    count: rows.length,
    getScrollElement: () => parentRef.current,
    estimateSize: () => 35, // estimated row height in px
    overscan: 5,
  });

  return (
    <div ref={parentRef} style={{ height: 400, overflow: "auto" }}>
      {/* full-height spacer so the scrollbar reflects the real list length */}
      <div style={{ height: virtualizer.getTotalSize(), position: "relative" }}>
        {virtualizer.getVirtualItems().map((virtualRow) => (
          <div
            key={virtualRow.key}
            style={{
              position: "absolute",
              top: 0,
              left: 0,
              width: "100%",
              height: virtualRow.size,
              transform: `translateY(${virtualRow.start}px)`,
            }}
          >
            {rows[virtualRow.index]}
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: Example: react-window
docs.text:
import { FixedSizeList } from "react-window";

function VirtualList({ rows }: { rows: string[] }) {
  return (
    <FixedSizeList height={400} width="100%" itemCount={rows.length} itemSize={35}>
      {({ index, style }) => <div style={style}>{rows[index]}</div>}
    </FixedSizeList>
  );
}

docs.title: When to Use Virtualization
docs.text:
• Use it for long, scrollable lists/grids/tables — hundreds to thousands of items (chat logs, feeds, data grids, file trees).
  • Don't use it for short lists (a few dozen items) — the complexity isn't worth it, and you lose native browser features.
  • Trade-offs to know: Ctrl+F find-in-page only sees rendered rows; SEO crawlers see only what's in the DOM; sticky headers, variable heights, and horizontal scroll need extra care.

Combine with pagination / infinite scroll

Virtualization controls DOM size; it doesn't reduce data size. For huge datasets, pair it with server-side pagination or infinite loading so you never hold the entire dataset in memory.

docs.title: What it Does
docs.text:
The React Compiler is a build-time tool (a Babel/SWC plugin) that automatically memoizes your components and hooks. It analyzes your code and inserts the equivalent of useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo precisely where they help — far more thoroughly and correctly than humans typically do by hand.

It became stable in 2025 and works with React 17+ via a runtime helper, though it shines with React 19.

// You write plain, readable React:
function ProductTable({ products, filter }: Props) {
  const visible = products
    .filter((p) => p.name.includes(filter))
    .sort((a, b) => a.price - b.price);
  return <List items={visible} />;
}

// The compiler transforms this so `visible` is recomputed only when
// `products` or `filter` change, and <List /> is skipped when `items` is stable —
// no manual useMemo/memo required.

docs.title: How to Think About Manual Memoization With the Compiler
docs.text:
The new default with the compiler

With the React Compiler enabled, stop writing useMemo/useCallback/React.memo for performance. Write clean, idiomatic React and let the compiler do the memoization. Manual memoization becomes redundant (and the compiler often does it better).

A few nuances:

  • Existing useMemo/useCallback keep working — you don't have to rip them out, and the compiler won't double-memoize. But new code shouldn't add them for perf.
  • useMemo for semantics (not perf) is still occasionally meaningful — e.g. guaranteeing a referential identity that an effect's dependency relies on — though the compiler usually handles this too.
  • The compiler requires your components to follow the Rules of React (pure render, no mutation of props/state during render). Enable the eslint-plugin-react-hooks rules; the compiler will bail out (and just leave code un-optimized) on components it can't prove are safe, rather than break them.
  • You can adopt it incrementally — enable it for a directory, or opt specific files in/out — so there's no big-bang migration.

It does not replace the structural techniques

The compiler memoizes; it does not restructure your component tree, split your contexts, fix bad keys, or virtualize long lists. The lessons in §5 and §6 still apply.

docs.title: 8. Keeping the UI Responsive: useTransition & useDeferredValue
docs.text: React 18 introduced concurrent features that let you mark some updates as non-urgent, so React can keep the UI responsive (typing, clicking) while heavy work happens in the background. These complement memoization — they're about prioritizing work, not skipping it.

docs.title: useTransition
docs.text:
Wrap a state update you're willing to interrupt in startTransition. The urgent part (the input value) updates immediately; the expensive part (filtering a huge list) is marked as a transition and won't block typing. isPending lets you show a subtle loading hint.

import { useState, useTransition } from "react";

function Search({ allItems }: { allItems: Item[] }) {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState("");
  const [results, setResults] = useState<Item[]>(allItems);
  const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();

  function handleChange(value: string) {
    setQuery(value); // urgent: keeps the input snappy

    startTransition(() => {
      // non-urgent: can be interrupted by faster keystrokes
      setResults(allItems.filter((i) => i.name.includes(value)));
    });
  }

  return (
    <>
      <input value={query} onChange={(e) => handleChange(e.target.value)} />
      {isPending && <span>Updating…</span>}
      <ResultList items={results} />
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: useDeferredValue
docs.text:
A simpler alternative when you don't own the state setter: derive a deferred copy of a value that "lags behind" during heavy renders. Great for feeding an expensive child off a fast-changing input.

import { useDeferredValue, useMemo, useState } from "react";

function Search({ allItems }: { allItems: Item[] }) {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState("");
  const deferredQuery = useDeferredValue(query); // lags during heavy work

  const results = useMemo(
    () => allItems.filter((i) => i.name.includes(deferredQuery)),
    [allItems, deferredQuery]
  );

  return (
    <>
      <input value={query} onChange={(e) => setQuery(e.target.value)} />
      <ResultList items={results} />
    </>
  );
}

Which one?

Use useTransition when you control the state update (you can wrap the setter). Use useDeferredValue when the value comes from props or you can't wrap the update. Both keep the input responsive while the expensive subtree catches up.

docs.title: 9. Measuring Performance
docs.text: You cannot optimize what you don't measure. Always profile before and after a change to confirm it actually helped.

docs.title: React DevTools Profiler
docs.text:
The React Developer Tools browser extension has a Profiler tab:

  1. Click Record, interact with the app, then stop.
  2. The flamegraph shows which components rendered and how long each took.
  3. The ranked chart lists components by render time — your worst offenders.
  4. Enable "Highlight updates when components render" (in DevTools settings) to see re-renders flash on screen — the fastest way to spot a component that re-renders when it shouldn't.

DevTools can also tell you why a component rendered (props changed, state changed, parent rendered, hooks changed) — turn on "Record why each component rendered" in the Profiler settings.

docs.title: The <Profiler> API
docs.text:
For programmatic measurement (e.g. logging to analytics, or in tests), wrap any subtree in <Profiler>:

import { Profiler, type ProfilerOnRenderCallback } from "react";

const onRender: ProfilerOnRenderCallback = (
  id,          // the `id` prop of the Profiler tree
  phase,       // "mount" | "update" | "nested-update"
  actualDuration, // time spent rendering this update
  baseDuration,   // estimated time to render the whole subtree without memoization
  startTime,
  commitTime
) => {
  console.log(`${id} [${phase}] took ${actualDuration.toFixed(2)}ms`);
};

function App() {
  return (
    <Profiler id="Sidebar" onRender={onRender}>
      <Sidebar />
    </Profiler>
  );
}

<Profiler> adds overhead — keep it out of hot production paths

The <Profiler> component itself costs a little CPU and memory. React strips it from production builds by default unless you opt into the profiling build. Use it for investigation and synthetic measurement, not as always-on instrumentation in production-critical paths.

Don't forget the basics

Browser DevTools' Performance panel, Lighthouse, and the Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) measure what the user actually feels. A component that renders in 3 ms but ships a 2 MB bundle is still slow to the user — code splitting (§3–4) often beats render-time micro-optimization.

docs.title: Summary & Checklist
docs.text:
A practical order of operations when something feels slow:

  1. Profile first. Use the React DevTools Profiler and browser Performance panel to find the actual bottleneck. Don't guess.
  2. Fix structure before adding memo. Colocate state, lift state down, split contexts, pass children as props, and use stable keys. These remove re-renders instead of skipping them.
  3. Reach for useMemo/useCallback/React.memo only for proven-expensive renders or load-bearing reference identity — and skip them entirely if you've enabled the React Compiler.
  4. Keep the UI responsive during heavy updates with useTransition / useDeferredValue.
  5. Shrink the bundle with React.lazy + <Suspense> and route-based code splitting.
  6. Virtualize long lists with @tanstack/react-virtual or react-window.
  7. Measure again to confirm the change actually helped — and revert it if it didn't.

Remember

The fastest code is the code that never runs. Most "React performance" wins come from not re-rendering and not shipping code, not from clever memoization.

docs.title: React Testing and Debugging
docs.text:
Tests give you the confidence to change code without breaking it, and good debugging tools turn "why is this broken?" into a five-minute fix instead of an afternoon. This chapter is the general testing reference for the guide: how to set up a test runner, write unit and integration tests, mock the network, run end-to-end (E2E) tests, and debug a running app.

Form-specific tests live in the Forms chapter

The React Forms chapter has a dedicated Testing Forms section (controlled forms, React Hook Form, validation/error assertions). This chapter covers the general toolkit and patterns; head there for form-focused examples instead of duplicating them here.

docs.title: Why Test?
docs.text:
• Catch regressions early — a failing test points at the broken commit instead of an angry user.
  • Document behavior — a test is an executable spec of what a component should do.
  • Enable refactoring — you can restructure internals freely as long as behavior stays green.
  • Speed up reviews — reviewers trust a PR with tests more than one without.

docs.title: The Testing Pyramid
docs.text:
Think of your test suite as a pyramid: lots of fast, cheap tests at the bottom, fewer slow, expensive tests at the top.

        ▲   E2E  (Playwright / Cypress)   ← few, slow, high confidence
       ▲▲▲  Integration  (RTL + MSW)      ← some, medium speed
      ▲▲▲▲▲ Unit  (Vitest / Jest)         ← many, fast, isolated
Layer Scope Tools Speed Unit A single function or component in isolation Vitest / Jest Milliseconds Integration Several units working together (component + state + DOM) React Testing Library Fast E2E The whole app in a real browser, like a user Playwright / Cypress Seconds

In practice the line between "unit" and "integration" in React is blurry — most component tests with React Testing Library are really small integration tests, and that's a good thing.

docs.title: Test Behavior, Not Implementation
docs.text:
The single most important testing principle in React:

The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you. — Kent C. Dodds, author of React Testing Library

  • Do assert on what the user sees and does: visible text, roles, what happens after a click.
  • Don't assert on internal state variables, the names of useState hooks, or how many times a function rendered.

If you rename a state variable or swap useState for useReducer and your tests break even though the UI behaves identically, your tests were coupled to the implementation.

// ❌ Coupled to implementation — breaks on harmless refactors
expect(wrapper.state("count")).toBe(1);

// ✅ Coupled to behavior — survives refactors
expect(screen.getByText("Count: 1")).toBeInTheDocument();

docs.title: Choosing a Runner: Vitest vs. Jest
docs.text:
This guide uses Vite, so Vitest is the natural choice — it reuses your existing vite.config (including path aliases and plugins), starts fast, and has a Jest-compatible API. Jest remains the most common runner overall and is the default in Create React App / many Next.js setups, so you'll see it everywhere.

Vitest (recommended here) Jest (alternative) Config Reuses vite.config.ts Separate jest.config + Babel/ts-jest Speed Very fast (esbuild, native ESM) Fast, but heavier ESM setup API describe / it / expect / vi describe / it / expect / jest Mock fn vi.fn(), vi.mock() jest.fn(), jest.mock()

The test code is almost identical

Apart from the mocking namespace (vi vs jest) and config, the assertions and queries are the same. Examples below use Vitest; the Jest equivalent is noted inline.

docs.title: Setting Up Vitest
docs.text:
npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event \
  @testing-library/jest-dom jsdom

Configure Vitest inside your Vite config (or a vitest.config.ts):

// vite.config.ts
/// <reference types="vitest/config" />
import { defineConfig } from "vite";
import react from "@vitejs/plugin-react";

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react()],
  test: {
    globals: true,            // use describe/it/expect without importing
    environment: "jsdom",     // a fake DOM so components can render
    setupFiles: "./src/test/setup.ts",
    css: true,
  },
});

// src/test/setup.ts
import "@testing-library/jest-dom"; // adds matchers like toBeInTheDocument()

Add a script and run it:

// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "test": "vitest",
    "test:run": "vitest run",
    "coverage": "vitest run --coverage"
  }
}

npm test          # watch mode
npm run test:run  # single run (CI)

Jest setup, for comparison

With Jest you install jest @testing-library/* jest-environment-jsdom, set testEnvironment: "jsdom" and setupFilesAfterEnv: ["@testing-library/jest-dom"] in jest.config.js, and run npx jest.

docs.title: Writing a Basic Unit Test
docs.text:
Pure functions are the easiest thing to test — no DOM, no rendering.

// math.ts
export const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
export const slugify = (s: string) =>
  s.trim().toLowerCase().replace(/\s+/g, "-");

// math.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest"; // omit with `globals: true`
import { add, slugify } from "./math";

describe("add", () => {
  it("adds two numbers", () => {
    expect(add(2, 3)).toBe(5);
  });
});

describe("slugify", () => {
  it("lowercases and dashes spaces", () => {
    expect(slugify("  Hello World ")).toBe("hello-world");
  });
});

Anatomy: describe groups related tests, it (alias test) is one case, and expect(...).matcher(...) makes the assertion. Common matchers: toBe, toEqual (deep), toContain, toThrow, toHaveBeenCalledWith, and the jest-dom DOM matchers (toBeInTheDocument, toHaveTextContent, toBeDisabled).

docs.title: Mocking Functions and Modules
docs.text:
A mock replaces a real dependency with a controllable stand-in so a test stays fast, isolated, and deterministic.

Spy / mock function — verify it was called, and control its return value:

import { vi, expect, it } from "vitest"; // jest.fn() / jest.spyOn() with Jest

it("calls the callback with the new value", () => {
  const onChange = vi.fn();           // a mock function
  onChange("hello");

  expect(onChange).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
  expect(onChange).toHaveBeenCalledWith("hello");
});

it("controls a return value", () => {
  const getUser = vi.fn().mockReturnValue({ id: 1, name: "Ada" });
  expect(getUser().name).toBe("Ada");
});

Module mock — replace an entire imported module (e.g. an API client):

// userService.ts
export async function fetchUser(id: number) {
  const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
  return res.json();
}

// profile.test.ts
import { vi, expect, it, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import { fetchUser } from "./userService";

vi.mock("./userService", () => ({
  fetchUser: vi.fn(),
}));

beforeEach(() => {
  vi.mocked(fetchUser).mockResolvedValue({ id: 1, name: "Ada" });
});

it("returns the mocked user", async () => {
  await expect(fetchUser(1)).resolves.toEqual({ id: 1, name: "Ada" });
});

Reset mocks between tests

Call vi.clearAllMocks() (or set clearMocks: true in config) in a beforeEach/afterEach so call history from one test never leaks into the next. Mock timers with vi.useFakeTimers() to test setTimeout/setInterval/debounce logic without real waiting.

docs.title: Snapshot Testing
docs.text:
A snapshot records a component's rendered output to a .snap file; later runs compare against it and fail if the output changed.

import { render } from "@testing-library/react";
import { expect, it } from "vitest";
import Badge from "./Badge";

it("matches the snapshot", () => {
  const { asFragment } = render(<Badge label="New" />);
  expect(asFragment()).toMatchSnapshot();
});

The first run writes the snapshot; commit it. When output changes intentionally, update with vitest -u (jest -u).

Use snapshots sparingly

Large snapshots are noisy — reviewers rubber-stamp diffs they don't understand, and every styling tweak turns them red. Prefer small, focused snapshots or explicit toBeInTheDocument assertions for most cases. Use inline snapshots (toMatchInlineSnapshot()) for tiny serializable values.

docs.title: Why RTL (and Not Enzyme)?
docs.text:
React Testing Library renders a component into a real (jsdom) DOM and lets you query and interact with it the way a user would. It deliberately gives you no access to component internals — that's what keeps tests resilient.

Enzyme is effectively dead — do not use it for new projects

Enzyme (Airbnb) pioneered shallow rendering and internal-state inspection, but it relies on private React internals and has no official adapter for React 18 or 19. It is unmaintained for modern React. The react-list outline mentions Enzyme, Shallow rendering, and wrapper.state() for historical context only — all new tests should use React Testing Library. If you inherit an Enzyme suite, migrate it to RTL over time.

React Testing Library Enzyme (legacy) Philosophy Test behavior via the DOM Test implementation / internals React 18/19 ✅ First-class ❌ No official adapter Shallow render ✗ (intentionally) ✓ (shallow) Reads state directly ✗ ✓ (wrapper.state()) Status Actively maintained, recommended Unmaintained

docs.title: Rendering a Component
docs.text:
// Counter.tsx
import { useState } from "react";

export default function Counter({ start = 0 }: { start?: number }) {
  const [count, setCount] = useState(start);
  return (
    <div>
      <p>Count: {count}</p>
      <button onClick={() => setCount((c) => c + 1)}>Increment</button>
    </div>
  );
}

// Counter.test.tsx
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import Counter from "./Counter";

describe("Counter", () => {
  it("renders the initial count", () => {
    render(<Counter start={3} />);
    expect(screen.getByText("Count: 3")).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});

render mounts the component; screen is the global query object scoped to the rendered output. RTL auto-cleans the DOM between tests (with globals: true).

docs.title: Queries: Prefer Roles and Labels
docs.text:
Queries come in three variants and several "by" suffixes:

Variant Returns Throws if not found? Use for getBy* element yes element that should already be present queryBy* element or null no asserting absence findBy* Promise<element> yes (after timeout) element that appears async

Prefer queries in this priority order (most accessible first): getByRole → getByLabelText → getByPlaceholderText → getByText → … → getByTestId (last resort).

screen.getByRole("button", { name: /increment/i }); // ✅ best — role + accessible name
screen.getByLabelText(/email/i);                     // ✅ form fields
screen.getByText(/count: 3/i);                       // ✅ visible text
screen.queryByText(/error/i);                        // ✅ assert it's NOT there
screen.getByTestId("counter");                       // ⚠️ only when nothing else fits

// Asserting absence with queryBy*
expect(screen.queryByRole("alert")).not.toBeInTheDocument();

docs.title: Simulating User Events with user-event
docs.text:
@testing-library/user-event fires realistic event sequences (a click fires pointerdown, mousedown, focus, mouseup, click) — far closer to reality than the low-level fireEvent. Always await it and create a session with setup().

import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import userEvent from "@testing-library/user-event";
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import Counter from "./Counter";

describe("Counter interactions", () => {
  it("increments when the button is clicked", async () => {
    const user = userEvent.setup();
    render(<Counter />);

    await user.click(screen.getByRole("button", { name: /increment/i }));
    await user.click(screen.getByRole("button", { name: /increment/i }));

    expect(screen.getByText("Count: 2")).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});

Common actions: user.type(input, "text"), user.clear(input), user.selectOptions(select, "value"), user.keyboard("{Enter}"), user.tab(), user.upload(input, file).

fireEvent still exists

fireEvent.click(el) dispatches a single raw event and is synchronous. Use it for edge cases (e.g. firing a scroll or change directly), but reach for user-event by default — it catches bugs fireEvent misses (like clicking a disabled button).

docs.title: Asserting DOM Changes
docs.text:
jest-dom adds expressive matchers:

expect(el).toBeInTheDocument();
expect(el).toBeVisible();
expect(el).toHaveTextContent("Saved");
expect(button).toBeDisabled();
expect(input).toHaveValue("hello");
expect(checkbox).toBeChecked();
expect(el).toHaveClass("active");
expect(el).toHaveAttribute("aria-expanded", "true");

docs.title: Testing Asynchronous Behavior
docs.text:
For UI that updates after a fetch, timeout, or promise, use findBy* (retries until the element appears) or waitFor (retries an assertion). Never use a bare setTimeout to "wait."

// UserCard.tsx
import { useEffect, useState } from "react";

export default function UserCard({ id }: { id: number }) {
  const [name, setName] = useState<string | null>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    fetch(`/api/users/${id}`)
      .then((r) => r.json())
      .then((u) => setName(u.name));
  }, [id]);

  if (!name) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  return <h2>{name}</h2>;
}

// UserCard.test.tsx
import { render, screen, waitFor } from "@testing-library/react";
import { describe, it, expect, vi, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import UserCard from "./UserCard";

beforeEach(() => {
  vi.spyOn(globalThis, "fetch").mockResolvedValue({
    ok: true,
    json: async () => ({ name: "Ada Lovelace" }),
  } as Response);
});

describe("UserCard", () => {
  it("shows the loading state then the user", async () => {
    render(<UserCard id={1} />);

    // Present immediately
    expect(screen.getByText(/loading/i)).toBeInTheDocument();

    // Appears after the fetch resolves — findBy* awaits it
    expect(await screen.findByRole("heading", { name: /ada lovelace/i }))
      .toBeInTheDocument();

    // Loading text is now gone
    await waitFor(() =>
      expect(screen.queryByText(/loading/i)).not.toBeInTheDocument()
    );
  });
});

Wrap shared providers in a custom render

If components need Router, theme, or store context, write a renderWithProviders helper that wraps render with your providers and re-export it from a single test-utils file. See the State Management and React Router chapters for the providers you'll need.

docs.title: 4. Mocking the Network with MSW
docs.text:
Spying on fetch (as above) works for one call, but it gets brittle fast. Mock Service Worker (MSW) intercepts requests at the network layer using handlers, so your component code runs unchanged — it makes a real fetch, and MSW answers. The same handlers work in tests, in Storybook, and in the browser during development.

npm install -D msw

Define handlers and a server:

// src/test/handlers.ts
import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw";

export const handlers = [
  http.get("/api/users/:id", ({ params }) =>
    HttpResponse.json({ id: Number(params.id), name: "Ada Lovelace" })
  ),

  http.post("/api/users", async ({ request }) => {
    const body = (await request.json()) as { name: string };
    return HttpResponse.json({ id: 99, ...body }, { status: 201 });
  }),
];

// src/test/server.ts
import { setupServer } from "msw/node";
import { handlers } from "./handlers";

export const server = setupServer(...handlers);

Wire it into your test setup so the server starts once and resets between tests:

// src/test/setup.ts
import "@testing-library/jest-dom";
import { beforeAll, afterEach, afterAll } from "vitest";
import { server } from "./server";

beforeAll(() => server.listen({ onUnhandledRequest: "error" }));
afterEach(() => server.resetHandlers()); // undo per-test overrides
afterAll(() => server.close());

Now UserCard from the previous section needs no fetch spy — MSW handles it. To test an error or empty state, override a handler for just that test:

import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw";
import { server } from "../test/server";
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import UserCard from "./UserCard";

it("handles a server error", async () => {
  server.use(
    http.get("/api/users/:id", () =>
      HttpResponse.json({ message: "Boom" }, { status: 500 })
    )
  );

  render(<UserCard id={1} />);
  expect(await screen.findByText(/loading/i)).toBeInTheDocument();
  // ...assert your component's error UI here
});

MSW v2 uses http and HttpResponse

MSW v2 (current) replaced the v1 rest.get / res(ctx.json(...)) API with the http namespace and the standard Response-based HttpResponse. If you find rest-based examples online, they're for the old v1.

docs.title: 5. End-to-End (E2E) Testing
docs.text: E2E tests drive a real browser against your running app, exercising the full stack the way a user does. They're slower and pricier than unit tests, so keep them for critical flows (login, checkout, the happy path of each major feature).

docs.title: Playwright (recommended)
docs.text:
Playwright (Microsoft) is the modern default: one tool runs Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit; it has built-in auto-waiting (no flaky manual sleeps), parallelism, tracing, and a codegen recorder. Its assertions auto-retry until they pass or time out.

npm init playwright@latest
# scaffolds playwright.config.ts, a tests/ folder, and installs browsers

// playwright.config.ts (essentials)
import { defineConfig, devices } from "@playwright/test";

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: "./e2e",
  use: { baseURL: "http://localhost:5173", trace: "on-first-retry" },
  webServer: {
    command: "npm run dev",        // Playwright starts your app for you
    url: "http://localhost:5173",
    reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI,
  },
  projects: [{ name: "chromium", use: { ...devices["Desktop Chrome"] } }],
});

Basic test + navigation:

// e2e/home.spec.ts
import { test, expect } from "@playwright/test";

test("navigates from home to the about page", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/");
  await expect(page.getByRole("heading", { name: /welcome/i })).toBeVisible();

  await page.getByRole("link", { name: /about/i }).click();
  await expect(page).toHaveURL(/.*about/);
  await expect(page.getByRole("heading", { name: /about us/i })).toBeVisible();
});

Filling and submitting a form:

test("logs in and lands on the dashboard", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/login");

  await page.getByLabel(/email/i).fill("user@example.com");
  await page.getByLabel(/password/i).fill("secret123");
  await page.getByRole("button", { name: /log in/i }).click();

  await expect(page).toHaveURL(/.*dashboard/);
  await expect(page.getByText(/welcome back/i)).toBeVisible();
});

Mocking an API response with page.route:

test("shows users from a mocked API", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.route("**/api/users", (route) =>
    route.fulfill({
      status: 200,
      contentType: "application/json",
      body: JSON.stringify([{ id: 1, name: "Ada Lovelace" }]),
    })
  );

  await page.goto("/users");
  await expect(page.getByText("Ada Lovelace")).toBeVisible();
});

Playwright queries feel like RTL

getByRole, getByLabel, and getByText exist in Playwright too, so the accessibility-first habits you build with RTL carry straight over. Run npx playwright codegen localhost:5173 to record clicks into a starter test, and npx playwright test --ui for the time-travel debugger.

docs.title: Cypress (alternative)
docs.text:
Cypress is the long-standing, popular E2E tool with an excellent interactive runner (watch commands replay live in a real browser) and a gentle learning curve. It runs in Chromium-family browsers, Firefox, and WebKit (experimental). Its API is chainable and also auto-retries.

npm install -D cypress
npx cypress open   # interactive runner

// cypress/e2e/login.cy.ts
describe("login flow", () => {
  it("logs in and reaches the dashboard", () => {
    // Mock the API
    cy.intercept("GET", "/api/users", {
      statusCode: 200,
      body: [{ id: 1, name: "Ada Lovelace" }],
    }).as("getUsers");

    cy.visit("/login");
    cy.findByLabelText(/email/i).type("user@example.com");
    cy.findByLabelText(/password/i).type("secret123");
    cy.findByRole("button", { name: /log in/i }).click();

    cy.url().should("include", "/dashboard");
    cy.contains(/welcome back/i).should("be.visible");
  });
});
Playwright (recommended) Cypress Browsers Chromium, Firefox, WebKit (all native) Chromium-family, Firefox, WebKit (experimental) Architecture Out-of-process, true parallelism Runs in the browser event loop Multi-tab / multi-origin ✅ Native Limited Auto-wait ✅ ✅ Best feature Tracing + codegen + multi-browser Interactive time-travel runner

Add @testing-library/cypress for RTL-style queries

Cypress's default selectors are CSS-based; install @testing-library/cypress to get cy.findByRole / cy.findByLabelText, keeping your accessibility-first habits.

docs.title: React Developer Tools
docs.text: Install React Developer Tools as a browser extension (Chrome, Firefox, Edge) — or use the standalone react-devtools package for React Native / non-browser targets. It adds two tabs to your browser DevTools: Components and Profiler.

docs.title: Components Tab — Inspect State & Props
docs.text:
• Browse the live component tree and search by component name.
  • Select a component to see its props, state, hooks, and context in the right panel.
  • Edit values inline — change a prop or state value and watch the UI update, no code change needed.
  • Hover a component to highlight the DOM it renders; click the inspector to go the other way (DOM → component).
  • Right-click → "Show source" jumps to where the component is defined; the bug icon logs the selected component's data to the console.

Find why a value is what it is

With a component selected, the Hooks section lists every hook in order. This is how you spot a stale closure, an unexpected context value, or a state that never updated — without scattering console.log everywhere.

docs.title: Profiler Tab — Find Slow Renders
docs.text:
The Profiler records why and how long components render:

  1. Open Profiler → click Record → interact with your app → Stop.
  2. The flamegraph shows each commit; wider/colored bars rendered longer.
  3. The ranked chart lists components by render time, worst first.
  4. Select a component to see "Why did this render?" (props changed, state changed, hooks changed, or parent re-rendered).

Use it to confirm an optimization actually helped — e.g. before/after adding React.memo, useMemo, or useCallback. (See the Performance Optimization chapter for those techniques.)

Highlight updates

In the Components tab settings, enable "Highlight updates when components render" to get a flashing outline on every re-render. Lots of flashing on an idle screen is a smell — something is re-rendering more than it should.

docs.title: Common Debugging Techniques
docs.text:
• console.log / console.table — quick and underrated; console.table(arr) prints arrays of objects as a grid.
  • debugger statement — pauses execution at that line when DevTools is open; far better than logging for stepping through logic.
  • screen.debug() — inside an RTL test, prints the current DOM so you can see what the test actually rendered. screen.logTestingPlaygroundURL() suggests the best query.
  • Source maps + breakpoints — set breakpoints directly in your .tsx source in the browser's Sources panel.
  • Strict Mode double-invoke — in development, <StrictMode> intentionally renders components and runs effects twice to surface impurities and missing cleanup. Don't "fix" the double log; fix the impure code it reveals.
  • The dependency-array lint rule — eslint-plugin-react-hooks catches missing useEffect/useCallback dependencies, the source of countless stale-data bugs.

docs.title: Error Boundaries
docs.text:
A JavaScript error during rendering will, by default, unmount your whole React tree — the user sees a blank page. An error boundary is a component that catches errors in its child tree and renders a fallback UI instead, while logging the error for you.

// ErrorBoundary.tsx
import { Component, type ErrorInfo, type ReactNode } from "react";

interface Props {
  fallback: ReactNode;
  children: ReactNode;
}
interface State {
  hasError: boolean;
}

export class ErrorBoundary extends Component<Props, State> {
  state: State = { hasError: false };

  // Render the fallback on the next render
  static getDerivedStateFromError(): State {
    return { hasError: true };
  }

  // Side effect: log to your monitoring service (Sentry, etc.)
  componentDidCatch(error: Error, info: ErrorInfo) {
    console.error("Caught by boundary:", error, info.componentStack);
  }

  render() {
    return this.state.hasError ? this.props.fallback : this.props.children;
  }
}

// Usage — wrap a risky subtree
<ErrorBoundary fallback={<p>Something went wrong. Please refresh.</p>}>
  <Dashboard />
</ErrorBoundary>

Error boundaries must be class components — but you don't have to write one

There is no hook equivalent of getDerivedStateFromError / componentDidCatch, so the boundary itself is a class. In practice most teams use react-error-boundary, a small library that gives you a <ErrorBoundary> with a FallbackComponent, a resetErrorBoundary callback to retry, and a useErrorBoundary hook — no class needed.

What error boundaries do NOT catch

They only catch errors thrown during rendering, in lifecycle methods, and in constructors of the components below them. They do not catch:

  • Errors in event handlers (use a try/catch there).
  • Asynchronous errors (setTimeout, promises, fetch rejections).
  • Errors in server-side rendering.
  • Errors thrown in the boundary itself.

Place boundaries at strategic points (per route, per widget) so one broken section doesn't take down the entire page.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Test behavior, not implementation — assert on what the user sees, not internal state.
  • Follow the pyramid: many fast unit tests, some integration tests, a few E2E tests.
  • Vitest is the runner of choice for Vite projects (Jest is the common alternative); both share a near-identical API.
  • React Testing Library + user-event render and drive components like a user; query by role/label and handle async with findBy* / waitFor.
  • Enzyme is dead for modern React — use RTL for all new tests.
  • MSW mocks the network at the request layer, so component code runs unchanged across tests, Storybook, and dev.
  • Playwright is the modern E2E default (Cypress the popular alternative); both auto-wait and can mock APIs.
  • React DevTools (Components + Profiler) is your primary debugging tool; pair it with debugger, screen.debug(), and error boundaries to keep failures contained and diagnosable.
  • For form-specific tests, see the Testing Forms section of the React Forms chapter.

docs.title: 1. Installing Node.js & npm
docs.text:
• Downloading and Installing Node.js

  • Node.js is a JavaScript runtime that enables running JavaScript outside the browser It is essential for React development. Follow these steps to install it:

  • Go to the official Node.js website: https://nodejs.org/
  • Download the latest LTS (Long-Term Support) version for stability.
  • Run the installer and follow the setup instructions.

  • Ensure you check the option to install npm (Node Package Manager).

  • Verifying Installation

    • Once installed, open the terminal and run: node -v
    • This should return the installed Node.js version.
    • To verify npm installation, run: npm -v
    • This should return the installed npm version.

  • Understanding Node.js Package Manager (npm)

    • npm is a package manager that comes with Node.js, used to install and manage JavaScript packages and dependencies for projects.
    • Common npm commands:
      • npm init - Initializes a new project.
      • npm install <package> - Installs a package.
      • npm install -g <package> - Installs a package globally.
      • npm update - Updates all packages.

docs.title: 2. Setting Up a React Project with Vite
docs.text:
• Creating and Running a New Project

  • Vite is a modern frontend build tool that offers a fast and optimized development experience.

  • To create a new React project using Vite, run:

    • npm create vite@latest my-vite-app -- --template react
    • With Yarn: yarn create vite my-vite-app --template react
    • With pnpm: pnpm create vite my-vite-app --template react
  • Navigate to the project directory:
    • cd my-vite-app
  • Install dependencies:
    • npm install

  • Then start the development server:

    • npm run dev

    By default, the app runs on http://localhost:5173/

  • Understanding Default Folder Structure Folder Structure

my-vite-app/
│-- node_modules/
│-- public/
│-- src/
│   ├── assets/
│   ├── components/
│   ├── App.jsx
│   ├── main.jsx
│-- .gitignore
│-- index.html
│-- package.json
│-- vite.config.js

Key files and folders:

  • src/: Main source code for components and logic.
  • public/: Static assets such as images and index.html.
  • vite.config.js: Configuration file for Vite.
  • index.html: The main HTML entry point.

Customizing Vite Configuration

  • Vite provides flexibility through vite.config.js.
  • You can modify settings like aliasing, plugins, and environment variables.

Example of aliasing paths in vite.config.js:

import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [react()],
  resolve: {
    alias: {
      '@components': '/src/components',
    },
  },
});

docs.title: 3. Project Structure of a React App
docs.text:
• src, public, node_modules, package.json Explained
  • src/: Contains React components, hooks, styles, and utility functions.
  • public/: Stores static files like images and fonts.
  • node_modules/: Installed dependencies.

  • package.json: Defines project metadata and dependencies.

  • Organizing Components, Hooks, and Utilities Recommended folder structure:

my-vite-app/
│-- src/
│   │-- components/
│   │-- hooks/
│   │-- utils/
│   │-- styles/
│   └── App.jsx
  • Best Practices for Folder Structure
  • A modular and reusable component refers to a self-contained, independent unit of code that can be used across different parts of a project or in multiple projects without modification.
  • Use separate folders for utilities and hooks.
    • Utilities are general helper functions used for tasks like formatting, calculations, or API calls. They work in any JavaScript project.
    • Hooks are special functions in React that let functional components manage state and side effects.
    • Utilities are stateless and reusable across different projects.
    • Hooks are React-specific and used inside functional components to handle things like state (useState) or side effects (useEffect).
    • Example: A utility might format a date, while a hook manages a counter’s state in a React component.
    • Maintain styles in a dedicated folder.

docs.title: 4. Understanding package.json & node_modules
docs.text:
• Managing Dependencies

    Dependencies are listed in package.json:

    "dependencies": {
  "react": "^18.0.0",
  "react-dom": "^18.0.0"
}

    Install dependencies with: npm install

  • npm Scripts Overview

    Common scripts in package.json:

    "scripts": {
  "dev": "vite",
  "build": "vite build",
  "preview": "vite preview"
}

    Run a script with: npm run dev , npm run build , npm run preview

  • Peer Dependencies and Dev Dependencies

    • Dependencies - Required for production.
    • Dev Dependencies - Required for development (--save-dev).
    • Peer Dependencies - Must be installed manually by the consuming project.

docs.title: 5. Running and Building a React Application
docs.text:
• Development Server and Build Process

    • To start the development server: npm run dev
    • To build for production: npm run build

  • This generates an optimized dist/ folder.

Using Environment Variables in React

  • Vite uses .env files for environment variables:
    • VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com
  • Access it in your app:
    • const apiUrl = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL;

docs.title: 6. Comparing Vite over CRA
docs.text:
• Benefits of Vite
    • Faster startup due to esbuild pre-bundling.
    • Optimized builds with efficient tree-shaking.
    • Hot Module Replacement (HMR) for instant updates.
    • Smaller bundle sizes due to optimized dependency handling.

docs.title: 1. What is JSX?
docs.text:
JSX (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that looks similar to HTML. It is used with React to describe the structure of the user interface in a declarative manner. JSX is not a requirement for using React, but it makes the code more readable and expressive.

  • Why JSX Exists
    • JSX simplifies the process of writing UI components by providing a syntax that resembles HTML, making it easier to visualize the structure of the UI. Under the hood, JSX is compiled to JavaScript calls that ultimately generate the virtual DOM elements.

Legacy vs. Automatic JSX Runtime

The examples below show the legacy transform, where JSX compiles to React.createElement() and therefore requires import React in scope. Since React 17, the automatic JSX runtime (react/jsx-runtime) is the default in tools like Vite, Next.js, and Create React App. With it, Babel/SWC imports the JSX helpers for you, so you no longer need import React just to use JSX. The React.createElement() snippets here are still useful for understanding what JSX compiles to.

  • Writing JSX Syntax

    • JSX allows developers to write HTML-like structures directly within JavaScript. A basic JSX snippet looks like this:
    const element = <h1>Hello, JSX!</h1>;

    It can also include nested elements:

    nested elements
    const element = (
  <div>
    <h1>Welcome to JSX</h1>
    <p>JSX makes writing UI code easier!</p>
  </div>
);

  • JSX Compilation Process JSX is not valid JavaScript, so it must be compiled before it can be executed by the browser. Babel, a popular JavaScript compiler, transpiles JSX into JavaScript functions that create React elements. With the legacy transform this produces:

    const element = React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello, JSX!");

    With the automatic runtime (React 17+), the compiler instead emits a call to _jsx imported from react/jsx-runtime, so you don't need import React yourself:

    import { jsx as _jsx } from "react/jsx-runtime";
const element = _jsx("h1", { children: "Hello, JSX!" });

    Babel/SWC automatically transforms JSX into these function calls, ensuring compatibility with JavaScript environments.

docs.title: 2. JSX Syntax and Expressions
docs.text:
• Embedding JavaScript Expressions JSX allows embedding JavaScript expressions within curly braces {}:

    const name = "Alice";
const element = <h1>Hello, {name}!</h1>;

  • Conditional Rendering with JSX JSX supports conditional rendering using JavaScript expressions. The simplest way is by using an if statement outside of JSX:

    let message;
if (isLoggedIn) {
  message = <h1>Welcome back!</h1>;
} else {
  message = <h1>Please log in.</h1>;
}

  • Using Ternary Operators and Logical && For inline conditions, the ternary operator is commonly used:

    const message = isLoggedIn ? <h1>Welcome back!</h1> : <h1>Please log in.</h1>;

    For simple conditions, the logical && operator can be used:

    const showMessage = isLoggedIn && <h1>Welcome back!</h1>;

docs.title: 3. Embedding JavaScript in JSX
docs.text:
• Using Variables Inside JSX You can directly use JavaScript variables inside JSX expressions:

    const title = "React Guide";
const header = <h1>{title}</h1>;

  • Inline Functions and Events JSX allows defining event handlers directly within elements:

    <button onClick={() => alert("Button clicked!")}>Click Me</button>

  • Template Literals in JSX You can use template literals to dynamically construct strings inside JSX:

    const user = "Alice";
const greeting = <h1>{`Hello, ${user}!`}</h1>;

docs.title: 4. JSX vs HTML Differences
docs.text:
• Self-Closing Tags, ClassName, and Attributes JSX requires self-closing tags for elements that don’t have children:

    <input type="text" />
<img src="image.jpg" alt="Image" />

    Since class is a reserved keyword in JavaScript, JSX uses className instead:

    <div className="container">Content</div>

    Attributes in JSX follow camelCase convention:

    <button onClick={handleClick}>Click</button>

  • Using Fragments in JSX JSX requires a single parent element, which can be cumbersome. Fragments help avoid unnecessary < div> wrappers:

    <>
  <h1>Title</h1>
  <p>Paragraph</p>
</>

    Alternatively, use : 

    <React.Fragment>
  <h1>Title</h1>
  <p>Paragraph</p>
</React.Fragment>

docs.title: 5. React.createElement() Behind the Scenes
docs.text:
• JSX Transpilation JSX is syntactic sugar for React.createElement(). The JSX code:

    const element = <h1>Hello, JSX!</h1>;

    is transpiled into:

    const element = React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello, JSX!");

  • Babel and Compilation Process Babel transforms JSX into valid JavaScript code using the React library. This ensures JSX can run in any JavaScript environment.

  • How JSX Converts to React Elements Each JSX element is turned into a call that produces a React element used to update the virtual DOM and render components efficiently. Under the legacy transform this is React.createElement(); under the automatic runtime (React 17+) it is the _jsx/_jsxs helpers imported from react/jsx-runtime. Either way the result is the same kind of React element — the automatic runtime just removes the need to import React in every file that uses JSX.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• JSX - JSX (JavaScript XML) is a syntax extension for JavaScript that looks similar to HTML.
  • Babel / SWC - JavaScript compilers that transpile JSX into JavaScript functions which create React elements.
  • Embedding expressions - JSX allows embedding JavaScript expressions within curly braces {}.
  • Conditional rendering - JSX supports conditional rendering using JavaScript expressions:
    • inline conditions -- ternary operator
    • simple conditions -- logical && operator
  • JSX vs HTML - self-closing tags, className (instead of class), and camelCase attributes.

Fragments let you group elements without adding an extra wrapper element to the DOM:

<></>
// or
<React.Fragment></React.Fragment>

JSX Transpilation -- JSX is syntactic sugar. Under the legacy transform it compiles to React.createElement():

const element = <h1>Hello, JSX!</h1>;

is transpiled into:

const element = React.createElement("h1", null, "Hello, JSX!");

Since React 17, the automatic JSX runtime is the default, so the compiler imports _jsx from react/jsx-runtime for you and import React is no longer required just for JSX.

docs.title: 1. Functional Components
docs.text: Functional components are simple JavaScript functions that return JSX. They are often used for stateless components.

docs.title: Stateless Components
docs.text:
Stateless components do not manage their own state. They only receive props and render UI accordingly.

const Greeting = ({ name }) => {
  return <h1>Hello, {name}!</h1>;
};

export default Greeting;

docs.title: Writing Function-Based Components
docs.text:
Functional components are concise and easier to read compared to class components.

function Welcome(props) {
  return <h1>Welcome, {props.user}!</h1>;
}

docs.title: Arrow Functions vs Regular Functions in Components
docs.text:
Arrow functions provide a more concise syntax, but both approaches work similarly.

const ArrowComponent = () => <p>Using an arrow function</p>;

function RegularComponent() {
  return <p>Using a regular function</p>;
}

docs.title: 2. Class Components
docs.text: Class components were the primary way to manage state before React Hooks were introduced.

docs.title: Understanding Component Classes
docs.text:
import React, { Component } from 'react';

class Hello extends Component {
  render() {
    return <h1>Hello, {this.props.name}!</h1>;
  }
}

docs.title: Lifecycle Methods in Class Components
docs.text:
Lifecycle methods allow components to run code at specific points in their lifecycle.

class LifecycleDemo extends Component {
  componentDidMount() {
    console.log('Component mounted');
  }

  render() {
    return <p>Lifecycle methods example</p>;
  }
}

docs.title: 3. Props and Prop Drilling
docs.text: Props allow components to pass data to each other.

docs.title: Passing Data Between Components
docs.text:
const Child = ({ message }) => <p>{message}</p>;

const Parent = () => <Child message="Hello from Parent!" />;

docs.title: Avoiding Prop Drilling with Context API
docs.text:
import { createContext, useContext } from 'react';

const MyContext = createContext();

const Parent = () => {
  return (
    <MyContext.Provider value="Hello from Context">
      <Child />
    </MyContext.Provider>
  );
};

const Child = () => {
  const value = useContext(MyContext);
  return <p>{value}</p>;
};

docs.title: Default Props and PropTypes
docs.text:
For function components, prefer default parameter values to set defaults. Component.defaultProps on a function component is deprecated as of React 19.

import PropTypes from 'prop-types';

// Use default parameters instead of Welcome.defaultProps
const Welcome = ({ name = 'Guest' }) => <h1>Welcome, {name}!</h1>;

Welcome.propTypes = {
  name: PropTypes.string,
};

docs.title: Best Practices for Reusable Components
docs.text:
• Keep components small and focused
  • Use props for configuration
  • Separate logic from UI

docs.title: Composing Components
docs.text:
const Header = () => <h1>Header</h1>;
const Footer = () => <h1>Footer</h1>;

const Page = () => (
  <div>
    <Header />
    <p>Page content</p>
    <Footer />
  </div>
);

docs.title: Smart (Container) vs Dumb (Presentational) Components
docs.text:
• Smart Components: Handle logic and state
  • Dumb Components: Only render UI
const DumbComponent = ({ text }) => <p>{text}</p>;

class SmartComponent extends Component {
  state = { text: 'Hello World' };

  render() {
    return <DumbComponent text={this.state.text} />;
  }
}

import React, { useState } from 'react';

const DumbComponent = ({ text }) => <p>{text}</p>;

const SmartComponent = () => {
  const [text, setText] = useState('Hello World');

  return <DumbComponent text={text} />;
};

docs.title: When to Use State in Components
docs.text: Use state when the component needs to track changes over time.
