    const { data } = await api.get("/me"); // pull fresh profile
    setUser(data.user);
  }, []);

  const logout = useCallback(async () => {
    await api.post("/logout"); // server clears the cookie
    setUser(null);
  }, []);

  const value = {
    user,
    isAuthenticated: !!user,
    isLoading,
    login,
    logout,
  };

  return <AuthContext.Provider value={value}>{children}</AuthContext.Provider>;
}

export function useAuth() {
  const ctx = useContext(AuthContext);
  if (!ctx) throw new Error("useAuth must be used within <AuthProvider>");
  return ctx;
}

Wrap your app once:

// main.jsx
<AuthProvider>
  <BrowserRouter>
    <App />
  </BrowserRouter>
</AuthProvider>

docs.title: Zustand Auth Store
docs.text:
For larger apps, a Zustand store avoids prop/context re-render concerns and is easy to read outside React (e.g. inside an axios interceptor).

// auth/useAuthStore.ts
import { create } from "zustand";
import api from "../api";

interface AuthState {
  user: User | null;
  isLoading: boolean;
  fetchMe: () => Promise<void>;
  login: (email: string, password: string) => Promise<void>;
  logout: () => Promise<void>;
}

export const useAuthStore = create<AuthState>((set) => ({
  user: null,
  isLoading: true,
  fetchMe: async () => {
    try {
      const { data } = await api.get("/me");
      set({ user: data.user });
    } catch {
      set({ user: null });
    } finally {
      set({ isLoading: false });
    }
  },
  login: async (email, password) => {
    await api.post("/login", { email, password });
    const { data } = await api.get("/me");
    set({ user: data.user });
  },
  logout: async () => {
    await api.post("/logout");
    set({ user: null });
  },
}));

docs.title: TanStack Query for Auth
docs.text:
/me is just server state, so TanStack Query is a natural fit — it gives you caching, isLoading, refetch-on-focus, and a cache to invalidate on logout for free.

// auth/useUser.ts
import { useQuery, useQueryClient, useMutation } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import api from "../api";

export function useUser() {
  return useQuery({
    queryKey: ["me"],
    queryFn: async () => (await api.get("/me")).data.user,
    retry: false,        // a 401 is an answer ("not logged in"), not a failure
    staleTime: 5 * 60_000,
  });
}

export function useLogout() {
  const qc = useQueryClient();
  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: () => api.post("/logout"),
    onSuccess: () => qc.clear(), // drop every cached query, including ["me"]
  });
}

function App() {
  const { data: user, isLoading } = useUser();
  if (isLoading) return <Splash />;
  return user ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />;
}

docs.title: Persisting Auth and Hydration
docs.text:
Don't persist the user object to localStorage

With HttpOnly cookies you do not need to persist auth yourself — the cookie is the persistence. On every page load, call /me; the cookie proves the session and the server returns the fresh profile. Mirroring the user into localStorage only risks showing stale data after a server-side revocation. The single source of truth is the cookie + the /me response.

The brief window between "page loaded" and "/me resolved" is hydration. Always render a loading state during it so you never flash the wrong UI:

function App() {
  const { isLoading } = useAuth();
  if (isLoading) return <FullPageSpinner />;
  return <Routes>{/* ... */}</Routes>;
}

docs.title: Protecting Routes in React
docs.text: With auth state in place, route guards become small wrapper components. These examples use React Router v6/v7.

docs.title: Private (Protected) Routes
docs.text:
Already shown in Route Protection Logic: wait for hydration, then render the page or redirect. Use the <Outlet /> form to guard a whole branch of nested routes at once:

// routes/RequireAuth.jsx
import { Navigate, Outlet, useLocation } from "react-router-dom";
import { useAuth } from "../auth/AuthContext";

export default function RequireAuth() {
  const { isAuthenticated, isLoading } = useAuth();
  const location = useLocation();

  if (isLoading) return <FullPageSpinner />;

  // Remember where the user was headed so we can return them after login.
  return isAuthenticated ? (
    <Outlet />
  ) : (
    <Navigate to="/login" replace state={{ from: location }} />
  );
}

docs.title: Public-Only Routes
docs.text:
Stop already-logged-in users from seeing /login or /register:

// routes/PublicOnly.jsx
import { Navigate, Outlet } from "react-router-dom";
import { useAuth } from "../auth/AuthContext";

export default function PublicOnly() {
  const { isAuthenticated, isLoading } = useAuth();
  if (isLoading) return null;
  return isAuthenticated ? <Navigate to="/dashboard" replace /> : <Outlet />;
}

docs.title: Role-Based Routes
docs.text:
Compose on top of RequireAuth to gate by role:

// routes/RequireRole.jsx
import { Navigate, Outlet } from "react-router-dom";
import { useAuth } from "../auth/AuthContext";

export default function RequireRole({ allow }) {
  const { user } = useAuth();
  const ok = user?.roles?.some((r) => allow.includes(r));
  return ok ? <Outlet /> : <Navigate to="/403" replace />;
}

docs.title: Wiring the Router
docs.text:
// App.jsx
<Routes>
  {/* public */}
  <Route element={<PublicOnly />}>
    <Route path="/login" element={<Login />} />
    <Route path="/register" element={<Register />} />
  </Route>

  {/* authenticated */}
  <Route element={<RequireAuth />}>
    <Route path="/dashboard" element={<Dashboard />} />
    <Route path="/profile" element={<Profile />} />

    {/* admin-only branch */}
    <Route element={<RequireRole allow={["admin"]} />}>
      <Route path="/admin" element={<AdminPanel />} />
    </Route>
  </Route>

  <Route path="/403" element={<Forbidden />} />
</Routes>

docs.title: Redirects with Return-To
docs.text:
After a successful login, send the user back to the page that bounced them:

// Login.jsx (relevant part)
import { useNavigate, useLocation } from "react-router-dom";
import { useAuth } from "../auth/AuthContext";

function Login() {
  const navigate = useNavigate();
  const location = useLocation();
  const { login } = useAuth();
  const from = location.state?.from?.pathname || "/dashboard";

  async function handleLogin(email, password) {
    await login(email, password);
    navigate(from, { replace: true }); // back where they wanted to go
  }
  // ...
}

docs.title: Attaching Credentials
docs.text:
With HttpOnly cookies you attach nothing manually — the browser sends the cookie as long as the axios instance opts in:

const api = axios.create({ baseURL: "/api", withCredentials: true });

If you are forced into the header approach (token in memory, not localStorage), attach it in a request interceptor — but cookies remain the safer default:

api.interceptors.request.use((config) => {
  const token = getAccessTokenFromMemory(); // module variable, never localStorage
  if (token) config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;
  return config;
});

docs.title: CSRF Protection with Cookies
docs.text:
Cookie auth is the right call for XSS safety, but cookies are sent automatically on cross-site requests too — which is exactly what CSRF exploits. Two defenses, used together:

  1. SameSite=Strict (or Lax) on the auth cookie blocks most cross-site sends. This alone covers a lot of cases.
  2. Double-submit CSRF token for state-changing requests: the server sets a readable (non-HttpOnly) XSRF-TOKEN cookie; the client echoes it back in a header. axios does this automatically via xsrfCookieName / xsrfHeaderName.
const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: "/api",
  withCredentials: true,
  xsrfCookieName: "XSRF-TOKEN",   // axios reads this cookie...
  xsrfHeaderName: "X-XSRF-TOKEN", // ...and sends it as this header
});

Why double-submit works

An attacker's site can trigger a request that carries your cookies, but it cannot read the XSRF-TOKEN cookie value (same-origin policy) to place it in the header. The server requires header == cookie, so forged requests fail.

docs.title: Refresh-Token Rotation and the Stampede Problem
docs.text:
A secure setup uses a short-lived access token and a long-lived refresh token, and rotates the refresh token on every use (each /refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one — a reused old token signals theft and revokes the whole family).

The interceptor from Axios Auto-Refresh Example already retries safely once. The remaining issue: if ten requests fail with 401 at the same instant, you fire ten /refresh calls. Queue them behind one shared promise instead:

// api.js (refresh queue)
let refreshPromise = null;

api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    const original = error.config;
    const is401 = error.response?.status === 401;
    const isRefresh = original?.url?.includes("/refresh");

    if (is401 && !original._retry && !isRefresh) {
      original._retry = true;

      // Reuse one in-flight refresh for all concurrent failures.
      refreshPromise = refreshPromise ?? api.post("/refresh");
      try {
        await refreshPromise;
        return api(original);
      } catch (e) {
        window.location.assign("/login");
        return Promise.reject(e);
      } finally {
        refreshPromise = null; // reset for the next expiry cycle
      }
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

docs.title: Token Storage Tradeoffs
docs.text:
Storage XSS-readable? CSRF-exposed? Verdict localStorage / sessionStorage ✔️ Yes (any injected script reads it) ❌ No Avoid In-memory (JS variable) Only while running; lost on refresh ❌ No OK for access token HttpOnly cookie ❌ No ✔️ Yes → mitigate with SameSite + CSRF token Recommended

The practical winner: access + refresh tokens in HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies, with CSRF defenses for mutations.

docs.title: XSS — the Real Threat to Tokens
docs.text:
If an attacker can run JavaScript in your origin, HttpOnly cookies save your token but the session is still abusable (the attacker rides the cookie via your own api instance). So XSS prevention is non-negotiable:

  • React escapes by default — never use dangerouslySetInnerHTML with untrusted input; sanitize with DOMPurify if you must.
  • Apply a strict Content-Security-Policy header.
  • Audit third-party scripts; they run with full access to your origin.

docs.title: Short-Lived Access + Refresh Tokens
docs.text:
• Access token: 5–15 minutes. Limits the damage window if it leaks.
  • Refresh token: days/weeks, rotated on every use, revocable server-side.
  • Refresh transparently via the interceptor; the user never notices.

docs.title: Logout and Revocation
docs.text:
Because the token is HttpOnly, the client cannot delete it — logout must hit the server, which clears the cookie and invalidates the refresh token.

// server logout
app.post("/logout", (req, res) => {
  revokeRefreshToken(req.cookies.refreshToken); // kill it server-side
  res.clearCookie("token");
  res.clearCookie("refreshToken");
  res.json({ message: "Logged out" });
});

Stateless JWTs can't be force-revoked

A signed access token is valid until it expires, even after "logout." Keep access tokens short, and maintain server-side state for refresh tokens (a denylist or rotation family) so a logout/breach response is immediate.

docs.title: Authentication Libraries for React
docs.text:
Rolling your own auth is instructive (next section) but error-prone. For production, a managed provider handles rotation, MFA, social login, and breach response for you.

Library Best for Notes Auth.js / NextAuth Next.js apps Open-source, self-hosted sessions, dozens of OAuth providers; pairs with HttpOnly cookies out of the box Clerk Fast B2C/SaaS startup Prebuilt React components (<SignIn />, <UserButton />), orgs/multi-tenancy, generous DX Auth0 Enterprise / B2B Mature, SAML/SSO, fine-grained rules; via Okta Firebase Auth Mobile + web, Google stack Simple SDK, social + phone auth, free tier Supabase Auth Postgres-centric apps Open-source, row-level security ties auth to the DB AWS Cognito / SuperTokens AWS shops / self-hosting Cognito = AWS-native; SuperTokens = open-source, self-host control 
// Clerk — guarding UI is declarative
import { SignedIn, SignedOut, SignInButton, UserButton } from "@clerk/clerk-react";

<header>
  <SignedOut><SignInButton /></SignedOut>
  <SignedIn><UserButton /></SignedIn>
</header>

When to build vs buy

Buy if you need MFA, SSO, compliance, or social login soon — security is not where you want hand-rolled bugs. Build only when requirements are simple and you want full control (and are ready to own rotation and revocation).

docs.title: Building a Custom Auth System
docs.text: A complete email/password flow tying the chapter together: context for state, cookies for tokens, an interceptor for refresh, and guarded routes.

docs.title: 1. The axios instance
docs.text:
// api.js — single shared instance (interceptor from §Secure API Communication)
import axios from "axios";
export default axios.create({ baseURL: "/api", withCredentials: true });

docs.title: 2. Signup with validation
docs.text:
// Register.jsx
import { useState } from "react";
import api from "./api";
import { useNavigate } from "react-router-dom";

export default function Register() {
  const [form, setForm] = useState({ email: "", password: "" });
  const [error, setError] = useState(null);
  const navigate = useNavigate();

  function validate({ email, password }) {
    if (!/^[^@\s]+@[^@\s]+\.[^@\s]+$/.test(email)) return "Invalid email";
    if (password.length < 8) return "Password must be 8+ characters";
    return null;
  }

  async function handleSubmit(e) {
    e.preventDefault();
    const msg = validate(form);
    if (msg) return setError(msg);
    try {
      await api.post("/register", form); // server creates user + sets cookies
      navigate("/dashboard");
    } catch (err) {
      setError(err.response?.data?.message ?? "Registration failed");
    }
  }

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>
      <input
        type="email"
        value={form.email}
        onChange={(e) => setForm({ ...form, email: e.target.value })}
        placeholder="email"
      />
      <input
        type="password"
        value={form.password}
        onChange={(e) => setForm({ ...form, password: e.target.value })}
        placeholder="password"
      />
      {error && <p role="alert">{error}</p>}
      <button type="submit">Create account</button>
    </form>
  );
}

docs.title: 3. Global state + auto-login on refresh
docs.text: Use the AuthProvider from State Management for Auth. Its useEffect call to /me is the auto-login: on every page load the HttpOnly cookie is validated and the user is restored — no extra code, nothing in localStorage.

docs.title: 4. Login
docs.text:
// Login.jsx — uses login() + return-to from earlier sections
const { login } = useAuth();
await login(email, password);
navigate(location.state?.from?.pathname || "/dashboard", { replace: true });

docs.title: 5. Logout
docs.text:
function LogoutButton() {
  const { logout } = useAuth();
  const navigate = useNavigate();
  return (
    <button
      onClick={async () => {
        await logout();          // server clears + revokes
        navigate("/login", { replace: true });
      }}
    >
      Log out
    </button>
  );
}

That's an end-to-end system: register → cookie set → state hydrated via /me → routes guarded → access token auto-refreshed → logout revokes server-side.

docs.title: Token Expiry Mid-Session
docs.text: The user is filling a form when the access token expires. They should not be kicked out — the refresh interceptor transparently mints a new token and replays the failed request. Only if refresh also fails do you redirect to login. This is exactly the behaviour built in Secure API Communication.

docs.title: Multi-Tab Synchronization
docs.text:
Log out in one tab and the others should follow. Broadcast the change and have every tab listen — BroadcastChannel (or a localStorage "signal" key for the classic storage-event approach):

// auth/useAuthSync.js
import { useEffect } from "react";
import { useAuth } from "./AuthContext";

const channel = new BroadcastChannel("auth");

export function broadcastLogout() {
  channel.postMessage("logout");
}

export function useAuthSync() {
  const { logout } = useAuth();
  useEffect(() => {
    const onMessage = (e) => {
      if (e.data === "logout") logout(); // clears state in THIS tab too
    };
    channel.addEventListener("message", onMessage);
    return () => channel.removeEventListener("message", onMessage);
  }, [logout]);
}

storage-event fallback

Older browsers without BroadcastChannel can flip a flag — localStorage.setItem("logout", Date.now()) — and listen via window.addEventListener("storage", ...). The storage event fires in other tabs only, which is precisely what cross-tab sync needs. Store a timestamp signal, never the token itself.

docs.title: Silent Refresh
docs.text:
Rather than wait for a 401, refresh proactively just before expiry so the user never sees a failed request. Schedule it from the access token's lifetime (returned by /login//me, e.g. expiresIn):

// auth/scheduleSilentRefresh.js
import api from "../api";

export function scheduleSilentRefresh(expiresInMs, onFail) {
  // refresh 60s before the access token actually expires
  const delay = Math.max(expiresInMs - 60_000, 0);
  return setTimeout(async () => {
    try {
      await api.post("/refresh");
    } catch {
      onFail?.();
    }
  }, delay);
}

docs.title: Preventing the Protected-UI Flash
docs.text: Never render protected content before hydration finishes. Gate on isLoading (see Persisting Auth and Hydration) so the dashboard never flickers for a split second before a redirect.

docs.title: OAuth 2.0 / OIDC Flow Overview
docs.text:
For third-party login, the modern, SPA-safe flow is Authorization Code with PKCE. The token exchange should happen on your backend so client secrets and tokens never touch the browser:

1. User clicks "Login with Google"
2. Browser → redirected to Google's consent screen (with PKCE code_challenge)
3. User approves → Google redirects back to /callback?code=...
4. Your BACKEND exchanges code (+ code_verifier) for tokens
5. Backend sets your own HttpOnly session cookie
6. Browser is now logged into YOUR app (no provider token in JS)

Don't do the token exchange in the browser

The SPA's job is to start the redirect and hand the code to your backend. Letting the frontend hold the provider's access token reintroduces the very XSS exposure HttpOnly cookies were chosen to avoid.

docs.title: Social Login (button → backend)
docs.text:
// the frontend just kicks off the server-driven flow
function GoogleLoginButton() {
  return (
    <a href="/api/auth/google">
      <button>Continue with Google</button>
    </a>
  );
}
// /api/auth/google (backend) redirects to Google;
// /api/auth/google/callback (backend) finishes the exchange and sets the cookie.

docs.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
docs.text: SSO lets one identity provider authenticate the user across many apps (typically SAML or OIDC in enterprises). To the React app it looks like any OAuth login — you redirect to the IdP and receive a session cookie back; the IdP keeps the user signed in across the suite.

docs.title: Authorization Guards as HOCs
docs.text:
A higher-order component is an alternative to wrapper routes when you want to guard a component anywhere it's used:

// auth/withRole.jsx
import { useAuth } from "./AuthContext";
import { Navigate } from "react-router-dom";

export function withRole(Component, allowed) {
  return function Guarded(props) {
    const { user, isLoading } = useAuth();
    if (isLoading) return <FullPageSpinner />;
    const ok = user?.roles?.some((r) => allowed.includes(r));
    return ok ? <Component {...props} /> : <Navigate to="/403" replace />;
  };
}

const AdminPanel = withRole(AdminPanelBase, ["admin"]);

docs.title: Server-Side Authorization (Next.js)
docs.text:
In Next.js App Router, check auth in a Server Component / middleware before the page renders — no flash, no client-side bypass:

// app/admin/page.tsx (Server Component)
import { cookies } from "next/headers";
import { redirect } from "next/navigation";
import { verifySession } from "@/lib/auth";

export default async function AdminPage() {
  const session = await verifySession(cookies().get("token")?.value);
  if (!session || !session.roles.includes("admin")) redirect("/login");
  return <AdminDashboard />;
}

docs.title: Testing Auth
docs.text: Use Vitest/Jest + React Testing Library for components and MSW to mock the auth API. The goal: assert behaviour (redirects, what renders) without a real backend.

docs.title: Mocking Auth State
docs.text:
Render components inside a fake provider so you control isAuthenticated/user:

// test-utils.jsx
import { AuthContext } from "../auth/AuthContext";

export function renderWithAuth(ui, { auth } = {}) {
  const value = {
    user: null,
    isAuthenticated: false,
    isLoading: false,
    login: vi.fn(),
    logout: vi.fn(),
    ...auth,
  };
  return render(
    <AuthContext.Provider value={value}>{ui}</AuthContext.Provider>
  );
}

Note

Export the AuthContext object (not just the provider) so tests can supply their own value directly.

docs.title: Protected-Route Tests
docs.text:
// RequireAuth.test.jsx
import { screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import { MemoryRouter, Routes, Route } from "react-router-dom";
import RequireAuth from "../routes/RequireAuth";
import { renderWithAuth } from "../test-utils";

function setup(auth) {
  return renderWithAuth(
    <MemoryRouter initialEntries={["/dashboard"]}>
      <Routes>
        <Route element={<RequireAuth />}>
          <Route path="/dashboard" element={<p>Dashboard</p>} />
        </Route>
        <Route path="/login" element={<p>Login Page</p>} />
      </Routes>
    </MemoryRouter>,
    { auth }
  );
}

test("redirects anonymous users to login", () => {
  setup({ isAuthenticated: false });
  expect(screen.getByText("Login Page")).toBeInTheDocument();
});

test("renders the page for authenticated users", () => {
  setup({ isAuthenticated: true, user: { id: 1 } });
  expect(screen.getByText("Dashboard")).toBeInTheDocument();
});

test("shows a spinner while auth is hydrating", () => {
  setup({ isLoading: true });
  expect(screen.queryByText("Dashboard")).not.toBeInTheDocument();
  expect(screen.queryByText("Login Page")).not.toBeInTheDocument();
});

docs.title: Mocking the Auth API with MSW
docs.text:
For integration tests of the real AuthProvider, intercept HTTP instead of faking context:

// mocks/handlers.js
import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw";

export const handlers = [
  http.get("/api/me", () =>
    HttpResponse.json({ user: { id: 1, roles: ["admin"] } })
  ),
  http.post("/api/login", () => HttpResponse.json({ ok: true })),
  http.post("/api/logout", () => HttpResponse.json({ ok: true })),
];

// return a 401 in a specific test to assert the logged-out path:
// server.use(http.get("/api/me", () => new HttpResponse(null, { status: 401 })));

Test the loop, not the token

You can't read HttpOnly cookies in tests either — and that's fine. Assert the observable behaviour: a 401 from /me shows the login screen, a 200 shows the dashboard, and a mid-session 401 triggers exactly one /refresh then a retry.

docs.title: What to Cover
docs.text:
• useAuth throws outside its provider.
  • Login success navigates to the return-to path.
  • Logout clears state and the TanStack Query cache.
  • The refresh interceptor retries once and stops on a /refresh 401.
  • Role guards redirect to /403 for the wrong role.

docs.title: Advanced API Scenarios
docs.text:
This chapter expands on §4 of the API Integration outline. The basic CRUD patterns get you fetching and mutating data; the patterns here keep that data fetching fast, resilient, and cheap under real-world conditions — flaky networks, rate limits, rapid user input, large datasets, and offline devices.

Two stacks run side by side throughout:

  • Plain axios v1 + hooks — the manual, "know what's happening" version.
  • TanStack Query v5 — the declarative version that bakes most of these scenarios in for free.

Stack assumptions

  • React 18/19 functional components and hooks only.
  • axios v1 — we use the standard AbortController / signal, not the deprecated CancelToken.
  • TanStack Query v5 — note the renamed/changed APIs: gcTime (was cacheTime), object-form filters, the new useInfiniteQuery signature, and placeholderData: keepPreviousData (which replaced keepPreviousData: true).

All examples assume the shared axios instance from the previous chapters:

// src/api/axiosClient.js
import axios from "axios";

const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL || "https://api.example.com",
  timeout: 10000,
  headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
});

export default api;

docs.title: 1. Retry Logic with Exponential Backoff
docs.text: Transient failures — a dropped connection, a 503 during a deploy, a brief 429 — usually succeed on a second try. Retrying blindly hammers the server, so we add exponential backoff (each wait doubles) plus jitter (a small random offset) so that many clients retrying at once don't synchronize into a thundering herd.

docs.title: A reusable retry helper
docs.text:
// src/api/withRetry.ts
import axios, { AxiosError, AxiosRequestConfig } from "axios";
import api from "./axiosClient";

const sleep = (ms: number) => new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, ms));

// Only retry on transient conditions, never on 4xx (except 429).
function isRetryable(error: unknown): boolean {
  if (!axios.isAxiosError(error)) return false;
  const err = error as AxiosError;
  if (err.code === "ERR_CANCELED") return false; // respect cancellation
  const status = err.response?.status;
  if (status === undefined) return true; // network error / timeout
  return status === 429 || status === 408 || (status >= 500 && status <= 599);
}

export async function requestWithRetry<T>(
  config: AxiosRequestConfig,
  { retries = 3, baseDelay = 500, maxDelay = 8000 } = {}
): Promise<T> {
  let attempt = 0;
  // eslint-disable-next-line no-constant-condition
  while (true) {
    try {
      const res = await api.request<T>(config);
      return res.data;
    } catch (error) {
      if (attempt >= retries || !isRetryable(error)) throw error;

      // Honour a server-provided Retry-After header if present.
      const retryAfter = axios.isAxiosError(error)
        ? Number(error.response?.headers?.["retry-after"])
        : NaN;

      const backoff = Math.min(baseDelay * 2 ** attempt, maxDelay);
      const jitter = Math.random() * backoff * 0.25; // ±25% jitter
      const wait = Number.isFinite(retryAfter) ? retryAfter * 1000 : backoff + jitter;

      await sleep(wait);
      attempt += 1;
    }
  }
}

Usage:

const users = await requestWithRetry<User[]>({ url: "/users", method: "get" });

Never retry non-idempotent writes blindly

GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent — safe to retry. A POST may create a duplicate. Retry writes only when the endpoint supports an idempotency key (a unique header the server uses to dedupe), e.g. headers: { "Idempotency-Key": crypto.randomUUID() }.

docs.title: With TanStack Query
docs.text:
Query handles retries declaratively. retry is a count or a predicate, and retryDelay computes the backoff.

import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

useQuery({
  queryKey: ["users"],
  queryFn: () => api.get("/users").then((r) => r.data),
  retry: (failureCount, error) => {
    const status = axios.isAxiosError(error) ? error.response?.status : undefined;
    if (status && status >= 400 && status < 500 && status !== 429) return false;
    return failureCount < 3;
  },
  retryDelay: (attempt) => Math.min(1000 * 2 ** attempt, 30_000),
});

docs.title: 2. Canceling Requests with AbortController
docs.text: When a component unmounts mid-flight, or a user fires a new search before the last finishes, the stale response is at best wasted work and at worst a race condition that overwrites fresh data. axios v1 supports the web-standard AbortController through the signal option.

docs.title: Cancel on unmount
docs.text:
import { useEffect, useState } from "react";
import axios from "axios";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function UserList() {
  const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);

  useEffect(() => {
    const controller = new AbortController();

    api
      .get("/users", { signal: controller.signal })
      .then((res) => setUsers(res.data))
      .catch((err) => {
        if (axios.isCancel(err)) return; // expected on unmount
        console.error(err);
      });

    return () => controller.abort(); // cleanup cancels in-flight request
  }, []);

  return <ul>{users.map((u) => <li key={u.id}>{u.name}</li>)}</ul>;
}

Detecting a cancellation in axios v1

Use axios.isCancel(err), or check err.code === "ERR_CANCELED" / err.name === "CanceledError". Always treat it as a non-error — bail out silently rather than showing the user a failure.

docs.title: Cancel the previous request (live search)
docs.text:
Keep a ref to the active controller and abort it before starting the next call, so only the latest query can win:

import { useRef, useState } from "react";
import axios from "axios";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function useLiveSearch() {
  const [results, setResults] = useState([]);
  const controllerRef = useRef(null);

  const search = async (query) => {
    controllerRef.current?.abort();          // cancel the previous request
    const controller = new AbortController();
    controllerRef.current = controller;

    try {
      const res = await api.get("/search", {
        params: { q: query },
        signal: controller.signal,
      });
      setResults(res.data);
    } catch (err) {
      if (!axios.isCancel(err)) throw err;
    }
  };

  return { results, search };
}

TanStack Query cancels for you

Query passes a signal into your queryFn. Forward it to axios and Query will automatically abort superseded or unmounted queries: 

useQuery({
  queryKey: ["search", q],
  queryFn: ({ signal }) => api.get("/search", { params: { q }, signal }).then((r) => r.data),
});

docs.title: 3. Debouncing & Throttling API Calls
docs.text:
Both limit how often a function runs, but with different timing:

Technique Fires… Use for Debounce once, after activity stops for N ms search-as-you-type, autosave Throttle at most once per N ms during activity scroll/resize handlers, rapid clicks

docs.title: A debounce hook
docs.text:
Debounce the value, then drive the request off the debounced value — this plays nicely with React's render model and avoids stale closures.

import { useEffect, useState } from "react";

function useDebouncedValue(value, delay = 400) {
  const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

  useEffect(() => {
    const id = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
    return () => clearTimeout(id); // reset timer on every keystroke
  }, [value, delay]);

  return debounced;
}

import { useEffect, useState } from "react";
import axios from "axios";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function SearchBox() {
  const [term, setTerm] = useState("");
  const debouncedTerm = useDebouncedValue(term, 400);
  const [results, setResults] = useState([]);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (!debouncedTerm) {
      setResults([]);
      return;
    }
    const controller = new AbortController();

    api
      .get("/search", { params: { q: debouncedTerm }, signal: controller.signal })
      .then((res) => setResults(res.data))
      .catch((err) => {
        if (!axios.isCancel(err)) console.error(err);
      });

    return () => controller.abort(); // cancel if term changes again
  }, [debouncedTerm]);

  return (
    <>
      <input value={term} onChange={(e) => setTerm(e.target.value)} />
      <ul>{results.map((r) => <li key={r.id}>{r.title}</li>)}</ul>
    </>
  );
}

Debounce + cancel together

Debouncing cuts the number of requests; AbortController cancels any that are still in flight when a newer one starts. Use both for live search — they solve different halves of the problem.

docs.title: A throttle helper
docs.text:
function throttle(fn, limit = 1000) {
  let waiting = false;
  let lastArgs = null;

  return function throttled(...args) {
    if (waiting) {
      lastArgs = args; // remember the latest call to run on the trailing edge
      return;
    }
    fn.apply(this, args);
    waiting = true;
    setTimeout(() => {
      waiting = false;
      if (lastArgs) {
        throttled.apply(this, lastArgs);
        lastArgs = null;
      }
    }, limit);
  };
}

With TanStack Query, debounce only the query key input (state), not the query itself — Query then dedupes and caches per debounced key automatically.

docs.title: Parallel — Promise.all vs Promise.allSettled
docs.text:
Run independent requests concurrently instead of one after another.

// Fail-fast: rejects as soon as ANY request fails.
export const getDashboard = async () => {
  const [users, posts, products] = await Promise.all([
    api.get("/users"),
    api.get("/posts"),
    api.get("/products"),
  ]);
  return { users: users.data, posts: posts.data, products: products.data };
};

// Resilient: never rejects — inspect each result individually.
export const getDashboardSafe = async () => {
  const results = await Promise.allSettled([
    api.get("/users"),
    api.get("/posts"),
    api.get("/products"),
  ]);

  return results.map((r) =>
    r.status === "fulfilled"
      ? { ok: true, data: r.value.data }
      : { ok: false, error: r.reason }
  );
};

Choosing between them

Use Promise.all when the page is useless without every piece (fail fast). Use Promise.allSettled for dashboards where each widget can succeed or fail on its own.

docs.title: Dependent / sequential requests
docs.text:
When request B needs data from request A, you must await A first.

export const getUserWithLatestOrder = async (userId) => {
  const { data: user } = await api.get(`/users/${userId}`);
  const { data: orders } = await api.get("/orders", {
    params: { userId: user.id, _sort: "createdAt", _order: "desc", _limit: 1 },
  });
  return { ...user, latestOrder: orders[0] ?? null };
};

In TanStack Query, express dependency with the enabled flag — the second query stays idle until the first resolves:

const { data: user } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ["user", userId],
  queryFn: () => api.get(`/users/${userId}`).then((r) => r.data),
});

const { data: orders } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ["orders", user?.id],
  queryFn: () => api.get("/orders", { params: { userId: user.id } }).then((r) => r.data),
  enabled: !!user?.id, // gate on the dependency
});

For many parallel queries that vary, use useQueries so each gets its own cache entry:

import { useQueries } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const results = useQueries({
  queries: ids.map((id) => ({
    queryKey: ["product", id],
    queryFn: () => api.get(`/products/${id}`).then((r) => r.data),
  })),
});

docs.title: 5. Conditional Requests
docs.text:
Don't fetch when you don't need to. Two flavours:

Client-side gating — skip the call until prerequisites are met. With raw hooks, guard inside the effect; with Query, use enabled:

useQuery({
  queryKey: ["profile", token],
  queryFn: () => api.get("/me").then((r) => r.data),
  enabled: !!token, // only fetch once authenticated
});

HTTP conditional requests — let the server skip the work. Send the ETag/Last-Modified you got last time; a 304 Not Modified means "use your cached copy," saving bandwidth.

let lastETag = null;

export const getConfigIfChanged = async () => {
  const res = await api.get("/config", {
    headers: lastETag ? { "If-None-Match": lastETag } : {},
    validateStatus: (s) => s === 200 || s === 304, // treat 304 as success
  });
  if (res.status === 304) return null; // unchanged — keep current data
  lastETag = res.headers.etag;
  return res.data;
};

docs.title: 6. Pagination & Infinite Scroll
docs.text:
Two server contracts dominate. Pick based on how the API exposes "the next set."

  • Offset / page-based — ?page=2&limit=20 or ?_start=20&_limit=20. Simple, jumpable, but can skip/duplicate rows if data shifts between pages.
  • Cursor-based — ?cursor=abc123&limit=20. The server returns an opaque nextCursor; stable under inserts/deletes and the right choice for feeds.

docs.title: Offset pagination with "keep previous data"
docs.text:
When the user clicks "next," show the current page until the new one arrives instead of flashing a spinner. In v5 this is placeholderData: keepPreviousData.

import { useState } from "react";
import { useQuery, keepPreviousData } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function getPosts(page: number, limit = 10) {
  return api
    .get("/posts", { params: { _page: page, _limit: limit } })
    .then((r) => ({
      items: r.data,
      total: Number(r.headers["x-total-count"]) || 0,
    }));
}

export function PostsTable() {
  const [page, setPage] = useState(1);

  const { data, isPlaceholderData, isPending } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["posts", { page }],          // object filter — v5 style
    queryFn: () => getPosts(page),
    placeholderData: keepPreviousData,      // v5 replacement for keepPreviousData: true
    staleTime: 30_000,
  });

  if (isPending) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  const hasMore = page * 10 < (data?.total ?? 0);

  return (
    <>
      <ul>{data.items.map((p) => <li key={p.id}>{p.title}</li>)}</ul>
      <button disabled={page === 1} onClick={() => setPage((p) => p - 1)}>
        Prev
      </button>
      <button
        disabled={isPlaceholderData || !hasMore}
        onClick={() => setPage((p) => p + 1)}
      >
        Next
      </button>
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: Cursor pagination + infinite scroll (useInfiniteQuery v5)
docs.text:
The v5 signature requires initialPageParam and gives getNextPageParam the signature (lastPage, allPages, lastPageParam, allPageParams).

import { useInfiniteQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

type FeedPage = { items: Post[]; nextCursor: string | null };

function fetchFeed({ pageParam }: { pageParam: string | null }): Promise<FeedPage> {
  return api
    .get("/feed", { params: { cursor: pageParam, limit: 20 } })
    .then((r) => r.data); // { items, nextCursor }
}

export function useFeed() {
  return useInfiniteQuery({
    queryKey: ["feed"],
    queryFn: fetchFeed,
    initialPageParam: null as string | null,   // required in v5
    getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextCursor ?? undefined, // undefined = no more pages
  });
}

Wire it to an IntersectionObserver sentinel so scrolling loads the next page:

import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";
import { useFeed } from "./useFeed";

export function Feed() {
  const { data, fetchNextPage, hasNextPage, isFetchingNextPage } = useFeed();
  const sentinelRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    const node = sentinelRef.current;
    if (!node) return;

    const observer = new IntersectionObserver(
      ([entry]) => {
        if (entry.isIntersecting && hasNextPage && !isFetchingNextPage) {
          fetchNextPage();
        }
      },
      { rootMargin: "200px" } // prefetch slightly before the user reaches the end
    );

    observer.observe(node);
    return () => observer.disconnect();
  }, [hasNextPage, isFetchingNextPage, fetchNextPage]);

  return (
    <>
      {data?.pages.flatMap((page) =>
        page.items.map((post) => <article key={post.id}>{post.title}</article>)
      )}
      <div ref={sentinelRef} />
      {isFetchingNextPage && <p>Loading more…</p>}
    </>
  );
}

docs.title: 7. Optimistic Updates & Rollback
docs.text: Apply the change to the UI immediately, before the server confirms, then roll back if the request fails. This makes likes, toggles, and reorders feel instant.

docs.title: Manual optimistic update
docs.text:
import { useState } from "react";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function LikeButton({ post }) {
  const [likes, setLikes] = useState(post.likes);
  const [pending, setPending] = useState(false);

  const like = async () => {
    const previous = likes;
    setLikes(previous + 1); // optimistic
    setPending(true);
    try {
      await api.patch(`/posts/${post.id}`, { likes: previous + 1 });
    } catch (err) {
      setLikes(previous); // rollback on failure
      console.error(err);
    } finally {
      setPending(false);
    }
  };

  return <button disabled={pending} onClick={like}>♥ {likes}</button>;
}

docs.title: Optimistic mutation with TanStack Query
docs.text:
The canonical pattern: cancel in-flight queries, snapshot the cache, write the optimistic value, restore the snapshot in onError, and resync in onSettled.

import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

function useToggleLike(postId: number) {
  const qc = useQueryClient();

  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: (liked: boolean) =>
      api.patch(`/posts/${postId}`, { liked }).then((r) => r.data),

    onMutate: async (liked) => {
      await qc.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ["post", postId] }); // avoid races
      const previous = qc.getQueryData(["post", postId]);     // snapshot
      qc.setQueryData(["post", postId], (old: any) => ({ ...old, liked })); // optimistic
      return { previous };                                    // context for rollback
    },

    onError: (_err, _liked, context) => {
      qc.setQueryData(["post", postId], context?.previous);   // rollback
    },

    onSettled: () => {
      qc.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["post", postId] });   // resync with server
    },
  });
}

Always cancel before snapshotting

Without cancelQueries, a refetch that resolves after your optimistic write can clobber it with stale server data. Cancelling first guarantees your optimistic value is the latest write to the cache.

docs.title: 8. Request Deduplication & Caching
docs.text: If three components mount at once and each asks for /users, you want one network request shared by all three. This is deduplication; caching extends it across time.

docs.title: Manual in-flight dedup
docs.text:
Cache the promise, not just the result, so concurrent callers await the same request:

const inflight = new Map<string, Promise<any>>();

export function dedupedGet<T>(url: string, config = {}): Promise<T> {
  const key = url + JSON.stringify(config);
  if (inflight.has(key)) return inflight.get(key)!;

  const promise = api
    .get<T>(url, config)
    .then((r) => r.data)
    .finally(() => inflight.delete(key)); // clear once settled

  inflight.set(key, promise);
  return promise;
}

docs.title: TanStack Query does this by default
docs.text:
Query dedupes by queryKey automatically, then caches results. The two timing knobs to understand:

useQuery({
  queryKey: ["users"],
  queryFn: () => api.get("/users").then((r) => r.data),
  staleTime: 60_000, // data is "fresh" for 60s — no refetch on remount/focus
  gcTime: 5 * 60_000, // unused data is garbage-collected after 5min (was cacheTime in v4)
});

staleTime vs gcTime

  • staleTime — how long data is considered fresh; while fresh, Query serves the cache and skips refetching. Default 0.
  • gcTime — how long an inactive (unobserved) cache entry survives before being garbage-collected. Default 5 minutes. Renamed from cacheTime in v5.

docs.title: 9. Polling vs Long-Polling
docs.text:
Both keep data fresh without WebSockets.

  • Polling (short polling) — ask on a fixed interval ("any updates?"). Simple, but wastes requests when nothing changed.
  • Long-polling — the server holds the request open until there's data (or a timeout), then you immediately reconnect. Fewer empty round-trips, near real-time, but ties up a connection.

docs.title: Polling with TanStack Query
docs.text:
useQuery({
  queryKey: ["job", jobId],
  queryFn: () => api.get(`/jobs/${jobId}`).then((r) => r.data),
  // Poll every 3s until the job finishes, then stop.
  refetchInterval: (query) =>
    query.state.data?.status === "done" ? false : 3000,
  refetchIntervalInBackground: false, // pause when the tab is hidden
});

docs.title: Long-polling loop
docs.text:
import axios from "axios";
import api from "../api/axiosClient";

export async function longPoll(onMessage, { signal } = {}) {
  let cursor = 0;
  while (!signal?.aborted) {
    try {
      const { data } = await api.get("/updates", {
        params: { since: cursor },
        timeout: 30_000, // longer than usual — the server holds the request
        signal,
      });
      if (data.events?.length) {
        onMessage(data.events);
        cursor = data.cursor;
      }
    } catch (err) {
      if (axios.isCancel(err)) break;
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, 1000)); // back off before reconnecting
    }
  }
}

When to reach for WebSockets/SSE instead

Polling is fine for low-frequency updates (job status, notifications). For high-frequency, bidirectional streams (chat, live cursors), use WebSockets; for one-way server pushes, Server-Sent Events. See the Real-Time Data chapter.

docs.title: 10. Rate Limiting & 429 Handling
docs.text:
A 429 Too Many Requests means you've exceeded the server's quota. The response usually carries a Retry-After header (seconds, or an HTTP date) telling you when to try again — respect it.

import axios from "axios";
import api from "./axiosClient";

api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    if (axios.isAxiosError(error) && error.response?.status === 429 && error.config) {
      const header = error.response.headers["retry-after"];
      const waitMs = parseRetryAfter(header) ?? 1000;
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, waitMs));
      return api.request(error.config); // replay the original request once
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

function parseRetryAfter(value?: string): number | null {
  if (!value) return null;
  const seconds = Number(value);
  if (!Number.isNaN(seconds)) return seconds * 1000; // "120"
  const date = Date.parse(value);                    // "Wed, 21 Oct 2025 07:28:00 GMT"
  return Number.isNaN(date) ? null : Math.max(0, date - Date.now());
}

To avoid hitting the limit, cap concurrency on the client with a small semaphore so you never have more than N requests in flight:

function pLimit(concurrency) {
  let active = 0;
  const queue = [];

  const next = () => {
    if (active >= concurrency || queue.length === 0) return;
    active++;
    const { fn, resolve, reject } = queue.shift();
    fn().then(resolve, reject).finally(() => {
      active--;
      next();
    });
  };

  return (fn) =>
    new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      queue.push({ fn, resolve, reject });
      next();
    });
}

// Usage: at most 4 concurrent uploads
const limit = pLimit(4);
await Promise.all(files.map((f) => limit(() => api.post("/upload", f))));

Combine 429 handling with backoff

A single retry on 429 can still fail under sustained load. For robustness, route 429 through the exponential-backoff helper from §1, which already honours Retry-After.

docs.title: 11. Error Normalization & a Global Error Handler
docs.text:
axios errors come in three shapes — error.response (server replied with a non-2xx), error.request (sent, no reply), and a setup error (neither). Code shouldn't repeat that triage everywhere. Normalize once into a predictable shape, in a response interceptor.

// src/api/errors.ts
import axios, { AxiosError } from "axios";

export type ApiError = {
  status: number | null;       // HTTP status, or null for network/setup errors
  code: string;                // machine-readable: "UNAUTHORIZED", "NETWORK", …
  message: string;             // human-readable, safe to display
  details?: unknown;           // raw server payload, for debugging
  isNetworkError: boolean;
};

export function normalizeError(error: unknown): ApiError {
  if (!axios.isAxiosError(error)) {
    return { status: null, code: "UNKNOWN", message: String(error), isNetworkError: false };
  }
  const err = error as AxiosError<any>;

  if (err.response) {
    const status = err.response.status;
    const serverMsg = err.response.data?.message;
    const codeByStatus: Record<number, string> = {
      400: "BAD_REQUEST",
      401: "UNAUTHORIZED",
      403: "FORBIDDEN",
      404: "NOT_FOUND",
      409: "CONFLICT",
      422: "VALIDATION",
      429: "RATE_LIMITED",
    };
    return {
      status,
      code: codeByStatus[status] ?? (status >= 500 ? "SERVER_ERROR" : "HTTP_ERROR"),
      message: serverMsg || `Request failed with status ${status}`,
      details: err.response.data,
      isNetworkError: false,
    };
  }

  if (err.request) {
    return {
      status: null,
      code: err.code === "ECONNABORTED" ? "TIMEOUT" : "NETWORK",
      message:
        err.code === "ECONNABORTED"
          ? "Request timed out."
          : "Network error — check your connection.",
      isNetworkError: true,
    };
  }

  return { status: null, code: "SETUP", message: err.message, isNetworkError: false };
}

Hook it into the instance so every rejection is already normalized:

import api from "./axiosClient";
import { normalizeError } from "./errors";

api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  (error) => {
    const normalized = normalizeError(error);

    // Cross-cutting reactions:
    if (normalized.code === "UNAUTHORIZED") {
      window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent("auth:logout")); // global logout
    }
    // Report unexpected server errors to your monitoring tool.
    if (normalized.status && normalized.status >= 500) {
      // Sentry.captureException(error);
    }

    return Promise.reject(normalized); // callers now receive ApiError, not AxiosError
  }
);

For React rendering errors, pair this with an Error Boundary; with TanStack Query, set a global handler via QueryCache:

import { QueryClient, QueryCache } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import toast from "react-hot-toast";
import type { ApiError } from "./errors";

const queryClient = new QueryClient({
  queryCache: new QueryCache({
    onError: (error) => toast.error((error as ApiError).message),
  }),
  defaultOptions: { queries: { retry: 1, staleTime: 30_000 } },
});

docs.title: 12. Request Queuing & Offline Handling
docs.text: On a flaky or offline connection, mutations should not just fail — queue them and flush when connectivity returns. The browser exposes navigator.onLine plus online/offline events.

docs.title: A simple persisted mutation queue
docs.text:
// src/api/offlineQueue.ts
import api from "./axiosClient";

type QueuedRequest = { id: string; method: string; url: string; data?: unknown };
const STORAGE_KEY = "offline-queue";

function load(): QueuedRequest[] {
  return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY) || "[]");
}
function save(queue: QueuedRequest[]) {
  localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, JSON.stringify(queue));
}

export function enqueue(req: Omit<QueuedRequest, "id">) {
  const queue = load();
  queue.push({ ...req, id: crypto.randomUUID() });
  save(queue);
}

export async function flushQueue() {
  if (!navigator.onLine) return;
  let queue = load();

  for (const req of [...queue]) {
    try {
      await api.request({ method: req.method, url: req.url, data: req.data });
      queue = queue.filter((q) => q.id !== req.id); // drop on success
      save(queue);
    } catch {
      break; // stop on first failure — preserve order, retry later
    }
  }
}

// Flush automatically when the connection returns.
window.addEventListener("online", flushQueue);

// Call site: try now, fall back to the queue when offline.
export async function saveDraft(draft: unknown) {
  if (!navigator.onLine) {
    enqueue({ method: "post", url: "/drafts", data: draft });
    return;
  }
  try {
    return await api.post("/drafts", draft);
  } catch (err) {
    enqueue({ method: "post", url: "/drafts", data: draft });
    throw err;
  }
}

Don't reinvent this for production

TanStack Query ships first-class offline support: set networkMode: "offlineFirst" and use its persistence plugins (persistQueryClient + an async storage persister) to pause mutations offline and resume them on reconnect. For true background sync that survives a page close, a Service Worker with the Background Sync API is the robust option.

docs.title: 13. Interceptors for Cross-Cutting Concerns
docs.text:
Interceptors are the single best place for behaviour that should apply to every request or response — so you don't sprinkle it across call sites. Common uses: auth tokens, request IDs, logging/timing, and centralized error handling.

// src/api/axiosClient.ts
import axios from "axios";

const api = axios.create({ baseURL: import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL, timeout: 10_000 });

// --- Request interceptor: auth + tracing ---
api.interceptors.request.use((config) => {
  const token = localStorage.getItem("accessToken");
  if (token) config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;
  config.headers["X-Request-Id"] = crypto.randomUUID();   // correlate logs
  (config as any).metadata = { start: performance.now() }; // for timing below
  return config;
});

// --- Response interceptor: timing + logging ---
api.interceptors.response.use(
  (response) => {
    const start = (response.config as any).metadata?.start ?? 0;
    const ms = performance.now() - start;
    if (import.meta.env.DEV) {
      console.debug(
        `${response.config.method?.toUpperCase()} ${response.config.url} → ${response.status} (${ms.toFixed(0)}ms)`
      );
    }
    return response;
  },
  (error) => Promise.reject(error)
);

export default api;

docs.title: Refresh-token interceptor (single-flight)
docs.text:
A common cross-cutting need: when a 401 arrives, refresh the token once and replay every request that failed during the refresh — not one refresh call per failed request.

let refreshing: Promise<string> | null = null; // shared promise for concurrent 401s

api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    const original = error.config;
    if (error.response?.status === 401 && original && !original._retried) {
      original._retried = true;

      refreshing ??= api
        .post("/auth/refresh")
        .then((r) => r.data.accessToken as string)
        .finally(() => {
          refreshing = null;
        });

      try {
        const token = await refreshing;          // all 401'd requests await the same call
        localStorage.setItem("accessToken", token);
        original.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;
        return api.request(original);            // replay the original request
      } catch (e) {
        window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent("auth:logout"));
        return Promise.reject(e);
      }
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

Interceptor ordering & loops

Request interceptors run in reverse registration order; response interceptors run in registration order. Guard refresh logic with a flag (_retried above) so a failed refresh can't trigger an infinite retry loop, and make sure the refresh call itself is exempt from the auth interceptor.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Scenario Plain axios / hooks TanStack Query v5 Retries + backoff custom requestWithRetry retry + retryDelay Cancellation AbortController + signal automatic via queryFn signal Debounce/throttle debounce hook + cancel debounce the query key Parallel / dependent Promise.all / enabled chains useQueries / enabled Pagination params + state placeholderData: keepPreviousData Infinite scroll manual cursor + observer useInfiniteQuery Optimistic updates snapshot + rollback onMutate / onError / onSettled Dedup & caching in-flight promise map by queryKey + staleTime/gcTime Polling setInterval / loop refetchInterval 429 handling Retry-After interceptor retry predicate + backoff Error normalization response interceptor → ApiError QueryCache.onError Offline queue persisted queue + online event networkMode + persisters

Rule of thumb

Reach for TanStack Query for server-state in any non-trivial app — it gives you caching, dedup, retries, and cancellation for free. Keep the manual axios patterns for one-off scripts, non-React code, and the rare case where you need precise control over the request lifecycle.

docs.title: State Management with API Data (React Query)
1️⃣ Without Libraries (Vanilla React)
docs.text:
• Store API Response in Component State
  • Use useState and useEffect to fetch and store data.

  • Example:

    const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);
const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
const [error, setError] = useState(null);

useEffect(() => {
  fetch("https://api.example.com/users")
    .then(res => res.json())
    .then(data => setUsers(data))
    .catch(err => setError(err))
    .finally(() => setLoading(false));
}, []);

  • Lift State Up to Parent

  • If multiple child components need the same data, store it in the parent and pass as props.

  • Example:

    function ParentComponent() {
  const [users, setUsers] = useState([]);
  // fetch data here and pass users to children
  return <ChildComponent users={users} />;
}

docs.title: 2️⃣ With React Query
docs.text:
• Automatic State Management
  • useQuery handles loading, error, and data states automatically.

  • Example:

    import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const fetchUsers = async () => {
  const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/users");
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to fetch");
  return res.json();
};

export default function UsersList() {
  const { data, isLoading, isError, error } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["users"],
    queryFn: fetchUsers
  });

  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
  if (isError) return <p>Error: {error.message}</p>;

  return (
    <ul>
      {data.map(user => (
        <li key={user.id}>{user.name}</li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

  • Key Advantages

  • Caching – Data is stored and reused automatically.
  • Background Updates – React Query refetches stale data in the background.
  • Automatic Error Handling – isError and error state provided.
  • Optimistic UI Updates – Can update UI immediately before server confirmation (with mutations).
  • Dependent Queries – Fetch one query only after another completes.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Comparison
docs.text: Feature Vanilla React React Query Loading / Error State Manual Automatic Caching ❌ None ✅ Built-in Refetch / Polling Manual ✅ Configurable Optimistic Updates Manual ✅ Built-in with mutations Dependent Queries Manual ✅ Supported Global DevTools ❌ ✅ React Query DevTools

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
• Since React is a UI library, there is no specific pattern for data fetching
  • We typically use the useEffect hook for data fetching and useState hook to maintain component state like loading, error state or the resulting data
  • If the data is needed throughout the app, then we tend to use state management libraries like Redux
  • Most of the state management libraries are good for working with client state. Ex. 'theme' for an application / whether a modal is open
  • State Management Libraries are not great for working with asynchronous or server state

docs.title: Client vs server state
docs.text:
• Client state:

  • Persisted in your app memory and accessing or updating it is synchronous

  • Server state:

  • Persisted remotely and requires asynchronous APIs for fetching or updating
  • Has shared ownership
  • Data can be updated by someone else without your knowledge
  • UI data may not be in sync with the remote server/database data
  • Challenging when you have to deal with caching, deduplication of multiple requests for the same data, updating stale data in the background, performance optimizations in pagination and lazy-loading, etc

docs.title: What is React Query?
docs.text:
• React Query is a powerful data-fetching and state management library for React applications.
  • It provides a set of hooks for fetching, caching, synchronizing, and updating server state in your UI, without needing complex global state management solutions like Redux.

Created by Tanner Linsley, React Query simplifies remote data management by:

  • Automatically caching fetched data
  • Keeping data fresh via background refetching
  • Avoiding duplicate network requests
  • Simplifying pagination, infinite scrolling, and optimistic updates

docs.title: Why Use React Query?
docs.text:
Traditionally, managing server state involved juggling global stores (Redux, MobX), side effects (Redux-Thunk, Redux-Saga), and manual caching logic. React Query eliminates this complexity by abstracting most of this away, offering:

  • ✅ Auto caching and background syncing
  • 🔁 Refetch on focus or reconnect
  • 💡 Stale-while-revalidate logic
  • 🚀 Performance optimization out of the box
  • 🔄 Support for paginated and infinite queries
  • 💥 Built-in retry and error recovery
  • 🔧 DevTools for debugging query states

React Query is especially useful in apps with a lot of asynchronous data (like dashboards, admin panels, and data-heavy UIs).

docs.title: Core Concepts Overview
docs.text:
Here are the fundamental building blocks of React Query:

  • Query (useQuery): Used to fetch and cache server data. Handles loading, error, and success states.
  • Declarative Data Fetching: React Query promotes a declarative approach to data fetching. You define queries and mutations using hooks like useQuery and useMutation. This leads to cleaner and more organized code.
  • Mutation (useMutation): Used to send data to the server (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE). Supports optimistic updates.
  • Query Keys: Unique identifiers for queries; essential for caching and invalidation.
  • Query Client: A central manager for all queries/mutations. Configures global settings like retry count and cache time.
  • Automatic Caching: React Query includes a built-in cache that stores query results. It automatically updates data when mutations occur, ensuring your UI remains consistent.
  • Background Data Sync: It can automatically refetch data in the background, keeping your data fresh without manual intervention.
  • Pagination and Infinite Scrolling: React Query provides utilities for handling pagination and infinite scrolling effortlessly.
  • Optimistic Updates: You can implement optimistic updates with ease, making your app feel more responsive.
  • DevTools: Visual debugging tool to inspect the query cache, query states, and performance.

docs.title: Comparison with Redux & SWR
docs.text:
Feature React Query Redux SWR Purpose Server state mgmt Global app & server state mgmt Server state mgmt Data Caching ✅ Built-in ❌ Manual ✅ Built-in Background Refetching ✅ Yes ❌ No ✅ Yes DevTools ✅ Yes ✅ (via Redux DevTools) ❌ No (community-based) Boilerplate Minimal High Minimal Optimistic Updates ✅ Yes ❌ Manual 🚧 Limited Pagination Support ✅ Yes ❌ Manual 🚧 Basic support Learning Curve Medium High Low

Summary: React Query is ideal when your app heavily interacts with remote data and you want to avoid over-engineering with global state libraries like Redux. It's more full-featured than SWR and offers deep control and performance optimizations for serious production use.

docs.title: 1. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
Go to react query docs for installation -- https://tanstack.com/query/latest/docs/framework/react/installation

# Install React Query
npm install @tanstack/react-query

# Optional: React Query Devtools
npm install @tanstack/react-query-devtools

also install react-router for routing in React

npm install react-router

docs.title: 2. Set Up Query Client
docs.text:
Create a QueryClient and wrap your app with QueryClientProvider in main.jsx.

import React from "react";
import ReactDOM from "react-dom/client";
import App from "./App";
import { QueryClient, QueryClientProvider } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { ReactQueryDevtools } from "@tanstack/react-query-devtools";

// Create a client
const queryClient = new QueryClient();

ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById("root")).render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <App />
      <ReactQueryDevtools initialIsOpen={false} />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  </React.StrictMode>
);

docs.title: 3. Create a Fetcher Function
docs.text:
Define a function that fetches data from your API. You can use fetch or axios.

// api/posts.js
export const fetchPosts = async () => {
  const response = await fetch("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts");
  if (!response.ok) {
    throw new Error("Network response was not ok");
  }
  return response.json();
};

// api/posts.js
import axios from "axios";
export const fetchPosts = async () => {
  try {
    const response = await axios.get("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts");
    return response.data;
  } catch (error) {
    throw new Error(error.response?.statusText || "Network request failed");
  }
};

docs.title: 4. Use React Query in a Component
docs.text:
Use useQuery to fetch data and handle loading, error, and success states automatically.

import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { fetchPosts } from "./api/posts";

export default function PostsList() {
  const { data, isLoading, isError, error } = useQuery({
    // the path becomes the queryKey here,
      // /posts --> ["posts"]
      // /posts/1 --> ["posts", 1]
      // /posts/2 --> ["posts", 2]
      // or for dynamic route
      // /posts/:id --> ["posts", post.id]
      // /posts/:id/comments --> ["posts", post.id, "comments"]
    queryKey: ["posts"],
    // the queryFn is a Callback Function that returns a promise
    queryFn: fetchPosts,
  });

  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
  if (isError) return <p>Error: {error.message}</p>;

  return (
    <ul>
      {data.map((post) => (
        <li key={post.id}>
          <h3>{post.title}</h3>
          <p>{post.body}</p>
        </li>
      ))}
    </ul>
  );
}

docs.title: 5. Key Concepts
docs.text:
• queryKey → Unique key for caching and refetching.
  • queryFn → Function returning a promise (fetcher).
  • Automatic caching → Data is cached and updated automatically.
  • Stale time & refetch → Configurable to control updates.
  • Devtools → Visualize query cache and state in the browser.

docs.title: QueryClient
docs.text:
• Purpose: Central object that manages queries, mutations, cache, and default configurations.

  • Key Options:

    • staleTime – The time during which cached data is considered fresh. While data is “fresh,” React Query will not automatically refetch it when a component mounts or window refocuses.
    • gcTime –The time after which unused (inactive) cached data is garbage-collected. “Unused” means no active queries or components are currently subscribing to it. (In React Query v3/v4 this option was called cacheTime; it was renamed to gcTime in v5.)
    • refetchOnWindowFocus – Refetch queries when window regains focus.
    • More key options are listed below

  • Example:

// src/main.jsx
import { QueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const queryClient = new QueryClient({
  // Default options: `staleTime`, `gcTime`, `refetchOnWindowFocus`.
  // here we are just create a QueryClient with Default Options
  defaultOptions: {
    queries: {
      // Configure how long the data should remain fresh, or won't turn into stale value.
      // Default staleTime = 0 ms
      // lets keep the value fresh for one minute
      staleTime: 1000 * 60, // 1 minute

      //The default value of gcTime in React Query is: 5 minutes (1000 * 60 * 5 ms)
      gcTime: 1000 * 60 * 5, // 5 minutes

      // Fires when the browser window/tab regains focus. 
      // When the user switches to another tab or window and comes back, React Query will refetch queries that are stale.
      // This ensures the UI shows the most up-to-date data without the user needing to refresh manually.
      refetchOnWindowFocus: true,   // default, refetch when window refocuses
      // refetchOnWindowFocus: false  // never refetch automatically on focus
      // refetchOnWindowFocus: 'always' // always refetch even if data is fresh

      // Fires when a component mounts that uses the query.
      // On component mount, React Query decides whether to refetch based on the staleness of the data.
      // refetchOnMount: true        // Refetch if the data is stale
      // refetchOnMount: false       // Never refetch on mount
      refetchOnMount: 'always'    // default, Always refetch, even if data is fresh
      // Useful when you want to ensure the component always shows up-to-date data on mount.
      // Can be set to false if you trust the cached data and want instant UI without unnecessary network requests.
    },
  },
});

  • staleTime = 1 min

    • During this time, data is “fresh.”
    • React Query will never show a loading state if a component mounts — it just serves the cached data.

  • After 1 min → data becomes stale

    • React Query considers it stale and can refetch it in the background if refetchOnMount or refetchOnWindowFocus is enabled.
    • But the cached data is still available, so the UI can display stale data immediately instead of showing Loading....

  • gcTime = 5 min

    • Even if no component is using the query, React Query keeps the data in memory for 5 minutes.
    • If a component mounts within that 5 minutes, it gets stale data immediately while the background refetch happens.
    • Only after 5 minutes of inactivity is the cache removed — then a new fetch will show Loading....

docs.title: QueryClientProvider
docs.text:
• Purpose: Wrap your app to provide the QueryClient instance globally.
  • Example:
import { QueryClientProvider } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { ReactQueryDevtools } from "@tanstack/react-query-devtools";

// Create a Client
const queryClient = new QueryClient({
  defaultOptions: {
    queries: {
      // every default query you want to give goes here.
    }
  }
});

function App() {
  return (
    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
      <MyAppComponents />
      {/* if you want to use react query devtools */}
      <ReactQueryDevtools initialIsOpen={false} />
    </QueryClientProvider>
  );
}

docs.title: useQuery
docs.text:
• Purpose: Fetch and cache data from an API. Handles loading, error, and success automatically.
  • Key Options: same Key Options as before
  • Example:
import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const fetchUsers = async () => {
  const res = await fetch("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users");
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to fetch users");
  return res.json();
};

export default function UsersList() {
  const { data, isLoading, isError, error } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["users"],
    queryFn: fetchUsers,
    staleTime: 1000 * 60, // 1 min
    retry: 2,
  });

  if (isLoading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
  if (isError) return <p>Error: {error.message}</p>;

  return (
    <ul>
      {data.map(user => <li key={user.id}>{user.name}</li>)}
    </ul>
  );
}

To know more: useQuery Reference

docs.title: useMutation
docs.text:
• Purpose: Perform POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE operations with automatic cache updates.
  • Key Options:
    • onSuccess – Called after successful mutation.
    • onError – Called if mutation fails.
    • onSettled – Called after either success or error.
  • Example:
import { useMutation, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const addUser = async (user) => {
  const res = await fetch("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users", {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify(user),
  });
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to add user");
  return res.json();
};

export default function AddUserForm() {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();
  const mutation = useMutation({
    mutationFn: addUser,
    onSuccess: () => {
      // Invalidate and refetch users query
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["users"] });
    },
    onError: (error) => console.error("Error:", error),
  });

  const handleSubmit = (e) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    const user = { name: "John Doe" };
    mutation.mutate(user);
  };

  return <button onClick={handleSubmit}>Add User</button>;
}

docs.title: useQueryClient
docs.text:
• Purpose: Access and manipulate the query cache manually.
  • Common Use Cases:
    • Invalidate queries
    • Prefetch queries
    • Update cached data optimistically
  • Example:
import { useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const queryClient = useQueryClient();
// refetch users
queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["users"] }); 
// optimistic update
queryClient.setQueryData(["users"], oldData => [...oldData, newUser]);

docs.title: React Query Architecture Map
docs.text:
┌────────────────────────────┐
               │      UI Component (Hook)   │
               │   e.g. useQuery/useMutation│
               └──────────────┬─────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
               ┌────────────────────────────┐
               │ QueryClient (central brain)│
               │ - Stores all queries       │
               │ - Caches results           │
               │ - Manages observers        │
               └──────────────┬─────────────┘
                              │
         ┌────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
         ▼                                          ▼
 ┌──────────────────────┐                  ┌───────────────────────┐
 │ Query Cache          │                  │ Mutation Cache        │
 │ - Data (stale/fresh) │                  │ - Pending mutations   │
 │ - Status (pending...)│                  │ - Retry/failure state │
 │ - Timestamps         │                  │ - Garbage collection  │
 └───────────┬──────────┘                  └────────────┬──────────┘
             │                                          │
             ▼                                          ▼
   ┌───────────────────────────┐              ┌───────────────────────────┐
   │ Query Observer            │              │ Mutation Observer         │
   │ - Subscribes to cache     │              │ - Subscribes to mutation  │
   │ - Notifies components     │              │ - Triggers UI updates     │
   │ - Applies retry logic     │              │ - Retries if configured   │
   └─────────────┬─────────────┘              └──────────────┬────────────┘
                 │                                           │
                 ▼                                           ▼
        ┌───────────────────────┐                   ┌────────────────────────┐
        │ Query Function (async)│                   │ Mutation Function      │
        │ - fetch('/api/data')  │                   │ - POST/PUT/DELETE reqs │
        │ - Axios/Fetch/GraphQL │                   │ - Sends updates        │
        └────────────┬──────────┘                   └────────────┬───────────┘
                     │                                           │
                     ▼                                           ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────┐                  ┌─────────────────────────┐
        │ Server / API / Database │                  │ Server / API / Database │
        └─────────────────────────┘                  └─────────────────────────┘

                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │        QueryClient         │
                │  (Central Manager/Cache)   │
                └──────────────┬─────────────┘
                               │
            ┌──────────────────┼────────────────────────┐
            │                  │                        │
   ┌────────▼─────────┐ ┌──────▼──────────┐ ┌───────────▼──────┐
   │ Query Cache      │ │ Mutation Cache  │ │ Devtools (UI)    │
   │ - Stores queries │ │ - Stores pending│ │ - Observes state │
   │ - Data + status  │ │   mutations     │ │ - Debugging      │
   └────────┬─────────┘ └──────┬──────────┘ └───────────┬──────┘
            │                  │                        │
            │                  │                        │
 ┌──────────▼─────────┐        │                        │
 │ Query Observer     │        │                        │
 │ (per component)    │        │                        │
 │ - Subscribes to    │        │                        │
 │   query updates    │        │                        │
 └─────────┬──────────┘        │                        │
           │                   │                        │
 ┌─────────▼─────────┐   ┌─────▼─────────┐       ┌──────▼─────────┐
 │ React Hook Layer  │   │ Mutation Hook │       │ Utilities      │
 │ (useQuery,        │   │ (useMutation) │       │ Hydration,     │
 │ useInfiniteQuery, │   │ - Trigger API │       │ Suspense, etc. │
 │ useSuspenseQuery, │   │ - Update cache│       │                │
 │ etc.)             │   │ - Invalidate  │       │                │
 └─────────┬─────────┘   └─────┬─────────┘       └────────────────┘
           │                   │
           │                   │
 ┌─────────▼───────────────────▼──────────────────────────────┐
 │                        Data Fetcher                        │
 │ (Axios, fetch, GraphQL, REST, gRPC, etc.)                  │
 │ - Executes actual network request                          │
 │ - Returns data or error                                    │
 └──────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
          ┌─────────▼────────────┐
          │ Query Lifecycle      │
          │ - pending → success  │
          │   / error            │
          │ - retries + backoff  │
          │ - garbage collection │
          └─────────┬────────────┘
                    │
         ┌──────────▼───────────┐
         │ Cache Policies       │
         │ - staleTime          │
         │ - gcTime             │
         │ - refetchOnWindow    │
         │   Focus, Reconnect   │
         │ - background updates │
         └──────────┬───────────┘
                    │
        ┌───────────▼──────────────────────────┐
        │ React Component Rendering            │
        │ - Reads from cache (fast UI)         │
        │ - Subscribes to changes (auto sync)  │
        │ - Suspense fallback if enabled       │
        │ - ErrorBoundary for failures         │
        └──────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 1. Component Mounts
docs.text:
• Calls useQuery / useInfiniteQuery / useSuspenseQuery.
  • Hook connects to Query Observer, which checks the Query Cache inside QueryClient.

docs.title: 2. Cache Check
docs.text:
• If data is fresh (within staleTime) → return cached data instantly (no network).
  • If data is stale or missing → trigger fetch from Data Fetcher.

docs.title: 3. QueryClient Controls
docs.text:
• Tracks query status: pending, success, error (plus a separate fetchStatus: fetching, paused, idle).
  • Manages:
  • Retries (with exponential backoff).
  • Garbage collection (based on gcTime).
  • Refetching policies:
    • On window focus.
    • On reconnect.
    • On intervals.

docs.title: 4. Query Updates
docs.text:
• Cache updated with new data (organized by queryKey).
  • All subscribed components automatically re-render with latest data.

docs.title: 5. Mutations (useMutation)
docs.text:
• Executes mutationFn (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE API call).
  • Features:
  • Optimistic updates → UI updates before server confirms.
  • Invalidate queries → triggers auto-refetch for related queries.
  • Rollback on error → revert changes if mutation fails.

docs.title: 6. Suspense + Error Boundaries
docs.text:
• useSuspenseQuery → UI suspends until data resolves.
  • If query fails → Error Boundary (or useQueryErrorResetBoundary) catches and handles error.

docs.title: 7. Hydration (SSR / SSG)
docs.text:
• Server prefetches data.
  • Data sent to client as dehydrated JSON.
