            setProgress(Math.round((e.loaded * 100) / total));
          },
        });
        setData(response.data);
        setStatus("success");
        return response.data;
      } catch (err) {
        if (axios.isCancel(err)) {
          setStatus("canceled");
          return null;
        }
        const axiosErr = err as AxiosError<{ message?: string }>;
        setError(
          axiosErr.response?.data?.message ?? axiosErr.message ?? "Upload failed"
        );
        setStatus("error");
        return null;
      } finally {
        controllerRef.current = null;
      }
    },
    [url, fieldName, validate]
  );

  const cancel = useCallback(() => {
    controllerRef.current?.abort();
  }, []);

  const reset = useCallback(() => {
    controllerRef.current?.abort();
    controllerRef.current = null;
    setStatus("idle");
    setProgress(0);
    setError(null);
    setData(null);
  }, []);

  return { status, progress, error, data, upload, cancel, reset };
}

docs.title: Using the hook
docs.text:
import { useFileUpload } from "./useFileUpload";

interface UploadResult {
  id: string;
  url: string;
}

function AvatarUploader() {
  const { status, progress, error, data, upload, cancel, reset } =
    useFileUpload<UploadResult>({
      url: "/upload/avatar",
      fieldName: "avatar",
      maxSize: 5 * 1024 * 1024,
      allowedTypes: ["image/png", "image/jpeg", "image/webp"],
    });

  return (
    <div>
      <input
        type="file"
        accept="image/*"
        disabled={status === "uploading"}
        onChange={(e) => e.target.files?.[0] && upload(e.target.files[0])}
      />

      {status === "uploading" && (
        <>
          <progress value={progress} max={100} /> {progress}%
          <button onClick={cancel}>Cancel</button>
        </>
      )}

      {status === "error" && <p style={{ color: "crimson" }}>{error}</p>}
      {status === "canceled" && <p>Upload canceled.</p>}
      {status === "success" && data && (
        <>
          <img src={data.url} alt="Uploaded avatar" width={120} />
          <button onClick={reset}>Upload another</button>
        </>
      )}
    </div>
  );
}

docs.title: ✅ Best Practices Recap
docs.text:
• Let axios set Content-Type for FormData — never hardcode the boundary.
  • Always guard progress math against a missing/zero total (event.total ?? 0, bail if <= 0).
  • Revoke every object URL (URL.revokeObjectURL) in cleanup to prevent memory leaks.
  • Validate on the client for UX, on the server for security — file.type and accept are spoofable.
  • Abort in-flight requests on unmount with AbortController; use axios.isCancel() to tell cancellation from failure.
  • Chunk large uploads (or use tus/Uppy) so failures resume instead of restarting.
  • Stream with fetch + ReadableStream when files won't fit in memory.
  • Upload large files directly to cloud via presigned URLs — keep the bytes off your own servers, and never leak your auth headers to the storage host.
  • Prefer object URLs over base64 data URLs for previews — they're faster and far lighter on memory.

docs.title: What is TypeScript?
docs.text:
• TypeScript is a strongly typed, compiled superset of JavaScript developed and maintained by Microsoft.
  • TypeScript is a programming language that builds on JavaScript by adding static types.
  • It helps you catch errors early in your code before running it.

It adds:

  • Static typing
  • Modern JavaScript (ES6+) features
  • Rich IDE support
  • Compile-time error checking

TypeScript files use the .ts or .tsx extension and compile down to plain JavaScript, ensuring compatibility with browsers and JavaScript runtimes.

docs.title: Why TypeScript? 🧠 Benefits Over JavaScript
docs.text:
Feature JavaScript TypeScript Static Typing ❌ No ✅ Yes Compile-time Checks ❌ No ✅ Yes Autocomplete & IntelliSense 🔸 Partial ✅ Full Refactoring Support 🔸 Limited ✅ Strong Interfaces & Enums ❌ No ✅ Yes Modern ES Support ✅ Yes (partially) ✅ Yes (with back-compatibility)

Main Benefits:

  • Early Error Detection: Catch bugs at compile time.
  • Improved Developer Productivity: With autocompletion, tooltips, and type safety.
  • Better Documentation: Types act as a form of documentation.
  • Scalability: Makes large codebases easier to maintain.

docs.title: TypeScript vs JavaScript ⚔️
docs.text:
Feature JavaScript TypeScript Language Type Dynamic Static (optionally) Syntax Flexible Superset of JS Runtime Interpreted Compiled to JS Tooling Good Excellent (with TS support) Learning Curve Easy Slightly Steeper

TypeScript doesn’t replace JavaScript — it enhances it. You still write JavaScript, but with optional types and compiler checks.

docs.title: Setting Up a TypeScript Project
docs.text:
1. Initialize with npm init -y and install TypeScript using npm install typescript --save-dev.
  2. Create a config using npx tsc --init and write .ts files in src/.
  3. Compile with npx tsc or use npx tsc --watch for live recompile.
  4. Use tsconfig.json to set rootDir, outDir, strict, etc.

docs.title: Running & Compiling TypeScript (tsc)
docs.text:
1. Compile a single file: npx tsc file.ts.
  2. Compile entire project (uses tsconfig.json): npx tsc.
  3. Enable watch mode: npx tsc --watch.
  4. Customize output with rootDir, outDir, target, etc. in tsconfig.json.

docs.title: tsc Commands
docs.text:
# Initialize a tsconfig.json file
tsc --init

# Compile a single TypeScript file
tsc file.ts

# Compile the whole project based on tsconfig.json
tsc

# Watch mode - recompile on file changes
tsc --watch

# Specify custom config file
tsc --project ./path/to/tsconfig.json

# Compile and emit to a specific output directory
tsc --outDir dist

# Enable strict type-checking
tsc --strict

# Skip emitting JS files (type-check only)
tsc --noEmit

# Enable incremental builds
tsc --incremental

# Clean build outputs (when using project references)
tsc --build --clean

# Target a specific ECMAScript version
tsc --target ES6

# Set module system (CommonJS, ESNext, etc.)
tsc --module commonjs

docs.title: API and TypeScript
docs.text: See also: Working with Third-Party Libraries introduces typing API clients like axios and fetch. This chapter expands on that with error handling, generics, and runtime validation.

docs.title: Typing API Responses
docs.text:
• It's important to define interfaces or types for the data you expect from an API.
  • This improves type safety and reduces runtime bugs.
// ✅ Defining expected response types
interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
  email: string;
}

docs.title: Fetching Data with fetch
docs.text:
• When using fetch, manually cast the returned data using as.
// ✅ Using fetch with TypeScript
async function fetchUser(): Promise<User> {
  const res = await fetch("/api/user");
  const data = await res.json();
  return data as User;
}

docs.title: Fetching with Axios
docs.text:
• Axios supports generics for type-safe responses.
// ✅ Using Axios with TypeScript
import axios from "axios";

async function getUser() {
  const response = await axios.get<User>("/api/user");
  console.log(response.data.id); // strongly typed
}

docs.title: Handling Errors in API Calls
docs.text:
• Use try/catch and narrow the error type for better safety.
// ✅ Error handling with type guards
try {
  const user = await fetchUser();
} catch (err) {
  if (err instanceof Error) {
    console.error("Error:", err.message);
  }
}

docs.title: Typing API Parameters
docs.text:
• You can define types for input/query parameters to enforce structure.
// ✅ Query parameters
interface QueryParams {
  search: string;
  page: number;
}

function getSearchUrl(params: QueryParams): string {
  const query = new URLSearchParams(params as any).toString();
  return `/api/search?${query}`;
}

docs.title: Generics with API Data
docs.text:
• Use generics to type reusable API functions.
// ✅ Generic API fetch
async function fetchData<T>(url: string): Promise<T> {
  const res = await fetch(url);
  const data = await res.json();
  return data as T;
}

// Usage:
const user = await fetchData<User>("/api/user");
const posts = await fetchData<Post[]>("/api/posts");

docs.title: Utility Types for API Responses
docs.text:
• Use Partial, Pick, or Omit when modifying or validating response data.
// ✅ Creating types for updating user info
type UpdateUserDTO = Partial<User>; // All fields optional
type PublicUser = Pick<User, "id" | "name">;
type PrivateUser = Omit<User, "email">;

docs.title: Zod or Yup for Runtime Validation (Optional)
docs.text:
• TypeScript only checks types at compile time.
  • For runtime validation, use libraries like zod or yup.
// ✅ Example with Zod
import { z } from "zod";

const UserSchema = z.object({
  id: z.number(),
  name: z.string(),
  email: z.string().email(),
});

type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>;

async function safeFetchUser(): Promise<User> {
  const res = await fetch("/api/user");
  const data = await res.json();
  return UserSchema.parse(data); // validates at runtime
}

docs.title: Typing External Libraries
docs.text:
• Many JavaScript libraries don't ship with built-in TypeScript types.
  • TypeScript will treat such imports as having any type, losing safety and autocomplete.
  • You can manually declare types or install community-maintained ones via @types.
// Without types (risky)
import someLib from "some-untyped-lib";
someLib.doSomething(); // No type info = no IntelliSense or safety

docs.title: Using @types Packages
docs.text:
• Use the DefinitelyTyped community types via npm: @types/package-name.
npm install --save-dev @types/lodash

import _ from "lodash";
const chunked = _.chunk([1, 2, 3, 4], 2); // Type-safe and fully typed
  • Type packages are always dev dependencies and match the original package's name.

docs.title: Handling Untyped Modules
docs.text:
• For libraries without type definitions, you can create a module declaration.
// types/my-lib.d.ts
declare module "untyped-lib" {
  export function doSomething(): void;
}

// main.ts
import { doSomething } from "untyped-lib";
doSomething();
  • Or use any temporarily while developing:
// main.ts
const untyped: any = require("some-legacy-lib");
untyped.runWild(); // no type checking

docs.title: Type Safety in APIs (like axios, fetch, etc.)
docs.text:
See also: the dedicated API and TypeScript chapter goes deeper on typing responses, error handling, generic fetch helpers, and runtime validation (Zod/Yup).

  • Strong typing with API clients ensures correct response and request structures.
// ✅ Using axios with types
import axios from "axios";

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
}

async function getUser(id: number): Promise<User> {
  const response = await axios.get<User>(`/api/users/${id}`);
  return response.data;
}

// ✅ Typing fetch manually
interface Post {
  id: number;
  title: string;
}

async function fetchPost(): Promise<Post> {
  const res = await fetch("/api/posts/1");
  const data: Post = await res.json();
  return data;
}
  • Always define interfaces for response and request payloads when calling APIs.

docs.title: 🔹 React Component Types
docs.text:
// Functional Component with props
type Props = { name: string };
const Greet: React.FC<Props> = ({ name }) => <h1>Hello, {name}</h1>;

// Without `React.FC` (preferred by some)
const GreetAlt = ({ name }: Props): JSX.Element => <h1>Hello, {name}</h1>;

docs.title: 🔹 JSX Types
docs.text:
const element: JSX.Element = <div>Hello</div>;
  • JSX.Element – Return type of JSX expressions.
  • ReactNode – Anything React can render. This is broad: it includes ReactElement, string, number, boolean, null, undefined, React fragments and portals, and arrays/iterables of any of these (so nested arrays of nodes are valid children).
  • ReactElement – More specific than ReactNode; a single element object with type/props metadata.
type ChildrenProps = {
  children: React.ReactNode;
};

docs.title: 🔹 DOM Element Types
docs.text:
const divRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement | null>(null);
const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement | null>(null);
const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement | null>(null);

Common DOM element types:

  • HTMLInputElement
  • HTMLDivElement
  • HTMLButtonElement
  • HTMLTextAreaElement
  • HTMLImageElement
  • HTMLFormElement
  • HTMLAnchorElement
  • HTMLCanvasElement

docs.title: 🔹 Synthetic Event Types (React Events)
docs.text:
function handleChange(e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) {
  console.log(e.target.value);
}

function handleClick(e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>) {
  console.log("Clicked!");
}

Common React event types:

  • React.ChangeEvent<T>
  • React.MouseEvent<T>
  • React.FormEvent<T>
  • React.KeyboardEvent<T>
  • React.FocusEvent<T>
  • React.DragEvent<T>
  • React.FormEvent<HTMLFormElement> (used for form onSubmit; there is no React.SubmitEvent)

docs.title: 🔹 useState, useRef, useReducer Hook Types
docs.text:
const [count, setCount] = useState<number>(0);

const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement | null>(null);

type State = { count: number };
type Action = { type: "inc" } | { type: "dec" };

function reducer(state: State, action: Action): State {
  switch (action.type) {
    case "inc": return { count: state.count + 1 };
    case "dec": return { count: state.count - 1 };
    default: return state;
  }
}
const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, { count: 0 });

docs.title: 🔹 useEffect Cleanup Type
docs.text:
useEffect(() => {
  const id = setInterval(() => console.log("Running..."), 1000);
  return () => clearInterval(id); // () => void
}, []);

docs.title: 🔹 Next.js Specific Types
docs.text:
import { GetStaticProps, GetServerSideProps, NextPage } from "next";

// Page component type
const HomePage: NextPage = () => <div>Welcome</div>;

// Static props
export const getStaticProps: GetStaticProps = async (context) => {
  return {
    props: { message: "Hello from SSG" },
  };
};

// Server-side props
export const getServerSideProps: GetServerSideProps = async (context) => {
  return {
    props: { message: "Hello from SSR" },
  };
};

Other Next.js types:

  • NextApiRequest / NextApiResponse (for API routes)
  • AppProps (for custom _app.tsx)
  • NextPageContext (for SSR pages)

docs.title: 🔹 Utility Types You’ll Often Use
docs.text:
Partial<Type>       // Make all properties optional
Required<Type>      // Make all properties required
Readonly<Type>      // Make all properties readonly
Pick<Type, Keys>    // Pick a subset of properties
Omit<Type, Keys>    // Remove specific keys from a type
Record<K, T>        // Object with keys K and values T

ReturnType<typeof func>  // Infer function return type
Parameters<typeof func>  // Infer function parameters

docs.title: ⚛️ Typing Functional Components
docs.text:
// Basic functional component with props
import type { JSX } from "react"

type GreetProps = {
    name: string
}

// hard to use 1
export const Greet1: React.FC<GreetProps> = (props) => {
    return (
        <h1>Hello, {props.name}</h1>
    )
}

// good use 2
export const Greet2 = ({name}: GreetProps) => {
    return (
        <h1>Hello, {name}</h1>
    )
}

// ✅ best use 3
export const Greet3 = (props: GreetProps) => {
    return (
        <h1>Hello, {props.name}</h1>
    )
}

// extra syntax 4
export const Greet4 = (props: GreetProps): JSX.Element => {
    return <h1>Hello, {props.name}</h1>
}

docs.title: 🎯 Typing Props and State
docs.text:
// 🎯 Typing Props and State

import React, {useState, type JSX} from 'react';

type CounterProps = {
    initialCount?: number
}

// hard to use 1
export const Counter1: React.FC<CounterProps> = ({initialCount = 0}) => {
    const [count, setCount] = useState(initialCount)

    return (
        <div className='m-4'>
            <button 
            onClick={() => setCount(count => count + 1)}
            >
                Count: {count}
            </button>
        </div>
    )
}


// good use 2 and JSX.Element type is extra Explicit Return
export const Counter2 = ({initialCount = 0}: CounterProps): JSX.Element => {
    const [count, setCount] = useState(initialCount)

    return (
        <div className='m-4'>
            <button 
            onClick={() => setCount(count => count + 1)}
            >
                Count: {count}
            </button>
        </div>
    )
}

// best use 3
export const Counter3 = (props: CounterProps) => {
    const [count, setCount] = useState(props.initialCount ?? 0)

    return (
        <div className='m-4'>
            <button 
            onClick={() => setCount(count => count + 1)}
            >
                Count: {count}
            </button>
        </div>
    )
}

docs.title: 🧷 useRef in TypeScript
docs.text:
• useRef lets you persist values between renders without causing a re-render.
  • Often used to reference DOM elements or store mutable variables.
import { useRef, useEffect } from 'react';

type InputRef = HTMLInputElement | null;

const UseRefExample = () => {
  const inputRef = useRef<InputRef>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    inputRef.current?.focus(); // Auto-focus on mount
  }, []);

  return <input ref={inputRef} type="text" placeholder="I will be focused" />;
};
  • useRef<InputRef>(null) ensures proper typing: inputRef.current is of type HTMLInputElement | null.

docs.title: 🧠 useReducer in TypeScript
docs.text:
• useReducer is useful for more complex state logic or state transitions.
  • Works like a mini Redux reducer.
import { useReducer } from 'react';

type Action = {type: 'increase'} | {type: 'decrease'}
type State = {count: number}

const reducerFunction = (state: State, action: Action) => {
    switch(action.type) {
        case "increase": return {count: state.count + 1}
        case "decrease": return {count: state.count - 1}
        default: return state
    }
}

export const ReducerExample = () => {
    const initialValue = {count: 0}
    const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducerFunction, initialValue)
    const handleIncrease = () => (dispatch({type: "increase"}))
    const handleDecrease = () => (dispatch({type: "decrease"}))
    return (
        <div>
            <p>Count: {state.count}</p>
            <button onClick={handleIncrease}>Increase</button>
            <button onClick={handleDecrease}>Decrease</button>
        </div>
    )
}
  • useReducer(reducer, initialState) returns [state, dispatch]
  • TypeScript infers types from State and Action, so you get full IntelliSense.

docs.title: 🧵 Custom Hooks with Generics
docs.text:
function useArray<T>(initial: T[]): [T[], (item: T) => void] {
  const [array, setArray] = useState<T[]>(initial);
  const add = (item: T) => setArray([...array, item]);
  return [array, add];
}

docs.title: 🧠 Context API with TypeScript
docs.text:
type Theme = 'light' | 'dark';
type ThemeContextType = {
  theme: Theme;
  toggleTheme: () => void;
};

const ThemeContext = createContext<ThemeContextType | undefined>(undefined);

docs.title: 🔌 React Router + TypeScript
docs.text:
import { useParams } from 'react-router-dom';

// react-router v6+: don't pass a generic shape to useParams.
// `id` is inferred as `string | undefined`, so guard / handle the missing case.
const DetailPage = () => {
  const { id } = useParams();
  return <div>Item ID: {id ?? "unknown"}</div>;
};

Note: useParams<RouteParams>() (passing an object type) was the react-router v5 style. In v6+ the generic was changed and each param is typed string | undefined. If you want to constrain the keys, you can write useParams<"id">() so only id is allowed, but the value is still string | undefined.

docs.title: 🧾 Forms & Controlled Inputs
docs.text:
const FormExample = () => {
  const [name, setName] = useState<string>("");

  const handleChange = (e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => {
    setName(e.target.value);
  };

  return <input value={name} onChange={handleChange} />;
};

docs.title: 🧰 React Hook Form + TypeScript
docs.text:
import { useForm, SubmitHandler } from "react-hook-form";

type Inputs = {
  email: string;
  password: string;
};

const MyForm = () => {
  const { register, handleSubmit } = useForm<Inputs>();

  const onSubmit: SubmitHandler<Inputs> = data => console.log(data);

  return (
    <form onSubmit={handleSubmit(onSubmit)}>
      <input {...register("email")} />
      <input type="password" {...register("password")} />
      <button type="submit">Submit</button>
    </form>
  );
};

docs.title: 🧱 Typing children in Props
docs.text:
type CardProps = {
  children: React.ReactNode;
};

const Card = ({ children }: CardProps) => <div className="card">{children}</div>;

docs.title: 🧱 Slot Pattern & Component Composition
docs.text:
type LayoutProps = {
  header: React.ReactNode;
  content: React.ReactNode;
};

const Layout = ({ header, content }: LayoutProps) => (
  <>
    <header>{header}</header>
    <main>{content}</main>
  </>
);

docs.title: 🪝 Advanced Custom Hook
docs.text:
function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number): T {
  const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

  useEffect(() => {
    const timer = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
    return () => clearTimeout(timer);
  }, [value, delay]);

  return debounced;
}

docs.title: 🧪 Testing React Components
docs.text:
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';
import MyComponent from './MyComponent';

test("renders correctly", () => {
  render(<MyComponent />);
  expect(screen.getByText("Hello")).toBeInTheDocument();
});

docs.title: 🚀 Higher Order Components (HOCs)
docs.text:
// Constrain `P` to an object type so the spread `{...props}` type-checks
// under strict React types (a bare `<P>` can be a non-object and makes the
// JSX spread error). `JSX.IntrinsicAttributes & P` keeps `key`/`ref` valid.
function withLogging<P extends object>(Component: React.ComponentType<P>) {
  return (props: P) => {
    console.log("Rendering", Component.displayName ?? Component.name);
    return <Component {...props} />;
  };
}

Note: With strict @types/react, function withLogging<P>(...) without the extends object constraint may not type-check — spreading an unconstrained generic into JSX is rejected. Adding P extends object (and, if needed, typing the inner component's props as JSX.IntrinsicAttributes & P) fixes it.

docs.title: 🔁 Generic Context Factory
docs.text:
type AuthContextType<T> = {
  user: T | null;
  login: (user: T) => void;
};

function createAuthContext<T>() {
  const context = createContext<AuthContextType<T> | undefined>(undefined);
  return context;
}

docs.title: 🧩 Typing Event Handlers
docs.text:
const handleClick = (event: React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>) => {
  console.log("Button clicked", event);
};

docs.title: 📁 Where to Keep Types, Interfaces, Enums
docs.text:
• Create a /types or /interfaces folder at the root or in src/
  • For shared types: src/types/global.d.ts or src/types/common.ts
  • Group by domain (e.g. auth/types.ts, user/interfaces.ts, etc.)
  • You can also colocate near components if types are very specific

docs.title: 🧠 Component-Level Generics
docs.text:
type ListProps<T> = {
  items: T[];
  renderItem: (item: T) => React.ReactNode;
};

function List<T>({ items, renderItem }: ListProps<T>) {
  return <ul>{items.map(renderItem)}</ul>;
}

docs.title: ✅ Summary
docs.text:
This section now covers:

  • Functional components
  • Props, State, Refs, Reducers
  • Forms, Controlled inputs
  • React Router
  • Custom hooks with generics
  • Context API
  • Typing children
  • Hook forms
  • Testing
  • HOCs
  • Slot pattern & composition
  • Advanced generic contexts
  • Event handler types
  • Where to place types/interfaces/enums
  • Generic React components

docs.title: Setting up Node.js with TS
docs.text:
• Install TypeScript and necessary tools:
npm install typescript ts-node @types/node --save-dev
  • Create a tsconfig.json:
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "CommonJS",
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true
  }
}
  • Entry file: src/index.ts
console.log("✅ Node + TypeScript is working!");

docs.title: Typing Express Apps
docs.text:
• Use @types/express for full type support.
npm install express
npm install --save-dev @types/express

import express, { Request, Response } from "express";

const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

app.get("/", (req: Request, res: Response) => {
  res.send("Hello from TypeScript + Express!");
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});

docs.title: Typing File System, Path, Process APIs
docs.text:
• Node’s built-in modules have types via @types/node.
import fs from "fs";
import path from "path";
import process from "process";

// ✅ Reading a file
const data = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, "data.txt"), "utf-8");
console.log(data);

// ✅ Environment Variables
console.log("Running in:", process.env.NODE_ENV);

docs.title: Writing CLI Apps with TS
docs.text:
• Use process.argv, readline, or third-party libs like yargs.
// cli.ts
const args = process.argv.slice(2);
console.log("Arguments:", args);

// Using readline for input
import readline from "readline";

const rl = readline.createInterface({
  input: process.stdin,
  output: process.stdout,
});

rl.question("What's your name? ", (name) => {
  console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`);
  rl.close();
});
  • Run CLI tool using ts-node:
npx ts-node src/cli.ts

docs.title: Decorators in TypeScript
docs.text: • Decorators are special annotations (prefixed with @) used to modify classes, methods, properties, etc.

docs.title: Legacy (experimental) decorators
docs.text:
• This is the older, TS-specific design. It uses the signature (target, propertyKey, descriptor) and requires enabling it in tsconfig.json:
{
  "experimentalDecorators": true
}

function Log(target: any, propertyKey: string, descriptor: PropertyDescriptor) {
  const original = descriptor.value;
  descriptor.value = function (...args: any[]) {
    console.log(`Calling ${propertyKey} with`, args);
    return original.apply(this, args);
  };
}

class User {
  @Log
  sayHello(name: string) {
    return `Hello, ${name}`;
  }
}

docs.title: Standard (Stage 3) decorators — TS 5.0+
docs.text:
• TypeScript 5.0+ ships the standard ECMAScript (Stage 3) decorators, which match the proposal that's headed into JavaScript itself.
  • They use a different signature — (value, context) — and do NOT require experimentalDecorators (do not set that flag if you want the standard form).
  • The context object carries metadata such as context.kind ("method", "field", "class", ...), context.name, and context.addInitializer.
// Standard / Stage-3 method decorator (TS 5.0+, no experimentalDecorators)
function Log(
  original: any,
  context: ClassMethodDecoratorContext
) {
  return function (this: any, ...args: any[]) {
    console.log(`Calling ${String(context.name)} with`, args);
    return original.apply(this, args);
  };
}

class User {
  @Log
  sayHello(name: string) {
    return `Hello, ${name}`;
  }
}

Note: the two designs are mutually exclusive in their signatures. Pick one — for new code prefer the standard decorators; use the experimental form only when a library (e.g. older Angular/NestJS/TypeORM setups) still requires it.

docs.title: TypeScript Compiler API
docs.text:
• You can programmatically analyze, manipulate, or transform TypeScript code using the Compiler API.
import ts from "typescript";

const source = "let x: number = 42;";
const sourceFile = ts.createSourceFile("test.ts", source, ts.ScriptTarget.ES2015);

ts.forEachChild(sourceFile, (node) => {
  if (ts.isVariableStatement(node)) {
    console.log("Variable statement found.");
  }
});
  • Useful for tools like linters, code mods, or custom transpilers.

docs.title: AST Parsing and Transformation
docs.text:
• TypeScript source code is converted into an AST (Abstract Syntax Tree).
  • You can traverse and transform the AST to create custom compile-time tools.
const transformer: ts.TransformerFactory<ts.SourceFile> = (context) => {
  return (rootNode) => {
    function visit(node: ts.Node): ts.Node {
      // Example: remove all `console.log` statements
      if (ts.isExpressionStatement(node) && ts.isCallExpression(node.expression)) {
        if (ts.isPropertyAccessExpression(node.expression.expression) &&
            node.expression.expression.expression.getText() === "console" &&
            node.expression.expression.name.getText() === "log") {
          return ts.factory.createEmptyStatement();
        }
      }
      return ts.visitEachChild(node, visit, context);
    }
    return ts.visitNode(rootNode, visit);
  };
};

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• TypeScript adds type safety but can slow down development if misconfigured.

Tips to improve performance:

  • Limit project scope with include and exclude in tsconfig.json.
  • Avoid unnecessary re-checks using incremental: true.
  • Use skipLibCheck: true to skip checking node_modules.
  • Prefer esnext module targets for faster builds.
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "incremental": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "target": "esnext"
  }
}

docs.title: Avoiding any
docs.text:
• Avoid using any as it disables type checking and defeats the purpose of TypeScript.
  • Use unknown when you don't know the type yet — it's safer and forces type narrowing.
// ❌ Bad
let value: any = "something";
value.doSomething(); // No error, but may fail at runtime

// ✅ Good
let value: unknown = "something";
if (typeof value === "string") {
  console.log(value.toUpperCase());
}

docs.title: Favoring unknown and never
docs.text:
• Use unknown over any to enforce type checking.
  • Use never to represent unreachable or impossible states.
function fail(message: string): never {
  throw new Error(message);
}

function exhaustiveCheck(value: "a" | "b") {
  if (value === "a") return;
  if (value === "b") return;

  // value is of type never here
  fail("Unhandled case");
}

docs.title: Structuring TypeScript Projects
docs.text:
• Use src/ for source files, types/ or @types/ for global types.
  • Keep interfaces and types close to the code or in shared folders.
project-root/
├── src/
│   ├── components/
│   ├── utils/
│   └── index.ts
├── types/
│   └── global.d.ts
├── tsconfig.json
└── package.json
  • Prefer named exports over default exports for clarity and tooling benefits.

docs.title: Consistent Naming Conventions
docs.text:
• Use PascalCase for types and interfaces:
interface UserProfile {
  name: string;
  age: number;
}
  • Use camelCase for variables and functions:
const getUserName = (user: UserProfile): string => user.name;
  • Prefix boolean variables with is, has, can, etc.:
let isVisible: boolean = true;

docs.title: Linting with ESLint + TypeScript
docs.text:
• Install ESLint with TypeScript support. The typescript-eslint meta-package bundles the parser and plugin together:
npm install --save-dev eslint typescript-eslint
  • As of ESLint v9 the flat config (eslint.config.js) is the default and the old .eslintrc.* format is deprecated. Use tseslint.config(...):
// eslint.config.js (ESLint v9+ flat config)
import eslint from "@eslint/js";
import tseslint from "typescript-eslint";

export default tseslint.config(
  eslint.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    rules: {
      "@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any": "warn",
      "@typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type": "off",
    },
  }
);

Legacy note: the older .eslintrc.js format (with parser, plugins, and extends: ["plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended"]) still works on ESLint v8 but is no longer recommended. Prefer flat config for new projects.

  • Run linting (flat config picks up files automatically — no --ext needed):
npx eslint .

docs.title: TypeScript with Jest / Vitest
docs.text:
• Install Jest + TypeScript support:
npm install --save-dev jest ts-jest @types/jest
  • Initialize Jest config:
npx ts-jest config:init
  • Example test: sum.test.ts
// ✅ sum.ts
export const sum = (a: number, b: number): number => a + b;

// ✅ sum.test.ts
import { sum } from "./sum";

test("adds 1 + 2 = 3", () => {
  expect(sum(1, 2)).toBe(3);
});
  • Add test script to package.json:
"scripts": {
  "test": "jest"
}

docs.title: Typing Mocks and Stubs
docs.text:
• Use jest.mock and jest.fn() for mocking dependencies.
  • You can type your mocks explicitly to retain type safety.
// service.ts
export const fetchData = (): string => "real data";

// service.test.ts
import * as service from "./service";

jest.mock("./service");

const mockedService = service as jest.Mocked<typeof service>;

mockedService.fetchData.mockReturnValue("mocked data");

test("uses mocked service", () => {
  expect(mockedService.fetchData()).toBe("mocked data");
});

docs.title: Using ts-jest or Other Compilers
docs.text:
• ts-jest compiles TypeScript before running tests, without needing a separate build step.
  • Alternatively, you can use babel-jest with a babel.config.js for custom transforms.
npm install --save-dev babel-jest @babel/preset-typescript

// babel.config.js
module.exports = {
  presets: ["@babel/preset-env", "@babel/preset-typescript"],
};
  • Then use jest as normal with .ts test files.

docs.title: Vitest (Alternative to Jest)
docs.text:
npm install -D vitest

// counter.ts
export const double = (x: number): number => x * 2;

// counter.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { double } from "./counter";

describe("double", () => {
  it("should double the number", () => {
    expect(double(2)).toBe(4);
  });
});
  • Add a script in package.json:
"scripts": {
  "test": "vitest"
}

docs.title: Common Type Errors
docs.text:
• "Type 'X' is not assignable to type 'Y'" Happens when a value is used in an incompatible context.
let age: number = "25"; // ❌ Error: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

// ✅ Fix:
let age: number = parseInt("25");
  • "Object is possibly 'undefined'" TypeScript warns if a variable may be undefined.
// ❌ Before: `str` can be undefined, so `.length` is unsafe
function getLength(str?: string): number {
  return str.length; // Error: 'str' is possibly 'undefined'
}

// ✅ After: guard against undefined (or use optional chaining)
function getLength(str?: string): number {
  return str ? str.length : 0;
  // alternatively: return str?.length ?? 0;
}

docs.title: Debugging with Source Maps
docs.text:
• Source maps let you debug TypeScript in browser or Node.js using original .ts files. tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "sourceMap": true,
    "outDir": "dist"
  }
}
  • Use browser DevTools or Node.js debuggers (like VSCode or Chrome DevTools).

docs.title: Using VSCode IntelliSense
docs.text:
• TypeScript powers VSCode IntelliSense: autocompletions, tooltips, and error hints.
  • Best practices:
  • Keep tsconfig.json properly configured.
  • Use .d.ts files for global types.
  • Avoid any to get accurate suggestions.

Tip: Hover over symbols to see inferred types and quick fixes.

docs.title: Fixing Type Mismatches
docs.text:
• Use type assertions when you know the type:
const input = document.querySelector("input") as HTMLInputElement;
console.log(input.value);
  • Use typeof, instanceof, or custom type guards for runtime type checks:
function printLength(value: unknown) {
  if (typeof value === "string") {
    console.log(value.length); // ✅ Safe
  }
}
  • Refactor complex types with interfaces, type aliases, and narrowing logic.
type ID = string | number;

function normalizeId(id: ID): string {
  return typeof id === "number" ? id.toString() : id;
}

docs.title: Glossary of TypeScript Terms
docs.text:
• Type Annotation – Explicitly declaring the type of a variable or parameter.
  • Interface – A structure used to define the shape of an object.
  • Type Alias – An alias for a type using type.
  • Literal Type – A type that can be a specific string, number, or boolean.
  • Union Type – A type composed of multiple types (A | B).
  • Intersection Type – Combines multiple types into one (A & B).
  • Generic – A way to write code that works with many types.
  • Enum – A named constant set (numeric or string values).
  • Tuple – An array with fixed length and types per index.

docs.title: Cheatsheet of Common Types
docs.text:
// Primitive Types
let str: string;
let num: number;
let bool: boolean;
let undef: undefined;
let nul: null;
let sym: symbol;

// Arrays and Tuples
let arr: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
let tuple: [string, number] = ["Age", 30];

// Object and Function
let obj: { name: string; age: number };
let func: (x: number) => string;

// Union and Intersection
let id: string | number;
type Admin = { role: string };
type User = { name: string };
type AdminUser = Admin & User;

// Literal and Enum
let direction: "left" | "right";
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }

// Type Alias and Interface
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
interface Shape { area(): number; }

// Generics
function wrap<T>(value: T): T[] {
  return [value];
}

docs.title: Useful Resources & Docs
docs.text:
• TypeScript Official Docs
  • TypeScript Handbook
  • TS Playground
  • DefinitelyTyped Repository
  • Awesome TypeScript

docs.title: Community and Support
docs.text:
• TypeScript Discord
  • Stack Overflow (TypeScript)
  • Reddit: r/typescript
  • GitHub Discussions

docs.title: Basic Types and Type Inference, Annotations & Assertions
Basic Types
docs.text:
// ✅ Primitive Types
let yourname: string = "Alice";
let age: number = 30;
let isActive: boolean = true;
let nothing: null = null;
let notDefined: undefined = undefined;
let normalNum = 9007199254740991; // Max safe integer in JS
let bigIntValue: bigint = 9007199254740992n; // Beyond safe limit
// go to tsconfig.json and change "target" value to "ES2020"
console.log(bigIntValue + 1n); // 9007199254740993n
let symbolValue: symbol = Symbol("id");

// ✅ Special Types
// variable should not be 'any' type like this
let anythingOne;
// Annotated as 'any', so the type stays 'any' — it is NOT inferred or
// "converted" to string. Reassigning to a number is allowed precisely
// because 'any' opts out of type checking (this is NOT type inference).
let anythingTwo: any = "can be anything";
anythingTwo = 20;

let uncertain: unknown = 42;
if (typeof uncertain === "number")
    uncertain.toFixed();

function sayHi(): void {
    console.log("Hi");
}

function throwError(): never {
    throw new Error("Crash!");
}
// throwError()

// ✅ Arrays and Tuples
let numbers: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
let names: Array<string> = ["Tom", "Jerry"];
// tuples are fixed means this [20, "score"] won't work
let tupleExample: [string, number] = ["score", 100];

// ✅ Enums 1
enum Direction {
    Up,
    Down,
    Left,
    Right
}
let move: Direction = Direction.Up;

// ✅ Enums 2
enum UserRoles {
    ADMIN = "admin",
    GUEST = "guest",
    SUPER_ADMIN = "super_admin"
}
let UserRole: UserRoles = UserRoles.ADMIN

// ✅ Literal Types
let response: "yes" | "no" = "yes";

// ✅ Type Aliases
type User = {
    username: string;
    age: number;
};
const user: User = { username: "Bob", age: 28 };

// ✅ Reference Types (Objects, Arrays, Functions)
let person: { username: string; isEmployed: boolean } = {
    username: "Eve",
    isEmployed: true
};

let greet: (username: string) => string = (username) => `Hello, ${username}`;

docs.title: Type Inference, Annotations & Assertions
docs.text:
// ✅ Type Inference
// TypeScript automatically infers the type based on the assigned value
let message = "Hello TypeScript";  // inferred as string
let count = 42;                    // inferred as number

// ✅ Type Annotations
// You explicitly declare the type of a variable
let title: string = "Typed Title";
let isActive: boolean = true;
let total: number;
total = 100;

// ✅ Type Assertions (Casting)
// You tell TypeScript the exact type of a value (used with `unknown` or `any`)
let someValue: unknown = "I am a string";

// Using `as` syntax (recommended)
// Type Casting
let strLength: number = (someValue as string).length;

// ✅ Angle-bracket syntax (not allowed in `.tsx` files)
// Equivalent to `as`, but can't be used in JSX (e.g., React projects)
let anotherValue: unknown = "Another string";
let anotherLength: number = (<string>anotherValue).length;

// ✅ Summary:
// - Use type inference when possible (cleaner and safer).
// - Use type annotations when type isn’t obvious or needed for clarity.
// - Use type assertions when you're sure of a value’s type (e.g., after `unknown`).
// - Prefer `as` syntax especially in JSX/React projects.

docs.title: 🔁 Type Assertions (aka Type Casting)
docs.text:
TypeScript sometimes cannot infer the correct type. Type assertions let you tell the compiler "trust me, I know what I’m doing."

They do not change the runtime type—only instruct the compiler.

let someValue: any = "Hello, world";
let strLength: number = (someValue as string).length;

or (in non-JSX files):

let strLength: number = (<string>someValue).length;

docs.title: ⚔️ as vs Angle-Bracket Syntax
DOM Casting
docs.text:
const el = document.querySelector("#my-input") as HTMLInputElement;
el.value = "Updated";

docs.title: Using unknown with assertions
docs.text:
function handle(data: unknown) {
  const parsed = data as string; // Asserting unknown as string
}

docs.title: ⚠️ Double Assertion (use carefully)
docs.text:
const crazy = ("text" as unknown) as number; // ⚠️ Avoid unless absolutely necessary

This should only be used in edge cases, like dealing with very dynamic third-party data.

docs.title: ✅ Best Practices
docs.text:
• Prefer inference when the type is clear.
  • Use annotations when inference fails or for clarity.
  • Use as syntax consistently, especially in React projects.
  • Avoid unnecessary or unsafe assertions — trust the type system.
  • Never use type assertions to suppress real type errors unless you are 100% confident.
// ❌ BAD
const user = getUser() as any;
user.nonExistentMethod();

// ✅ BETTER
const user = getUser() as { name: string; age: number };

docs.title: Functions, Objects and Classes in TypeScript
Function Types
docs.text:
• Function types let you define the expected argument and return types. They help catch mismatches at compile time and improve readability.
// ✅ Function Types
function add(x: number, y: number): number {
  return x + y;
}
const multiply: (a: number, b: number) => number = (a, b) => a * b;

docs.title: Optional & Default Parameters
docs.text:
• You can mark parameters as optional with ?, or assign default values. This provides flexibility in how functions are called.
// ✅ Optional & Default Parameters
function greet(name: string = "Guest", title?: string): string {
  return title ? `${title} ${name}` : `Hello, ${name}`;
}
greet();                    // "Hello, Guest"
greet("Alice", "Ms.");      // "Ms. Alice"

docs.title: Rest Parameters
docs.text:
• Rest parameters allow functions to accept multiple arguments as an array. They are useful for variable-length inputs like sumAll(...nums).
// ✅ Rest Parameters
function sumAll(...nums: number[]): number {
  return nums.reduce((total, n) => total + n, 0);
}
sumAll(1, 2, 3, 4); // 10

docs.title: Function Overloads
docs.text:
• Function overloading lets you define multiple signatures for a single function. It helps provide different behavior based on input types.
// ✅ Function Overloads
function formatValue(value: number): string;
function formatValue(label: string): string;
function formatValue(label: string, value: number | string): string;
function formatValue(a: string | number, b?: string | number): string {
  if (typeof b === "undefined") {
    // Only one argument
    if (typeof a === "number") return a.toLocaleString();
    return a; // just a string label
  }

  // Two arguments
  if (typeof b === "number") {
    return `${a}: ${b.toLocaleString()}`;
  }

  return `${a}: ${b}`;
}

console.log(formatValue(1000));                 // "1,000"
console.log(formatValue("price", 9999));        // "price: 9,999"
console.log(formatValue("Name", "Alice"));      // "Name: Alice"
console.log(formatValue("Score", 42));          // "Score: 42"
console.log(formatValue("Just a label"));       // "Just a label"

docs.title: this in Functions
docs.text:
• In TypeScript, you can explicitly type the this context of a function. This prevents incorrect usage and makes method binding clearer.
// ✅ `this` in Functions
const obj = {
  count: 0,
  increment(this: { count: number }) {
    this.count++;
  }
};
obj.increment();

docs.title: Arrow Functions
docs.text:
• Arrow functions are concise and do not have their own this context. They are useful for callbacks but should be used carefully inside objects.
// ✅ Arrow Functions
const square = (n: number): number => n * n;

// Arrow functions don’t have their own `this`
const counter = {
  count: 0,
  inc: () => {
    // `this` here refers to the enclosing scope, not `counter`.
    // Under `strict` / `noImplicitThis` this is a *compile-time* error
    // ("'this' implicitly has type 'any'"), so it's caught by the type
    // checker rather than only failing (as undefined) at runtime.
    console.log(this.count);
  }
};

docs.title: Function Return Types
docs.text:
• You can explicitly define a function's return type for clarity and safety. TypeScript can infer return types, but explicit ones help with readability.
// ✅ Function Return Types
function log(msg: string): void {
  console.log(msg); // no return value
}
function getUser(): { name: string; age: number } {
  return { name: "John", age: 30 };
}

docs.title: Anonymous & Callback Functions
docs.text:
• Anonymous functions are used inline, often as callbacks. You can type callbacks explicitly for safety and documentation.
// ✅ Anonymous & Callback Functions
const numbers = [1, 2, 3];
const doubled = numbers.map(function (num) {
  return num * 2;
});

const tripled = numbers.map((n: number): number => n * 3);

// Callback with explicit type
function processInput(callback: (input: string) => void) {
  callback("Hello from callback");
}
processInput((msg) => console.log(msg));

docs.title: Object Type Annotations
docs.text:
• TypeScript lets you define object shapes using type annotations. This provides structure, validation, and IntelliSense during development.
// ✅ Object Type Annotation
const user: { name: string; age: number } = {
  name: "Alice",
  age: 25
};

docs.title: Creating and Using Interfaces
docs.text:
• Interfaces define the shape of an object and are reusable across your code. They're ideal for modeling structured data.
// ✅ Interface Usage
interface User {
  name: string;
  age: number;
}

const newUser: User = {
  name: "Bob",
  age: 32
};

docs.title: Optional and Readonly Properties
docs.text:
• Use ? to make properties optional.
  • Use readonly to prevent reassignment after initialization.
// ✅ Optional and Readonly
interface Product {
  name: string;
  price?: number; // optional
  readonly id: string; // cannot be changed
}

const item: Product = {
  name: "Book",
  id: "abc123"
};

docs.title: Index Signatures
docs.text:
• Index signatures allow dynamic keys with a consistent value type.
// ✅ Index Signature
interface StringMap {
  [key: string]: string;
}

const colors: StringMap = {
  primary: "#fff",
  secondary: "#000"
};

docs.title: Interface vs Type Alias
docs.text:
• Both interface and type can define object shapes.
  • interface supports declaration merging and is preferred for objects.
  • type can alias any type (including primitives, unions, etc.).
// ✅ Interface
interface Person {
  name: string;
}

// ✅ Type Alias
type Animal = {
  species: string;
};

docs.title: Extending Interfaces
docs.text:
• Interfaces can extend one or more other interfaces to build on top of them.
// ✅ Extending Interfaces
interface Shape {
  color: string;
}
interface Circle extends Shape {
  radius: number;
}

const circle: Circle = {
  color: "blue",
  radius: 10
};

docs.title: Union & Intersection Types
docs.text:
• Use union (|) to allow multiple types.
  • Use intersection (&) to combine multiple types into one.

See also: Advanced Types revisits unions, intersections, and discriminated unions alongside type guards, mapped, and conditional types.

// ✅ Union Type

let value: string | number;
value = "hello"; // ✅
value = 42;      // ✅
value = true;    // ❌ Error: boolean not allowed

type Status = "success" | "error" | "loading";
let apiStatus: Status;
apiStatus = "loading"; // ✅
apiStatus = "fail";    // ❌

// Union with Interfaces
interface Dog {
    bark: () => void;
}
interface Cat {
    meow: () => void;
}
type Pet = Dog | Cat;
function makeSound(pet: Pet) {
    if ('bark' in pet) pet.bark();
    else pet.meow();
}



// ✅ Intersection Types (&)

// Intersection with Types
type A = { a: number };
type B = { b: string };

type AB = A & B;

const obj: AB = {
    a: 10,
    b: "hello"
};


// Intersection with Interfaces
interface Position {
    x: number;
    y: number;
}
interface Size {
    width: number;
    height: number;
}
type Rectangle = Position & Size;

const box: Rectangle = {
    x: 0,
    y: 0,
    width: 100,
    height: 50
};


// Mixed Use of Union and Intersection
interface AdminTwo { role: "admin"; accessLevel: number };
interface UserTwo { role: "user"; email: string };
type Profile = { name: string };

// Named `PersonShape` to avoid colliding with the `interface Person` and
// `class Person` declared elsewhere in this file (TS2300 duplicate identifier).
type PersonShape = (AdminTwo & Profile) | (UserTwo & Profile);

const p1: PersonShape = {
    role: "admin",
    accessLevel: 10,
    name: "John"
};

const p2: PersonShape = {
    role: "user",
    email: "john@example.com",
    name: "John"
};

docs.title: Nested Interfaces & Types
docs.text:
• Interfaces and types can be nested to reflect complex data structures.
// ✅ Nested Types
interface Address {
  street: string;
  city: string;
}
interface Employee {
  name: string;
  address: Address;
}

const emp: Employee = {
  name: "John",
  address: {
    street: "123 Main St",
    city: "New York"
  }
};

docs.title: Interface Merging
docs.text:
• Interfaces with the same name automatically merge their declarations.
// ✅ Interface Merging
interface Config {
  theme: string;
}
interface Config {
  layout: "grid" | "list";
}

const settings: Config = {
  theme: "dark",
  layout: "grid"
};

docs.title: Type Aliases for Functions & Objects
docs.text:
• You can define reusable object/function types using type.
// ✅ Function Type Alias
type Logger = (message: string) => void;

const log: Logger = (msg) => console.log(msg);

docs.title: Discriminated Unions
docs.text:
• Combine union types with a discriminator field to create safer type checks.
// ✅ Discriminated Unions
interface Dog {
  kind: "dog";
  bark: () => void;
}
interface Cat {
  kind: "cat";
  meow: () => void;
}
type Pet = Dog | Cat;

function speak(pet: Pet) {
  if (pet.kind === "dog") {
    pet.bark();
  } else {
    pet.meow();
  }
}

docs.title: Class Syntax
docs.text:
• Classes in TypeScript are similar to those in other OOP languages. They encapsulate data and behavior in reusable blueprints.
// ✅ Basic Class Syntax
class Person {
  name: string;
  constructor(name: string) {
    this.name = name;
  }
  greet() {
    console.log(`Hello, my name is ${this.name}`);
  }
}

const p = new Person("Alice");
p.greet(); // Hello, my name is Alice

docs.title: Constructors & Access Modifiers (public, private, protected)
docs.text:
• public: accessible from anywhere (default)
  • private: accessible only within the class
  • protected: accessible in the class and subclasses
// ✅ Access Modifiers
class User {
  public username: string;
  private password: string;
  protected email: string;

  constructor(username: string, password: string, email: string) {
    this.username = username;
    this.password = password;
    this.email = email;
  }

  public checkPassword(input: string): boolean {
    return this.password === input;
  }
}

docs.title: Readonly & Static Members
docs.text:
• readonly: can only be assigned once (at declaration or in constructor)
  • static: belongs to the class, not instances
// ✅ Readonly & Static
class AppConfig {
  readonly version: string = "1.0.0";
  static appName: string = "MyApp";

  printVersion() {
    console.log(`Version: ${this.version}`);
  }
}

console.log(AppConfig.appName); // "MyApp"

docs.title: Getters and Setters
docs.text:
• Use get and set for controlled access to properties.
// ✅ Getters and Setters
class Temperature {
  private _celsius: number;

  constructor(celsius: number) {
    this._celsius = celsius;
  }

  get fahrenheit(): number {
    return this._celsius * 1.8 + 32;
  }

  set fahrenheit(f: number) {
    this._celsius = (f - 32) / 1.8;
  }
}

const temp = new Temperature(25);
console.log(temp.fahrenheit); // 77
temp.fahrenheit = 86;
// Logs the Temperature instance, e.g. Temperature { _celsius: 30 }
// (the private _celsius now holds ~30, since 86°F = 30°C).
console.log(temp);

docs.title: Implementing Interfaces
docs.text:
• Classes can implement one or more interfaces to enforce structure.
// ✅ Implementing Interfaces
interface Printable {
  print(): void;
}

class Document implements Printable {
  print() {
    console.log("Printing document...");
  }
}

docs.title: Inheritance and super()
docs.text:
• Use extends to inherit from another class.
  • Call super() to invoke the parent’s constructor.
// ✅ Inheritance & super()
class Animal {
  constructor(public name: string) {}

  move() {
    console.log(`${this.name} moves.`);
  }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
  constructor(name: string, public breed: string) {
    super(name);
  }

  bark() {
    console.log("Woof!");
  }
}

const d = new Dog("Buddy", "Beagle");
d.move(); // Buddy moves.
d.bark(); // Woof!

docs.title: Abstract Classes
docs.text:
• Abstract classes define common structure but cannot be instantiated directly.
  • Subclasses must implement abstract methods.
// ✅ Abstract Classes
abstract class Shape {
  constructor(public color: string) {}
  abstract area(): number;
}

class Circle extends Shape {
  constructor(color: string, public radius: number) {
    super(color);
  }

  area(): number {
    return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2;
  }
}

docs.title: Generics in Classes
docs.text:
• Use generics to create reusable and type-safe class structures.
// ✅ Generic Class
class Box<T> {
  contents: T;
  constructor(value: T) {
    this.contents = value;
  }
  get(): T {
    return this.contents;
  }
}

const stringBox = new Box<string>("Hello");
const numberBox = new Box<number>(123);

docs.title: Parameter Properties
docs.text:
• TypeScript lets you declare and initialize class properties directly in the constructor.
// ✅ Parameter Properties
class Car {
  constructor(public make: string, private year: number) {}

  getAge(): number {
    return new Date().getFullYear() - this.year;
  }
}

const myCar = new Car("Toyota", 2020);
console.log(myCar.make); // Toyota
// console.log(myCar.year); ❌ Error: year is private

docs.title: 🧩 What are Mixins?
docs.text:
Mixins are a way to compose multiple classes or behaviors into a single class. Instead of using inheritance from one base class, mixins allow you to combine functionality from multiple sources.

They are a powerful pattern to achieve code reuse without deep class hierarchies.

docs.title: 🚦 Why Use Mixins?
docs.text:
• To avoid tight coupling of class hierarchies.
  • To compose reusable logic (e.g., logging, timestamping, serialization).
  • When you want to simulate multiple inheritance in TypeScript (since JS/TS only supports single inheritance).

docs.title: 🔧 Basic Mixin Pattern
docs.text:
type Constructor<T = {}> = new (...args: any[]) => T;

function Timestamped<TBase extends Constructor>(Base: TBase) {
  return class extends Base {
    timestamp = new Date();
  };
}

function Activatable<TBase extends Constructor>(Base: TBase) {
  return class extends Base {
    isActive = false;
    activate() {
      this.isActive = true;
    }
    deactivate() {
      this.isActive = false;
    }
  };
}

docs.title: 🏗️ Using the Mixins
docs.text:
class User {
  constructor(public name: string) {}
}

const Mixed = Timestamped(Activatable(User));

const user = new Mixed("Alice");

console.log(user.timestamp);  // Current Date
console.log(user.isActive);   // false

user.activate();
console.log(user.isActive);   // true

docs.title: 🧠 How it Works
docs.text:
• Constructor<T> defines a generic class constructor type.
  • Each mixin is a higher-order class function that takes a base class and returns an extended version of it.
  • You can chain mixins together to apply multiple behaviors.

docs.title: ⚠️ Things to Know
docs.text:
• TypeScript does not support multiple inheritance, so mixins are a workaround.
  • Mixing in properties and methods may require type assertions or interfaces to keep TypeScript happy.
  • If you're using strictPropertyInitialization, you might need to override that with definite assignment (!) or set values in constructor.

docs.title: ✅ Best Practices
docs.text:
• Keep mixins modular and reusable.
  • Use mixins only when simple composition/inheritance won’t suffice.
  • Document clearly what each mixin adds to avoid confusion.

docs.title: 🧪 Example with Interface Support
docs.text:
interface Timestamped {
  timestamp: Date;
}
interface Activatable {
  isActive: boolean;
  activate(): void;
  deactivate(): void;
}

const mixedUser = new Mixed("Bob") as User & Timestamped & Activatable;

docs.title: 🧰 When to Use Mixins
docs.text:
• When you want to compose multiple traits into a class.
  • In libraries or frameworks to build pluggable behavior.
  • For large codebases where inheritance trees become too complex.

docs.title: Union and Intersection Types
docs.text:
See also: Functions, Objects and Classes covers the basics of unions, intersections, and discriminated unions with interfaces. This chapter focuses on the more advanced angles.

  • Union (|): A variable can be one of multiple types.
  • Intersection (&): Combines multiple types into one.
// ✅ Union Type
type Status = "success" | "error" | "loading";
let state: Status = "success";

// ✅ Intersection Type
interface A { a: string }
interface B { b: number }

type AB = A & B;

const example: AB = {
  a: "hello",
  b: 123
};

docs.title: Discriminated Unions
docs.text:
• Use a common literal field (kind) to distinguish between types in a union.
  • Helps TypeScript narrow types automatically.
// ✅ Discriminated Unions
interface Circle {
  kind: "circle";
  radius: number;
}
interface Square {
  kind: "square";
  side: number;
}
type Shape = Circle | Square;

function area(shape: Shape): number {
  switch (shape.kind) {
    case "circle":
      return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2;
    case "square":
      return shape.side ** 2;
  }
}

docs.title: Type Guards
docs.text:
• Narrow down types using runtime checks like typeof, instanceof, or custom type predicates.
// ✅ typeof Guard
function print(value: string | number) {
  if (typeof value === "string") {
    console.log("String:", value.toUpperCase());
  } else {
    console.log("Number:", value.toFixed(2));
  }
}

// ✅ Custom Type Guard
interface Cat {
  meow(): void;
}
interface Dog {
  bark(): void;
}

function isDog(animal: Dog | Cat): animal is Dog {
  return (animal as Dog).bark !== undefined;
}

docs.title: keyof, typeof, in Operators
docs.text:
• keyof gets all property keys of a type.
  • typeof refers to the type of a variable or value.
  • in checks if a property exists in a type.
// ✅ keyof
type Person = { name: string; age: number };
type PersonKeys = keyof Person; // "name" | "age"

// ✅ typeof
const point = { x: 1, y: 2 };
type Point = typeof point;

// ✅ in Operator
type ReadonlyPerson = {
  [K in keyof Person]: Readonly<Person[K]>
};

docs.title: Mapped Types
docs.text:
• Create new types by transforming existing ones using keyof and in.
// ✅ Mapped Type
type Optional<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]?: T[K];
};

type User = { id: number; name: string };
type OptionalUser = Optional<User>; // All properties optional

docs.title: Conditional Types
docs.text:
• Define types based on a condition using extends.
// ✅ Conditional Types
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? "Yes" : "No";

type A = IsString<string>;  // "Yes"
type B = IsString<number>;  // "No"

docs.title: Template Literal Types
docs.text:
• Construct string literal types using template syntax.
// ✅ Template Literal Types
type Lang = "en" | "fr" | "de";
type FileName = `index.${Lang}.html`;

const file: FileName = "index.en.html"; // valid

docs.title: Generic Functions
docs.text:
• Generics allow you to write functions that work with any data type while preserving type safety.
// ✅ Generic Function
function identity<T>(arg: T): T {
  return arg;
}

const output1 = identity<string>("hello");
const output2 = identity<number>(42);

docs.title: Generic Interfaces & Types
docs.text:
• You can use generics in interfaces and type aliases to define reusable structures.
// ✅ Generic Interface
interface Box<T> {
  value: T;
}

const stringBox: Box<string> = { value: "TypeScript" };
const numberBox: Box<number> = { value: 123 };

docs.title: Constraints with Generics (extends)
docs.text:
• You can limit what types can be passed to a generic by using extends.
// ✅ Generic Constraints
interface HasLength {
  length: number;
}

function logLength<T extends HasLength>(input: T): void {
  console.log(input.length);
}

logLength("hello");
logLength([1, 2, 3]);
// logLength(123); ❌ Error: number has no `length`

docs.title: Default Type Parameters
docs.text:
• Provide a default type if none is specified when the generic is used.
// ✅ Default Type Parameters
type ApiResponse<T = string> = {
  data: T;
  success: boolean;
};

const res1: ApiResponse = { data: "ok", success: true };
const res2: ApiResponse<number> = { data: 200, success: true };

docs.title: Utility Types (Partial, Required, Readonly, Record, etc.)
docs.text:
• TypeScript provides built-in generic utility types to transform other types.
interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
  email?: string;
}

// ✅ Partial: All properties optional
type PartialUser = Partial<User>;

// ✅ Required: All properties required
type RequiredUser = Required<User>;

// ✅ Readonly: Properties cannot be reassigned
type ReadonlyUser = Readonly<User>;

// ✅ Record: Construct an object type with specific keys and value type
type Role = "admin" | "editor" | "viewer";
type RoleMap = Record<Role, string>;

const roles: RoleMap = {
  admin: "Alice",
  editor: "Bob",
  viewer: "Charlie"
};

docs.title: ES Modules in TypeScript
docs.text:
• TypeScript supports standard ES Modules (import / export) to organize code into separate files.
  • Each file is treated as a module if it contains at least one import or export.
// file: math.ts
export function add(a: number, b: number): number {
  return a + b;
}

// file: main.ts
import { add } from "./math";
console.log(add(2, 3)); // 5

docs.title: Import / Export Syntax
docs.text:
• You can export declarations individually or as a default.
  • Imports can be named, aliased, or default imports.
// ✅ Named Export
export const PI = 3.14;

// ✅ Default Export
export default function greet(name: string) {
  return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

// ✅ Import Variants
import greet from "./greet";         // default import
import { PI } from "./constants";    // named import
import * as MathUtils from "./math"; // namespace import

docs.title: Module Resolution
docs.text:
• TypeScript uses module resolution strategies to locate imported modules:
  • "node" (aka node10) — the classic Node CommonJS lookup; now legacy
  • "node16" / "nodenext" — modern Node resolution that honors package.json "exports"/"imports" and the ESM-vs-CJS distinction (use these for current Node.js projects)
  • "bundler" — for projects built with a bundler (Vite, esbuild, webpack, etc.); supports "exports" without requiring file extensions on imports. Common default for Vite/modern frontend setups.
  • "classic" (for very old TypeScript projects; avoid in new code)
  • Controlled via tsconfig.json under moduleResolution.
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "node",
    "baseUrl": "./src",
    "paths": {
      "@utils/*": ["utils/*"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Using TypeScript with Node.js / Deno
docs.text: • TypeScript works with Node.js and Deno through built-in support or configuration.

docs.title: Node.js
docs.text:
• Use ts-node or compile to JavaScript using tsc.
  • Enable ES module support via type: "module" in package.json.
{
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "ts-node src/main.ts"
  }
}

docs.title: Deno
docs.text:
• Deno supports TypeScript natively. Just run .ts files directly.
deno run --allow-read main.ts

docs.title: Legacy Namespaces
docs.text:
• Namespaces were used in pre-ESModule TypeScript for organizing code.
  • Now considered legacy in favor of ES Modules.
  • Still useful in global script-based projects.
  • Use namespace in older codebases without bundlers or module systems.
  • ❌ Don’t mix namespace with import/export in the same file.
// ✅ Legacy Namespace
namespace MathUtils {
    export function add(a: number, b: number): number {
        return a + b
    }
    export function sub(a: number, b: number): number {
        return a - b 
    }
}
console.log(MathUtils.add(23,27))
console.log(MathUtils.sub(23,8))

docs.title: What are .d.ts Files?
docs.text:
• Declaration files (.d.ts) contain type definitions for JavaScript code.
  • They allow TypeScript to understand the shape of external libraries or modules without their source code.
  • Think of them as type-only headers.
// math.d.ts
declare function add(a: number, b: number): number;

// main.ts
/// <reference path="./math.d.ts" />
add(2, 3); // TypeScript understands the type

docs.title: Writing Custom Type Declarations
docs.text:
• Create your own .d.ts files when using plain JavaScript or third-party libraries without types.
// myLib.d.ts
declare module "my-lib" {
  export function greet(name: string): string;
}

// usage.ts
import { greet } from "my-lib";
greet("TypeScript");
  • You can also define types globally by omitting declare module.
// global.d.ts
interface Window {
  myApp: {
    version: string;
  };
}

docs.title: Using DefinitelyTyped (@types)
docs.text:
• The DefinitelyTyped project provides type definitions for thousands of libraries.
  • Install types using npm:
npm install --save-dev @types/lodash

import _ from "lodash";
_.chunk(["a", "b", "c", "d"], 2); // Fully typed!
  • Type packages follow the naming convention: @types/<package-name>

docs.title: Ambient Declarations
docs.text:
• Use declare to tell TypeScript about types that exist elsewhere (e.g., globally provided by a script or environment).
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Declare Demo</title>
  <script>
    // This defines a global variable, available at runtime
    window.APP_VERSION = "3.5.1";
  </script>
  <script src="dist/app.js" defer></script>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Check console for output</h1>
</body>
</html>

// TypeScript doesn't know this global exists.
// We must use `declare` to inform the compiler:
declare const APP_VERSION: string;
console.log("App Version is:", APP_VERSION);

// Likewise
// Global function
declare function showToast(msg: string): void;
// It's assumed this function is defined somewhere else
showToast("Hello!"); 

// Class From External Script
declare class Logger {
    log(msg: string): void;
}
const logger = new Logger();
logger.log("Logging!");

// Module Declaration
declare module "my-legacy-lib" {
    export function doSomething(): void;
}

// ambient.d.ts
declare const API_URL: string;
declare function fetchData(endpoint: string): Promise<any>;
  • Ambient declarations are useful when working with global variables or external JS.
// usage.ts
console.log(API_URL);
fetchData("/users").then(console.log);

docs.title: Type Checking JavaScript Files
docs.text:
• TypeScript can perform type checking on .js files.
  • Useful for gradually migrating JavaScript projects to TypeScript.
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "checkJs": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*.js"]
}

// example.js
function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

add("1", 2); // ❌ TypeScript will warn about type mismatch

docs.title: allowJs and checkJs
docs.text:
• allowJs: lets the compiler include .js files in compilation.
  • checkJs: enables type-checking on .js files.
// tsconfig.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "allowJs": true,
    "checkJs": true
  }
}
  • This allows mixed JS and TS projects.

docs.title: Using JSDoc for Typing JS
docs.text:
• You can use JSDoc comments to annotate types in .js files.
// ✅ JSDoc Typing
/**
 * @param {number} a
 * @param {number} b
 * @returns {number}
 */
function multiply(a, b) {
  return a * b;
}
  • Works great with IDEs and checkJs enabled.
/**
 * @typedef {Object} User
 * @property {string} name
 * @property {number} age
 */

/** @type {User} */
const user = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

docs.title: Migrating from JS to TS
docs.text:
• Migrate step-by-step with minimal disruption:
# 1. Enable JS support
tsc --init --allowJs --checkJs

# 2. Rename .js → .ts/.tsx one file at a time

# 3. Replace JSDoc with native TypeScript types

# 4. Fix type errors gradually and configure strict options

// JS
function greet(name) {
  return "Hello, " + name;
}

// TS
function greet(name: string): string {
  return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

docs.title: tsconfig.json Explained
docs.text:
• tsconfig.json configures how TypeScript compiles your project.
  • Common fields:
  • compilerOptions: compiler behavior
  • include: files to include
  • exclude: files to ignore
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "es6",
    "module": "esnext",
    "strict": true,
    "outDir": "./dist",
    "rootDir": "./src"
  },
  "include": ["src"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}

docs.title: Strict Mode Options
docs.text:
• Enabling strict turns on all strict type-checking options:
  • strictNullChecks
  • noImplicitAny
  • strictFunctionTypes
  • alwaysStrict, etc.
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true
  }
}
  • These catch subtle bugs and make your code safer.

docs.title: Useful Compiler Options
docs.text:
• noImplicitAny: Warns when TypeScript infers any.
  • strictNullChecks: Disallows null and undefined where not explicitly allowed.
  • esModuleInterop: Fixes default- and namespace-import interop between CommonJS and ES modules, so import express from "express" works for CommonJS packages without * as workarounds (it also implies allowSyntheticDefaultImports).
  • resolveJsonModule: Allows importing .json files.
  • baseUrl & paths: Configure absolute import paths.
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "noImplicitAny": true,
    "strictNullChecks": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "baseUrl": "./src",
    "paths": {
      "@utils/*": ["utils/*"]
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Source Maps & Output Targets
docs.text:
• sourceMap: true generates .map files for debugging in devtools.
  • outDir and rootDir control output and input folder structure.
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "sourceMap": true,
    "outDir": "./build",
    "rootDir": "./src"
  }
}
  • Run the compiler:
tsc --project tsconfig.json

docs.title: With Babel
docs.text:
• Use @babel/preset-typescript to strip types while using Babel.
npm install --save-dev @babel/preset-typescript

// babel.config.json
{
  "presets": ["@babel/preset-env", "@babel/preset-typescript"]
}

docs.title: With Webpack
docs.text:
• Use ts-loader or babel-loader with webpack.config.js.
module.exports = {
  entry: "./src/index.ts",
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.ts$/,
        use: "ts-loader",
        exclude: /node_modules/
      }
    ]
  },
  resolve: {
    extensions: [".ts", ".js"]
  },
  output: {
    filename: "bundle.js",
    path: __dirname + "/dist"
  }
};

docs.title: With Vite
docs.text:
• Vite has built-in TypeScript support; just use .ts files.
npm create vite@latest
# Choose framework, then select TypeScript template

// vite.config.ts (optional enhancements)
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';

export default defineConfig({
  build: {
    sourcemap: true
  }
});
